text: Mr. Noah Claypole: receiving a free pardon from the Crown in consequence of being admitted approver against Fagin: and considering his profession not altogether as safe a one as he could wish: was, for some little time, at a loss for the means of a livelihood, not burdened with too much work. After some consideration, he went into business as an informer, in which calling he realises a genteel subsistence. His plan is, to walk out once a week during church time attended by Charlotte in respectable attire. The lady faints away at the doors of charitable publicans, and the gentleman being accommodated with three-penny worth of brandy to restore her, lays an information next day, and pockets half the penalty. Sometimes Mr. Claypole faints himself, but the result is the same.

Having secured a full pardon for testifying against Fagin, and deciding his former criminal life was far too hazardous, Mr. Noah Claypole spent some time searching for an easy way to make ends meet. Eventually, he established himself as a professional informant, a trade that allows him to live quite comfortably. His routine involves walking out once a week during Sunday services with Charlotte, who dresses quite respectably for the occasion. She makes a show of fainting at the doorstep of a kind-hearted innkeeper, and when Noah is sold a small glass of brandy to help her recover, he reports the violation the next day and keeps half the fine. On occasion, Mr. Claypole performs the fainting spell himself, but the profit remains identical.

“I do, I do; but no baily—I deserved that place,” wailed Henery, signifying wasted genius by gazing blankly at visions of a high destiny apparently visible to him on Billy Smallbury’s smock-frock. “There, ’twas to be, I suppose. Your lot is your lot, and Scripture is nothing; for if you do good you don’t get rewarded according to your works, but be cheated in some mean way out of your recompense.”

“I do, I do; but no steward’s place for me—I earned that post fair and square,” wailed Tobit, marking his squandered gifts by staring blankly at the ghost of a grand destiny he seemed to see stretched across Jeb Merton’s frayed smock-frock. “Ah well, ’twas fated, I reckon. Your lot is your lot, and the Good Book’s nothing but empty words; for if you do good every day of your life, you don’t get rewarded according to your labors, just cheated out of your rightful due in some low, shabby trick.”

Where should she live? The obvious place was Blooms-End. The old rooms, it is true, were not much higher than the between-decks of a frigate, necessitating a sinking in the floor under the new clock-case she brought from the inn, and the removal of the handsome brass knobs on its head, before there was height for it to stand; but, such as the rooms were, there were plenty of them, and the place was endeared to her by every early recollection. Clym very gladly admitted her as a tenant, confining his own existence to two rooms at the top of the back staircase, where he lived on quietly, shut off from Thomasin and the three servants she had thought fit to indulge in now that she was a mistress of money, going his own ways, and thinking his own thoughts.

Where would she make her home? The obvious answer was Blooms-End. It was true the old rooms had barely more headroom than the between-decks of a frigate; the tall new clock case she’d brought from the inn could only fit after they’d sunk a small patch of the floor beneath it and removed the handsome brass knobs that crowned its top to make enough vertical space. But for all their minor inconveniences, the house had plenty of rooms to spare, and every inch of it was dear to her, woven through with her warmest early recollections. Clym welcomed her as a tenant gladly, restricting his own living quarters to two small rooms at the top of the back staircase. He lived quietly there, fully separated from Thomasin and the three servants she’d chosen to employ now that her fortune let her indulge such comforts, going about his own business and tending to his own private thoughts, undisturbed.

As Martin Eden went down the steps, his hand dropped into his coat pocket. It came out with a brown rice paper and a pinch of Mexican tobacco, which were deftly rolled together into a cigarette. He drew the first whiff of smoke deep into his lungs and expelled it in a long and lingering exhalation. “By God!” he said aloud, in a voice of awe and wonder. “By God!” he repeated. And yet again he murmured, “By God!” Then his hand went to his collar, which he ripped out of the shirt and stuffed into his pocket. A cold drizzle was falling, but he bared his head to it and unbuttoned his vest, swinging along in splendid unconcern. He was only dimly aware that it was raining. He was in an ecstasy, dreaming dreams and reconstructing the scenes just past.

As Adrian Vale descended the stairs, his hand slipped into his coat pocket. It emerged with a sheet of brown rice paper and a pinch of Mexican tobacco, which he quickly and skillfully rolled together into a cigarette. He drew the first breath of smoke deep into his lungs and let it go in one long, slow exhalation. “By God!” he cried aloud, in a tone of awe and amazement. “By God!” he said again. And once more he muttered, “By God!” Then his hand went to his collar, which he tore from the shirt and thrust into his pocket. A cold drizzle was falling, but he left his head bare beneath it and unfastened his vest, striding onward in magnificent unconcern. He was only vaguely conscious that it was raining. He was in an ecstasy, dreaming and remaking in his mind the scenes of a moment before.

They stood silently looking upon Eustacia, who, as she lay there still in death, eclipsed all her living phases. Pallor did not include all the quality of her complexion, which seemed more than whiteness; it was almost light. The expression of her finely carved mouth was pleasant, as if a sense of dignity had just compelled her to leave off speaking. Eternal rigidity had seized upon it in a momentary transition between fervour and resignation. Her black hair was looser now than either of them had ever seen it before, and surrounded her brow like a forest. The stateliness of look which had been almost too marked for a dweller in a country domicile had at last found an artistically happy background.

They stood in silence, gazing at Eustacia, who, lying there in death, surpassed all her living forms. Her pallor did not fully describe the complexion she had worn; it seemed something beyond mere whiteness, almost luminous. The expression of her delicately shaped mouth was gentle, as though some inward dignity had only just persuaded her to stop speaking. A timeless stillness had arrested it in a fleeting passage between ardour and surrender. Her black hair, looser than either of them had ever seen it, spread about her brow like a forest. The proud bearing of her face, which had once seemed almost too pronounced for life in a country home, had now at last found a background fit for it, and one strangely and artistically beautiful.

_Your boy, as no doubt you know, dreamt constantly of such a world peace as this that I foreshadow; he was more generous than his country. He could envisage war and hostility only as misunderstanding. He thought that a world that could explain itself clearly would surely be at peace. He was scheming always therefore for the perfection and propagation of Esperanto or Ido, or some such universal link. My youngster too was full of a kindred and yet larger dream, the dream of human science, which knows neither king nor country nor race_....

Your son, as you must be aware, was perpetually dreaming of a global harmony similar to the one I am envisioning; he possessed a generosity of spirit that transcended his own nation. To him, war and enmity were simply the results of profound misunderstanding. He was convinced that a world capable of articulating its thoughts clearly would inevitably live in peace. This is why he was always strategizing to improve and spread a universal language, such as Esperanto or Ido, to serve as a common bond. My own child was driven by a related, perhaps even more expansive, ambition: the ideal of a unified human science, a pursuit that acknowledges no sovereign, no border, and no racial divide.

“When they had been separated for some time,” returned Mr. Brownlow, “and your mother, wholly given up to continental frivolities, had utterly forgotten the young husband ten good years her junior, who, with prospects blighted, lingered on at home, he fell among new friends. This circumstance, at least, you know already.”

“When they had been parted for some time,” returned Mr. Brownlow, “and your mother, having surrendered herself entirely to continental frivolities, had quite forgotten the young husband who was a full ten years her junior, and who, with his prospects ruined, remained at home in misery, he fell in with new acquaintances. This much, at any rate, you already know.”

For many days, Oliver remained in the Jew’s room, picking the marks out of the pocket-handkerchief, (of which a great number were brought home,) and sometimes taking part in the game already described: which the two boys and the Jew played, regularly, every morning. At length, he began to languish for fresh air, and took many occasions of earnestly entreating the old gentleman to allow him to go out to work with his two companions.

For many days, Oliver stayed confined to the Jew’s chamber, diligently removing the stitched marks from pocket-handkerchiefs—great bundles of which were delivered to the house—and occasionally joining in the morning game already described, which the two boys and the Jew observed without fail. In time, he began to yearn for open air and seized every chance to implore the old gentleman to let him venture out to work alongside his two companions.

The attendant did as she was told: shaking her head meanwhile, to intimate that the woman would not die so easily; having done so, she resumed her seat by the side of the other nurse, who had by this time returned. The mistress, with an expression of impatience, wrapped herself in her shawl, and sat at the foot of the bed.

The servant performed the task as bid, shaking her head to suggest the patient would not expire so quickly; having finished, she sat back down beside the fellow-watcher, who had by then returned. The mistress, with a look of irritation, pulled her shawl tight and took a seat at the bed’s end.

“To give him his due he’s a clever, learned fellow in his way—a’most as clever as Clym Yeobright used to be. He was brought up to better things than keeping the Quiet Woman. An engineer—that’s what the man was, as we know; but he threw away his chance, and so ’a took a public house to live. His learning was no use to him at all.”

To give him his fair due, he’s a sharp, well-learned fellow in his own line—nearly as quick-witted as Lorne Hale used to be back in his heyday. He was raised for far finer things than tending the Resting Hare. A civil engineer, that’s what the man was, as all of us around here know; but he squandered his chance, so he took on running a public house to make his living. All his years of learning never served him a single bit of good at all.

He put his automobile in the garage, and then went round under the black shadow of his cedars to the front door. He had no key, and for a long time he failed to rouse his wife by flinging pebbles and gravel at her half-open window. But at last he heard her stirring and called out to her.

He pulled his car into the garage, then circled around the side of the house under the deep, inky shadow cast by his cedar trees to get to the front door. He didn’t have his key on him, and for what felt like ages, he couldn’t rouse his wife no matter how many pebbles and bits of loose gravel he tossed at her half-open bedroom window. Finally, though, he caught the faint sound of her stirring inside, and called up to her.

But he found her in the hospital very much hurt indeed. She had been smashed in some complicated manner that left the upper part of her body intact, and lying slantingly upon pillows. Over the horror of bandaged broken limbs and tormented flesh below sheets and a counterpane were drawn. Morphia had been injected, he understood, to save her from pain, but presently it might be necessary for her to suffer. She lay up in her bed with an effect of being enthroned, very white and still, her strong profile with its big nose and her straggling hair and a certain dignity gave her the appearance of some very important, very old man, of an aged pope for instance, rather than of an old woman. She had made no remark after they had set her and dressed her and put her to bed except "send for Hughie Britling, The Dower House, Matching's Easy. He is the best of the bunch." She had repeated the address and this commendation firmly over and over again, in large print as it were, even after they had assured her that a telegram had been despatched.

But when he reached the hospital, he found her badly injured indeed. She had been crushed in some intricate way that had left the upper part of her body unharmed, propped at an angle among the pillows. Beneath the sheets and counterpane, the horror of bandaged, shattered limbs and tortured flesh was hidden from view. Morphia, he understood, had been given to spare her pain, though before long she might have to endure it. She lay there in bed with a look almost of being enthroned, very pale and motionless, her strong profile, prominent nose, untidy hair, and a certain dignity making her seem less like an old woman than some immensely important, very ancient man, an aged pope perhaps. After they had lifted her, dressed her, and put her to bed, she had said nothing except, “Send for Hughie Britling, The Dower House, Matching’s Easy. He is the best of the bunch.” She had gone on repeating the address and that praise steadily, as if in capital letters, even after they had told her a telegram had already been sent.

"Three thousand years ahead perhaps," said Mr. Britling, "there will still be men with the same sadness.... And yet--and yet.... No. Just now I have no elasticity. It is not in my nature to despair, but things are pressing me down. I don't recover as I used to recover. I tell myself still that though the way is long and hard the spirit of hope, the spirit of creation, the generosities and gallantries in the heart of man, must end in victory. But I say that over as one repeats a worn-out prayer. The light is out of the sky for me. Sometimes I doubt if it will ever come back. Let younger men take heart and go on with the world. If I could die for the right thing now--instead of just having to live on in this world of ineffective struggle--I would be glad to die now, Carmine...."

“I confess, my son, that I have always looked forward to your marriage with our dear Elizabeth as the tie of our domestic comfort and the stay of my declining years. You were attached to each other from your earliest infancy; you studied together, and appeared, in dispositions and tastes, entirely suited to one another. But so blind is the experience of man that what I conceived to be the best assistants to my plan may have entirely destroyed it. You, perhaps, regard her as your sister, without any wish that she might become your wife. Nay, you may have met with another whom you may love; and considering yourself as bound in honour to Elizabeth, this struggle may occasion the poignant misery which you appear to feel.”

“I’ll be frank with you, my son: I have long held out hope that your marriage to our dear Elizabeth would be the thread that knits our household’s comfort tight, and a steady support for me as I enter my final years. The two of you were fond of one another from earliest babyhood, learned side by side, and seemed perfectly matched in every turn of temperament and every personal taste. But human foresight is so painfully blind that the very details I thought would ensure this plan’s success may have undone it completely. You may see Elizabeth as nothing more than your sister, with no desire to ever make her your wife. What’s more, you might have met another woman you love, and believing you are honor-bound to Elizabeth, that internal struggle could be the cause of the sharp, gnawing misery you’ve been carrying so plainly of late.”

“It is this,” said Kraft, punctuating in a puddle of spilled coffee with a stiff forefinger. “Caesar had his Brutus—the cotton has its bollworm, the chorus girl has her Pittsburger, the summer boarder has his poison ivy, the hero has his Carnegie medal, art has its Morgan, the rose has its—”

“It comes to this,” said Harren, tapping with a rigid forefinger at a little pool of spilled coffee. “Caesar had his Brutus—the cotton plant has its bollworm, the chorus girl has her Pittsburg man, the summer boarder has his poison ivy, the hero has his Carnegie medal, art has its Morgan, the rose has its—”

“Let me think,” said Mrs. Hurst, poking the fire superfluously.... “Yes—bless you, ever so many young men. You see, Farmer Oak, she’s so good-looking, and an excellent scholar besides—she was going to be a governess once, you know, only she was too wild. Not that her young men ever come here—but, Lord, in the nature of women, she must have a dozen!”

“Let me think a second,” Mrs. Hurst said, prodding at the fire for no real purpose.... “Oh right—bless your heart, there are loads of young men interested in her. Look, Farmer Oak, she’s so pretty, and a really sharp student on top of that—she was going to become a governess once, you know? Only she was way too wild for that kind of rigid, quiet work. It’s not like any of those suitors ever turn up around here, mind you—but come on, that’s just how women are, isn’t it? She must have a whole dozen of them on the line!”

Between these various excursions--they took him round and about the world, so to speak, they cast him away on tropical beaches, they left him dismasted on desolate seas, they involved the most startling interventions and the most inconvenient consequences--there were interludes of penetrating philosophy. For some years the suspicion had been growing up in Mr. Britling's mind that in planting this persuasion in his being, the mysterious processes of Nature had been, perhaps for some purely biological purpose, pulling, as people say, his leg, that there were not these perfect responses, that loving a woman is a thing one does thoroughly once for all--or so--and afterwards recalls regrettably in a series of vain repetitions, and that the career of the Pilgrim of Love, so soon as you strip off its credulous glamour, is either the most pitiful or the most vulgar and vile of perversions from the proper conduct of life. But this suspicion had not as yet grown to prohibitive dimensions with him, it was not sufficient to resist the seasons of high tide, the sudden promise of the salt-edged breeze, the invitation of the hovering sea-bird; and he was now concealing beneath the lively surface of activities with which Mr. Direck was now familiar, a very extensive system of distresses arising out of the latest, the eighth of these digressional adventures....

These assorted exploits carried him on what felt like a full circuit of the globe, stranding him on sun-scorched tropical shores, leaving him adrift in a mastless, battered craft on empty, desolate seas, and tangling him up in the most shocking last-minute interventions and wildly inconvenient aftereffects. In the lulls between these misadventures, he would sink into stretches of sharp, incisive philosophical reflection.
For years, a quiet doubt had been building in Mr. Britling’s mind: that in searing this all-consuming drive for romance into his core, the unfathomable workings of nature had been, to use the common turn of phrase, pulling his leg for some mere biological end. He had come to suspect there were no perfectly matched, reciprocal connections after all, that to love a woman deeply is a feat one pulls off fully once, maybe twice at most, and every subsequent attempt is little more than a pale, regretful echo of that first feeling, a string of hollow repetitions. He’d grown to think that the whole career of the so-called Pilgrim of Love, once you strip away the starry-eyed, naive gloss people paint over it, is either the most pitiable farce or the cheapest, crassest deviation from how a person ought to conduct their life.
But this doubt had not yet grown large enough to curb his impulses entirely. It was no match for those sudden high tides of longing, the sharp, briny promise of a salt-kissed wind off the water, the quiet call of the seagull circling low overhead. Right now, beneath the energetic, bustling facade of the daily routines Mr. Direck knew so well, he was hiding a vast, tangled web of heartache and trouble, all sprung from his most recent, eighth foray into these romantic digressions.

She thanked him incoherently, as if for a favor. And it was not until she had returned home to North Oakland and conferred with her own kind, and had the Portuguese grocer investigate, that she really knew that she was the owner of the little house in which she had lived and for which she had paid rent so long.

Her gratitude spilled out in broken phrases, like someone acknowledging an unexpected kindness. Only after she went back to her place in North Oakland, spoke with her own people, and sent the Portuguese shopkeeper to look into the matter, did the truth settle upon her—that the modest home she had inhabited for years, paying rent month after month, now belonged to her.

The traveller fared slowly on his way, who fared towards Paris from England in the autumn of the year one thousand seven hundred and ninety-two. More than enough of bad roads, bad equipages, and bad horses, he would have encountered to delay him, though the fallen and unfortunate King of France had been upon his throne in all his glory; but, the changed times were fraught with other obstacles than these. Every town-gate and village taxing-house had its band of citizen-patriots, with their national muskets in a most explosive state of readiness, who stopped all comers and goers, cross-questioned them, inspected their papers, looked for their names in lists of their own, turned them back, or sent them on, or stopped them and laid them in hold, as their capricious judgment or fancy deemed best for the dawning Republic One and Indivisible, of Liberty, Equality, Fraternity, or Death.

The traveller made his way slowly, he who journeyed toward Paris from England in the autumn of the year seventeen hundred and ninety-two. He would have found delay enough in bad roads, bad conveyances, and bad horses, even had the fallen and unhappy King of France still sat upon his throne in all his splendour; but the altered times presented hindrances of another sort besides these. At every town gate and village tollhouse stood their bands of citizen-patriots, national muskets in a most ready and explosive condition, stopping all who came and went, questioning them closely, examining their papers, searching for their names in lists of their own, sending them back, or onward, or into custody, as their whim or caprice dictated, in the service of the dawning Republic One and Indivisible, of Liberty, Equality, Fraternity, or Death.

“We have other plans for you, Ruth, dear, your father and I—oh, no, no; no man picked out for you, or anything like that. Our plans go no farther than your marrying some man in your own station in life, a good and honorable gentleman, whom you will select yourself, when you love him.”

“We’ve got something else in mind for you, Ruth, darling—your father and I—oh, no, no; not some fellow we’ve chosen for you, nothing of that sort. Our hopes don’t stretch beyond you wedding a man of your own standing, a decent and upright gentleman, someone you’ll pick for yourself, once you’re in love with him.”

“You forget how I talked when you first met me. I have learned a new language since then. Before that time I talked as that girl talks. Now I can manage to make myself understood sufficiently in your language to explain that you do not know that other girl’s language. And do you know why she carries herself the way she does? I think about such things now, though I never used to think about them, and I am beginning to understand—much.”

“You don’t recall how I spoke when we first crossed paths. I’ve picked up a whole different way of speaking since those days. Back then, I sounded just like that girl does. Now I’ve gotten good enough in your tongue to tell you that you don’t grasp the language she’s using. And have you any idea why she holds herself the way she does? I ponder such matters nowadays, though I never used to, and I’m starting to see—quite a bit.”

“It was on one of these days, when my cottagers periodically rested from labour—the old man played on his guitar, and the children listened to him—that I observed the countenance of Felix was melancholy beyond expression; he sighed frequently, and once his father paused in his music, and I conjectured by his manner that he inquired the cause of his son’s sorrow. Felix replied in a cheerful accent, and the old man was recommencing his music when someone tapped at the door.

It fell on one of those soft afternoons when the small cottage household took their weekly rest from field work and fence mending—the gray-bearded patriarch plucked gently at his mandolin, the two young grandchildren perched cross-legged on the hearthrug hanging on every note—that I noticed Elias’s face held a quiet melancholy too heavy to mask. He huffed frequent, ragged sighs, and once his grandfather stilled his fingers on the instrument’s strings; I guessed from the soft tilt of his head that he was asking what weighed on his grandson’s mind. Elias answered in a bright, forced tone, and the old man had just lifted his hands to resume playing when a sharp rap sounded on the cottage door.

Within a hundred miles, and in the light of other fires, there were other functionaries less fortunate, that night and other nights, whom the rising sun found hanging across once-peaceful streets, where they had been born and bred; also, there were other villagers and townspeople less fortunate than the mender of roads and his fellows, upon whom the functionaries and soldiery turned with success, and whom they strung up in their turn. But, the fierce figures were steadily wending East, West, North, and South, be that as it would; and whosoever hung, fire burned. The altitude of the gallows that would turn to water and quench it, no functionary, by any stretch of mathematics, was able to calculate successfully.

Within a hundred-mile radius, illuminated by the glow of distant blazes, other officials met grimmer fates that night and on many nights thereafter—dawn discovered them dangling above streets once tranquil, in the very towns where they’d grown up. Likewise, there were other peasants and townsfolk less lucky than the road-mender and his companions, against whom the authorities and soldiers prevailed, stringing them up in retaliation. Yet the relentless figures pressed steadily onward in every direction, regardless; and no matter who swung, the fires kept burning. No official, no matter how clever his reckoning, could ever compute the height of the scaffold that might dissolve into a flood and extinguish the flames.

“You can’t discuss Spencer with me,” he cried. “You do not know any more about Spencer than do his own countrymen. But it is no fault of yours, I grant. It is just a phase of the contemptible ignorance of the times. I ran across a sample of it on my way here this evening. I was reading an essay by Saleeby on Spencer. You should read it. It is accessible to all men. You can buy it in any book-store or draw it from the public library. You would feel ashamed of your paucity of abuse and ignorance of that noble man compared with what Saleeby has collected on the subject. It is a record of shame that would shame your shame.

“You can’t talk to me about Spencer,” he burst out. “You understand no more of Spencer than his own compatriots do. But I don’t blame you, I admit. It’s simply one symptom of the disgraceful ignorance of our age. I stumbled across a specimen of it on my way here tonight. I was perusing an essay on Spencer by Saleeby. You ought to read it. It’s available to everyone. You can purchase it at any bookshop or borrow it from the public library. You’d feel mortified by your feeble insults and your ignorance of that great man, once you see what Saleeby has gathered on the matter. It’s a chronicle of disgrace that would make your disgrace blush.”

“’Twas that I didn’t know you in this light, mis’ess; and being a man of the mournfullest make, I was scared a little, that’s all. Oftentimes if you could see how terrible down I get in my mind, ’twould make ’ee quite nervous for fear I should die by my hand.”

“It was only that I did not know you in this light, ma’am; and being a man of the most melancholy cast, I was somewhat frightened, that was all. Many a time, if you could see how grievously cast down I grow in my spirits, it would set you to trembling, for fear I might come to take my own life.”

“By the two signs,” answers Tobin, trying to explain, “which ye display according to the reading of the Egyptian palmist from the sole of me hand, ye’ve been nominated to offset with good luck the lines of trouble leading to the nigger man and the blonde lady with her feet crossed in the boat, besides the financial loss of a dollar sixty-five, all so far fulfilled according to Hoyle.”

“By the two signs,” Tobin answers, trying to make himself clear, “which you are showing according to the Egyptian palmist’s reading of the sole of my hand, you’ve been selected to balance out with good luck the lines of trouble leading to the Negro man and the blonde lady with her feet crossed in the boat, along with the financial loss of a dollar sixty-five, all of it so far coming true according to Hoyle.”

At any other time, this remonstrance, and the tone in which it was delivered, would have had the desired effect; but the girl being really weak and exhausted, dropped her head over the back of the chair, and fainted, before Mr. Sikes could get out a few of the appropriate oaths with which, on similar occasions, he was accustomed to garnish his threats. Not knowing, very well, what to do, in this uncommon emergency; for Miss Nancy’s hysterics were usually of that violent kind which the patient fights and struggles out of, without much assistance; Mr. Sikes tried a little blasphemy: and finding that mode of treatment wholly ineffectual, called for assistance.

Under any other circumstances, his sharp rebuke and the aggressive tone he used would have had the intended impact. But the girl was so genuinely weak and spent that her head simply fell back against the chair; she lost consciousness before Mr. Sikes could even utter the usual string of curses he typically used to season his threats. He was quite unsure how to handle this unfamiliar crisis, as Nancy’s fits were normally the volatile kind that a person eventually thrashes through without much help. Sikes tried a bit of swearing, but finding that particular remedy entirely useless, he finally called out for assistance.

The days were too short. There was so much he wanted to study. He cut his sleep down to five hours and found that he could get along upon it. He tried four hours and a half, and regretfully came back to five. He could joyfully have spent all his waking hours upon any one of his pursuits. It was with regret that he ceased from writing to study, that he ceased from study to go to the library, that he tore himself away from that chart-room of knowledge or from the magazines in the reading-room that were filled with the secrets of writers who succeeded in selling their wares. It was like severing heart strings, when he was with Ruth, to stand up and go; and he scorched through the dark streets so as to get home to his books at the least possible expense of time. And hardest of all was it to shut up the algebra or physics, put note-book and pencil aside, and close his tired eyes in sleep. He hated the thought of ceasing to live, even for so short a time, and his sole consolation was that the alarm clock was set five hours ahead. He would lose only five hours anyway, and then the jangling bell would jerk him out of unconsciousness and he would have before him another glorious day of nineteen hours.

The days never stretched long enough for him. There was an endless list of subjects he ached to dig into. He trimmed his sleep down to five hours first, and found he could get by perfectly well on that amount of rest. When he tested four and a half, though, he had to grudgingly bump it back up to five. He would have gladly devoted every single waking minute to any one of his pursuits, if he could. It always stung to set his writing aside to hit the textbooks, to step away from his studies to head to the library, to tear himself away from that vault of reference materials or the reading room magazines crammed with trade secrets from writers who’d cracked the code of selling their work. When he was with Ruth, standing up to leave felt like yanking his own heartstrings out by the root; he’d race home down the dark streets as fast as he could, to cut down the time he lost away from his books. The hardest part of all, though, was shutting his algebra or physics textbooks, tucking his notebook and pencil to the side, and letting his heavy, tired eyes slip shut for sleep. He hated the thought of pausing his life even for that short stretch, and his only comfort was knowing his alarm was set to go off five hours later. He was only losing five hours, after all, and then that harsh jangling bell would yank him out of his unconscious haze, and he’d have a whole other glorious nineteen-hour day stretched out ahead of him.

He had many invitations to dinner, some of which he accepted. Persons got themselves introduced to him in order to invite him to dinner. And he went on puzzling over the little thing that was becoming a great thing. Bernard Higginbotham invited him to dinner. He puzzled the harder. He remembered the days of his desperate starvation when no one invited him to dinner. That was the time he needed dinners, and went weak and faint for lack of them and lost weight from sheer famine. That was the paradox of it. When he wanted dinners, no one gave them to him, and now that he could buy a hundred thousand dinners and was losing his appetite, dinners were thrust upon him right and left. But why? There was no justice in it, no merit on his part. He was no different. All the work he had done was even at that time work performed. Mr. and Mrs. Morse had condemned him for an idler and a shirk and through Ruth had urged that he take a clerk’s position in an office. Furthermore, they had been aware of his work performed. Manuscript after manuscript of his had been turned over to them by Ruth. They had read them. It was the very same work that had put his name in all the papers, and, it was his name being in all the papers that led them to invite him.

He received dozens of dinner invitations, and said yes to a handful of them. People went out of their way to get formally introduced to him just so they could extend a dinner invite. All the while, he kept turning over the same small, swelling puzzle in his mind. When Bernard Higginbotham invited him to dinner, that confusion only deepened. He thought back to the days when he was half-dead with starvation, when no one had ever asked him out to eat. That was when he’d needed those dinners most: he’d grown weak and lightheaded for lack of food, shedding pounds from sheer, unrelenting hunger. It was such a cruel paradox. When he’d been desperate for a hot meal, no one had offered him one. Now, when he could afford to buy a hundred thousand dinners on a whim and had even started losing his appetite for them, invites were being pressed on him from every direction. What was the reason? There was no fairness to it, no credit he had newly earned. He was exactly the same man he’d always been. Every line of the work he’d produced had already been written back in those hungry days. Mr. and Mrs. Morse had dismissed him as a lazy idler and a shirker, and had pushed Ruth to convince him to take a clerk’s position at an office. They’d even seen all that work of his, too: Ruth had handed them manuscript after manuscript, and they’d read each one. It was that exact same work that had gotten his name printed in every newspaper, and it was that very newspaper notoriety, not the work itself, that was leading them to invite him now.

Justine shook her head mournfully. “I do not fear to die,” she said; “that pang is past. God raises my weakness and gives me courage to endure the worst. I leave a sad and bitter world; and if you remember me and think of me as of one unjustly condemned, I am resigned to the fate awaiting me. Learn from me, dear lady, to submit in patience to the will of heaven!”

Justine shook her head in sorrow. “I no longer fear the end,” she said; “that particular agony has passed. God has bolstered my frailty, granting me the fortitude to face this ultimate trial. I depart from a world of grief and bitterness; if you will only keep me in your heart as one wrongly convicted, I can accept the destiny before me. Let my example show you, dear lady, how to yield with patience to the divine will.”

Not a single bleat. He went to the hedge; a gap had been broken through it, and in the gap were the footprints of the sheep. They were not in the plantation. He called again: the valleys and farthest hills resounded as when the sailors invoked the lost Hylas on the Mysian shore; but no sheep. He passed through the trees and along the ridge of the hill. On the extreme summit, where the ends of the two converging hedges of which we have spoken were stopped short by meeting the brow of the chalk-pit, he saw the younger dog standing against the sky—dark and motionless as Napoleon at St. Helena.

There was not a single bleat to be heard. He walked over to the hedgerow, where a gap had been torn through the thick, tangled growth, and sheep hoof prints were clearly pressed into the soft earth at the opening. He was rather surprised that the flock would break through fencing at this time of year, but he instantly chalked the behavior up to their well-documented fondness for ivy in the cold winter months, and a great deal of the vine grew thick in the plantation just beyond the hedge. He clambered through the gap. The sheep were nowhere to be seen in the plantation. He called out for them again, his voice bouncing off the valley walls and the farthest hilltops, ringing bright as the cries of sailors invoking the lost Hylas on the Mysian shore; still there was no sign of the flock, no answering bleat. He pushed through the tree line and tramped along the hill’s ridge. At the very summit, where the two converging hedges we have spoken of stop short where they meet the steep brow of the chalk pit, he spotted the younger dog silhouetted against the open sky—dark, utterly motionless, as stoic and still as Napoleon stranded on St. Helena.

And even though her attire was remarkably plain, he knew he would be thoroughly pleased to sit across a table from her.

So the preacher continued, speaking of different improvements, and lastly of the invention of daguerreotypes and photographs. He called the attention of his hearers to this almost miraculous art of indelibly fixing the expression of a countenance, and drew a lesson as to the permanent effect of our daily looks and expression on those among whom we live. He considered at length the vast amount of happiness which had been caused by bringing pictures of loved ones within the reach of all; the increase of family affection and general good feeling which must have resulted from the invention; he suggested a possible change in the civilization of the older nations through the constant sending home, by prosperous adopted citizens, of photographs of themselves and of their homes, and alluded to the effect which this must have had upon immigration.

The preacher pressed on, running through a range of technological advancements before landing finally on the invention of daguerreotypes and modern photography. He drew his audience’s attention to this nearly miraculous craft, which could capture and preserve the exact expression of a person’s face permanently, and wove in a moral lesson about the lasting impression our own everyday expressions and demeanor leave on everyone we live and interact with regularly. He spent considerable time outlining the enormous volume of joy the innovation had sparked by putting portraits of beloved friends and family within nearly everyone’s reach, and noted how much deeper familial bonds and broader communal goodwill must have grown as a direct result of the technology. He even floated the idea that the cultures of older, long-established nations might shift over time, as immigrants who’d found success in their adopted countries regularly sent home snapshots of themselves and their new households, and touched on how this trend had almost certainly had a measurable impact on immigration rates.

All the while that Mr. Britling had been indulging in these imaginative slaughterings and spending the tears and hate that had gathered in his heart, his reason had been sitting apart and above the storm, like the sun waiting above thunder, like a wise nurse watching and patient above the wild passions of a child. And all the time his reason had been maintaining silently and firmly, without shouting, without speech, that the men who had made this hour were indeed not devils, were no more devils than Mr. Britling was a devil, but sinful men of like nature with himself, hard, stupid, caught in the same web of circumstance. "Kill them in your passion if you will," said reason, "but understand. This thing was done neither by devils nor fools, but by a conspiracy of foolish motives, by the weak acquiescences of the clever, by a crime that was no man's crime but the natural necessary outcome of the ineffectiveness, the blind motives and muddleheadedness of all mankind."

All the time Mr. Britling had been lost in these mental massacres, pouring out the grief and fury that had pooled inside him, his reason had hovered detached above the turmoil—like the sun poised beyond a storm, like a calm nurse watching over a child's wild tantrums with patient stillness. And through it all, that reason had been holding its ground, quietly and unwaveringly, without outcry or declaration, insisting that the men who had brought about this moment were not fiends, no more fiends than Mr. Britling himself was a fiend, but flawed human beings of the same kind as he—stubborn, obtuse, tangled in the same nets of circumstance. "Rage against them, kill them in your heart if you must," reason seemed to say, "but see clearly. This was not the work of demons or fools, but of foolish impulses conspiring together, of clever people yielding weakly, of a crime that belonged to no single person but sprang inevitably from the fecklessness, the blind drives, the muddled thinking of all humanity."

It’s not like she was actually all that bothered by his refusal to play along with the unspoken rules. The clock ticked on. The women threw off their nervousness, and titters and giggling became more frequent. Then came a dead silence. Every one was waiting for the end. Some persons may have noticed how extraordinarily the striking of quarters seems to quicken the flight of time. It was hardly credible that the jack had not got wrong with the minutes when the rattle began again, the puppet emerged, and the four quarters were struck fitfully as before. One could almost be positive that there was a malicious leer upon the hideous creature’s face, and a mischievous delight in its twitchings. Then followed the dull and remote resonance of the twelve heavy strokes in the tower above. The women were impressed, and there was no giggle this time.

The wall-mounted timepiece ticked on unceasingly. The gathered women had shaken off their initial jitters by then, peals of snickering and breathless giggles rising louder by the minute. Abruptly, a dead hush fell over the room. Every last person held their breath, waiting for the final strike. Most folks know the strange trick quarter-hour chimes play, making time feel like it slips by far faster than it ought. It felt impossible that the gears hadn’t slipped and misaligned the minutes when the familiar rattle of the mechanism kicked off, the gnarled wooden jack popped out of its niche, and the four quarter chimes clanged stutteringly just as they always did. You could almost swear the grotesque little figure wore a sneer of petty malice on its carved face, that its jerky little movements hummed with unkind glee at the group’s tension. A moment later, the deep, thrumming boom of twelve heavy bell strokes rolled down from the stone church tower looming above the cottage. The women went rigid with quiet awe, and not a single giggle broke the heavy silence that followed.

Well, she certainly was a curious one, was Morgan le Fay. I have seen a good many kinds of women in my time, but she laid over them all for variety. And how sharply characteristic of her this episode was. She had no more idea than a horse of how to photograph a procession; but being in doubt, it was just like her to try to do it with an axe.

Oh, Morgan le Fay was undoubtedly one of the oddest souls I’ve ever known, no two ways about it. I’ve crossed paths with every sort of woman over the years, but none could hold a candle to her for sheer, unmatchable eccentricity. This little incident summed her up so sharply it might as well have had her name carved right into it. She didn’t have the faintest clue how to photograph a public procession, not a scrap more sense for it than a plow horse would; but when she was stuck for a way forward, grabbing an axe to get the job done was exactly the harebrained, signature move she’d make.

In the evening, at which season of all others Saint Antoine turned himself inside out, and sat on door-steps and window-ledges, and came to the corners of vile streets and courts, for a breath of air, Madame Defarge with her work in her hand was accustomed to pass from place to place and from group to group: a Missionary--there were many like her--such as the world will do well never to breed again. All the women knitted. They knitted worthless things; but, the mechanical work was a mechanical substitute for eating and drinking; the hands moved for the jaws and the digestive apparatus: if the bony fingers had been still, the stomachs would have been more famine-pinched.

At twilight, when the district of Saint Antoine turned itself out into the streets, its people sat on doorsteps and leaned from windows, seeking a breath of air in the wretched alleys. Madame Defarge moved among them with her knitting, passing from group to group like a dark apostle—one of a type the world would be wise never to produce again. All the women there were knitting. They crafted useless things, yet the repetitive motion served as a physical substitute for sustenance. Their fingers worked in place of their idle jaws; had those skeletal hands ever gone still, the gnawing ache of famine in their bellies would have been far more agonizing.

The lightning now was the colour of silver, and gleamed in the heavens like a mailed army. Rumbles became rattles. Gabriel from his elevated position could see over the landscape at least half-a-dozen miles in front. Every hedge, bush, and tree was distinct as in a line engraving. In a paddock in the same direction was a herd of heifers, and the forms of these were visible at this moment in the act of galloping about in the wildest and maddest confusion, flinging their heels and tails high into the air, their heads to earth. A poplar in the immediate foreground was like an ink stroke on burnished tin. Then the picture vanished, leaving the darkness so intense that Gabriel worked entirely by feeling with his hands.

A third bolt flared. Above, in the immense hollows of the sky, strange and violent maneuvers were unfolding. The lightning had turned silver now, glittering across the heavens like an armored legion. Rumbles sharpened into rattles. From his high vantage, Gabriel could survey at least six miles of the country before him. Every hedge, bush, and tree stood out with the precision of an engraving. In a paddock in that same direction, a herd of heifers was caught mid-gallop in the wildest, most frantic disorder, hooves and tails flung high, heads driven low. A poplar in the near foreground cut the scene like a stroke of ink on polished tin. Then the vision collapsed, and the darkness that followed was so absolute that Gabriel worked solely by touch.

I seemed to believe the boy, I didn't know why. _Something_ in me seemed to believe him--my consciousness, as you may say; but my reason didn't. My reason straightway began to clamor; that was natural. I didn't know how to go about satisfying it, because I knew that the testimony of men wouldn't serve--my reason would say they were lunatics, and throw out their evidence. But all of a sudden I stumbled on the very thing, just by luck. I knew that the only total eclipse of the sun in the first half of the sixth century occurred on the 21st of June, A.D. 528, O.S., and began at 3 minutes after 12 noon. I also knew that no total eclipse of the sun was due in what to _me_ was the present year--i.e., 1879. So, if I could keep my anxiety and curiosity from eating the heart out of me for forty-eight hours, I should then find out for certain whether this boy was telling me the truth or not.

I found myself trusting the scruffy young boy, for no logical reason I could name. Some quiet, unshakable part of me accepted his story outright—call it my core awareness, if you like—even as my rational mind pushed back immediately. My logical side raised a raucous, entirely justified protest. I had no idea how to verify his claim, because I knew any eyewitness accounts from other people would never satisfy my skepticism; my reason would just dismiss them all as deranged, tossing their testimony out entirely. Then out of nowhere, a perfect test occurred to me entirely by luck. I’d memorized years prior that the only total solar eclipse in the first half of the sixth century took place on June 21, 528 A.D. by the old calendar, starting precisely three minutes past noon. I also knew beyond doubt that no total solar eclipse was forecast for what I understood to be the current year, 1879. That meant, if I could keep my roiling anxiety and ravenous curiosity from gnawing me raw for forty-eight hours, I would soon have irrefutable proof of whether the boy had been telling the truth or not.

But, from the hour when she had taken the white head to her fresh young bosom in the garret of Saint Antoine, she had been true to her duties. She was truest to them in the season of trial, as all the quietly loyal and good will always be.

Had the sudden blow of her misfortune, and the dizzying, churning chaos of the era, left the Doctor’s daughter too stunned to do anything but wait for whatever came next in listless despair, her lot would have been no different from that of countless others. Yet ever since the moment she’d pressed that silvered head to her soft, youthful breast in the cramped Saint Antoine garret, she had never faltered in her obligations. And she honored those duties most faithfully in her season of hardship, just as all quietly loyal, good-hearted people unfailingly do.

The devastated wife of the innocent man newly condemned to die crumpled the moment the sentence was read, as though she had been struck by a mortal blow. Yet she uttered not a sound; the quiet voice deep within her was too firm, insisting that of all people on earth, she was the one who must uphold him in his misery rather than add to it, and that unshakable conviction lifted her almost at once, even out of the throes of that devastating shock.

It was a chill, damp, windy night, when the Jew: buttoning his great-coat tight round his shrivelled body, and pulling the collar up over his ears so as completely to obscure the lower part of his face: emerged from his den. He paused on the step as the door was locked and chained behind him; and having listened while the boys made all secure, and until their retreating footsteps were no longer audible, slunk down the street as quickly as he could.

When Boldwood went to bed he placed the valentine in the corner of the looking-glass. He was conscious of its presence, even when his back was turned upon it. It was the first time in Boldwood’s life that such an event had occurred. The same fascination that caused him to think it an act which had a deliberate motive prevented him from regarding it as an impertinence. He looked again at the direction. The mysterious influences of night invested the writing with the presence of the unknown writer. Somebody’s—some _woman’s_—hand had travelled softly over the paper bearing his name; her unrevealed eyes had watched every curve as she formed it; her brain had seen him in imagination the while. Why should she have imagined him? Her mouth—were the lips red or pale, plump or creased?—had curved itself to a certain expression as the pen went on—the corners had moved with all their natural tremulousness: what had been the expression?

When Marlowe had retired for the night, he set the valentine in the angle of the mirror. Even with his face turned away from it, he remained aware of its presence. Never before in his life had any such thing befallen him. The same enchantment that led him to believe the act must have been meant for a purpose kept him from treating it as an insult. He looked once more at the address. The dim powers of the night endowed the handwriting with the living nearness of the unknown sender. Someone’s—some _woman’s_—hand had moved with quiet touch across the sheet that bore his name; her hidden eyes had followed every line as she traced it; her mind, while doing so, had pictured him. Why had she pictured him? Her mouth—were the lips scarlet or colorless, full or finely wrinkled?—must have shaped itself to some peculiar expression as the pen advanced, the corners of it stirring with all their natural softness: what expression had it been?

Necessity is a thing beyond God--beyond good and ill, beyond space and time, a mystery everlastingly impenetrable. God is nearer than that. Greater than Nature or Necessity, for he is a spirit and they are blind, but not controlling them.... Not yet...."

Bang! One saddle empty. Bang! another one. Bang--bang, and I bagged two. Well, it was nip and tuck with us, and I knew it. If I spent the eleventh shot without convincing these people, the twelfth man would kill me, sure. And so I never did feel so happy as I did when my ninth downed its man and I detected the wavering in the crowd which is premonitory of panic. An instant lost now could knock out my last chance. But I didn't lose it. I raised both revolvers and pointed them--the halted host stood their ground just about one good square moment, then broke and fled.

Crack! One rider tumbled from his saddle. Crack! a second followed. Crack—crack, and I had dropped two more. The contest was balanced on a knife's edge, and I was fully aware of it. If I fired my eleventh round without breaking their resolve, the twelfth man would surely be the death of me. So you can imagine the rush of relief when my ninth shot brought down its target and I caught that subtle tremor in the group that signals the onset of panic. A single wasted second now could forfeit my final opportunity. But I didn't waste it. I leveled both pistols and took aim—the stalled assembly held firm for just about one solid, square instant, then shattered and scattered in flight.

They sheared in the great barn, called for the nonce the Shearing-barn, which on ground-plan resembled a church with transepts. It not only emulated the form of the neighbouring church of the parish, but vied with it in antiquity. Whether the barn had ever formed one of a group of conventual buildings nobody seemed to be aware; no trace of such surroundings remained. The vast porches at the sides, lofty enough to admit a waggon laden to its highest with corn in the sheaf, were spanned by heavy-pointed arches of stone, broadly and boldly cut, whose very simplicity was the origin of a grandeur not apparent in erections where more ornament has been attempted. The dusky, filmed, chestnut roof, braced and tied in by huge collars, curves, and diagonals, was far nobler in design, because more wealthy in material, than nine-tenths of those in our modern churches. Along each side wall was a range of striding buttresses, throwing deep shadows on the spaces between them, which were perforated by lancet openings, combining in their proportions the precise requirements both of beauty and ventilation.

The annual sheep shearing took place inside the sprawling outbuilding known that season only as the Clipping Shed, whose floor plan mirrored a Gothic parish church complete with projecting transepts. It not only echoed the silhouette of the small stone church that served the surrounding hamlet, but rivaled it in centuries of weathered history. No local could say for certain if the barn had once stood among a cluster of medieval convent outbuildings; every trace of those earlier grounds had long eroded away. The deep overhanging porches on either flank, tall enough to fit a farm cart piled high with unthreshed wheat sheaves, were framed by heavy pointed stone arches cut in broad, unadorned strokes, their very simplicity lending a stately grandeur absent in far more ornate later constructions. Its deep nut-brown roof, dusted with a fine age-born film and reinforced by massive timber collars, curved braces, and crosswise diagonal beams, boasted a far more dignified design, owing to its lavish old-growth materials, than fully nine out of ten contemporary church roofs. Running the length of each side wall stood a row of sturdy, projecting buttresses that cast long, deep shadows across the recesses between them, each recess pierced by a narrow lancet opening whose balanced dimensions met the exact dual needs of aesthetic grace and steady cross-ventilation.

But why should I dwell upon the incidents that followed this last overwhelming event? Mine has been a tale of horrors; I have reached their _acme_, and what I must now relate can but be tedious to you. Know that, one by one, my friends were snatched away; I was left desolate. My own strength is exhausted, and I must tell, in a few words, what remains of my hideous narration.

Yet what reason have I to linger over the incidents that followed that final, world-shattering blow? Every word I have spoken so far has been stitched from dread and bloodshed; I have already scaled the highest, most ghoulish peak of this nightmare, and the scraps left to tell will almost certainly feel hollow and wearisome to you. Understand this, if nothing else: my dearest companions were torn from my side one after the next, until no one remained but me, adrift in utter ruin. I have no more strength left to spin out the story at length, so I will give you the last of this ghastly account as briefly as I am able.

He made his way up to the house. It was still so early that none of the household’s residents showed the slightest sign of stirring. Oliver came to a stop and peered into the garden. A small boy was pulling weeds from one of the tiny planting beds, and when he caught sight of Oliver’s figure, he lifted his pale face, revealing the features of one of Oliver’s old companions. A soft rush of gladness washed over Oliver at getting to see him before he left: though the boy was a few years younger than him, he had once been his dearest little friend and playmate. More times than they could keep track of, the two of them had been beaten, half-starved, and shut up together in the same cramped, cold space.

Under that strain the dignity of England broke, and revealed a malignity less focussed and intense than the German, but perhaps even more distressing. No paternal government had organised the British spirit for patriotic ends; it became now peevish and impatient, like some ill-trained man who is sick, it directed itself no longer against the enemy alone but fitfully against imagined traitors and shirkers; it wasted its energies in a deepening and spreading net of internal squabbles and accusations. Now it was the wily indolence of the Prime Minister, now it was the German culture of the Lord Chancellor, now the imaginative enterprise of the First Lord of the Admiralty that focussed a vindictive campaign. There began a hunt for spies and of suspects of German origin in every quarter except the highest; a denunciation now of "traitors," now of people with imaginations, now of scientific men, now of the personal friend of the Commander-in-Chief, now of this group and then of that group.... Every day Mr. Britling read his three or four newspapers with a deepening disappointment.

Beneath the crushing weight of the conflict, the composure of the realm splintered, revealing a spite that was less methodical than that of the enemy, yet perhaps more unsettling. Because no state hand had molded the national character for the service of the Crown, the public spirit turned irritable and frantic, much like a sick man who has never learned self-restraint. It ceased to strike only at the invader, choosing instead to lash out at imagined turncoats and slackers; it dissipated its strength in a tangled, widening thicket of domestic feuds and recriminations. At one moment, the perceived lethargy of the Premier was the target; at another, the continental sympathies of the High Chancellor, or the reckless daring of the Naval Secretary. A frantic search for infiltrators and those of foreign extraction began in every corner of the land, though it stopped short of the aristocracy. There were outbursts against "subversives," against men of intellect, against scientists, and even the intimate associates of the Field Marshal. Day after day, Arthur Penhaligon studied the morning papers, his spirit sinking deeper into a profound and heavy disillusionment.

The grace of her home, so starkly opposed to the grimness of his own quarters, joined with her simple proximity to renew his vow to conquer the world. Though he possessed a natural affinity for beauty and a raw hunger to craft it, his labors were dedicated solely to her. He was, before all else, a suitor. All other aspirations were but servants to his passion.

"But now see what a good bruddykins I am. I have seen a fight, a real exciting fight, and I have kept it to the last to tell you about.... It was a fight in the air. And the British won. It began with a German machine appearing, very minute and high, sailing towards our lines a long way to the left. We could tell it was a German because of the black cross; they decorate every aeroplane with a black Iron Cross on its wings and tail; that our officer could see with his glasses. (He let me look.) Suddenly whack, whack, whack, came a line of little puffs of smoke behind it, and then one in front of it, which meant that our anti-aircraft guns were having a go at it. Then, as suddenly, Archibald stopped, and we could see the British machine buzzing across the path of the German. It was just like two birds circling in the air. Or wasps. They buzzed like wasps. There was a little crackling--like brushing your hair in frosty weather. They were shooting at each other. Then our lieutenant called out, 'Hit, by Jove!' and handed the glasses to Park and instantly wanted them back. He says he saw bits of the machine flying off.

“But now just see what a good little bruddykins I am. I’ve seen a fight, a proper thrilling fight, and I’ve saved it till last to tell you about.... It was a fight in the air. And the British won. It started with a German machine appearing, very tiny and high up, gliding toward our lines a long way off to the left. We could tell it was German because of the black cross; they put a black Iron Cross on the wings and tail of every aeroplane, and our officer could make it out through his glasses. (He let me have a look.) Then suddenly, whack, whack, whack, a row of little smoke puffs came behind it, and then one in front of it, which meant our anti-aircraft guns were having a shot at it. Then, just as suddenly, Archibald broke off, and we could see the British machine buzzing across the German’s path. It was exactly like two birds wheeling in the sky. Or wasps. They buzzed like wasps. There was a faint crackling sound—like brushing your hair in frosty weather. They were firing at one another. Then our lieutenant cried out, ‘Hit, by Jove!’ and gave the glasses to Park, then at once wanted them back. He says he saw pieces flying off the machine.”

For a long time I was their only care. My mother had much desired to have a daughter, but I continued their single offspring. When I was about five years old, while making an excursion beyond the frontiers of Italy, they passed a week on the shores of the Lake of Como. Their benevolent disposition often made them enter the cottages of the poor. This, to my mother, was more than a duty; it was a necessity, a passion—remembering what she had suffered, and how she had been relieved—for her to act in her turn the guardian angel to the afflicted. During one of their walks a poor cot in the foldings of a vale attracted their notice as being singularly disconsolate, while the number of half-clothed children gathered about it spoke of penury in its worst shape. One day, when my father had gone by himself to Milan, my mother, accompanied by me, visited this abode. She found a peasant and his wife, hard working, bent down by care and labour, distributing a scanty meal to five hungry babes. Among these there was one which attracted my mother far above all the rest. She appeared of a different stock. The four others were dark-eyed, hardy little vagrants; this child was thin and very fair. Her hair was the brightest living gold, and despite the poverty of her clothing, seemed to set a crown of distinction on her head. Her brow was clear and ample, her blue eyes cloudless, and her lips and the moulding of her face so expressive of sensibility and sweetness that none could behold her without looking on her as of a distinct species, a being heaven-sent, and bearing a celestial stamp in all her features.

For years I stood as the sole focus of my parents’ devotion. My mother had long ached for a little girl to raise alongside me, but I remained their only child for nearly a decade. When I was about five years old, while we were traveling through the borderlands of southern France, my parents spent a week camped along the shores of Lac d’Annecy. Their generous natures often drew them to stop at the humble cottages of local peasant families. For my mother, these visits were far more than an obligation; they were a compulsion, a deep calling, for she remembered her own childhood of hardship, and the strangers who had lifted her out of it, and she longed to pay that kindness forward as a guardian to those suffering misfortune. On one of their afternoon rambles, a ragged little cottage tucked into the crook of a valley caught their eye, looking unusually forlorn, the cluster of shivering, half-dressed children loitering on its threshold making plain the family was crippled by dire poverty. One day, when my father had traveled alone to Grenoble on business, my mother brought me with her to call on the household. Inside, she found a farmer and his wife, both hardworking but bowed low by constant worry and backbreaking toil, doling out a meager crust of bread and thin broth to their five ravenous small children. Of all the children, one caught my mother’s gaze instantly, holding it far longer than the rest. She seemed to come from an entirely different line. The four other children had dark, flashing eyes and the rough, sturdy bearing of little urchins used to running wild in the hills; this girl was slight, and pale as a lily blossom. Her hair was the color of spun sunlight, and even tangled and unkempt beneath her tattered shawl, it looked like a gilded crown marking her apart. Her forehead was smooth and broad, her blue eyes as clear as a summer sky, and the curve of her lips and the soft shape of her face held such gentle warmth and quiet sensitivity that no one could look at her without thinking she was something other than ordinary, a creature sent down from the heavens, every line of her face bearing the mark of something divine.

Latterly he had had much sleepless misery. In the day life was tolerable, but in the night--unless he defended himself by working, the losses and cruelties of the war came and grimaced at him, insufferably. Now he would be haunted by long processions of refugees, now he would think of the dead lying stiff and twisted in a thousand dreadful attitudes. Then again he would be overwhelmed with anticipations of the frightful economic and social dissolution that might lie ahead.... At other times he thought of wounds and the deformities of body and spirit produced by injuries. And sometimes he would think of the triumph of evil. Stupid and triumphant persons went about a world that stupidity had desolated, with swaggering gestures, with a smiling consciousness of enhanced importance, with their scornful hatred of all measured and temperate and kindly things turned now to scornful contempt. And mingling with the soil they walked on lay the dead body of Hugh, face downward. At the back of the boy's head, rimmed by blood-stiffened hair--the hair that had once been "as soft as the down of a bird"--was a big red hole. That hole was always pitilessly distinct. They stepped on him--heedlessly. They heeled the scattered stuff of his exquisite brain into the clay....

Lately, sleep had become a torment. By daylight, existence was bearable, but at night—unless he fended it off with work—the war’s losses and brutalities would leer at him, unendurable. Sometimes he was pursued by endless columns of refugees; other times he pictured the dead, rigid and contorted in a thousand hideous poses. Then he would be swamped by visions of the catastrophic economic and social collapse that might be coming. At still other moments, he brooded on wounds and the mutilations of flesh and spirit that injury inflicted. And occasionally he would dwell on the victory of wickedness. Stupid, triumphant figures strutted through a world that stupidity had laid waste, swaggering, smiling with a smug sense of their own swollen importance, their disdainful hatred of all that was measured, moderate, and gentle now hardened into scornful contempt. And trampled into the dirt they walked on lay Hugh’s body, facedown. At the back of the boy’s head, fringed with hair stiffened by blood—hair that had once been “soft as a bird’s down”—was a large red cavity. That cavity was always pitilessly sharp. They stepped on him—unthinking. They ground the scattered fragments of his fine mind into the mud.

Our new money was not only handsomely circulating, but its language was already glibly in use; that is to say, people had dropped the names of the former moneys, and spoke of things as being worth so many dollars or cents or mills or milrays now. It was very gratifying. We were progressing, that was sure.

Our new coinage was not merely passing hands in fine style, but its vocabulary had already slipped quite readily into common speech; that is to say, the old denominations had been forgotten, and people now talked of things as costing so many dollars, or cents, or mills, or milrays. It was highly gratifying. We were advancing, there could be no doubt of that.

Idealism had never attracted Martin, but the exposition it now received at the hands of Norton was a revelation. The logical plausibility of it, that made an appeal to his intellect, seemed missed by Kreis and Hamilton, who sneered at Norton as a metaphysician, and who, in turn, sneered back at them as metaphysicians. _Phenomenon_ and _noumenon_ were bandied back and forth. They charged him with attempting to explain consciousness by itself. He charged them with word-jugglery, with reasoning from words to theory instead of from facts to theory. At this they were aghast. It was the cardinal tenet of their mode of reasoning to start with facts and to give names to the facts.

Martin had never felt any affinity for idealism, yet the way Norton articulated it proved to be a profound awakening. Its logical coherence spoke directly to Martin’s intellect, a merit seemingly lost on Kreis and Hamilton, who mocked Norton as a mere metaphysician—only for him to hurl the same insult back at them. Concepts of phenomenon and noumenon were traded like blows. They indicted him for trying to account for consciousness using only itself, while he accused them of linguistic trickery, claiming they derived theories from vocabulary rather than reality. This charge left them horrified, for it was the fundamental principle of their logic to begin with raw facts and only then assign them names.

As the circumstances of his marriage illustrate his character, I cannot refrain from relating them. One of his most intimate friends was a merchant who, from a flourishing state, fell, through numerous mischances, into poverty. This man, whose name was Beaufort, was of a proud and unbending disposition and could not bear to live in poverty and oblivion in the same country where he had formerly been distinguished for his rank and magnificence. Having paid his debts, therefore, in the most honourable manner, he retreated with his daughter to the town of Lucerne, where he lived unknown and in wretchedness. My father loved Beaufort with the truest friendship and was deeply grieved by his retreat in these unfortunate circumstances. He bitterly deplored the false pride which led his friend to a conduct so little worthy of the affection that united them. He lost no time in endeavouring to seek him out, with the hope of persuading him to begin the world again through his credit and assistance.

Since the particulars of his union lay bare the core of his nature, I cannot hold myself back from recounting them. One of his dearest confidants was a trader who, once at the height of commercial success, had been reduced to penury by a string of unforeseen calamities. This man, Theron by name, carried a stubborn, unyielding streak, and refused to scrape by in obscurity and want in the very nation where he had once been celebrated for his status and lavish generosity. After settling every last one of his debts with scrupulous integrity, he retreated with his only daughter to the city of Innsbruck, where he passed his days unrecognized and mired in hardship. My father cherished Theron with the most sincere of bonds, and was profoundly aggrieved that his friend would flee in the face of such misfortune. He railed against the misplaced pride that had driven Theron to act in a manner so unworthy of the deep affection that bound the two men together. He wasted no time in trying to track him down, hoping to convince him to rebuild his livelihood with the support of his connections and financial backing.

"Meanwhile I've got something to tell you. I've been through a fight, a big fight, and I haven't got a scratch. I've taken two prisoners with my lily hand. Men were shot close to me. I didn't mind that a bit. It was as exciting as one of those bitter fights we used to have round the hockey goal. I didn't mind anything till afterwards. Then when I was in the trench in the evening I trod on something slippery--pah! And after it was all over one of my chums got it--sort of unfairly. And I keep on thinking of those two things so much that all the early part is just dreamlike. It's more like something I've read in a book, or seen in the _Illustrated London News_ than actually been through. One had been thinking so often, how will it feel? how shall I behave? that when it came it had an effect of being flat and ordinary.

In the meantime, I have news for you. I’ve survived a major engagement, a real battle, and I haven't sustained a single scratch. I even managed to capture two prisoners with my own bare hands. Men were being cut down right beside me, but I didn't find it troubling at all. It was every bit as exhilarating as those fierce contests we used to have around the hockey goal. I wasn't bothered by anything until later. But that evening in the trench, I stepped on something slick—the memory still revolts me. And then, after the worst was over, one of my mates was killed—it seemed so senseless, so unjust. I find myself dwelling on those two moments so much that the earlier combat feels like a dream. It feels more like a story I’ve read or a sketch in the *Illustrated London News* than something I actually experienced. I had wondered so often how it would feel and how I would hold up that, when it finally happened, the whole thing felt strangely flat and ordinary.

Earlier, I had seized a wooden bowl and hurried past the king toward the brook, only ten yards distant. By the time I returned and stepped inside, the king was already there, unfastening the shutter over the window-hole to admit fresh air and daylight. A vile odor saturated the room. I raised the bowl to the woman's lips, and just as she clutched it with desperate, claw-like hands, the shutter swung wide, spilling harsh light across her face. Smallpox.

The sawyer himself did not participate in the conference, but abided at a little distance, like an outer satellite who was not to speak until required, or to offer an opinion until invited.
