Yet his own resolve stayed firm, for all his deep affection for her. The only lasting impact of her words was to drive him back to his studies with even greater intensity, determined to secure the kind of practical success that would allow him to effectively counter her fancies with the weight of his own accomplishments.

It was a soft, reposeful summer landscape, as lovely as a dream, and as lonesome as Sunday. The air was full of the smell of flowers, and the buzzing of insects, and the twittering of birds, and there were no people, no wagons, there was no stir of life, nothing going on. The road was mainly a winding path with hoof-prints in it, and now and then a faint trace of wheels on either side in the grass--wheels that apparently had a tire as broad as one's hand.

It was a gentle, hushed summer scene, lovely as a vision and lonely as a Sabbath morning. The air was laden with the fragrance of blossoms, the hum of insects, and the light chirping of birds; yet there were no people, no carts, no movement of life at all, nothing stirring anywhere. The road was little more than a meandering track marked by hoofprints, with here and there a dim wheel-rut showing in the grass on either side—ruts made by wheels that seemed to wear tires as broad as a hand.

“I see the lives for which I lay down my life, peaceful, useful, prosperous and happy, in that England which I shall see no more. I see Her with a child upon her bosom, who bears my name. I see her father, aged and bent, but otherwise restored, and faithful to all men in his healing office, and at peace. I see the good old man, so long their friend, in ten years’ time enriching them with all he has, and passing tranquilly to his reward.

I see the lives I give my own to protect: quiet, purposeful, thriving and joyful, in that Wessex countryside I will never set foot in again. I see Elowen holding a small boy pressed to her bosom, the child that bears my given name. I see her father, stooped now with the weight of years, but whole in every other regard, steadfast in his work as the village healer to all who come to him, his heart finally at rest. I see that kind white-haired gentleman who has been their loyal friend for decades, gifting them every bit of his care and fortune over the next ten years, before slipping gently to his well-earned eternal rest.

The untaught peasant beheld the elements around him and was acquainted with their practical uses. The most learned philosopher knew little more. He had partially unveiled the face of Nature, but her immortal lineaments were still a wonder and a mystery. He might dissect, anatomise, and give names; but, not to speak of a final cause, causes in their secondary and tertiary grades were utterly unknown to him. I had gazed upon the fortifications and impediments that seemed to keep human beings from entering the citadel of nature, and rashly and ignorantly I had repined.

The unschooled homesteader observed the natural forces surrounding his small plot and knew their practical functions intimately. The most erudite natural scholar understood barely anything beyond that. He had pulled back a corner of the wild world’s veil, but its unchanging, ageless features remained as much a marvel and enigma as they were to the farmer. He could cut apart specimens, map their internal structures, and assign taxonomical labels to every piece; yet even setting aside the question of ultimate purpose, the second and third order forces driving those systems were entirely beyond his grasp. I had stared for years at the ramparts and barriers that seemed to bar mortal minds from stepping into nature’s innermost stronghold, and foolhardy and unknowing as I was, I had railed against them.

As for the roof he vaunted, he might have found _that_ shutting out the sky in a new way--to wit, for ever, from the eyes of the bodies into which its lead was fired, out of the barrels of a hundred thousand muskets.

The span of time left to him was far shorter than Count Voss dared to imagine. Had someone held up a vision before him that night of his grand Valrose chateau as it would stand only a handful of years later, alongside fifty other aristocratic estates reduced to the same dire state, he would have struggled to pick his own out from the lineup of soot-caked, looted, fire-gnawed wrecks. As for the steep, solid roof he had so often vaunted as impervious to every storm, he would have found it blocking out the sky in a grim, unforeseen way: its melted lead cast into shot, fired from the barrels of a hundred thousand muskets, to shut out the light forever from the eyes of the bodies it was driven into.

“I’m sure that all I know is that she arrived in the morning and died in the evening without further parley. What Oak and Mr. Boldwood told me was only these few words. ‘Little Fanny Robin is dead, Joseph,’ Gabriel said, looking in my face in his steady old way. I was very sorry, and I said, ‘Ah!—and how did she come to die?’ ‘Well, she’s dead in Casterbridge Union,’ he said, ‘and perhaps ’tisn’t much matter about how she came to die. She reached the Union early Sunday morning, and died in the afternoon—that’s clear enough.’ Then I asked what she’d been doing lately, and Mr. Boldwood turned round to me then, and left off spitting a thistle with the end of his stick. He told me about her having lived by seampstering in Melchester, as I mentioned to you, and that she walked therefrom at the end of last week, passing near here Saturday night in the dusk. They then said I had better just name a hint of her death to you, and away they went. Her death might have been brought on by biding in the night wind, you know, ma’am; for people used to say she’d go off in a decline: she used to cough a good deal in winter time. However, ’tisn’t much odds to us about that now, for ’tis all over.”

“All I can tell you, ma’am, is that she came in the morning and was gone by nightfall, without any more to be said. The only words Gabriel Oak and Mr. Boldwood gave me were these. ‘Little Fanny Robin is dead, Joseph,’ Gabriel said, fixing his gaze on me in that calm, old-fashioned manner of his. I felt a real sorrow, and I asked, ‘Ah—and what brought her to it?’ ‘She’s dead in the Casterbridge Union,’ he answered, ‘and the how of it hardly signifies now. She was taken in there early on Sunday morning, and by afternoon she was gone—that much is plain.’ I pressed then about her recent doings, and Mr. Boldwood swung toward me, ceasing to pick at a thistle with the tip of his staff. He told me she had kept herself by needlework in Melchester, as I passed on to you before, and that she set out walking from there at the close of last week, going by this way on Saturday evening in the half-light. They then said I should just breathe a word of her passing to you, and off they went. It may have been the night air that finished her, you know, ma’am; for folk used to say she would sink in a consumption—she had a bad cough in the cold months. Still, it makes little difference to us now, since it’s all done with.”

As the memory of past misfortunes pressed upon me, I began to reflect on their cause—the monster whom I had created, the miserable dæmon whom I had sent abroad into the world for my destruction. I was possessed by a maddening rage when I thought of him, and desired and ardently prayed that I might have him within my grasp to wreak a great and signal revenge on his cursed head.

Freedom, however, would have remained a sterile mercy had my restoration to sanity not coincided with a hunger for vengeance. As the shadow of my former calamities lengthened over my mind, I contemplated their source—the grotesque entity of my own forging, the vile apparition I had cast into the world to be my ruin. A delirious fury seized my heart at the mere thought of him, and I fervently entreated the heavens that he might fall into my clutches, allowing me to execute a terrible and absolute judgment upon his accursed life.

Indeed they were. Mortification at the overthrow of his notable scheme; hatred of the girl who had dared to palter with strangers; and utter distrust of the sincerity of her refusal to yield him up; bitter disappointment at the loss of his revenge on Sikes; the fear of detection, and ruin, and death; and a fierce and deadly rage kindled by all; these were the passionate considerations which, following close upon each other with rapid and ceaseless whirl, shot through the brain of Fagin, as every evil thought and blackest purpose lay working at his heart.

Indeed, they were. Shame at the collapse of his cunning design; loathing for the girl who had ventured to trifle with strangers; and a total disbelief in the truth of her refusal to surrender him; sharp disappointment at being robbed of his vengeance on Slyke; the terror of discovery, of ruin, and of death; and a fierce, consuming fury kindled by them all—these were the turbulent impulses that, one after another in swift and unbroken succession, rushed through the mind of Rendel, while every evil notion and darkest intent worked deep within his breast.

“Having thus arranged my dwelling and carpeted it with clean straw, I retired, for I saw the figure of a man at a distance, and I remembered too well my treatment the night before to trust myself in his power. I had first, however, provided for my sustenance for that day by a loaf of coarse bread, which I purloined, and a cup with which I could drink more conveniently than from my hand of the pure water which flowed by my retreat. The floor was a little raised, so that it was kept perfectly dry, and by its vicinity to the chimney of the cottage it was tolerably warm.

Once I’d tidied my makeshift hovel and lined its floor with fresh, dry rushes, I drew back into the shadows—there was the silhouette of a man on the horizon, and the brutality I’d suffered at the hands of strangers the previous night was too sharp in my memory to risk letting him spot me. Before hiding, though, I’d secured my day’s sustenance: a loaf of rough, gritty bread I’d pilfered from a cottage doorstep, and a chipped clay cup that let me sip from the clear stream trickling past my refuge far easier than cupping my palms. The plank floor of my shelter sat a few inches above the ground, so it stayed completely free of damp, and its position right up against the cottage’s chimney stack kept it decently warm even in the cool late autumn air.

“Let’s come closer,” whispered Samway; and they approached on tiptoe. There was no disbelieving the report any longer. Troy’s face was almost close to the pane, and he was looking in. Not only was he looking in, but he appeared to have been arrested by a conversation which was in progress in the malt-house, the voices of the interlocutors being those of Oak and the maltster.

“Move nearer,” breathed Ashford; and on silent feet they crept forward. The sound left no room for doubt now. Troy had pressed his face nearly to the glass, peering within. Yet it was not merely the act of looking that held him; he seemed caught fast by some exchange unfolding inside the malt-house, the speakers unmistakably Oak and the maltster.

It was now intensely dark. The fog was much heavier than it had been in the early part of the night; and the atmosphere was so damp, that, although no rain fell, Oliver’s hair and eyebrows, within a few minutes after leaving the house, had become stiff with the half-frozen moisture that was floating about. They crossed the bridge, and kept on towards the lights which he had seen before. They were at no great distance off; and, as they walked pretty briskly, they soon arrived at Chertsey.

The dark had settled so thick it could almost be touched. Fog clung far heavier than it had in the first hours of the night, and the air hummed with dampness; though no rain fell, Luka’s hair and eyebrows crusted stiff with half-frozen drifting vapor within minutes of stepping outside the cottage door. They crossed the weathered stone bridge, making straight for the faint warm lights he had spied through the mist earlier. The glow was not far off, and as they moved at a sharp, steady clip, they covered the remaining ground quickly, pulling up on the edge of Harlow’s Ford before Luka could fully register how fast they’d walked.

“If it had been possible, Miss Manette, that you could have returned the love of the man you see before yourself--flung away, wasted, drunken, poor creature of misuse as you know him to be--he would have been conscious this day and hour, in spite of his happiness, that he would bring you to misery, bring you to sorrow and repentance, blight you, disgrace you, pull you down with him. I know very well that you can have no tenderness for me; I ask for none; I am even thankful that it cannot be.”

“Had it been within the realm of possibility, Miss Manette, for you to return the affection of the man who stands before you—squandered, dissolute, and ruined as you know him to be—he would be certain, even in his greatest happiness, that he could only bring you to wretchedness. He would burden you with sorrow and regret, staining your life and dragging you down into his own disgrace. I understand perfectly that you can feel no love for me; I do not seek it; indeed, I am grateful that such a thing can never be.”

“My dear friend, I have a charmed life in this city. I have been a Bastille prisoner. There is no patriot in Paris--in Paris? In France--who, knowing me to have been a prisoner in the Bastille, would touch me, except to overwhelm me with embraces, or carry me in triumph. My old pain has given me a power that has brought us through the barrier, and gained us news of Charles there, and brought us here. I knew it would be so; I knew I could help Charles out of all danger; I told Lucie so.--What is that noise?” His hand was again upon the window.

“My dear companion, I lead a protected life within these city walls. I was once an inmate of the Bastille. There is not a single revolutionary in Paris—nay, in all of France—who, recognizing me as a former captive of that fortress, would dare lay a hand on me, unless it were to smother me with affection or bear me aloft in victory. The suffering of my past has granted me an influence that saw us safely past the gates, secured word of Charles’s condition, and guided us to this very spot. I was certain of this outcome; I was convinced I could rescue Charles from his peril, and I shared this assurance with Lucie. What is that sound?” Once more, his hand reached for the windowpane.

Elizabeth alone had the power to draw me from these fits; her gentle voice would soothe me when transported by passion and inspire me with human feelings when sunk in torpor. She wept with me and for me. When reason returned, she would remonstrate and endeavour to inspire me with resignation. Ah! It is well for the unfortunate to be resigned, but for the guilty there is no peace. The agonies of remorse poison the luxury there is otherwise sometimes found in indulging the excess of grief.

Only Clara had power to recall me from those paroxysms; her soft voice would calm me when passion had driven me to frenzy, and awaken in me some remnant of human feeling when I had sunk into a dull stupor. She wept with me and for me. When reason came back, she would gently reprove me and strive to teach me resignation. Ah! it is well for the wretched to be resigned; but for the guilty, there is no peace. The pangs of remorse taint the mournful sweetness that is sometimes found in surrendering oneself to the full indulgence of grief.

“For the love of Heaven, of justice, of generosity, of the honour of your noble name, I supplicate you, Monsieur heretofore the Marquis, to succour and release me. My fault is, that I have been true to you. Oh Monsieur heretofore the Marquis, I pray you be you true to me!

By all that is holy, by justice, by mercy, and for the sake of your family’s honorable name, I beg of you, Monsieur who was once the Marquis, to rescue me and grant me my freedom. My only crime is that I remained loyal to you. Oh, Monsieur formerly the Marquis, I pray that you will now be loyal to me!

As I looked on him, his countenance expressed the utmost extent of malice and treachery. I thought with a sensation of madness on my promise of creating another like to him, and trembling with passion, tore to pieces the thing on which I was engaged. The wretch saw me destroy the creature on whose future existence he depended for happiness, and with a howl of devilish despair and revenge, withdrew.

As I looked at him, his face showed the full measure of malice and treachery. My mind went half mad as I remembered my promise to make another being like him, and, trembling with fury, I ripped apart the work I had been engaged upon. The wretch saw me destroy the creature on whose future existence his own happiness depended, and with a howl of fiendish despair and vengeance, he turned away and left.

“Now, dear Victor, I dare say you wish to be indulged in a little gossip concerning the good people of Geneva. The pretty Miss Mansfield has already received the congratulatory visits on her approaching marriage with a young Englishman, John Melbourne, Esq. Her ugly sister, Manon, married M. Duvillard, the rich banker, last autumn. Your favourite schoolfellow, Louis Manoir, has suffered several misfortunes since the departure of Clerval from Geneva. But he has already recovered his spirits, and is reported to be on the point of marrying a lively pretty Frenchwoman, Madame Tavernier. She is a widow, and much older than Manoir; but she is very much admired, and a favourite with everybody.

Well, my dearest Elias, I suspect you are eager to be indulged in a little gossip concerning the good people of Avignon. The lovely Miss Elara Voss has already received a steady stream of congratulatory visits on her approaching marriage to a young English gentleman, Edward Hale, Esquire. Her plain, unassuming sister Margot wed Monsieur Thibault, the wealthy local banker, last autumn. Your favorite boyhood schoolmate, Julien Rousseau, has suffered a string of small misfortunes since the departure of Florian from Avignon. But he has already recovered his usual good cheer, and word has it he is on the point of marrying a quick-witted, charming Frenchwoman, Madame Leroy. She is a widow, and significantly older than Rousseau; but she is very much admired, and a universal favorite with everyone in town.

“Come, brighten up, dearest; we shall be all right again. Some day perhaps I shall see as well as ever. And I solemnly promise that I’ll leave off cutting furze as soon as I have the power to do anything better. You cannot seriously wish me to stay idling at home all day?”

“Lift your spirits, my love; the clouds will part soon enough. One morning, perhaps, my sight will return as clear as it ever was. And I vow to you now, on my honour, that the moment a worthier occupation presents itself, I shall lay down the furze-hook for good. Surely you don’t truly desire to see me loitering about the house from dawn to dusk?”

At last he reached the summit, and a wide and novel prospect burst upon him with an effect almost like that of the Pacific upon Balboa’s gaze. The broad steely sea, marked only by faint lines, which had a semblance of being etched thereon to a degree not deep enough to disturb its general evenness, stretched the whole width of his front and round to the right, where, near the town and port of Budmouth, the sun bristled down upon it, and banished all colour, to substitute in its place a clear oily polish. Nothing moved in sky, land, or sea, except a frill of milkwhite foam along the nearer angles of the shore, shreds of which licked the contiguous stones like tongues.

At last he crested the summit, and a vast, unfamiliar panorama broke upon him with an impact almost like that of the Pacific on Balboa’s eyes. The wide, metallic sea, traced only by faint streaks that seemed etched upon its surface too lightly to disturb its uniform smoothness, spanned his entire forward view and curved away to the right, where, near Budmouth town and harbor, the sun blazed down upon it, stripping away all color and replacing it with a lucent, oily sheen. Nothing stirred in sky, land, or sea, save a fringe of milk-white foam along the nearer angles of the coast, tatters of which lapped the adjacent stones like tongues.

But the vision of that first fight still lingered under his eyelids, and as he watched he saw it dissolve and reshape into the series of fights which had followed. Six months later Cheese-Face (that was the boy) had whipped him again. But he had blacked Cheese-Face’s eye that time. That was going some. He had always stayed and taken his medicine. Cheese-Face had been a little fiend at fighting, and had never once shown mercy to him. But he had stayed! He had stayed with it!

That Ruth had little faith in his power as a writer, did not alter her nor diminish her in Martin’s eyes. In the breathing spell of the vacation he had taken, he had spent many hours in self-analysis, and thereby learned much of himself. He had discovered that he loved beauty more than fame, and that what desire he had for fame was largely for Ruth’s sake. It was for this reason that his desire for fame was strong. He wanted to be great in the world’s eyes; “to make good,” as he expressed it, in order that the woman he loved should be proud of him and deem him worthy.

The fact that Ruth doubted his talent as an author did not change Martin’s feelings or lower her in his estimation. During a brief respite from his work, he spent many hours looking inward, learning a great deal about his own character. He realized that he cherished beauty far more than he desired status, and that his ambition was mostly for Ruth’s benefit. This was the source of his powerful drive. He wanted to achieve public greatness—to "make it," as he put it—so that the woman he loved would be proud of him and consider him worthy of her.

Hence people who began by beholding him ended by perusing him. His countenance was overlaid with legible meanings. Without being thought-worn he yet had certain marks derived from a perception of his surroundings, such as are not unfrequently found on men at the end of the four or five years of endeavour which follow the close of placid pupilage. He already showed that thought is a disease of flesh, and indirectly bore evidence that ideal physical beauty is incompatible with emotional development and a full recognition of the coil of things. Mental luminousness must be fed with the oil of life, even though there is already a physical need for it; and the pitiful sight of two demands on one supply was just showing itself here.

And so strangers who first glanced idly at Kael ended up poring over every line of his face, as if decoding a half-faded handwritten manuscript. Every plane of his countenance bore unmistakeable, readable layers of lived experience. Though he did not yet carry the haggard creases of a life spent entirely in rumination, faint, persistent marks scored his skin, left by years of watching and parsing the world around him—marks often seen on those four or five years out from the soft, uncomplicated bubble of their formal schooling, when they have spent every waking hour striving to carve out a place for themselves. He already embodied the quiet truth that thinking is a slow erosion of the flesh, and bore unspoken witness to the fact that unmarred, ideal physical beauty cannot coexist with deep emotional maturation, nor with the full, heavy recognition of how tangled and knotted the web of human life truly is. The bright glow of a sharp, curious mind must be fed with the same finite store of life oil that fuels the body’s most basic needs; here, the quiet, heart-wrenching sight of those two competing claims drawing from the same limited supply was already impossible to overlook.

My father was enraptured on finding me freed from the vexations of a criminal charge, that I was again allowed to breathe the fresh atmosphere and permitted to return to my native country. I did not participate in these feelings, for to me the walls of a dungeon or a palace were alike hateful. The cup of life was poisoned for ever, and although the sun shone upon me, as upon the happy and gay of heart, I saw around me nothing but a dense and frightful darkness, penetrated by no light but the glimmer of two eyes that glared upon me. Sometimes they were the expressive eyes of Henry, languishing in death, the dark orbs nearly covered by the lids and the long black lashes that fringed them; sometimes it was the watery, clouded eyes of the monster, as I first saw them in my chamber at Ingolstadt.

My father was overjoyed to see me cleared of the torments of a criminal accusation, free once more to breathe the open air and allowed to return to my homeland. I could not share his delight, for to me a prison’s walls and a palace’s chambers were equally loathsome. The draught of life had been forever tainted, and though the sun fell on me as it does on the light-hearted and the joyful, I perceived nothing around me but a thick and terrible darkness, broken by no gleam save the glint of two eyes that stared back at me. At times they were Henry’s speaking eyes, fading in death, their dark pupils almost veiled by the lids and the long black lashes that edged them; at other times they were the clouded, watery eyes of the creature, just as I had first beheld them in my room at Ingolstadt.

Winter again came round, with its winds, frosts, tame robins, and sparkling starlight. The year previous Thomasin had hardly been conscious of the season’s advance; this year she laid her heart open to external influences of every kind. The life of this sweet cousin, her baby, and her servants, came to Clym’s senses only in the form of sounds through a wood partition as he sat over books of exceptionally large type; but his ear became at last so accustomed to these slight noises from the other part of the house that he almost could witness the scenes they signified. A faint beat of half-seconds conjured up Thomasin rocking the cradle, a wavering hum meant that she was singing the baby to sleep, a crunching of sand as between millstones raised the picture of Humphrey’s, Fairway’s, or Sam’s heavy feet crossing the stone floor of the kitchen; a light boyish step, and a gay tune in a high key, betokened a visit from Grandfer Cantle; a sudden break-off in the Grandfer’s utterances implied the application to his lips of a mug of small beer, a bustling and slamming of doors meant starting to go to market; for Thomasin, in spite of her added scope of gentility, led a ludicrously narrow life, to the end that she might save every possible pound for her little daughter.

Winter returned with its frost-edged breath, its gusting songs, confiding sparrows, and glitter of stars on clear nights. The year before, Thomasin had scarcely noticed the slow creep of the cold; this year she yielded her spirit to every touch of the world outside. The existence of that gentle cousin, her child, and the household help reached Clym only as a sequence of murmurs passing through the wooden wall while he bent over books set in unusually large print; yet in time his hearing grew so finely tuned to these modest stirrings from the adjoining rooms that he could almost see the life they betrayed. A soft, half-second pulse conjured Thomasin swaying the cradle, a trembling drone told him she was crooning the infant toward sleep, a gritty scrape like millstones grinding brought before him the heavy tread of Humphrey, Fairway, or Sam crossing the flagged kitchen floor; a light, youthful step and a sprightly air in a high pitch announced a call from Grandfer Cantle; a sudden halt in the old man’s chatter hinted at a mug of small beer pressed to his mouth, a flurry of doors and hurried thumps meant preparations for market; for Thomasin, despite her widened circle of refinement, lived a comically pinched existence, determined to save every possible shilling for her little girl.

“Oh, forgive me,” he cried, springing to his feet. “I’m not myself, you know. I forgot you were here.” He put his hand to his head. “You see, I’m not just right. I’ll take you home. We can go out by the servants’ entrance. No one will see us. Pull down that veil and everything will be all right.”

“I beg your indulgence,” he exclaimed, rising with a frantic suddenness. “My mind is quite unmoored; I had lost all sense of your being here.” He clutched his forehead as if to steady himself. “I am not quite whole today. I shall see you back to your door, but we must slip through the rear galley to remain unobserved. Lower your netting, and we shall pass through the shadows without incident.”

The members of this board were very sage, deep, philosophical men; and when they came to turn their attention to the workhouse, they found out at once, what ordinary folks would never have discovered—the poor people liked it! It was a regular place of public entertainment for the poorer classes; a tavern where there was nothing to pay; a public breakfast, dinner, tea, and supper all the year round; a brick and mortar elysium, where it was all play and no work. “Oho!” said the board, looking very knowing; “we are the fellows to set this to rights; we’ll stop it all, in no time.” So, they established the rule, that all poor people should have the alternative (for they would compel nobody, not they), of being starved by a gradual process in the house, or by a quick one out of it. With this view, they contracted with the water-works to lay on an unlimited supply of water; and with a corn-factory to supply periodically small quantities of oatmeal; and issued three meals of thin gruel a day, with an onion twice a week, and half a roll of Sundays. They made a great many other wise and humane regulations, having reference to the ladies, which it is not necessary to repeat; kindly undertook to divorce poor married people, in consequence of the great expense of a suit in Doctors’ Commons; and, instead of compelling a man to support his family, as they had theretofore done, took his family away from him, and made him a bachelor! There is no saying how many applicants for relief, under these last two heads, might have started up in all classes of society, if it had not been coupled with the workhouse; but the board were long-headed men, and had provided for this difficulty. The relief was inseparable from the workhouse and the gruel; and that frightened people.

The members of this board were very wise, far-seeing, philosophical men; and when they turned their attention to the workhouse, they immediately discovered what ordinary people would never have guessed—the poor actually liked it! It had become a sort of public amusement for the lower classes; a tavern where nothing had to be paid; a public breakfast, dinner, tea, and supper all year long; a brick-and-mortar paradise, where there was nothing but leisure and no labor at all. “Ha!” said the board, with a knowing air; “we are the people to put this right; we’ll put a stop to it in no time.” So they laid down the rule that every poor person should be given the choice (for they would force no one, certainly not they) of being slowly starved inside the house, or quickly starved outside it. With this in mind, they made a contract with the waterworks to provide an unlimited supply of water; and with a corn mill to furnish, at intervals, tiny amounts of oatmeal; and they served three meals a day of weak gruel, with an onion twice a week and half a roll on Sundays. They also introduced many other sensible and benevolent rules, concerning the women, which it is not necessary to repeat; graciously undertook to separate poor married couples, on account of the enormous cost of a suit in Doctors’ Commons; and, instead of making a man keep his family, as had previously been done, took his family away from him and turned him into a bachelor! It is impossible to say how many claimants for relief under these last two arrangements might have appeared in every rank of society, had it not been linked to the workhouse; but the board were shrewd men and had foreseen this problem. Relief could not be had apart from the workhouse and the gruel; and that terrified people.

Came a beautiful fall day, warm and languid, palpitant with the hush of the changing season, a California Indian summer day, with hazy sun and wandering wisps of breeze that did not stir the slumber of the air. Filmy purple mists, that were not vapors but fabrics woven of color, hid in the recesses of the hills. San Francisco lay like a blur of smoke upon her heights. The intervening bay was a dull sheen of molten metal, whereon sailing craft lay motionless or drifted with the lazy tide. Far Tamalpais, barely seen in the silver haze, bulked hugely by the Golden Gate, the latter a pale gold pathway under the westering sun. Beyond, the Pacific, dim and vast, was raising on its sky-line tumbled cloud-masses that swept landward, giving warning of the first blustering breath of winter.

A magnificent autumn day arrived, warm and slow-moving, charged with the quiet intensity of the shifting season. It was a true California Indian summer, marked by a misty sun and stray currents of air that left the stillness of the atmosphere undisturbed. Ethereal purple hazes, less like vapor and more like tapestries of pure pigment, tucked themselves into the hollows of the hills. San Francisco appeared as a faint smudge of smoke upon its ridges. The bay stretched out between them with the flat luster of liquid metal, its surface dotted with sailboats that sat frozen or wandered with the sluggish tide. Distant Tamalpais, shrouded in a silver glow, loomed beside the Golden Gate, which lay like a shimmering amber road under the sinking sun. Farther out, the vast and shadowy Pacific churned up great heaps of clouds on the horizon, sending them shoreward to signal the first cold gusts of the coming winter.

Oliver considered a little while; and at last said, he should think it would be a much better thing to be a book-seller; upon which the old gentleman laughed heartily, and declared he had said a very good thing. Which Oliver felt glad to have done, though he by no means knew what it was.

Oliver mulled it over for a short while, then finally said he thought being a bookseller would be a far better choice. At that, the old gentleman laughed heartily, his shoulders shaking with the force of it, and announced that Oliver had come out with an extremely sharp, clever line. Oliver was quietly pleased to have earned that reaction, even though he had no clue at all what was so noteworthy about the thing he’d just said.

He spoke this with a voice so modulated to the different feelings expressed in his speech, with an eye so full of lofty design and heroism, that can you wonder that these men were moved? They looked at one another and were unable to reply. I spoke; I told them to retire and consider of what had been said, that I would not lead them farther north if they strenuously desired the contrary, but that I hoped that, with reflection, their courage would return.

His oratory was delivered with a resonance so perfectly attuned to the shifting passions of his soul, and with a gaze so alight with noble ambition, that it was no marvel the mariners stood transfixed. They traded wordless glances, their tongues bound by the sheer weight of his conviction. I stepped forward then, urging them to withdraw and weigh his exhortations in the quiet of their own minds; I pledged that I would not force our prow deeper into the frozen latitudes should they remain set against it, yet I harbored a silent hope that, with prayerful reflection, their guttering spirits might once more catch fire.

She looked so charming and fair that his cool mood about Weatherbury people was changed. He had not expected her to exercise this power over him in the twinkling of an eye. Should he go on, and care nothing? He could not bring himself to do that. Beyond a politic wish to remain unknown, there suddenly arose in him now a sense of shame at the possibility that his attractive young wife, who already despised him, should despise him more by discovering him in so mean a condition after so long a time. He actually blushed at the thought, and was vexed beyond measure that his sentiments of dislike towards Weatherbury should have led him to dally about the country in this way.

She seemed so lovely and delicate that his former indifference toward the people of Mellstock gave way at once. He had not imagined that she could so suddenly exert this dominion over him, and in a moment. Was he to go on, and care for nothing? He could not persuade himself to do so. More than any prudent desire to remain unrecognized, there sprang up in him a feeling of shame at the thought that his comely young wife, who already held him in contempt, might despise him yet further on discovering him after so many years in so pitiful a plight. He positively colored at the notion, and was bitterly annoyed that his aversion to Mellstock folk should have led him to loiter about the countryside in this fashion.

The lady was a little flurried by Mr. Britling's too detailed examination of her haul. "What good is blacking?" he asked. She would not hear him. She felt he was trying to spoil her morning. She declared she must get on back to her home. "Don't say I didn't warn you," she said. "I've got no end of things to do. There's peas! I want to show cook how to bottle our peas. For this year--it's lucky, we've got no end of peas. I came by here just for the sake of telling you." And with that she presently departed--obviously ruffled by Mrs. Britling's lethargy and Mr. Britling's scepticism.

Mrs. Arbuthnot grew visibly agitated as Mr. Sterling subjected her bundle of purchases to an overly meticulous inspection. "What earthly use is this stove-paste?" he demanded. She refused to acknowledge the question, sensing a deliberate attempt to dampen the spirit of her morning. She announced her immediate departure for the rectory. "Do not claim you were not apprised," she said. "I have a thousand tasks to attend to. There are the beans! I must direct the kitchen in the proper method of pickling the harvest. It is a fortunate year; the crop is truly endless. I called here simply to pass on the word." With this, she took her leave, clearly piqued by Mrs. Sterling’s listless apathy and Mr. Sterling’s relentless cynicism.

I'm quitting," he declared. "I won't stand for it. I'm walking away for good. What's the point of me working like a dog all week, saving every minute I can, only for them to come along and dump all these extra starched pieces on me? This is a free country, and I'm going to tell that heavy-set boss exactly what I think of him. I won't be subtle about it, either. Plain English is plenty good enough for me. The nerve of him, sneaking in those extra starch jobs!

Mr. Direck's mind was in a perplexity only too common during the stresses of that tragic year. He was entangled in a paradox; like a large majority of Americans at that time his feelings were quite definitely pro-Ally, and like so many in that majority he had a very clear conviction that it would be wrong and impossible for the United States to take part in the war. His sympathies were intensely with the Dower House and its dependent cottage; he would have wept with generous emotion to see the Stars and Stripes interwoven with the three other great banners of red, white and blue that led the world against German imperialism and militarism, but for all that his mind would not march to that tune. Against all these impulses fought something very fundamental in Mr. Direck's composition, a preconception of America that had grown almost insensibly in his mind, the idea of America as a polity aloof from the Old World system, as a fresh start for humanity, as something altogether too fine and precious to be dragged into even the noblest of European conflicts. America was to be the beginning of the fusion of mankind, neither German nor British nor French nor in any way national. She was to be the great experiment in peace and reasonableness. She had to hold civilisation and social order out of this fray, to be a refuge for all those finer things that die under stress and turmoil; it was her task to maintain the standards of life and the claims of humanitarianism in the conquered province and the prisoners' compound, she had to be the healer and arbitrator, the remonstrance and not the smiting hand. Surely there were enough smiting hands.

Mr. Direck’s mind was caught in a confusion only too familiar in that year of tragedy. He was held fast by a contradiction; like most Americans then, his feelings were plainly pro-Ally, and like so many of them he was firmly convinced that it would be both wrong and impossible for the United States to enter the war. His sympathies were warmly with the Dower House and the little dependent cottage; he would have been moved to tears at the sight of the Stars and Stripes woven together with the three other great red, white, and blue banners that were leading the world against German imperialism and militarism, but even so his mind refused to follow that impulse. Set against all such emotions was something very deep in Mr. Direck’s makeup, an idea of America that had settled almost without his noticing it: America as a state apart from the Old World order, a fresh beginning for mankind, something far too precious and too noble to be dragged into even the most righteous of European wars. America was to be the start of humanity’s blending, neither German nor British nor French nor national in any old sense. She was meant to be the great experiment in peace and common sense. She must keep civilisation and social order outside this conflict, serve as a refuge for all those finer things that perish under strain and disorder; it was her duty to preserve the standards of life and the demands of humanity in conquered provinces and prison camps, to be the healer and judge, the voice of protest and not the striking hand. Surely there were already enough striking hands.

Towards evening Wildeve came. Since Thomasin’s marriage Mrs. Yeobright had shown him that grim friendliness which at last arises in all such cases of undesired affinity. The vision of what ought to have been is thrown aside in sheer weariness, and browbeaten human endeavour listlessly makes the best of the fact that is. Wildeve, to do him justice, had behaved very courteously to his wife’s aunt; and it was with no surprise that she saw him enter now.

Wildeve arrived as evening approached. Since Thomasin’s marriage, Mrs. Yeobright had adopted a sort of grim civility toward him—the kind of forced warmth that eventually develops in all such unwanted relationships. The dream of what might have been is cast aside out of sheer fatigue, and exhausted human effort half-heartedly accepts the reality of the situation. To give him credit, Wildeve had behaved quite politely toward his wife’s aunt, so his entrance now caused her no surprise.

The judge transferred himself to another vehicle while the broad-cheeked trumpeters sounded a flourish; and, the cars and javelin-men having been marshalled into a procession, the whole company moved on toward the town, save only the Weatherbury men, who, so soon as they had seen the judge depart, turned back again to their labour.

When the girl got into the open street, she sat down upon a doorstep; and seemed, for a few moments, wholly bewildered and unable to pursue her way. Suddenly she arose; and hurrying on, in a direction quite opposite to that in which Sikes was awaiting her return, quickened her pace, until it gradually resolved into a violent run. After completely exhausting herself, she stopped to take breath: and, as if suddenly recollecting herself, and deploring her inability to do something she was bent upon, wrung her hands, and burst into tears.

When the girl reached the open street, she sank down on a doorstep and seemed, for a few moments, utterly bewildered, unable to go on. Then, all at once, she sprang up and hurried away in a direction quite opposite to that in which Sikes was waiting for her return. She quickened her pace until it broke into a frantic run. When she had quite exhausted herself, she stopped to catch her breath, and then, as though she had suddenly remembered something and bitterly regretted her inability to do what she had resolved upon, she wrung her hands and burst into tears.

Martin’s thoughts were far away—so far away that he was rarely aware that he was thinking. It was only by an effort that he occasionally responded. And yet this was Joe, whom he had always liked. But Joe was too keen with life. The boisterous impact of it on Martin’s jaded mind was a hurt. It was an aching probe to his tired sensitiveness. When Joe reminded him that sometime in the future they were going to put on the gloves together, he could almost have screamed.

His mind had wandered into a profound distance—a space so far removed that he was scarcely conscious of his own reflections. Only with a straining of the spirit did he manage an occasional word. This was Silas, a friend he had long cherished, yet the man’s zest for living was simply too piercing. The raucous collision of such energy against Elias’s blunted intellect was an agony. It was a sharp instrument digging into his frayed nerves. When Silas recalled their promise to one day box together, Elias felt a cry of pure anguish nearly rupture his throat.

The wine was red wine, and had stained the ground of the narrow street in the suburb of Saint Antoine, in Paris, where it was spilled. It had stained many hands, too, and many faces, and many naked feet, and many wooden shoes. The hands of the man who sawed the wood, left red marks on the billets; and the forehead of the woman who nursed her baby, was stained with the stain of the old rag she wound about her head again. Those who had been greedy with the staves of the cask, had acquired a tigerish smear about the mouth; and one tall joker so besmirched, his head more out of a long squalid bag of a nightcap than in it, scrawled upon a wall with his finger dipped in muddy wine-lees--BLOOD.

So Mr. Britling's thoughts shaped themselves in words as he prowled one night in March, chill and melancholy, across a rushy meadow under an overcast sky. The death squeal of some little beast caught suddenly in a distant copse had set loose this train of thought. "Life struggling under a birth curse?" he thought. "How nearly I come back at times to the Christian theology!... And then, Redemption by the shedding of blood."

Thus did Mr. Britling’s ideas take the form of words as he wandered one cold and somber night in March, crossing a reed-filled meadow under a heavy, clouded sky. The agonizing shriek of a small animal caught in a distant thicket had prompted this grim line of reasoning. "Is existence a struggle against some primal curse?" he mused. "How often I find myself drifting back toward the tenets of Christian theology... and that notion of Redemption through the pouring out of blood."

Never, since the first meeting, had they seemed so far apart as that day. His confession, told in frankness, with the spirit of revolt behind, had repelled her. But she was more shocked by the repulsion itself than by the cause of it. It pointed out to her how near she had drawn to him, and once accepted, it paved the way for greater intimacy. Pity, too, was aroused, and innocent, idealistic thoughts of reform. She would save this raw young man who had come so far. She would save him from the curse of his early environment, and she would save him from himself in spite of himself. And all this affected her as a very noble state of consciousness; nor did she dream that behind it and underlying it were the jealousy and desire of love.

From the day they first met right up to that moment, the gulf between them had never felt so wide. The unvarnished confession he’d laid bare, sharp with the fire of rebellion running under every word, had made her recoil. Yet what startled her more than the reason for that reaction was the reaction itself. That jolt of distaste drove home just how close she’d grown to him, and once she let herself acknowledge that truth, it cleared a path for them to grow even closer. It also stirred up pity in her, along with soft, idealistic fantasies of helping him turn his life around. She would rescue this rough, unrefined young man who’d already come such a long way on his own. She would pull him free of the heavy curse of his troubled upbringing, and she would save him from his own worst impulses, even if he fought her every step of the way. To her, this entire impulse felt like the height of selfless, noble virtue; it never once crossed her mind that tangled beneath it all were the sharp, unacknowledged pangs of jealousy and the hungry want of romantic love.

As the soldiers assisted me across the court the stillness was so profound that if I had been blindfold I should have supposed I was in a solitude instead of walled in by four thousand people. There was not a movement perceptible in those masses of humanity; they were as rigid as stone images, and as pale; and dread sat upon every countenance. This hush continued while I was being chained to the stake; it still continued while the fagots were carefully and tediously piled about my ankles, my knees, my thighs, my body. Then there was a pause, and a deeper hush, if possible, and a man knelt down at my feet with a blazing torch; the multitude strained forward, gazing, and parting slightly from their seats without knowing it; the monk raised his hands above my head, and his eyes toward the blue sky, and began some words in Latin; in this attitude he droned on and on, a little while, and then stopped. I waited two or three moments; then looked up; he was standing there petrified. With a common impulse the multitude rose slowly up and stared into the sky. I followed their eyes, as sure as guns, there was my eclipse beginning! The life went boiling through my veins; I was a new man! The rim of black spread slowly into the sun's disk, my heart beat higher and higher, and still the assemblage and the priest stared into the sky, motionless. I knew that this gaze would be turned upon me, next. When it was, I was ready. I was in one of the most grand attitudes I ever struck, with my arm stretched up pointing to the sun. It was a noble effect. You could _see_ the shudder sweep the mass like a wave. Two shouts rang out, one close upon the heels of the other:

Shepherds who attended with their flocks from long distances started from home two or three days, or even a week, before the fair, driving their charges a few miles each day—not more than ten or twelve—and resting them at night in hired fields by the wayside at previously chosen points, where they fed, having fasted since morning. The shepherd of each flock marched behind, a bundle containing his kit for the week strapped upon his shoulders, and in his hand his crook, which he used as the staff of his pilgrimage. Several of the sheep would get worn and lame, and occasionally a lambing occurred on the road. To meet these contingencies, there was frequently provided, to accompany the flocks from the remoter points, a pony and waggon into which the weakly ones were taken for the remainder of the journey.

Shepherds arriving from distant regions set out two or three days, or sometimes a full week, before the fair. They drove their livestock slowly, covering no more than ten or twelve miles a day, and stopped each night at pre-selected fields rented along the way. There, the animals finally grazed after a day spent on the move without food. The shepherd walked at the back of his flock, a pack containing his supplies for the week cinched to his shoulders, using his crook as a staff for the long journey. Along the route, some sheep would inevitably grow weary or start to limp, and occasionally a lamb was born mid-trek. To manage these occurrences, a pony and cart often accompanied the flocks from the more remote areas, providing a ride for the weak or injured for the rest of the trip.

The customers at Bogle’s were her slaves. Six tables full she could wait upon at once. They who were in a hurry restrained their impatience for the joy of merely gazing upon her swiftly moving, graceful figure. They who had finished eating ate more that they might continue in the light of her smiles. Every man there—and they were mostly men—tried to make his impression upon her.

The patrons at Marden’s were her captives. She could attend to six tables at a time. Those who were pressed for time held back their impatience for the pleasure of merely watching her quick, graceful figure glide among them. Those who had finished their meals ordered more, so they might linger longer in the warmth of her smiles. Every man in the room—and men made up the greater part of the company—endeavored to leave some mark upon her.

“I understand how little solace an unfamiliar voice can offer to someone crushed beneath the weight of such a peculiar calamity. Yet I trust you shall soon depart this sorrowful place, since surely proof can swiftly be gathered to absolve you of this accusation.”

The form of Henery Fray advanced to the fire, stamping the snow from his boots when about half-way there. The speech and entry had not seemed to be at all an abrupt beginning to the maltster, introductory matter being often omitted in this neighbourhood, both from word and deed, and the maltster having the same latitude allowed him, did not hurry to reply. He picked up a fragment of cheese, by pecking upon it with his knife, as a butcher picks up skewers.

“Mark me,” came a voice, drifting through the malt-house doorway just as it swung wide, “we’ll have a fair noon, and a squall will bite by moonrise.” The speaker was Elias Crane, who strode toward the hearth, pausing halfway to knock the sleet from his soles. Neither the words nor the entrance struck the maltman as sudden; in that country, folk often shed preambles from speech and action alike, and the maltman, granted the same freedom, took his time before answering. He speared a shard of cheese with his knife, lifting it as a surgeon might lift a suture.

He leaned his head back and closed his eyes; and like a child, crying, that forgets its grief in watching the sunlight percolate through the tear-dimmed films over the pupils, so Martin forgot his sickness, the presence of Ruth, everything, in watching the masses of vegetation, shot through hotly with sunshine that took form and blazed against this background of his eyelids. It was not restful, that green foliage. The sunlight was too raw and glaring. It hurt him to look at it, and yet he looked, he knew not why.

He tilted his head back and shut his eyes; much like a sobbing child who finds distraction from its misery by observing the sunbeams filtering through tear-streaked lashes, Martin drifted away from his ailment and Ruth’s presence. He was consumed by the sight of lush foliage, ignited by a searing sun that flared brilliantly against the inner surface of his eyelids. There was no comfort in that verdant glow. The light felt too brutal and intense. It pained him to behold it, yet he continued to stare, compelled by a force he did not understand.

Elias clutched what looked like a carpet satchel in his fist—the very same one he’d carried when he arrived. It seemed for all the world as if he intended to leave town again that very evening.

“These words are formed by the rusty iron point with which I write with difficulty in scrapings of soot and charcoal from the chimney, mixed with blood, in the last month of the tenth year of my captivity. Hope has quite departed from my breast. I know from terrible warnings I have noted in myself that my reason will not long remain unimpaired, but I solemnly declare that I am at this time in the possession of my right mind--that my memory is exact and circumstantial--and that I write the truth as I shall answer for these my last recorded words, whether they be ever read by men or not, at the Eternal Judgment-seat.

These lines are traced, with painful labor, by the blunted iron tip with which I write, upon fragments of soot and charcoal scraped from the hearth and mingled with my own blood, in the final month of the tenth year of my imprisonment. All hope has wholly deserted my heart. By dreadful admonitions I have marked in myself, I know my reason cannot long remain entire; yet I do most solemnly avow that, at this hour, I am in full possession of my senses—that my recollection is exact and particular—and that I record the truth, as I shall answer for these my final words, whether they are ever read by mortal eyes or never, before the Eternal Judgment Seat.

Higher still, a chaotic tangle of placards vied for attention, marking the offices of palmists, tailors, musicians, and physicians. At the very top, drawn curtains and the stark white of milk bottles on the ledges signaled the beginning of the residential quarters.

“Your heart is kind, and your counsel is not without wisdom. Yet it would be disgrace for a sovereign to cower, and disgrace for a girded warrior to stay his blade when souls cry out for aid. Enough. _Massacres of boys! That indeed is the essence of modern war. The killing off of the young. It is the destruction of the human inheritance, it is the spending of all the life and material of the future upon present-day hate and greed. Fools and knaves, politicians, tricksters, and those who trade on the suspicions and thoughtless, generous angers of men, make wars; the indolence and modesty of the mass of men permit them. Are you and I to suffer such things until the whole fabric of our civilisation, that has been so slowly and so laboriously built up, is altogether destroyed?_

I inquired the way to the inn, but no one replied. I then moved forward, and a murmuring sound arose from the crowd as they followed and surrounded me, when an ill-looking man approaching tapped me on the shoulder and said, “Come, sir, you must follow me to Mr. Kirwin’s to give an account of yourself.”

As this singular exchange persisted, I noted the throng of bystanders swelling in number. Their visages betrayed a volatile blend of inquisitiveness and resentment, a sight that stirred within me both a sense of harassment and a deepening dread. I sought the direction of the local tavern, yet my appeals were met with a stubborn silence. I attempted to advance, but a low rumble of voices rose from the mob as they trailed my steps and began to encircle me; just then, a fellow of coarse appearance stepped forward, laying a heavy hand upon my shoulder. "Come, sir," he commanded, "you must accompany me to Magistrate Vance's to offer a reckoning of your conduct."

At these moments I wept bitterly and wished that peace would revisit my mind only that I might afford them consolation and happiness. But that could not be. Remorse extinguished every hope. I had been the author of unalterable evils, and I lived in daily fear lest the monster whom I had created should perpetrate some new wickedness. I had an obscure feeling that all was not over and that he would still commit some signal crime, which by its enormity should almost efface the recollection of the past. There was always scope for fear so long as anything I loved remained behind. My abhorrence of this fiend cannot be conceived. When I thought of him I gnashed my teeth, my eyes became inflamed, and I ardently wished to extinguish that life which I had so thoughtlessly bestowed. When I reflected on his crimes and malice, my hatred and revenge burst all bounds of moderation. I would have made a pilgrimage to the highest peak of the Andes, could I, when there, have precipitated him to their base. I wished to see him again, that I might wreak the utmost extent of abhorrence on his head and avenge the deaths of William and Justine.

In those hours I cried without restraint, longing for calm to return to my spirit so I could offer them comfort and joy. Yet it was impossible. Guilt smothered every flicker of hope. I had set in motion irreparable suffering and lived in constant dread that the creature I had brought into being would commit some fresh atrocity. A dim intuition told me the story was far from finished and that he would still enact a crime so monstrous it would nearly blot out all memory of what had come before. Fear had endless ground to stand on as long as anyone I cherished remained alive. My loathing for that demon defies description. When his image entered my mind, I ground my teeth, my eyes burned with fury, and I burned to snuff out the existence I had so recklessly ignited. Dwelling on his wickedness and spite, my hatred and thirst for vengeance shattered all restraint. I would have trekked to the loftiest summit of the Andes if, once there, I could have hurled him into the depths below. I yearned to face him again, to pour the full measure of my detestation upon him and exact justice for the deaths of William and Justine.

He began to fret and rage. He could not lie in peace in his bed; he got up and prowled about his room, blundering against chairs and tables in the darkness.... We were too stupid to do the most obvious things; we were sending all these boys into hardship and pitiless danger; we were sending them ill-equipped, insufficiently supported, we were sending our children through the fires to Moloch, because essentially we English were a world of indolent, pampered, sham good-humoured, old and middle-aged men. (So he distributed the intolerable load of self-accusation.) Why was he doing nothing to change things, to get them better? What was the good of an assumed modesty, an effort at tolerance for and confidence in these boozy old lawyers, these ranting platform men, these stiff-witted officers and hide-bound officials? They were butchering the youth of England. Old men sat out of danger contriving death for the lads in the trenches. That was the reality of the thing. "My son!" he cried sharply in the darkness. His sense of our national deficiencies became tormentingly, fantastically acute. It was as if all his cherished delusions had fallen from the scheme of things.... What was the good of making believe that up there they were planning some great counter-stroke that would end in victory? It was as plain as daylight that they had neither the power of imagination nor the collective intelligence even to conceive of a counter-stroke. Any dull mass may resist, but only imagination can strike. Imagination! To the end we should not strike. We might strike through the air. We might strike across the sea. We might strike hard at Gallipoli instead of dribbling inadequate armies thither as our fathers dribbled men at the Redan.... But the old men would sit at their tables, replete and sleepy, and shake their cunning old heads. The press would chatter and make odd ambiguous sounds like a shipload of monkeys in a storm. The political harridans would get the wrong men appointed, would attack every possible leader with scandal and abuse and falsehood....

Restlessness curdled into white-hot fury in his bones. He could not settle back into the crumpled sheets of his bed for even a minute; he hauled himself upright and stalked the perimeter of his chamber, knocking his shins against the edges of armchairs and sideboards in the pitch black. We were far too thick-headed to see what was staring us right in the face; we were shuttling all these boys headlong into grinding hardship and unforgiving peril, sending them off with threadbare kits, no proper backup, feeding our own flesh and blood to the insatiable war god, because at our core we Britons were a nation run by lazy, overindulged, performatively genial old and middle-aged men. That was how he parceled out the unbearable weight of his own guilt to keep from drowning in it. Why was he sitting idly by instead of fighting to fix this rot? What use was the false modesty he clung to, the patience he extended to these sodden old barristers, these blowhard stump speakers, these rigid, slow-witted commanders and inflexible bureaucratic cogs? They were slaughtering Britain’s young men by the thousands. Grey-haired men sat far from the front lines, plotting death for the boys huddled in mud-filled trenches. That was the unvarnished truth of it. “My boy!” he gasped, the words torn out of him in the dark. His awareness of every flaw in our national leadership sharpened to a vicious, almost delirious edge. It felt like every fond, naive belief he’d ever held about our country had crumbled to dust at his feet. What was the point of pretending the men in command upstairs were drafting some brilliant counter-offensive that would win the war for good? It was as obvious as the sun at noon that they had neither the creative vision nor the shared sense to even dream up such a plan. Any slow, lumbering herd can hunker down and defend itself, but it takes bold imagination to go on the attack. Imagination! We’d never summon the nerve to strike by the end of it all. We could send bombers through the sky. We could land troops across the Channel. We could have hit the beaches at the Dardanelles with overwhelming force instead of trickling half-strength battalions there the way our grandfathers dribbled reinforcements to the Siege of Sevastopol. But the old men would sit at their oak desks, bellies full of port and half-asleep, and wag their crafty, wrinkled heads. The papers would prattle on, spitting contradictory nonsense like a cargo hold full of chattering macaques caught in a gale. The vultures of Westminster would hand posts to the least qualified men, tear down every competent would-be leader with lies, smears, and petty vitriol.

Only two types of people could have drifted into the enclosure with the spectral grace of that shadowy figure: a woman or a traveler. He had likely recognized the household's vulnerability on this specific night and chosen it for his bold intrusion. To turn this suspicion into a certainty, a group of travelers was currently known to be camped down in Weatherbury Bottom.

The different accidents of life are not so changeable as the feelings of human nature. I had worked hard for nearly two years, for the sole purpose of infusing life into an inanimate body. For this I had deprived myself of rest and health. I had desired it with an ardour that far exceeded moderation; but now that I had finished, the beauty of the dream vanished, and breathless horror and disgust filled my heart. Unable to endure the aspect of the being I had created, I rushed out of the room and continued a long time traversing my bed-chamber, unable to compose my mind to sleep. At length lassitude succeeded to the tumult I had before endured, and I threw myself on the bed in my clothes, endeavouring to seek a few moments of forgetfulness. But it was in vain; I slept, indeed, but I was disturbed by the wildest dreams. I thought I saw Elizabeth, in the bloom of health, walking in the streets of Ingolstadt. Delighted and surprised, I embraced her, but as I imprinted the first kiss on her lips, they became livid with the hue of death; her features appeared to change, and I thought that I held the corpse of my dead mother in my arms; a shroud enveloped her form, and I saw the grave-worms crawling in the folds of the flannel. I started from my sleep with horror; a cold dew covered my forehead, my teeth chattered, and every limb became convulsed; when, by the dim and yellow light of the moon, as it forced its way through the window shutters, I beheld the wretch—the miserable monster whom I had created. He held up the curtain of the bed; and his eyes, if eyes they may be called, were fixed on me. His jaws opened, and he muttered some inarticulate sounds, while a grin wrinkled his cheeks. He might have spoken, but I did not hear; one hand was stretched out, seemingly to detain me, but I escaped and rushed downstairs. I took refuge in the courtyard belonging to the house which I inhabited, where I remained during the rest of the night, walking up and down in the greatest agitation, listening attentively, catching and fearing each sound as if it were to announce the approach of the demoniacal corpse to which I had so miserably given life.

The shifting fortunes of existence are less mutable than the passions that stir the human soul. For nigh on two years I had laboured without cease, bent wholly upon breathing vitality into a lifeless frame. Rest and vigour I had sacrificed to this end. My yearning had burned with a flame that scorned all moderation; yet now, the work complete, the loveliness of the vision dissolved, and a suffocating loathing and dread flooded my spirit. Unable to bear the sight of the thing I had fashioned, I fled the chamber and paced my bedroom for hours, powerless to quiet my thoughts toward slumber. At last exhaustion overcame the storm within me, and I cast myself upon the bed fully clothed, striving to seize a brief oblivion. But it was futile; I slept, truly, yet was harried by the most savage visions. I fancied I beheld my beloved Clara, radiant with health, strolling the lanes of Heidelberg. Overjoyed and astonished, I clasped her to me, but as I pressed the first kiss to her mouth, her lips grew ashen with the pallor of the grave; her countenance seemed to shift, and I found myself clutching the body of my departed sister; a winding-sheet swathed her shape, and I glimpsed the grave-worms writhing in the folds of the linen. I started awake in terror; an icy sweat glazed my brow, my teeth clattered, and every muscle spasmed; when, by the sickly amber gleam of the moon forcing through the shutter slats, I saw the fiend—the wretched abomination I had conjured into being. He drew aside the bed-curtain; and his eyes, if eyes they could be named, were trained upon me. His mouth gaped, and he mumbled some guttural noise, while a leer creased his cheeks. He may have uttered words, but I heard nothing; one arm extended, as if to seize me, but I wrenched free and plunged down the stairs. I sought sanctuary in the courtyard of the dwelling I occupied, and there I stayed till dawn, striding to and fro in a fever of agitation, straining my ears, starting at and dreading every murmur as though it heralded the approach of that demon-corpse to which I had so wretchedly bestowed life.

“But even if he has acted wickedly,” Rose went on earnestly, “think of how young he still is. He may never have known a mother’s tender love, or the quiet comfort of a steady home; harsh mistreatment and blows, or the pangs of hunger when there was no bread to eat, may have driven him to fall in with men who forced him into wrongdoing. Aunt, dear aunt, for mercy’s sake, stop to consider this before you let them drag this ailing child off to a prison that will, no matter what, be the grave of every chance he has to mend his ways. “Very well; we will let it be so. There is little use in unravelling that now. Sons must be blind if they will. Why is it that a woman can see from a distance what a man cannot see close? Clym must do as he will—he is nothing more to me. And this is maternity—to give one’s best years and best love to ensure the fate of being despised!”

“So be it, then. There’s no sense picking apart that tangled mess at this point. Sons will be willfully blind if that’s what they choose, I suppose. How is it a woman can spot the plain truth from half a mile off, when a man can’t see the very same thing staring him right in the face? Kael can do as he pleases—he is nothing to me any longer. And this is the sum of motherhood, is it? To give your finest years and deepest love just to guarantee you end up scorned and cast aside!”

His tale had occupied the whole day, and the sun was upon the verge of the horizon when he departed. I knew that I ought to hasten my descent towards the valley, as I should soon be encompassed in darkness; but my heart was heavy, and my steps slow. The labour of winding among the little paths of the mountain and fixing my feet firmly as I advanced perplexed me, occupied as I was by the emotions which the occurrences of the day had produced. Night was far advanced when I came to the halfway resting-place and seated myself beside the fountain. The stars shone at intervals as the clouds passed from over them; the dark pines rose before me, and every here and there a broken tree lay on the ground; it was a scene of wonderful solemnity and stirred strange thoughts within me. I wept bitterly, and clasping my hands in agony, I exclaimed, “Oh! stars and clouds and winds, ye are all about to mock me; if ye really pity me, crush sensation and memory; let me become as nought; but if not, depart, depart, and leave me in darkness.”

His story had taken up the entire day, and by the time he left, the sun was already close to the horizon. I knew I should make haste down into the valley, since darkness would soon close around me; yet my heart was heavy, and my pace was slow. The effort of finding my way along the narrow mountain paths and setting my feet securely as I went wore on me, all the more because my mind was occupied by the feelings stirred up by the day’s events. It was well into night when I reached the resting place halfway down and sat beside the fountain. The stars came and went as clouds drifted across them; dark pines stood before me, and here and there a shattered tree lay upon the ground. It was a scene of deep solemnity, and it awakened strange thoughts in me. I wept uncontrollably, and wringing my hands in torment, I cried, “Oh stars, and clouds, and winds, you are all about to taunt me; if you truly have pity on me, destroy feeling and memory, let me be as nothing; but if you do not, then go, go, and leave me in darkness.”

The cast was the conception of a male fused with the delivery of a female. No boy who had ever aimed at sparrow, hare, or field mouse in his youth could have flung with such pure foolishness as now revealed itself.

In cool, fresh weather Mrs. Yeobright would have found no inconvenience in walking to Alderworth, but the present torrid attack made the journey a heavy undertaking for a woman past middle age; and at the end of the third mile she wished that she had hired Fairway to drive her a portion at least of the distance. But from the point at which she had arrived it was as easy to reach Clym’s house as to get home again. So she went on, the air around her pulsating silently, and oppressing the earth with lassitude. She looked at the sky overhead, and saw that the sapphirine hue of the zenith in spring and early summer had been replaced by a metallic violet.

In cool, fresh weather Mrs. Yeobright would have found the walk to Alderworth no trouble at all, but this fierce spell of heat made the journey a serious effort for a woman well past middle age; and by the end of the third mile she wished she had engaged Fairway to drive her at least part of the way. Yet from where she now stood, Clym’s house was no farther to reach than her own home. So she kept on, while the air about her throbbed in stillness and burdened the earth with a weary heaviness. I can't explain what it is," the girl replied. "I only know that it’s the truth, and not just for me, but for hundreds of others as miserable and broken as I am. I have to go back. I almost regretted that my theory about the well's trouble was correct, because I had another one that had a showy point or two about it for a miracle. I remembered that in America, many centuries later, when an oil well ceased to flow, they used to blast it out with a dynamite torpedo. If I should find this well dry and no explanation of it, I could astonish these people most nobly by having a person of no especial value drop a dynamite bomb into it. It was my idea to appoint Merlin. However, it was plain that there was no occasion for the bomb. One cannot have everything the way he would like it. A man has no business to be depressed by a disappointment, anyway; he ought to make up his mind to get even. That is what I did. I said to myself, I am in no hurry, I can wait; that bomb will come good yet. And it did, too.

I nearly wished my notion about the well’s failure hadn’t been right, for I’d spun another idea with a couple of flashy touches that would have seemed miraculous. I recalled how in America, ages later, when an oil well stopped pumping, they’d fire a dynamite charge to break it open. If I came upon this well bone-dry with no obvious cause, I could dazzle the locals splendidly by having some thoroughly expendable soul drop a dynamite bomb down it. I’d meant to volunteer Merlin. But clearly, there was no need for the bomb. You can’t always arrange things to your liking. A fellow shouldn’t let a setback sink his spirits, anyhow; he ought to resolve to settle the score. That’s precisely what I did. I told myself, there’s no rush, I can bide my time; that bomb will still prove its worth. And so it did.

Slavery was dead and gone; all men were equal before the law; taxation had been equalized. The telegraph, the telephone, the phonograph, the typewriter, the sewing-machine, and all the thousand willing and handy servants of steam and electricity were working their way into favor. We had a steamboat or two on the Thames, we had steam warships, and the beginnings of a steam commercial marine; I was getting ready to send out an expedition to discover America.

Chattel slavery had been fully abolished; every person stood equal under the nation’s legal code; tax burdens had been standardized fairly across all ranks of society. Wire telegraphs, domestic telephones, wax cylinder phonographs, office typewriters, motorized sewing machines, and thousands of other nimble, obedient innovations powered by steam and electricity were steadily growing in popularity across households and workplaces. We already operated a pair of steam ferries up and down the Severn, boasted a small fleet of steam-powered naval warships, and were laying the first foundations of a transoceanic steam merchant fleet; I was just putting the final touches on plans to launch an exploratory expedition to discover the Americas.

In fact, if it had not been for a good-hearted turnpike-man, and a benevolent old lady, Oliver’s troubles would have been shortened by the very same process which had put an end to his mother’s; in other words, he would most assuredly have fallen dead upon the king’s highway. But the turnpike-man gave him a meal of bread and cheese; and the old lady, who had a shipwrecked grandson wandering barefoot in some distant part of the earth, took pity upon the poor orphan, and gave him what little she could afford—and more—with such kind and gentle words, and such tears of sympathy and compassion, that they sank deeper into Oliver’s soul, than all the sufferings he had ever undergone.

Had it not been for a kindly tollgate keeper and a charitable old woman, Oliver’s miseries might well have ended just as his mother’s had—struck down lifeless upon the public road. The tollgate keeper offered him a portion of bread and cheese, and the old woman, whose own shipwrecked grandson roamed barefoot in some far corner of the world, looked with pity on the friendless orphan. She gave him what modest help she could spare—and more besides—along with words so tender and tears of such heartfelt sympathy that they carved themselves deeper into Oliver’s heart than all the hardships he had ever borne.
