He toiled on all day, recollecting, at the last moment, that he was to have dinner at the Morses’. Thanks to Brissenden, his black suit was out of pawn and he was again eligible for dinner parties. Down town he stopped off long enough to run into the library and search for Saleeby’s books. He drew out “The Cycle of Life,” and on the car turned to the essay Norton had mentioned on Spencer. As Martin read, he grew angry. His face flushed, his jaw set, and unconsciously his hand clenched, unclenched, and clenched again as if he were taking fresh grips upon some hateful thing out of which he was squeezing the life. When he left the car, he strode along the sidewalk as a wrathful man will stride, and he rang the Morse bell with such viciousness that it roused him to consciousness of his condition, so that he entered in good nature, smiling with amusement at himself. No sooner, however, was he inside than a great depression descended upon him. He fell from the height where he had been up-borne all day on the wings of inspiration. “Bourgeois,” “trader’s den”—Brissenden’s epithets repeated themselves in his mind. But what of that? he demanded angrily. He was marrying Ruth, not her family.

Elias worked through the entire day, only remembering at the eleventh hour that he’d promised to attend dinner at the Halewoods’. Thanks to his old friend Jude, his formal black suit was out of hock, so he was once again presentable for formal social gatherings. He cut his trip downtown short enough to duck into the public library and hunt up Thorne’s published works. He checked out *The Rhythm of Existence*, and once he was seated on the streetcar, flipped straight to the essay Owen had referenced about the philosopher Spencer. As Elias read, a hot fury bubbled up in him. His cheeks burned, his jaw clamped tight, and without noticing he fisted his hand, released it, then fisted it again, as if he was getting a firmer hold on some loathsome creature he was wringing the life out of. When he stepped off the streetcar, he tramped down the sidewalk the way any seething man would, and he jabbed the Halewoods’ doorbell so hard that the harsh buzz jolted him back to awareness of how worked up he was. He smoothed his expression by the time the door opened, chuckling softly at his own ridiculous temper. The second he crossed the threshold, though, a heavy, cold gloom settled over him. He dropped straight down from the high he’d ridden all day, buoyed by the wings of his own creative drive. “Middle-class philistines,” “merchant’s hovel”—Jude’s snide labels for the household looped over and over in his head. But so what? he snapped silently to himself. He was marrying Lila, not her whole tedious family.

They had adequately expressed the pause his vision had put in the conversation. It was a miracle. Never had he so loftily framed a lofty thought. But never had he attempted to frame lofty thoughts in words. That was it. That explained it. He had never tried. But Swinburne had, and Tennyson, and Kipling, and all the other poets. His mind flashed on to his “Pearl-diving.” He had never dared the big things, the spirit of the beauty that was a fire in him. That article would be a different thing when he was done with it. He was appalled by the vastness of the beauty that rightfully belonged in it, and again his mind flashed and dared, and he demanded of himself why he could not chant that beauty in noble verse as the great poets did. The painful thing observable about all this business was the alacrity with which this oppressed community had turned their cruel hands against their own class in the interest of the common oppressor. This man and woman seemed to feel that in a quarrel between a person of their own class and his lord, it was the natural and proper and rightful thing for that poor devil's whole caste to side with the master and fight his battle for him, without ever stopping to inquire into the rights or wrongs of the matter. This man had been out helping to hang his neighbors, and had done his work with zeal, and yet was aware that there was nothing against them but a mere suspicion, with nothing back of it describable as evidence, still neither he nor his wife seemed to see anything horrible about it.

The most agonizing aspect of the whole affair was the eagerness with which this subjugated people turned their violent hands upon their own peers to benefit their shared oppressor. This man and woman seemed convinced that in any dispute between a commoner and his lord, it was the natural and righteous duty of the entire lower class to defend the master, taking up his fight without ever pausing to consider the justice of the case. The man had even assisted in the execution of his neighbors, carrying out the deed with fervor despite knowing the accusations rested on nothing but flimsy suspicion devoid of evidence; yet, to him and his wife, there was nothing remotely ghastly about the betrayal.

Six years had elapsed, passed in a dream but for one indelible trace, and I stood in the same place where I had last embraced my father before my departure for Ingolstadt. Beloved and venerable parent! He still remained to me. I gazed on the picture of my mother, which stood over the mantel-piece. It was an historical subject, painted at my father’s desire, and represented Caroline Beaufort in an agony of despair, kneeling by the coffin of her dead father. Her garb was rustic, and her cheek pale; but there was an air of dignity and beauty, that hardly permitted the sentiment of pity. Below this picture was a miniature of William; and my tears flowed when I looked upon it. While I was thus engaged, Ernest entered: he had heard me arrive, and hastened to welcome me: “Welcome, my dearest Victor,” said he. “Ah! I wish you had come three months ago, and then you would have found us all joyous and delighted. You come to us now to share a misery which nothing can alleviate; yet your presence will, I hope, revive our father, who seems sinking under his misfortune; and your persuasions will induce poor Elizabeth to cease her vain and tormenting self-accusations.—Poor William! he was our darling and our pride!”

Six years had gone by, years that seemed like a dream save for one lasting and unforgotten mark, and I found myself in the very spot where I had last held my father in my arms before setting out for Ingolstadt. Dear and revered parent! He was still alive to me there. My eyes rested on the portrait of my mother that hung above the mantelpiece. It was a historical scene, painted at my father’s request, and showed Caroline Beaufort in the depth of despair, kneeling beside the coffin of her dead father. Her dress was that of a peasant, and her face was pale; yet such dignity and beauty shone through her that pity was scarcely the feeling it inspired. Beneath this painting hung a miniature of William, and tears came to my eyes as I looked at it. While I remained thus absorbed, Ernest came in; he had learned of my return and hurried to greet me. “Welcome, my dearest Victor,” he said. “Ah, I wish you had come three months earlier, and then you would have found us all happy and rejoicing. You come now to share in a sorrow that nothing can soften; yet I hope your presence will restore our father, who seems to be sinking beneath his grief, and your counsel will persuade poor Elizabeth to give up those vain and cruel reproaches against herself.—Poor William! he was our idol and our pride!”

He began by recounting his first meeting with Uncle Capen some thirty years earlier, noting that even then, he had viewed him as an elderly man. He recalled telling him at the time that he was already descending into the twilight of his life, with one foot in the grave. The speaker highlighted Uncle Capen’s moral character, attributing his longevity to his virtuous habits. Since he had given up smoking roughly forty years prior, the speaker advised the young people in attendance to avoid tobacco if they hoped to reach such an advanced age. Furthermore, he had been a total abstainer from alcohol since turning seventy; the speaker urged those who wished to rival his lifespan to follow this example. The speaker concluded with a poignant reference to the relatives in the front pew, comparing their grief to that of John the Baptist’s disciples after his beheading. Then, another hymn was sung.

Nah. First she moved to a garrison town on the far side of Wessex, then she spent several months earning her keep doing seamstress work in Melchester, boarding with a very respectable widow who takes in that kind of piece work. She only turned up at the Union house Sunday morning, as far as I know, and folks around here figure she tramped every last step of the way from Melchester. I can’t tell you why she left her position, because I truly don’t know, and I’d never lie just to make up an answer. That’s all there is to the story, ma’am.

She struggled miserably against this femininity, which insisted on forcing in unsolicited emotions with ever-increasing force. While his reproaches poured over her, she had tried to escape disturbance by fastening her thoughts on the trees, the sky, or any trifling object within sight, but no cleverness could help her now.

By the time they had turned into the Bethnal Green Road, the day had fairly begun to break. Many of the lamps were already extinguished; a few country waggons were slowly toiling on, towards London; now and then, a stage-coach, covered with mud, rattled briskly by: the driver bestowing, as he passed, an admonitory lash upon the heavy waggoner who, by keeping on the wrong side of the road, had endangered his arriving at the office, a quarter of a minute after his time. The public-houses, with gas-lights burning inside, were already open. By degrees, other shops began to be unclosed, and a few scattered people were met with. Then, came straggling groups of labourers going to their work; then, men and women with fish-baskets on their heads; donkey-carts laden with vegetables; chaise-carts filled with live-stock or whole carcasses of meat; milk-women with pails; an unbroken concourse of people, trudging out with various supplies to the eastern suburbs of the town. As they approached the City, the noise and traffic gradually increased; when they threaded the streets between Shoreditch and Smithfield, it had swelled into a roar of sound and bustle. It was as light as it was likely to be, till night came on again, and the busy morning of half the London population had begun.

By the time they had turned into Bethnal Green Road, dawn had fairly set in. Most of the lamps were already out; a few country wagons still laboured slowly onward toward London; and now and then a mud-spattered stage-coach rattled past at speed, the driver giving, as he went by, a warning crack of the whip to some heavy waggoner who, by keeping to the wrong side of the road, had nearly delayed his arrival at the office by a quarter of a minute. The public houses, their gaslights still shining within, were already open. Little by little, other shops began to be unshuttered, and a few isolated figures appeared upon the road. Then came scattered parties of labourers bound for their work; then men and women with fish-baskets balanced on their heads; donkey-carts heaped with vegetables; chaise-carts crowded with live stock or whole carcasses of meat; milk-women carrying pails; and an unbroken stream of people, trudging out with all manner of supplies toward the eastern suburbs of the town. As they drew nearer the City, the noise and movement increased by degrees; and when they threaded the streets between Shoreditch and Smithfield, it had risen to a deafening roar of bustle and confusion. The light was as full as it was likely to be until night returned, and the busy morning life of half the population of London had begun.

Martin went back to his pearl-diving article, which would have been finished sooner if it had not been broken in upon so frequently by his attempts to write poetry. His poems were love poems, inspired by Ruth, but they were never completed. Not in a day could he learn to chant in noble verse. Rhyme and metre and structure were serious enough in themselves, but there was, over and beyond them, an intangible and evasive something that he caught in all great poetry, but which he could not catch and imprison in his own. It was the elusive spirit of poetry itself that he sensed and sought after but could not capture. It seemed a glow to him, a warm and trailing vapor, ever beyond his reaching, though sometimes he was rewarded by catching at shreds of it and weaving them into phrases that echoed in his brain with haunting notes or drifted across his vision in misty wafture of unseen beauty. It was baffling. He ached with desire to express and could but gibber prosaically as everybody gibbered. He read his fragments aloud. The metre marched along on perfect feet, and the rhyme pounded a longer and equally faultless rhythm, but the glow and high exaltation that he felt within were lacking. He could not understand, and time and again, in despair, defeated and depressed, he returned to his article. Prose was certainly an easier medium.

Martin resumed his article on pearl diving, which might have reached completion sooner had it not been so often interrupted by his ventures into verse. His poems were love poems, born of thoughts of Ruth, yet they remained unfinished. He could not, in a single day, master the art of singing in exalted lines. Rhyme, meter, and form posed challenges enough, but beyond these lay something intangible and elusive—a quality he recognized in all great poetry but could never seize and hold within his own. It was the fugitive soul of poetry itself that he felt and pursued without success. To him, it shimmered like a soft, drifting radiance, a warm and trailing mist forever out of reach, though occasionally he was granted shreds of it, which he wove into phrases that rang in his mind with lingering music or passed before his eyes like the hazy breath of unseen loveliness. It was maddening. He burned to give voice to his feelings, yet could only stammer prosaically, as everyone stammered. He read his fragments aloud. The meter stepped along in perfect measure, and the rhyme beat out an equally flawless cadence, but the glow and high rapture he felt inside were absent. Bewildered, time and again, he fell back in defeat and dejection upon his article. Prose was, without question, a gentler medium.

Secondly, these missionaries would gradually, and without creating suspicion or exciting alarm, introduce a rudimentary cleanliness among the nobility, and from them it would work down to the people, if the priests could be kept quiet. This would undermine the Church. I mean would be a step toward that. Next, education--next, freedom --and then she would begin to crumble. It being my conviction that any Established Church is an established crime, an established slave-pen, I had no scruples, but was willing to assail it in any way or with any weapon that promised to hurt it. Why, in my own former day--in remote centuries not yet stirring in the womb of time--there were old Englishmen who imagined that they had been born in a free country: a “free” country with the Corporation Act and the Test still in force in it--timbers propped against men's liberties and dishonored consciences to shore up an Established Anachronism with.

For another thing, these missionaries would slowly introduce basic standards of cleanliness among the nobility, no suspicion or alarm roused in the process, and the practice would seep down to the general population if the priests could be kept from interfering. That would undermine the Church’s foundations— or rather, it would be a critical first step toward that end. Next would come widespread education, then liberty, and at that point the institution would start to crack and crumble. I hold the unshakable conviction that any state-established Church is state-sanctioned crime, an official, institutionalized slave pen, so I had no moral scruples about the work; I was ready to assail it by any method, with any weapon that promised to do real damage. Why, in my own former lifetime—those distant centuries that have not yet stirred in the womb of unwritten time—there were old Englishmen who genuinely believed they had been born in a free country: a “free” nation where the Corporation Act and Test Act remained fully in force, heavy timbers propped against men’s liberties and dishonored consciences, all to shore up that state-endorsed anachronism.

There Julian crouched, gripped by a private horror that defied description, his body convulsing as a deathly chill broke across his flesh. Then, cutting through the stillness, the wind delivered a frantic hubbub from afar—the discordant bellows of a mob seized by shock. To a soul so isolated, any human resonance, however threatening, was a reprieve from the void. The advent of a visible enemy reignited his spent spirit; he hauled himself to his feet and plunged headlong into the gloom.

Boldwood had not been outside his garden since his meeting with Bathsheba in the road to Yalbury. Silent and alone, he had remained in moody meditation on woman’s ways, deeming as essentials of the whole sex the accidents of the single one of their number he had ever closely beheld. By degrees a more charitable temper had pervaded him, and this was the reason of his sally to-night. He had come to apologize and beg forgiveness of Bathsheba with something like a sense of shame at his violence, having but just now learnt that she had returned—only from a visit to Liddy, as he supposed, the Bath escapade being quite unknown to him.

Mr. Barlow had not passed beyond his garden gate since his encounter with Miss Vale upon the road to Yelbury. There, in solitude and silence, he had brooded darkly upon the conduct of women, taking the accidents of the one woman he had ever closely observed for the very constitution of the whole sex. Little by little, however, a gentler spirit had come upon him; and it was this, more than anything else, that had sent him forth that night. He had come to ask pardon of Miss Vale, and to offer an apology for his violence, with something of shame in the recollection of it, having only then discovered that she had returned—so he supposed, merely from a visit to Lydia, the matter at Bath being wholly unknown to him.

Mr. Jarvis Lorry and Miss Manette, emerging from the wine-shop thus, joined Monsieur Defarge in the doorway to which he had directed his own company just before. It opened from a stinking little black courtyard, and was the general public entrance to a great pile of houses, inhabited by a great number of people. In the gloomy tile-paved entry to the gloomy tile-paved staircase, Monsieur Defarge bent down on one knee to the child of his old master, and put her hand to his lips. It was a gentle action, but not at all gently done; a very remarkable transformation had come over him in a few seconds. He had no good-humour in his face, nor any openness of aspect left, but had become a secret, angry, dangerous man.

Mr. Jarvis Lorry and Miss Manette stepped out of the wine-shop and joined Monsieur Defarge at the entrance where he had just gathered his own group. The doorway led from a foul little dark courtyard and served as the common entry to a massive block of buildings, packed with countless tenants. In the dim, tile-floored vestibule that gave onto the dim, tile-floored stairway, Monsieur Defarge dropped to one knee before his former master’s daughter and pressed her hand to his lips. The gesture was tender, yet the execution was anything but; in a matter of moments, a striking change had swept over him. All trace of geniality and openness had vanished from his face, leaving behind a guarded, wrathful, menacing figure.

He had made a toilet of a nicely-adjusted kind—of a nature between the carefully neat and the carelessly ornate—of a degree between fine-market-day and wet-Sunday selection. He thoroughly cleaned his silver watch-chain with whiting, put new lacing straps to his boots, looked to the brass eyelet-holes, went to the inmost heart of the plantation for a new walking-stick, and trimmed it vigorously on his way back; took a new handkerchief from the bottom of his clothes-box, put on the light waistcoat patterned all over with sprigs of an elegant flower uniting the beauties of both rose and lily without the defects of either, and used all the hair-oil he possessed upon his usually dry, sandy, and inextricably curly hair, till he had deepened it to a splendidly novel colour, between that of guano and Roman cement, making it stick to his head like mace round a nutmeg, or wet seaweed round a boulder after the ebb.

He had contrived an attire of a nicely balanced sort, neither over-nice nor slovenly, lying somewhere between a bargain-day finery and a Sunday picked in rain. He scoured his silver watch-chain with whiting till it shone, fitted fresh laces to his boots, inspected the brass eyelets, and then went to the farthest heart of the plantation for a new cane, which he sharply trimmed as he came back. From the bottom of his clothes-chest he drew a clean handkerchief, put on the pale waistcoat scattered all over with little sprigs of an elegant blossom that seemed to unite the graces of rose and lily without the failings of either, and then poured into his normally dry, sandy, and hopelessly curling hair every drop of oil he had. By the time he had done, it was darkened into a wonderfully strange hue, midway between guano and Roman cement, and clung to his head as mace clings about a nutmeg, or wet weed to a rock when the tide has gone out.

Bathsheba went home, her mind occupied with a new trouble, which being rather harassing than deadly was calculated to do good by diverting her from the chronic gloom of her life. She was set thinking a great deal about Oak and of his wish to shun her; and there occurred to Bathsheba several incidents of her latter intercourse with him, which, trivial when singly viewed, amounted together to a perceptible disinclination for her society. It broke upon her at length as a great pain that her last old disciple was about to forsake her and flee. He who had believed in her and argued on her side when all the rest of the world was against her, had at last like the others become weary and neglectful of the old cause, and was leaving her to fight her battles alone.

Bathsheba headed home, her thoughts caught up in a fresh anxiety. Since this trouble was more an irritation than a tragedy, it proved beneficial by drawing her away from the persistent melancholy of her existence. She pondered Oak’s behavior and his apparent wish to avoid her, recalling various recent interactions. Though these moments seemed minor individually, they collectively suggested a clear reluctance to be near her. Eventually, it hit her with a sharp sense of grief that her final loyal follower was about to desert her. The man who had trusted her and defended her when the rest of the world turned away had finally, like everyone else, grown tired of the old loyalty, leaving her to face her struggles entirely alone.

As she went back over the particulars of his looks and words—things she had then hardly noticed—it grew clear to her how much they had been governed by his awareness of this new development. “If he had been the sort of man to nurse resentment against a woman who had refused him, he would have boasted of his good fortune in triumphant tones; instead of that, he said nothing at all, out of consideration for my unhappiness, and only suggested that he still loved me, as someone above him.”

And often, at such times, he would abruptly see slouch in among the company a young hoodlum in square-cut coat and under a stiff-rim Stetson hat. As he rose from his chair and stepped forward across the platform, he saw stalk through the wide door at the rear of the great room the young hoodlum with the square-cut coat and stiff-rim hat. Five hundred fashionably gowned women turned their heads, so intent and steadfast was Martin’s gaze, to see what he was seeing. But they saw only the empty centre aisle. He saw the young tough lurching down that aisle and wondered if he would remove the stiff-rim which never yet had he seen him without. Straight down the aisle he came, and up the platform. Martin could have wept over that youthful shade of himself, when he thought of all that lay before him. Across the platform he swaggered, right up to Martin, and into the foreground of Martin’s consciousness disappeared. The five hundred women applauded softly with gloved hands, seeking to encourage the bashful great man who was their guest. And Martin shook the vision from his brain, smiled, and began to speak.

Often during those moments, he’d suddenly notice a young tough slouching into the gathering, wearing a boxy coat and a stiff-brimmed Stetson. It came upon him one afternoon at the Gallina Society in Oakland. As he got up from his seat and moved toward the front of the stage, he watched the young hoodlum in the boxy coat and rigid-brim hat stride through the broad doorway at the back of the hall. Five hundred elegantly dressed women turned their heads, drawn by the intensity and fixity of Martin’s stare, trying to spot what he saw. But they found only the vacant central aisle. He watched the young rough shamble down that aisle and wondered if he’d take off the stiff brim, which Martin had never yet glimpsed him without. Straight down the aisle he came, then up onto the stage. Martin felt like weeping over that spectral young self when he considered everything the boy still had to face. The figure swaggered across the platform, right up to Martin, and then faded from the foreground of his mind. The five hundred women applauded gently with gloved hands, trying to hearten the shy, celebrated man who was their guest. And Martin shook the image from his thoughts, smiled, and began his speech.

Five dollars for five thousand words, ten words for a cent—that particular thought took up its residence in his brain, and he could no more escape it than he could the “$3.85” under his eyelids. A change seemed to come over the latter, and he watched curiously, till “$2.00” burned in its stead. Ah, he thought, that was the baker. The next sum that appeared was “$2.50.” It puzzled him, and he pondered it as if life and death hung on the solution. He owed somebody two dollars and a half, that was certain, but who was it? To find it was the task set him by an imperious and malignant universe, and he wandered through the endless corridors of his mind, opening all manner of lumber rooms and chambers stored with odds and ends of memories and knowledge as he vainly sought the answer. After several centuries it came to him, easily, without effort, that it was Maria. With a great relief he turned his soul to the screen of torment under his lids. He had solved the problem; now he could rest. But no, the “$2.50” faded away, and in its place burned “$8.00.” Who was that? He must go the dreary round of his mind again and find out.

Five dollars for five thousand words, ten words to a cent—this specific calculation had wedged itself firmly in his skull, and he could no more shake it loose than he could dismiss the glowing “$3.85” burned into the backs of his eyelids. The figure shifted abruptly, and he watched it through heavy lids, curious, until “$2.00” blazed in its place. Ah, he thought, that was what he owed the baker. Next, “$2.50” flickered into view. It stumped him, and he turned the number over and over as if life itself hung on working out who it was for. He definitely owed someone two dollars and fifty cents, that much was certain, but who? The cold, imperious world seemed to demand he find the answer at once, so he wandered the endless winding halls of his memory, prying open every cluttered storage closet and dusty cranny stuffed with half-forgotten facts and random fragments of the past, turning up empty no matter how hard he looked. It felt like centuries passed before the answer hit him out of nowhere, no effort at all: that was Maria. He exhaled in sharp relief, ready to let his frayed mind rest now that he’d cracked the puzzle. But no sooner had he relaxed than the “$2.50” dimmed, and “$8.00” burned bright in its spot. Who did he owe eight dollars to? He’d have to drag himself through that same tedious, exhausting sweep of his memories all over again to find out.

The heaven being spread with this pallid screen and the earth with the darkest vegetation, their meeting-line at the horizon was clearly marked. In such contrast the heath wore the appearance of an instalment of night which had taken up its place before its astronomical hour was come: darkness had to a great extent arrived hereon, while day stood distinct in the sky. Looking upwards, a furze-cutter would have been inclined to continue work; looking down, he would have decided to finish his faggot and go home. The distant rims of the world and of the firmament seemed to be a division in time no less than a division in matter. The face of the heath by its mere complexion added half an hour to evening; it could in like manner retard the dawn, sadden noon, anticipate the frowning of storms scarcely generated, and intensify the opacity of a moonless midnight to a cause of shaking and dread.

The sky was draped in a pale shroud, while the earth lay clothed in the deepest vegetation, so that the line where they met at the horizon stood out with stark precision. In this sharp division, the heath looked like a patch of night that had settled there before its appointed hour: darkness had already claimed much of the ground, though day still shone clearly overhead. A furze-cutter glancing upward would have felt inclined to keep working; glancing down, he would have thought better of it, bound his bundle, and made for home. The far edges of land and sky seemed to mark a rift in time as much as in substance. The very complexion of the heath added half an hour to the evening; in the same way it could hold back the dawn, cast a gloom over noon, herald the frown of storms not yet born, and deepen the blackness of a moonless midnight into something that made men tremble.

In due time we were challenged by the warders, from the castle walls, and after a parley admitted. I have nothing pleasant to tell about that visit. But it was not a disappointment, for I knew Mrs. le Fay by reputation, and was not expecting anything pleasant. She was held in awe by the whole realm, for she had made everybody believe she was a great sorceress. All her ways were wicked, all her instincts devilish. She was loaded to the eyelids with cold malice. All her history was black with crime; and among her crimes murder was common. I was most curious to see her; as curious as I could have been to see Satan. To my surprise she was beautiful; black thoughts had failed to make her expression repulsive, age had failed to wrinkle her satin skin or mar its bloomy freshness. She could have passed for old Uriens' granddaughter, she could have been mistaken for sister to her own son.

Before long, the guards posted on the castle ramparts called out to halt us, and we were let inside only after a short exchange. I don’t have a single nice thing to say about that visit, but that didn’t come as a letdown. I’d heard all about Mrs. le Fay’s reputation long before we arrived, so I’d never gone in anticipating a pleasant experience anyway. The entire kingdom feared her, because she’d convinced every last person she was a powerful sorceress. Every choice she made was cruel, every instinct she had was twisted. She brimmed with unfeeling, spiteful malice from head to toe. Her entire past was stained with cruelty, and murder was far from an uncommon offense for her. I’d been desperate to meet her, as curious about her as I would have been to lay eyes on the devil himself. I was shocked to find she was stunningly beautiful: the darkness of her mind hadn’t twisted her face into something ugly, and the years hadn’t etched a single wrinkle into her smooth, silken skin, or dimmed its soft, dewy glow. She could easily have passed for old Uriens’ granddaughter, and anyone would have mistaken her for her own son’s sister.

_I do not think you Germans realise how steadily you were conquering the world before this war began. Your science was five years, your social and economic organisation was a quarter of a century in front of ours.... Never has it so lain in the power of a great people to lead and direct mankind towards the world republic and universal peace. It needed but a certain generosity of the imagination...._

I don’t believe you people of the German Commonwealth fully grasp how methodically you were winning global preeminence in the years before this conflict erupted. If you’d poured even half the drive and ingenuity you’ve sunk into this bloodshed into winning over the hearts and minds of people across the globe peacefully, I am convinced you could have claimed world leadership without a single shot fired, no soul left to contest your claim. Your scientific advances ran a full five years ahead of ours, while your social welfare frameworks and economic governance were a full twenty-five years more sophisticated than any system we had built to date. There has never been a moment where a great people held more power to steer all of humanity toward a global commonwealth and universal, enduring peace. All it would have required was a small measure of generous, far-seeing imagination.

As the time dragged on I got nervously afraid we shouldn't have enough of it left for our needs; so I made several premature attempts, and merely delayed things by it; for I couldn't seem to touch a padlock, there in the dark, without starting a rattle out of it which interrupted somebody's sleep and made him turn over and wake some more of the gang.

I stopped there, but the king was delighted and entirely satisfied. That night, we waited in silence for our fellow captives to drift off and signal their slumber in the usual way, for it is unwise to risk much on such desperate men if it can be avoided. It is always better to keep your own counsel. They likely tossed and turned no more than they did any other night, yet to my ears, it felt as though they would never settle into a steady rhythm of snoring. As the hours crawled by, I grew frantic that we would run out of darkness before our work was done. My impatience led me to make several premature movements that only served to hinder us; in the gloom, I could not so much as touch a padlock without it clattering, a sound that startled someone from his sleep and caused him to stir, waking the rest of the group in turn.

It was a “bluff” you know. At such a time it is sound judgment to put on a bold face and play your hand for a hundred times what it is worth; forty-nine times out of fifty nobody dares to “call,” and you rake in the chips. But just this once--well, things looked squally! In just no time, five hundred knights were scrambling into their saddles, and before you could wink a widely scattering drove were under way and clattering down upon me. I snatched both revolvers from the holsters and began to measure distances and calculate chances.

It was all a ruse, you see. When you’re stuck in a tight spot like that, the only sound call is to hold your head high and play your hand for a hundred times its actual worth; forty-nine times out of fifty, no one has the grit to call your bet, and you walk away with the whole pot. But this one time? Well, things were looking very bad, very fast. Barely two heartbeats had passed before five hundred mounted riders were scrambling into their stirrups, and before I could blink, a ragged, spreading charge was under way, thundering straight toward me. I wrenched both revolvers from their leather holsters, my head already spinning to measure the distance between us and weigh my slim odds.

His ancient head swayed with a frail motion as his dim, drifting eyes roved over the gathering. The same look of wretchedness I had seen in the young servant was visible on every face in the room—the silent endurance of brute animals who recognize that they must suffer without complaint.

When I had concluded my narration, I said, “This is the being whom I accuse and for whose seizure and punishment I call upon you to exert your whole power. It is your duty as a magistrate, and I believe and hope that your feelings as a man will not revolt from the execution of those functions on this occasion.”

Once I’d reached the end of my account, I leaned forward slightly and spoke sharply, “This is the creature I hold accountable for every atrocity I’ve just recounted, and I demand you marshal every resource at your disposal to seize it and see it punished. You are bound to this by your oath as a justice, and I trust, even hope, that your own moral compass as a private person will not rebel against fulfilling that office where this matter is concerned.”

The night passed away, and the sun rose from the ocean; my feelings became calmer, if it may be called calmness when the violence of rage sinks into the depths of despair. I left the house, the horrid scene of the last night’s contention, and walked on the beach of the sea, which I almost regarded as an insuperable barrier between me and my fellow creatures; nay, a wish that such should prove the fact stole across me. I desired that I might pass my life on that barren rock, wearily, it is true, but uninterrupted by any sudden shock of misery. If I returned, it was to be sacrificed or to see those whom I most loved die under the grasp of a dæmon whom I had myself created.

The night wore away, and the sun rose out of the sea. My feelings grew quieter, if quietness it can be called when the fury of rage sinks down into the depths of despair. I left the house, the dreadful scene of the previous night’s conflict, and wandered along the shore, which I almost thought of as an impassable wall between myself and my fellow beings; indeed, the wish that this might truly be so crossed my mind. I longed to spend my life upon that barren rock, lonely, it is true, but free from any sudden blow of misery. If I returned, it would be to be offered up, or to watch those I loved most die in the grasp of a demon whom I myself had made.

The idea took hold of Mr. Direck's mind that until they got through the park gate it would be quite out of order to say anything. The lane and the road and the stile and the gate were all so much preliminary stuff to be got through before one could get to business. But after the little white gate the way was clear, the park opened out and one could get ahead without bothering about the steering. And Mr. Direck had, he felt, been diplomatically involved in lanes and by-ways long enough.

Mr. Direck was fully settled in the belief that bringing up what he needed to say before they passed through the park gate would be entirely out of line. The winding lane, the open road, the wooden stile, and the entry gate all felt like nothing more than formalities to work through before he could get to the actual point. Once they stepped past that small white gate, though, the path would clear, the whole park would open up around them, and they could move forward without having to fuss over navigating every tricky little turn. And Mr. Direck felt he’d already spent far too long tiptoeing around diplomatic niceties, stuck in those meandering, roundabout lead-up moments.

Martin shook his head, but gave no explanations. How could he explain? He was appalled at the awful intellectual chasm that yawned between him and his people. He could never cross it and explain to them his position,—the Nietzschean position, in regard to socialism. There were not words enough in the English language, nor in any language, to make his attitude and conduct intelligible to them. Their highest concept of right conduct, in his case, was to get a job. That was their first word and their last. It constituted their whole lexicon of ideas. Get a job! Go to work! Poor, stupid slaves, he thought, while his sister talked. Small wonder the world belonged to the strong. The slaves were obsessed by their own slavery. A job was to them a golden fetich before which they fell down and worshipped.

Martin shook his head, but offered no explanation. How could he? He was horrified by the vast intellectual gulf that yawned between himself and his people. He could never bridge it and make them understand his position—the Nietzschean position—toward socialism. There were not enough words in English, or in any language, to render his attitude and conduct intelligible to them. Their highest notion of right conduct, as it applied to him, was that he should get a job. That was their first word and their last. It made up the whole vocabulary of their thought. Get a job! Go to work! Poor, foolish slaves, he thought, as his sister went on talking. No wonder the world belonged to the strong. The slaves were forever preoccupied with their own slavery. To them a job was a golden idol before which they knelt and worshipped.

Look into the world; think how many hearts you would be proud to gain, are there. Confide some other passion to me, if you will; I will be the truest, warmest, and most faithful friend you have.”

“It is this,” Rose replied, “that you must strive to forget me—not as your old, deeply cherished companion, for that would cut me to the quick—but as the object of your romantic love. Look out into the world, and think of how many hearts there are whose regard you would be proud to earn. If you wish to confide any other feelings to me, I will be the truest, warmest, most faithful friend you could ever have.”

We are still surrounded by mountains of ice, still in imminent danger of being crushed in their conflict. The cold is excessive, and many of my unfortunate comrades have already found a grave amidst this scene of desolation. Frankenstein has daily declined in health; a feverish fire still glimmers in his eyes, but he is exhausted, and when suddenly roused to any exertion, he speedily sinks again into apparent lifelessness.

The cold is extreme, and many of my unhappy companions have already found their graves in this waste of desolation. Frankenstein’s health has daily declined; a feverish fire yet burns in his eyes, but he is worn out, and when suddenly summoned to effort, he quickly sinks again into seeming lifelessness.

It was still the beaming time of evening, though night was stealthily making itself visible low down upon the ground, the western lines of light raking the earth without alighting upon it to any extent, or illuminating the dead levels at all. The sun had crept round the tree as a last effort before death, and then began to sink, the shearers’ lower parts becoming steeped in embrowning twilight, whilst their heads and shoulders were still enjoying day, touched with a yellow of self-sustained brilliancy that seemed inherent rather than acquired.

The evening still held its bright, warm glow, even as night crept in quiet and unassuming low to the ground. Slanted western rays skimmed the earth’s surface but barely settled there, doing nothing at all to illuminate the flat, featureless stretches of plain. The sun had slid around the tree trunk in one final, fading push before dipping out of sight, and as it sank lower, the shearers’ legs and lower torsos were swallowed up by deepening, bronze-tinged twilight. Their heads and shoulders, though, still basked in the last of the daylight, gilded with a bright honey-yellow glow that looked as if it emanated from their own skin rather than being cast down by the fading sun.

Coggan furnished a suitable reply. Oak went on with his shearing, and said not another word. Pestilent moods had come, and teased away his quiet. Bathsheba had shown indications of anointing him above his fellows by installing him as the bailiff that the farm imperatively required. He did not covet the post relatively to the farm: in relation to herself, as beloved by him and unmarried to another, he had coveted it. His readings of her seemed now to be vapoury and indistinct. His lecture to her was, he thought, one of the absurdest mistakes. Far from coquetting with Boldwood, she had trifled with himself in thus feigning that she had trifled with another. He was inwardly convinced that, in accordance with the anticipations of his easy-going and worse-educated comrades, that day would see Boldwood the accepted husband of Miss Everdene. Gabriel at this time of his life had out-grown the instinctive dislike which every Christian boy has for reading the Bible, perusing it now quite frequently, and he inwardly said, “‘I find more bitter than death the woman whose heart is snares and nets!’” This was mere exclamation—the froth of the storm. He adored Bathsheba just the same.

Coggan gave a fitting response. Oak kept shearing, and didn’t speak another word. Sour, gnawing restlessness had settled over him, chasing off his usual quiet calm. Bathsheba had dropped hints that she might elevate him above the rest of the workers, naming him the bailiff the farm so desperately needed. The position itself, as just a role managing the farm, didn’t appeal to him at all: what he’d coveted was the closeness it would bring to her, the woman he loved, who was not yet promised to anyone else. All his previous guesses about her feelings and intentions felt foggy and untrustworthy now. That sharp lecture he’d given her, he thought, was one of the most idiotic mistakes he’d ever made. She hadn’t been flirting with Boldwood at all; instead, she’d been toying with him, putting on a whole show of leading another man on just to rattle him. He was convinced deep down that his laid-back, less educated coworkers had been right all along—by the end of the day, Boldwood would be Miss Everdene’s accepted fiancé. At this point in his life, Gabriel had outgrown the automatic, instinctive dislike of reading the Bible that nearly every Christian boy carries through childhood, and he pored over its pages fairly often now. In his head, he bit out a verse he knew well: “‘I find more bitter than death the woman whose heart is snares and nets!’” That was just a burst of angry frustration, nothing more—froth floating on the surface of a roiling storm. He adored Bathsheba just as fiercely as he always had.

Sometimes he glanced over the magazines and newspapers to see how “Ephemera” was being maltreated. It had made a hit. But what a hit! Everybody had read it, and everybody was discussing whether or not it was really poetry. The local papers had taken it up, and daily there appeared columns of learned criticisms, facetious editorials, and serious letters from subscribers. Helen Della Delmar (proclaimed with a flourish of trumpets and rolling of tomtoms to be the greatest woman poet in the United States) denied Brissenden a seat beside her on Pegasus and wrote voluminous letters to the public, proving that he was no poet.

Every so often he flipped through magazines and local dailies to track how badly “Transience” was being torn apart. It had blown up in the public consciousness. But what a bizarre, bruising kind of hit it was! Everyone had read it, and every social circle and street corner hummed with debates over whether it qualified as real poetry at all. Regional papers had latched onto the conversation, and each day brought columns of stuffy academic criticism, snarky facetious editorials, and earnest, lengthy letters from subscribed readers. Elara Voss—proclaimed with blaring trumpets and rolling drum fanfare to be the greatest woman poet in the United States—refused to grant Hale a seat beside her on Pegasus, and wrote reams of open letters to the public, laying out exhaustive proof that he was no true poet.

On a street corner, standing under a gas-light and looking over the elevated road at the moon, was a man. A long man he was, dressed decent, with a segar between his teeth, and I saw that his nose made two twists from bridge to end, like the wriggle of a snake. Tobin saw it at the same time, and I heard him breathe hard like a horse when you take the saddle off. He went straight up to the man, and I went with him.

At the turning of a street, beneath a gas lamp and gazing across the elevated track toward the moon, stood a man. He was a lank fellow, respectably dressed, with a cigar between his teeth, and I observed that his nose bent in two crooked turns from bridge to tip, like the twisting of a snake. Tobias saw it at the same moment, and I heard him draw a hard breath, as a horse does when the saddle is lifted from its back. He walked at once up to the man, and I followed with him.

For the first six months after Oliver Twist was removed, the system was in full operation. It was rather expensive at first, in consequence of the increase in the undertaker’s bill, and the necessity of taking in the clothes of all the paupers, which fluttered loosely on their wasted, shrunken forms, after a week or two’s gruel. But the number of workhouse inmates got thin as well as the paupers; and the board were in ecstasies.

During the initial half-year following Oliver Twist’s departure, the scheme ran at full tilt. At the outset it proved somewhat costly, owing to a steep rise in the undertaker’s charges and the need to alter the paupers’ garments, which, after a fortnight of thin gruel, hung slackly on their gaunt and dwindling frames. Yet the workhouse population thinned out along with the paupers themselves, and the board was enraptured.

The peasant woman, perceiving that my mother fixed eyes of wonder and admiration on this lovely girl, eagerly communicated her history. She was not her child, but the daughter of a Milanese nobleman. Her mother was a German and had died on giving her birth. The infant had been placed with these good people to nurse: they were better off then. They had not been long married, and their eldest child was but just born. The father of their charge was one of those Italians nursed in the memory of the antique glory of Italy—one among the _schiavi ognor frementi,_ who exerted himself to obtain the liberty of his country. He became the victim of its weakness. Whether he had died or still lingered in the dungeons of Austria was not known. His property was confiscated; his child became an orphan and a beggar. She continued with her foster parents and bloomed in their rude abode, fairer than a garden rose among dark-leaved brambles.

The peasant woman, seeing that my mother had turned eyes of wonder and admiration upon the lovely girl, hastened to tell her story. She was not her own child, but the daughter of a nobleman of Milan. Her mother had been German and had died in bringing her into the world. The infant had been entrusted to these kind people to be nursed; at that time they were in better circumstances. They had only lately been married, and their first child had just been born. The girl’s father was one of those Italians who had been reared on memories of ancient Italy’s glory—one of the _schiavi ognor frementi,_ who labored for the freedom of their country. He had fallen a victim to its weakness. Whether he had died, or still lay wasting away in the prisons of Austria, no one knew. His estate had been seized; his child was left an orphan and a beggar. She remained with her foster parents and grew up in their humble dwelling, fairer than a garden rose among dark-leaved brambles.

Martin took a bath, after which he found that the head laundryman had disappeared. Most likely he had gone for a glass of beer Martin decided, but the half-mile walk down to the village to find out seemed a long journey to him. He lay on his bed with his shoes off, trying to make up his mind. He did not reach out for a book. He was too tired to feel sleepy, and he lay, scarcely thinking, in a semi-stupor of weariness, until it was time for supper. Joe did not appear for that function, and when Martin heard the gardener remark that most likely he was ripping the slats off the bar, Martin understood. He went to bed immediately afterward, and in the morning decided that he was greatly rested. Joe being still absent, Martin procured a Sunday paper and lay down in a shady nook under the trees. The morning passed, he knew not how. He did not sleep, nobody disturbed him, and he did not finish the paper. He came back to it in the afternoon, after dinner, and fell asleep over it.

After his bath, Julian discovered that the chief launderman had vanished. Most likely he had strolled down for a glass of ale, Julian thought, but the half-mile trudge to the village to make certain seemed a journey of formidable length. He stretched out on his bed with his shoes off, trying to settle the matter. He did not reach for a book. He was too spent to be sleepy, and he lay with scarcely a thought in a half-conscious stupor of fatigue until supper time came. Owen did not show up for that meal, and when Julian overheard the gardener say that he was probably wrenching the slats from the tavern, Julian understood at once. He went to bed straight afterward, and by morning felt himself immensely refreshed. With Owen still away, Julian bought a Sunday newspaper and lay down in a cool patch of shade beneath the trees. How the morning slipped by he could not have said. He neither slept nor was disturbed by anyone, and he did not get to the end of the paper. He returned to it after dinner in the afternoon and dozed off with it in his hands.

He crossed over towards Bathsheba, who turned to greet him with a carriage of perfect ease. He spoke to her in low tones, and she instinctively modulated her own to the same pitch, and her voice ultimately even caught the inflection of his. She was far from having a wish to appear mysteriously connected with him; but woman at the impressionable age gravitates to the larger body not only in her choice of words, which is apparent every day, but even in her shades of tone and humour, when the influence is great.

He came across to Bathsheba, who turned to meet him with an air of complete composure. He addressed her quietly, and she, by instinct, lowered her own voice to match his; before long, her speech had even taken on his inflection. She was by no means eager to seem secretly linked with him; yet a woman at an impressionable age is drawn toward the stronger presence not only in her choice of words, as is seen every day, but even in the finer shades of tone and feeling, when the influence is powerful.

He had no light and careless touches in his constitution, either for good or for evil. Stern in the outlines of action, mild in the details, he was serious throughout all. He saw no absurd sides to the follies of life, and thus, though not quite companionable in the eyes of merry men and scoffers, and those to whom all things show life as a jest, he was not intolerable to the earnest and those acquainted with grief. Being a man who read all the dramas of life seriously, if he failed to please when they were comedies, there was no frivolous treatment to reproach him for when they chanced to end tragically.

There was nothing light or whimsical in his makeup, neither in virtue nor in vice. Resolute in the broad strokes of conduct, gentle in the finer points, he moved through life with unbroken gravity. He perceived no ridiculous edges to human foolishness, and so, while he was never quite congenial to the laughers and mockers—those for whom existence is a jest—he was not unbearable to the sincere or to those who had known sorrow. A man who took every scene of life’s drama in earnest, if he failed to charm when the play was comic, at least no flippant handling could be held against him when the final act turned tragic.

"My dear Parent, this is a swearing letter. I must let go to somebody. And somehow none of the other chaps are convenient. I don't know if I ought to be put against a wall and shot for it, but I hereby declare that all the officers of this battalion over and above the rank of captain are a constellation of incapables--and several of the captains are herewith included. Some of them are men of a pleasant disposition and carefully aborted mental powers, and some are men of an unpleasant disposition and no mental powers at all. And I believe--a little enlightened by your recent letter to _The Times_--that they are a fair sample of the entire 'army' class which has got to win this war. Usually they are indolent, but when they are thoroughly roused they are fussy. The time they should spend in enlarging their minds and increasing their military efficiency they devote to keeping fit. They are, roughly speaking, fit--for nothing. They cannot move us thirty miles without getting half of us left about, without losing touch with food and shelter, and starving us for thirty-six hours or so in the process, and they cannot count beyond the fingers of one hand, not having learnt to use the nose for arithmetical operations.... I conclude this war is going to be a sort of Battle of Inkerman on a large scale. We chaps in the ranks will have to do the job. Leading is 'off.'...

My dear Father, consider this a letter of pure frustration. I need to vent to someone, and none of the other fellows here are suitable. I might deserve to be lined up and shot for saying so, but I’m declaring here and now that every officer in this battalion ranked higher than captain is a total incompetent—and that goes for a few of the captains as well. Some are pleasant enough but have effectively shut down their minds, while others are simply unpleasant and brainless. Judging by your latest letter to the paper, they seem to be a standard sample of the officer class tasked with winning this war. They are typically lazy, yet when they are finally motivated, they become obsessively fussy. The time they ought to spend sharpening their intellects and improving their tactical skills is instead wasted on staying "fit." In reality, they are fit for nothing. They can't manage a thirty-mile march without losing half the men, losing track of our food and quarters, and leaving us to go hungry for thirty-six hours. They can’t even count past five, having never learned to use their brains for simple arithmetic. I’ve concluded this war will be like the Battle of Inkerman on a massive scale. We in the ranks will have to get the job done ourselves. True leadership is off the table.

“The day I mean,” said Yeobright, his voice growing louder and harsher, “was the day you shut the door against my mother and killed her. O, it is too much—too bad!” He leant over the footpiece of the bedstead for a few moments, with his back towards her; then rising again—“Tell me, tell me! tell me—do you hear?” he cried, rushing up to her and seizing her by the loose folds of her sleeve.

“The day I’m talking about,” Yeobright said, his voice rising and growing rougher with rage, “was the day you shut the door in my mother’s face and killed her. Oh, this is too much—too unforgivable!” He leaned over the bed’s footboard for a few long seconds, his back turned to her, then pushed himself upright again. “Tell me, answer me! Answer me—do you hear me?” he shouted, surging forward to fist the loose, hanging fold of her sleeve.

Eating his last slices of bread and ham, and drinking from the bottle of cider he had taken the precaution to bring with him, he got into the lonely waggon. Here he spread half of the hay as a bed, and, as well as he could in the darkness, pulled the other half over him by way of bed-clothes, covering himself entirely, and feeling, physically, as comfortable as ever he had been in his life. Inward melancholy it was impossible for a man like Oak, introspective far beyond his neighbours, to banish quite, whilst conning the present untoward page of his history. So, thinking of his misfortunes, amorous and pastoral, he fell asleep, shepherds enjoying, in common with sailors, the privilege of being able to summon the god instead of having to wait for him.

Finishing the last of his bread and ham, and taking a draught from the flask of cider he had wisely brought with him, he climbed into the desolate cart. There he made one half of the hay serve him for a couch, and, as far as the blackness would allow, drew the rest over him in place of coverings, so that he was hidden wholly beneath it, and, in body at least, felt as easy as ever he had done in his days. Yet the inward heaviness it was beyond a man like Dale, who brooded more deeply than those about him, altogether to dismiss while turning over this ill-starred chapter of his life. Thus, meditating on his troubles, both tender and rural, he sank at last to sleep, shepherds possessing, like mariners, the rare advantage of being able to call up the deity rather than wait to be summoned by him.

The footsteps were incessant, and the hurry of them became more and more rapid. The corner echoed and re-echoed with the tread of feet; some, as it seemed, under the windows; some, as it seemed, in the room; some coming, some going, some breaking off, some stopping altogether; all in the distant streets, and not one within sight.

The footfalls never relented, their harried pace growing swifter by the moment. The street corner rang and rang again with the drumming of soles; some sounded as if they sat just beneath the windows, others as if they moved through the very room itself; some advanced, some retreated, some cut off abruptly, some halted entirely—all of them echoed up from far-off thoroughfares, not a single figure in view.

If this journey had taken place during my days of study and happiness, it would have afforded me inexpressible pleasure. But a blight had come over my existence, and I only visited these people for the sake of the information they might give me on the subject in which my interest was so terribly profound. Company was irksome to me; when alone, I could fill my mind with the sights of heaven and earth; the voice of Henry soothed me, and I could thus cheat myself into a transitory peace. But busy, uninteresting, joyous faces brought back despair to my heart. I saw an insurmountable barrier placed between me and my fellow men; this barrier was sealed with the blood of William and Justine, and to reflect on the events connected with those names filled my soul with anguish.

Had I undertaken this voyage in the years of my contentment and learning, it would have filled me with unutterable delight. But a shadow had fallen across my life, and I sought out these people only for what they might reveal about the matter that consumed me with such dreadful intensity. The presence of others wearied me; in solitude, I could steep my mind in the visions of earth and sky; Henry’s voice brought me comfort, and I could deceive myself into a fleeting calm. Yet bustling, indifferent, cheerful faces plunged me back into despair. I perceived an impassable gulf fixed between me and my fellow creatures, a gulf sealed with the blood of William and Justine, and to dwell upon the memories those names summoned wracked my soul with torment.

The mender of roads went home, with the dust going on before him according to the set of the wind, and was soon at the fountain, squeezing himself in among the lean kine brought there to drink, and appearing even to whisper to them in his whispering to all the village. When the village had taken its poor supper, it did not creep to bed, as it usually did, but came out of doors again, and remained there. A curious contagion of whispering was upon it, and also, when it gathered together at the fountain in the dark, another curious contagion of looking expectantly at the sky in one direction only. Monsieur Gabelle, chief functionary of the place, became uneasy; went out on his house-top alone, and looked in that direction too; glanced down from behind his chimneys at the darkening faces by the fountain below, and sent word to the sacristan who kept the keys of the church, that there might be need to ring the tocsin by-and-bye.

The stone-breaker made his way back, the grit of the path swirling ahead of him on the shifting gusts. He reached the communal well, pressing through the throng of scrawny oxen that had come for water, and appeared to murmur his secrets into their ears as he had to the rest of the hamlet. Once the people had consumed their sparse evening crusts, they did not retreat to their beds as was their habit, but emerged once more into the lanes to wait. A restless infection of hushed talk pervaded the air, joined by a collective, fixed gaze toward a solitary quarter of the night sky. Master Vane, the local overseer, grew troubled; he ascended to his rooftop in solitude, watching that same distant point. From the shelter of his chimneys, he observed the shadowed, expectant faces gathered at the water below, and dispatched a runner to the sexton with word that the iron tongue of the church bell might soon be summoned to cry out.

"Very nearly two-thirds of the papers read before our Massachusetts society since my connection with it, have dealt with that very question," Mr. Direck began. "And one of our most influential members was able to secure the services of a very able and highly trained young woman from Michigan University, to make a digest of all these representative utterances. We are having it printed in a thoroughly artistic mariner, as the club book for our autumn season. The drift of her results is that religion isn't the same thing as religions. That most religions are old and that religion is always new.... Well, putting it simply, religion is the perpetual rediscovery of that Great Thing Out There.... What the Great Thing is goes by all sorts of names, but if you know it's there and if you remember it's there, you've got religion.... That's about how she figured it out.... I shall send you the book as soon as a copy comes over to me.... I can't profess to put it as clearly as she puts it. She's got a real analytical mind. But it's one of the most suggestive lil' books I've ever seen. It just takes hold of you and _makes_ you think."

“Almost two-thirds of the papers read before our Massachusetts association during my time with it have concerned that very point,” said Mr. Vandeleer at last. “One of our most influential men was able to engage a remarkably capable and well-trained young lady from the University of Michigan to prepare a digest of all those representative utterances. We are having it issued in a most tasteful manner as the club volume for our autumn season. The drift of her conclusions is that religion is not the same thing as religions. That most religions are old, while religion itself is forever new.... Well, to put it simply, religion is the continual rediscovery of that Great Thing Beyond.... What that Great Thing may be is called by many names, but if you know it is there and if you keep in mind that it is there, then you have religion.... That is about the way she worked it out.... I shall send you the book as soon as a copy reaches me.... I should not pretend to state it as clearly as she does. She has a genuinely analytical mind. But it is one of the most suggestive little books I have ever seen. It grips you and makes you think.”

On the marriage morning, Doctor Manette had made it his one urgent and express request to Charles Darnay, that the secret of this name should be--unless he, the Doctor, dissolved the obligation--kept inviolate between them. Nobody else knew it to be his name; his own wife had no suspicion of the fact; Mr. Lorry could have none.

On the day of the wedding, Doctor Manette had pressed upon Charles Darnay a single, emphatic plea: that the truth of that name should remain a sacred trust between them alone, unless he himself chose to release Darnay from the vow. No one else was aware it belonged to him; even his wife had no inkling of the matter, and Mr. Lorry could have none.

After the door was closed and locked, Dulcie fell upon her bed, crushing her black tip, and cried for ten minutes. General Kitchener was her only friend. He was Dulcie’s ideal of a gallant knight. He looked as if he might have a secret sorrow, and his wonderful moustache was a dream, and she was a little afraid of that stern yet tender look in his eyes. She used to have little fancies that he would call at the house sometime, and ask for her, with his sword clanking against his high boots. Once, when a boy was rattling a piece of chain against a lamp-post she had opened the window and looked out. But there was no use. She knew that General Kitchener was away over in Japan, leading his army against the savage Turks; and he would never step out of his gilt frame for her. Yet one look from him had vanquished Piggy that night. Yes, for that night.

Once the door was securely bolted, Dulcie collapsed onto her bed, flattening the black feather on her hat, and wept for ten minutes. General Kitchener was her solitary companion, the very image of a chivalrous hero. He appeared to harbor some hidden grief, and his magnificent moustache was the stuff of dreams; she felt a slight intimidation from the mixture of sternness and warmth in his eyes. She frequently daydreamed that he might visit her one day, his saber clinking against his tall boots as he called her name. Once, when a boy rattled a chain against a streetlamp, she had even thrown open the window to check. But it was no use. She knew the General was far away in Japan, leading his forces against the fierce Turks, and he would never actually step out of his gold-leafed frame for her. Still, a single glance at him had defeated Piggy this evening. Yes, at least for tonight.

He left escort and escorted without saying a word, however, and went into the guard-room; meanwhile, they sat upon their horses outside the gate. Looking about him while in this state of suspense, Charles Darnay observed that the gate was held by a mixed guard of soldiers and patriots, the latter far outnumbering the former; and that while ingress into the city for peasants’ carts bringing in supplies, and for similar traffic and traffickers, was easy enough, egress, even for the homeliest people, was very difficult. A numerous medley of men and women, not to mention beasts and vehicles of various sorts, was waiting to issue forth; but, the previous identification was so strict, that they filtered through the barrier very slowly. Some of these people knew their turn for examination to be so far off, that they lay down on the ground to sleep or smoke, while others talked together, or loitered about. The red cap and tri-colour cockade were universal, both among men and women.

He left both his armed escort and the man they’d been accompanying without a single syllable of explanation, stepping straight into the cramped guard post; the rest of the party remained mounted just beyond the gate, shifting restlessly in their saddles. Strung taut between dread and uncertainty as he waited, Elias Thorne scanned the gate perimeter, noting the sentry detail was a jumble of regular army troops and citizen patriots, the latter far outstripping the former in number. He also saw that while entry into the city was nearly unimpeded for peasant carts hauling grain and produce, and for other small-scale traders and their wares, exiting the walls was brutally difficult, even for the most unassuming, harmless-looking residents. A chaotic throng of men, women, even children, not to mention draft animals and rickety carts of every description, had clustered at the barrier waiting to leave; the required pre-exit identity checks were so rigorous that only a handful of people slipped through each minute. Dozens in the crowd knew their turn for questioning was still hours off, so they’d stretched out on the hard packed dirt to nap or roll rough tobacco, while others clustered in chattering knots or wandered aimlessly along the edge of the throng. The red liberty cap and tricolor cockade were near-universal, pinned to hats, coats, and frayed neck scarves alike on every man and woman in the press of people.

The glimmering vision was rent asunder and dissipated by Arthur, who, all evening, had been trying to draw his wild man out. Martin Eden remembered his decision. For the first time he became himself, consciously and deliberately at first, but soon lost in the joy of creating in making life as he knew it appear before his listeners’ eyes. He had been a member of the crew of the smuggling schooner _Halcyon_ when she was captured by a revenue cutter. He saw with wide eyes, and he could tell what he saw. He brought the pulsing sea before them, and the men and the ships upon the sea. He communicated his power of vision, till they saw with his eyes what he had seen. He selected from the vast mass of detail with an artist’s touch, drawing pictures of life that glowed and burned with light and color, injecting movement so that his listeners surged along with him on the flood of rough eloquence, enthusiasm, and power. At times he shocked them with the vividness of the narrative and his terms of speech, but beauty always followed fast upon the heels of violence, and tragedy was relieved by humor, by interpretations of the strange twists and quirks of sailors’ minds.

The shimmering tableau was shattered and scattered by Arthur, who had spent the entire evening coaxing forth his untamed companion. Martin Eden recalled his resolve. For the first time he stepped fully into himself, at first with conscious intent, but swiftly surrendering to the rapture of shaping life as he had lived it into vivid being before his audience. He had sailed among the crew of the smuggler _Halcyon_ on the night a revenue cutter ran her down. He had watched with hungry eyes, and he knew how to render what he saw. He summoned the heaving ocean into the room, the men and the vessels that rode upon it. He lent them his own sight until they gazed through his eyes at all he had witnessed. With an artist’s instinct he plucked from the immense clutter of particulars, sketching scenes that blazed and smoldered with light and hue, infusing such motion that his hearers were swept beside him on a torrent of raw eloquence, fervor, and force. He startled them at turns with the starkness of the tale and his coarse words, yet loveliness always came hard upon the heels of brutality, and sorrow was leavened by laughter, by glimpses into the crooked and curious workings of seamen’s minds.

Such were the professor’s words—rather let me say such the words of the fate—enounced to destroy me. As he went on I felt as if my soul were grappling with a palpable enemy; one by one the various keys were touched which formed the mechanism of my being; chord after chord was sounded, and soon my mind was filled with one thought, one conception, one purpose. So much has been done, exclaimed the soul of Frankenstein—more, far more, will I achieve; treading in the steps already marked, I will pioneer a new way, explore unknown powers, and unfold to the world the deepest mysteries of creation.

So fell Dr. Corvin’s words—no, better to call them the edicts of fate, spoken expressly to unmake me. As he continued speaking, I felt my soul locked in grapple with a foe I could almost touch; each turn of his lecture plucked at the hidden wires strung through the core of my being, one after another, chord after resonant chord, until my whole mind narrowed to a single thought, a single vision, a single unshakable purpose. So much has already been achieved, the soul of Elias Thorne exulted—more, far more, will I accomplish; treading the faint paths others have already marked, I will cut an untrodden way forward, probe powers no mortal has ever uncovered, and lay bare the deepest, most closely guarded mysteries of creation for all the world to witness.

He sat down.

Oliver _did_ see it in his mind’s eye as distinctly as if he had not altered his position; but he thought it better not to worry the kind old lady; so he smiled gently when she looked at him; and Mrs. Bedwin, satisfied that he felt more comfortable, salted and broke bits of toasted bread into the broth, with all the bustle befitting so solemn a preparation. Oliver got through it with extraordinary expedition. He had scarcely swallowed the last spoonful, when there came a soft rap at the door. “Come in,” said the old lady; and in walked Mr. Brownlow.

Oliver could picture it perfectly in his mind, as clearly as if he had never moved an inch; yet he decided it was kinder not to trouble the gentle old woman. So when she glanced at him, he offered a soft smile, and Mrs. Bedwin, reassured that he was more at ease, set about crumbling toasted bread into the broth with all the busy solemnity the task demanded. Oliver finished it with remarkable speed. He had barely taken the final spoonful when a light knock sounded at the door. "Come in," said the old lady, and Mr. Brownlow entered.

This new life of the Doctor’s was an anxious life, no doubt; still, the sagacious Mr. Lorry saw that there was a new sustaining pride in it. Nothing unbecoming tinged the pride; it was a natural and worthy one; but he observed it as a curiosity. The Doctor knew, that up to that time, his imprisonment had been associated in the minds of his daughter and his friend, with his personal affliction, deprivation, and weakness. Now that this was changed, and he knew himself to be invested through that old trial with forces to which they both looked for Charles’s ultimate safety and deliverance, he became so far exalted by the change, that he took the lead and direction, and required them as the weak, to trust to him as the strong. The preceding relative positions of himself and Lucie were reversed, yet only as the liveliest gratitude and affection could reverse them, for he could have had no pride but in rendering some service to her who had rendered so much to him. “All curious to see,” thought Mr. Lorry, in his amiably shrewd way, “but all natural and right; so, take the lead, my dear friend, and keep it; it couldn’t be in better hands.”

The Professor’s present existence was fraught with tension, certainly; yet, the observant Mr. Sterling noted a fresh, bolstering dignity within him. No vanity tainted this self-assurance; it was a wholesome and justifiable merit, though he watched its emergence as a singular phenomenon. The Professor understood that, until this hour, his long confinement had been linked in the thoughts of his daughter and his companion solely with his private agony, his loss, and his fragility. Since this perception had shifted—and he realized that his former ordeal now granted him a peculiar authority upon which they both relied for Julian’s eventual rescue and freedom—he felt so uplifted by the transformation that he assumed the mantle of guidance, demanding that they, as the vulnerable, lean upon him as the resilient. The established roles between him and Clara had turned about, yet only through the mechanism of profound devotion and thankfulness; for his only satisfaction lay in offering protection to the one who had so tirelessly nurtured him. “A remarkable sight,” mused Mr. Sterling with his characteristic kindly insight, “yet entirely fitting and proper; therefore, assume the command, my good friend, and hold it fast; it could not rest with a more capable soul.”
