He stood with the leather bridle looped over his forearm, crusted thick with road splatter, until the rattle and creak of the mail coach wheels faded entirely out of earshot and the hush of the night settled thick around him once more, before he turned to pick his way down the hill.

“Listen once again then, Jacques!” said the man with the restless hand and the craving air. “The name of that prisoner was Damiens, and it was all done in open day, in the open streets of this city of Paris; and nothing was more noticed in the vast concourse that saw it done, than the crowd of ladies of quality and fashion, who were full of eager attention to the last--to the last, Jacques, prolonged until nightfall, when he had lost two legs and an arm, and still breathed! “Listen once more, Jacques!” urged the man whose hand twitched and whose expression betrayed an insatiable hunger. “That prisoner’s name was Damiens, and the whole thing took place in broad daylight, right out in the open streets of Paris; and what stood out most among the enormous crowd that witnessed it was the flock of highborn, stylish women, utterly absorbed right up to the final moment—right to the very end, Jacques, dragged out until dusk, when he’d already lost both legs and an arm, and was still drawing breath! And it was carried out—tell me, how old are you?”

Her presence brought memories of such things as Bourbon roses, rubies, and tropical midnight; her moods recalled lotus-eaters and the march in Athalie; her motions, the ebb and flow of the sea; her voice, the viola. In a dim light, and with a slight rearrangement of her hair, her general figure might have stood for that of either of the higher female deities. The new moon behind her head, an old helmet upon it, a diadem of accidental dewdrops round her brow, would have been adjuncts sufficient to strike the note of Artemis, Athena, or Hera respectively, with as close an approximation to the antique as that which passes muster on many respected canvases.

She evoked the scent of damask blooms, the fire of garnets, and the stillness of an equatorial dusk; her temper suggested the dreams of the Lethe and the gravity of a processional hymn; her movements, the rhythmic pulse of the tide; her voice, the deep wood of a cello. In the fading light, with a mere shifting of her tresses, her form might have served as a model for the supreme queens of Olympus. A silver crescent poised behind her, a rusted helm upon her brow, or a circlet of stray dewdrops would have been ornaments enough to summon the presence of Diana, Minerva, or Juno, offering a resemblance to the ancient world as faithful as those portrayals found upon the walls of the most esteemed galleries.

“You should have heard the people groan,” said Chitling; “the officers fought like devils, or they’d have torn him away. He was down once, but they made a ring round him, and fought their way along. You should have seen how he looked about him, all muddy and bleeding, and clung to them as if they were his dearest friends. I can see ’em now, not able to stand upright with the pressing of the mob, and draggin him along amongst ’em; I can see the people jumping up, one behind another, and snarling with their teeth and making at him; I can see the blood upon his hair and beard, and hear the cries with which the women worked themselves into the centre of the crowd at the street corner, and swore they’d tear his heart out!”

“You should have heard the crowd moan,” said Chitling; “the constables battled like demons, or they’d have ripped him away. He fell once, but they formed a circle around him and forced a path through. You should have seen how he stared around, all caked in mud and bleeding, and held onto them as though they were his closest companions. I can still see them now, barely able to stand upright from the crush of the mob, hauling him along in their midst; I can see the people leaping up, one after another, baring their teeth and lunging at him; I can see the blood matting his hair and beard, and hear the shrieks with which the women pushed themselves into the heart of the throng at the street corner, swearing they’d rip his heart out!”

They led him through a paved room under the court, where some prisoners were waiting till their turns came, and others were talking to their friends, who crowded round a grate which looked into the open yard. There was nobody there to speak to _him_; but, as he passed, the prisoners fell back to render him more visible to the people who were clinging to the bars: and they assailed him with opprobrious names, and screeched and hissed. He shook his fist, and would have spat upon them; but his conductors hurried him on, through a gloomy passage lighted by a few dim lamps, into the interior of the prison.

He was escorted through a flagstone chamber beneath the courtroom, where several defendants waited for their hearings while others conversed with friends pressed against a metal grate overlooking the yard. No one had come for him; yet, as he walked by, the other inmates drew back to ensure the crowd at the bars could see him clearly. The onlookers met him with a barrage of slurs, howling and hissing as he passed. He brandished his fist and would have spat at them, but his guards forced him onward, through a somber corridor lit by a few flickering lamps and into the heart of the jail.

One night there were sounds of revelry in the big brick tenement-house next door but one to McGary’s Family Café. The sounds seemed to emanate from the apartments of the Walsh family. The sidewalk was obstructed by an assortment of interested neighbours, who opened a lane from time to time for a hurrying messenger bearing from McGary’s goods pertinent to festivity and diversion. The sidewalk contingent was engaged in comment and discussion from which it made no effort to eliminate the news that Norah Walsh was being married.

Late one evening, raucous party noises drifted out of the large brick tenement two doors down from McGary’s Family Café. The commotion seemed to be coming straight from the Walsh family’s apartment. The sidewalk out front was crammed with curious neighbors, who’d part to clear a narrow path every so often for rushed messengers hauling party supplies and refreshments from McGary’s for the festivities. The cluster of onlookers loitering on the pavement chattered back and forth, making no effort to hide that the celebration was for Norah Walsh’s wedding.

“Oh, Sandy, you are come at last--how I have longed for you! Sit by me--do not leave me--never leave me again, Sandy, never again. Where is your hand?--give it me, dear, let me hold it--there --now all is well, all is peace, and I am happy again--_we_ are happy again, isn't it so, Sandy? You are so dim, so vague, you are but a mist, a cloud, but you are _here_, and that is blessedness sufficient; and I have your hand; don't take it away--it is for only a little while, I shall not require it long.... Was that the child?... Hello-Central!... she doesn't answer. Asleep, perhaps? Bring her when she wakes, and let me touch her hands, her face, her hair, and tell her good-bye.... Sandy! Yes, you are there. I lost myself a moment, and I thought you were gone.... Have I been sick long? It must be so; it seems months to me. And such dreams! such strange and awful dreams, Sandy! Dreams that were as real as reality--delirium, of course, but _so_ real! Why, I thought the king was dead, I thought you were in Gaul and couldn't get home, I thought there was a revolution; in the fantastic frenzy of these dreams, I thought that Clarence and I and a handful of my cadets fought and exterminated the whole chivalry of England! But even that was not the strangest. I seemed to be a creature out of a remote unborn age, centuries hence, and even _that_ was as real as the rest! Yes, I seemed to have flown back out of that age into this of ours, and then forward to it again, and was set down, a stranger and forlorn in that strange England, with an abyss of thirteen centuries yawning between me and you! between me and my home and my friends! between me and all that is dear to me, all that could make life worth the living! It was awful --awfuler than you can ever imagine, Sandy. Ah, watch by me, Sandy --stay by me every moment--_don't_ let me go out of my mind again; death is nothing, let it come, but not with those dreams, not with the torture of those hideous dreams--I cannot endure _that_ again.... Sandy?...”

“Oh, Sandy, you have come at last—how I have yearned for you! Sit here beside me—do not go away—never leave me again, Sandy, never again. Where is your hand? Give it to me, dear; let me keep it—there—now all is right, all is calm, and I am happy once more—we are happy once more, aren’t we, Sandy? You are so faint, so indistinct, you are only a mist, a cloud, but you are here, and that is blessing enough; and I have your hand; do not take it away—it is only for a little while, I shall not need it for long.... Was that the child?... Hello-Central!... she does not answer. Asleep, perhaps? Bring her when she wakes, and let me touch her hands, her face, her hair, and say good-bye to her.... Sandy! Yes, you are there. I lost myself for a moment, and I thought you had gone.... Have I been ill long? It must be so; it feels like months to me. And such dreams! such strange, terrible dreams, Sandy! Dreams that were as real as waking life—delirium, of course, but so real! Why, I thought the king was dead, I thought you were in Gaul and could not come home, I thought there was a revolution; in the wild madness of those dreams, I thought that Clarence and I and a few of my cadets fought and wiped out all the chivalry of England! But even that was not the strangest part. I seemed to be some creature from a far-off unborn age, centuries ahead, and even that felt as real as everything else! Yes, I seemed to have leaped back out of that age into this one of ours, and then forward to it again, and was left there, a stranger and abandoned in that strange England, with an abyss of thirteen centuries opening between me and you! between me and my home and my friends! between me and all that is precious to me, all that could make life worth living! It was dreadful—more dreadful than you can ever imagine, Sandy. Ah, watch over me, Sandy—stay with me every moment—don’t let me lose my mind again; death is nothing, let it come, but not with those dreams, not with the torment of those hideous dreams—I cannot bear that again.... Sandy?...”

Then came the battleship raid on Whitby and Scarborough, and the killing among other victims of a number of children on their way to school. This shocked Mr. Britling absurdly, much more than the Belgian crimes had done. They were _English_ children. At home!... The drowning of a great number of people on a torpedoed ship full of refugees from Flanders filled his mind with pitiful imaginings for days. The Zeppelin raids, with their slow crescendo of blood-stained futility, began before the end of 1914.... It was small consolation for Mr. Britling to reflect that English homes and women and children were, after all, undergoing only the same kind of experience that our ships have inflicted scores of times in the past upon innocent people in the villages of Africa and Polynesia....

Other times, Van der Pant would recount brutal stories of death and disfigurement unfolding in Antwerp. German forces occupying Belgium shot women constantly, not just for serious espionage charges, but for petty, insignificant infractions. Then came the German battleship raid on Whitby and Scarborough, which killed, among many other victims, a group of children making their way to school. This news hit Mr. Britling far harder than the atrocities reported in Belgium ever had, a reaction he almost felt was irrational. Those were *English* children, killed on their own home soil! When a ship packed with Flemish refugees was torpedoed, drowning hundreds of passengers, he couldn’t stop turning over piteous, unshakable images of that loss in his head for days afterward. Before 1914 drew to a close, Zeppelin raids started too, building slowly in a crescendo of senseless, blood-soaked destruction. It brought Mr. Britling precious little comfort to remind himself that British homes, women, and children were only suffering the exact same kind of violence British naval ships had inflicted scores of times in the past, on innocent villagers in small settlements across Africa and Polynesia.

The letters reinforced the photographs in their reminder how kind and pleasant a race mankind can be. Until the wild asses of nationalism came kicking and slaying amidst them, until suspicion and jostling greed and malignity poison their minds, until the fools with the high explosives blow that elemental goodness into shrieks of hate and splashes of blood. How kindly men are--up to the very instant of their cruelties! His mind teemed suddenly with little anecdotes and histories of the goodwill of men breaking through the ill-will of war, of the mutual help of sorely wounded Germans and English lying together in the mud and darkness between the trenches, of the fellowship of captors and prisoners, of the Saxons at Christmas fraternising with the English.... Of that he had seen photographs in one of the daily papers....

The letters and photographs together served as a poignant reminder of how decent and amiable humanity could be. That is, until the savage impulses of nationalism begin their violent trampling, until paranoia and grasping selfishness infect the mind, and until the idiots with high explosives shatter that fundamental goodness into cries of rage and spatters of blood. People are remarkably gentle—right until the moment they become cruel. Suddenly, his head was filled with stories of human kindness defying the malice of war: accounts of wounded Germans and Englishmen helping each other while stranded in the dark mud between the lines, of the camaraderie shared between prisoners and their captors, and of the Saxons mingling with the British on Christmas Day. He remembered seeing the photographs of that scene in one of the daily papers.

He walked along towards home without attending to paths. If anyone knew the heath well it was Clym. He was permeated with its scenes, with its substance, and with its odours. He might be said to be its product. His eyes had first opened thereon; with its appearance all the first images of his memory were mingled, his estimate of life had been coloured by it: his toys had been the flint knives and arrow-heads which he found there, wondering why stones should “grow” to such odd shapes; his flowers, the purple bells and yellow furze: his animal kingdom, the snakes and croppers; his society, its human haunters. Take all the varying hates felt by Eustacia Vye towards the heath, and translate them into loves, and you have the heart of Clym. He gazed upon the wide prospect as he walked, and was glad.

He cut across the heath toward home, not bothering to stick to any marked footpath. Nobody knew this stretch of moorland better than Clym, that much was certain. Every sweeping vista, every crumb of its peaty soil, every sharp, earthy scent had seeped into his very bones. You could fairly call him the heath’s own creation. He’d first opened his eyes to its skyline; every earliest memory he held was tangled up with its rough contours, and it had dyed every belief he held about life its own earthy hue. As a small boy, his playthings were the flint blades and arrowheads he dug up in its turf, marveling that stones could “grow” into such strange, sharp shapes; his favorite flowers were its purple heather bells and bright yellow gorse; his idea of the entire animal kingdom was its slithering snakes and plump game birds; his first sense of community came from the other drifters and wanderers who haunted its expanse. If you took every layered, bitter hatred Eustacia Vye held for the heath and flipped every last one into fierce, abiding affection, you’d have a perfect blueprint of Clym’s heart. He kept his gaze fixed on the wide, open spread of land stretching out ahead as he walked, and felt a quiet, unshakable gladness settle over him.

On reaching Casterbridge he left the horse and trap at an inn, and at five minutes before ten came back to the bridge at the lower end of the town, and sat himself upon the parapet. The clocks struck the hour, and no Fanny appeared. In fact, at that moment she was being robed in her grave-clothes by two attendants at the Union poorhouse—the first and last tiring-women the gentle creature had ever been honoured with. The quarter went, the half hour. A rush of recollection came upon Troy as he waited: this was the second time she had broken a serious engagement with him. In anger he vowed it should be the last, and at eleven o’clock, when he had lingered and watched the stone of the bridge till he knew every lichen upon their face and heard the chink of the ripples underneath till they oppressed him, he jumped from his seat, went to the inn for his gig, and in a bitter mood of indifference concerning the past, and recklessness about the future, drove on to Budmouth races.

When he reached Casterbridge, he left the horse and trap at an inn, then, five minutes before ten, returned to the bridge at the lower end of the town and took a seat on the parapet. The clocks struck the hour, and Fanny did not appear. At that very moment, in fact, she was being dressed in her grave-clothes by two attendants at the Union poorhouse—the first and last dressers the gentle girl had ever been privileged to have. The quarter hour passed, then the half hour. As Troy waited, a flood of memory came over him: this was the second time she had failed to keep a serious appointment with him. In anger, he swore it would be the last. By eleven o’clock, after he had sat there long enough to study the bridge stones until he knew every patch of lichen on their faces and to hear the ring of the ripples beneath them until the sound became unbearable, he sprang up, went back to the inn for his gig, and, in a bitter mood of indifference toward the past and carelessness about the future, drove on to the Budmouth races.

He and I were raised here in New York, just like two brothers, together. I was eighteen and Jimmy was twenty. The next morning I was to start for the West to make my fortune. You couldn’t have dragged Jimmy out of New York; he thought it was the only place on earth. Well, we agreed that night that we would meet here again exactly twenty years from that date and time, no matter what our conditions might be or from what distance we might have to come. We figured that in twenty years each of us ought to have our destiny worked out and our fortunes made, whatever they were going to be.”

Then he sat down to rest by the side of the milestone, and began to think, for the first time, where he had better go and try to live.

Well, the priest did very well, considering. He got in all the details, and that is a good thing in a local item: you see, he had kept books for the undertaker-department of his church when he was younger, and there, you know, the money's in the details; the more details, the more swag: bearers, mutes, candles, prayers --everything counts; and if the bereaved don't buy prayers enough you mark up your candles with a forked pencil, and your bill shows up all right. And he had a good knack at getting in the complimentary thing here and there about a knight that was likely to advertise--no, I mean a knight that had influence; and he also had a neat gift of exaggeration, for in his time he had kept door for a pious hermit who lived in a sty and worked miracles.

The priest managed admirably, all things considered. He packed in every particular, which is the secret of a good local notice: you see, in his younger days he had kept the books for the burial side of his church, and in that line the profit lies in the particulars—the more particulars, the more profit: pallbearers, hired mourners, tapers, orisons—nothing is overlooked; and if the grieving family scrimps on prayers, you simply mark up the candles with a split pencil, and the account comes out handsomely. He also had a fine instinct for slipping in a flattering word now and then about a lord who was likely to bring custom—no, I mean a lord who carried weight; and he possessed a tidy talent for embellishment, having once kept the gate for a devout hermit who lived in a pigsty and performed wonders.

A perception of the dilemma in which his love had placed him came back in full force. In spite of Eustacia’s apparent willingness to wait through the period of an unpromising engagement, till he should be established in his new pursuit, he could not but perceive at moments that she loved him rather as a visitant from a gay world to which she rightly belonged than as a man with a purpose opposed to that recent past of his which so interested her. It meant that, though she made no conditions as to his return to the French capital, this was what she secretly longed for in the event of marriage; and it robbed him of many an otherwise pleasant hour. Along with that came the widening breach between himself and his mother. Whenever any little occurrence had brought into more prominence than usual the disappointment that he was causing her it had sent him on lone and moody walks; or he was kept awake a great part of the night by the turmoil of spirit which such a recognition created. If Mrs. Yeobright could only have been led to see what a sound and worthy purpose this purpose of his was and how little it was being affected by his devotions to Eustacia, how differently would she regard him!

As he strayed farther from the enchanted circle of his goddess-like beloved, a sorrow unlike any he had known settled upon his features. The full weight of the predicament his affection had wrought descended upon him once more. Although Eustacia seemed prepared to endure an indefinite and unpromising betrothal until he found his footing in a fresh vocation, he could not shake the sense that she cherished him less as a man pursuing a path at odds with the glittering past she so admired, and more as an emissary from that very realm of brilliance to which she felt herself native. This meant that, while she never demanded his return to Paris, she secretly yearned for it as the outcome of their union; and the thought poisoned many an hour that might otherwise have been sweet. Alongside this grew the ever-deepening rift between him and his mother. Whenever some trifling incident threw into sharper relief the distress he was causing her, it drove him out upon solitary and brooding rambles, or kept him tossing through the small hours in a tempest of self-reproach. If only Mrs. Yeobright could have been made to understand how sound and honorable his ambition truly was, and how little it was tainted by his devotion to Eustacia, how altered her gaze upon him would have been.

Even had you felt careless about your own affliction, you might have refrained from singing out of sheer pity for mine. God! if I were a man in such a position I would curse rather than sing.”

Why do you drive me, Julian, to utter such harshness? I merit as much compassion as you—more, I believe. For you can sing! It would be a strange moment that found me singing beneath a cloud so dark. Believe me, my love, I could weep with a passion that would stun and confuse a spirit as elastic as yours. Even if you were careless of your own grief, you might have ceased your song out of mere pity for mine. Lord! were I a man in your stead, I would curse rather than sing.

In the first fright and horror of her situation, Miss Pross passed the body as far from it as she could, and ran down the stairs to call for fruitless help. Happily, she bethought herself of the consequences of what she did, in time to check herself and go back. It was dreadful to go in at the door again; but, she did go in, and even went near it, to get the bonnet and other things that she must wear. These she put on, out on the staircase, first shutting and locking the door and taking away the key. She then sat down on the stairs a few moments to breathe and to cry, and then got up and hurried away.

In the first shock and terror of her plight, Miss Pross kept as far from the body as she could, and ran down the stairs to summon help that could not be of use. Happily, she remembered in time what the consequences of such a step must be, and checked herself, turning back. It was dreadful to enter the room again; yet she did enter, and even came near enough to take the bonnet and the other things she needed to wear. She put them on in the staircase, first shutting and locking the door and carrying off the key. Then she sat for a few moments on the stairs to catch her breath and to cry, and afterward rose and hurried away.

“Safie related that her mother was a Christian Arab, seized and made a slave by the Turks; recommended by her beauty, she had won the heart of the father of Safie, who married her. The young girl spoke in high and enthusiastic terms of her mother, who, born in freedom, spurned the bondage to which she was now reduced. She instructed her daughter in the tenets of her religion and taught her to aspire to higher powers of intellect and an independence of spirit forbidden to the female followers of Muhammad. This lady died, but her lessons were indelibly impressed on the mind of Safie, who sickened at the prospect of again returning to Asia and being immured within the walls of a harem, allowed only to occupy herself with infantile amusements, ill-suited to the temper of her soul, now accustomed to grand ideas and a noble emulation for virtue. The prospect of marrying a Christian and remaining in a country where women were allowed to take a rank in society was enchanting to her.

Safie recounted how her mother, a Christian Arab, had been captured and enslaved by the Turks; her grace and beauty had won the affection of Safie’s father, who eventually married her. The girl spoke with fervent pride of this woman, who, having been born free, deeply resented the chains of her later life. She had raised Safie in the doctrines of her own faith, urging her to cultivate a strength of mind and an autonomy of spirit that were typically forbidden to women in her father's culture. Although her mother died, these lessons were permanently engraved upon Safie’s soul. She was repulsed by the thought of returning to Asia to be confined within a harem, restricted to the frivolous and childish diversions that were ill-suited to a mind now devoted to grand concepts and the pursuit of virtue. The chance to marry a Christian and live in a nation where women were granted a social standing of their own was a truly captivating prospect.

As a result, they decided that it was time to waive etiquette and send their greatest and best against me. To the astonishment of that little world, I lassoed Sir Lamorak de Galis, and after him Sir Galahad. So you see there was simply nothing to be done now, but play their right bower--bring out the superbest of the superb, the mightiest of the mighty, the great Sir Launcelot himself!

The crowd demanded a repeat, and then another, and yet another still. Once I had unhorsed five of their number, the armored ranks grew somber, pausing to hold a desperate council. They concluded that the time for mere pageantry was over and that they must deploy their most formidable legends. To the utter bewilderment of the assembly, my hempen coil brought down Sir Valerius, followed swiftly by the peerless Sir Percival. There was nothing left for them now but to reveal their ultimate gambit—to summon the paragon of paragons, the titan of the realm, the illustrious Sir Roland himself!

“And yet in reason it should clearly be the other way; it should be five hundred times as easy to foretell the last as the first, for, indeed, it is so close by that one uninspired might almost see it. In truth, the law of prophecy doth contradict the likelihoods, most strangely making the difficult easy, and the easy difficult.”

By every rule of intellect, the opposite should surely hold; it ought to be a thousandfold more effortless to envision the finish than the start, since the former looms so near that a man without vision might almost perceive it. Indeed, the art of augury upends all reason, curiously casting the obvious into shadow while illuminating the impossible.

Before this I was not unacquainted with the more obvious laws of electricity. On this occasion a man of great research in natural philosophy was with us, and excited by this catastrophe, he entered on the explanation of a theory which he had formed on the subject of electricity and galvanism, which was at once new and astonishing to me. All that he said threw greatly into the shade Cornelius Agrippa, Albertus Magnus, and Paracelsus, the lords of my imagination; but by some fatality the overthrow of these men disinclined me to pursue my accustomed studies. It seemed to me as if nothing would or could ever be known. All that had so long engaged my attention suddenly grew despicable. By one of those caprices of the mind which we are perhaps most subject to in early youth, I at once gave up my former occupations, set down natural history and all its progeny as a deformed and abortive creation, and entertained the greatest disdain for a would-be science which could never even step within the threshold of real knowledge. In this mood of mind I betook myself to the mathematics and the branches of study appertaining to that science as being built upon secure foundations, and so worthy of my consideration.

Prior to this moment, I was already familiar with the most straightforward, well-documented laws of electricity. That day, we were joined by a prominent researcher in the natural sciences, and fired up by the strange destructive event we’d just witnessed, he walked us through a theory he’d developed around electricity and galvanism, ideas that felt entirely groundbreaking and mind-blowing to me at the time. Everything he laid out completely overshadowed the work of Cornelius Agrippa, Albertus Magnus, and Paracelsus, the three thinkers who’d long reigned over my intellectual fantasies. But for some perverse reason, seeing these idols of mine discredited made me lose all interest in the studies I’d spent so much time on up to that point. I was suddenly convinced that there was no real truth out there waiting to be uncovered, that no lasting, reliable knowledge was even possible. Every topic that had held my fascination for years felt cheap and worthless all at once. Caught up in one of those impulsive, irrational mental shifts that hit most of us hardest when we’re young, I abandoned all my old pursuits overnight. I wrote off natural history and every related field as a stunted, failed experiment, and felt nothing but scorn for this supposed area of study that couldn’t even cross the first threshold of genuine, verifiable knowledge. In that frame of mind, I turned my full attention to mathematics and all its related subfields, since they were built on solid, unshakable foundations, and seemed the only disciplines truly worth my time and effort.

“If it had pleased _God_ to put it in the hard heart of either of the brothers, in all these frightful years, to grant me any tidings of my dearest wife--so much as to let me know by a word whether alive or dead--I might have thought that He had not quite abandoned them. But, now I believe that the mark of the red cross is fatal to them, and that they have no part in His mercies. And them and their descendants, to the last of their race, I, Alexandre Manette, unhappy prisoner, do this last night of the year 1767, in my unbearable agony, denounce to the times when all these things shall be answered for. I denounce them to Heaven and to earth.”

“Had the Almighty seen fit to move the cold hearts of either brother during these horrific years, allowing me even a shred of news regarding my beloved wife—just a single word to say if she lived or had died—I might have felt they weren't completely beyond His grace. But I am now convinced that the sign of the red cross has sealed their doom, and they are excluded from His mercy. Therefore, on this final night of 1767, I, Alexandre Manette, a wretched prisoner in my deepest suffering, denounce them and every one of their descendants to the eventual day of judgment. I condemn them before both Heaven and earth.”

They talked. They talked about many things, and now Brissenden and now Martin took turn in ordering Scotch and soda. Martin, who was extremely strong-headed, marvelled at the other’s capacity for liquor, and ever and anon broke off to marvel at the other’s conversation. He was not long in assuming that Brissenden knew everything, and in deciding that here was the second intellectual man he had met. But he noted that Brissenden had what Professor Caldwell lacked—namely, fire, the flashing insight and perception, the flaming uncontrol of genius. Living language flowed from him. His thin lips, like the dies of a machine, stamped out phrases that cut and stung; or again, pursing caressingly about the inchoate sound they articulated, the thin lips shaped soft and velvety things, mellow phrases of glow and glory, of haunting beauty, reverberant of the mystery and inscrutableness of life; and yet again the thin lips were like a bugle, from which rang the crash and tumult of cosmic strife, phrases that sounded clear as silver, that were luminous as starry spaces, that epitomized the final word of science and yet said something more—the poet’s word, the transcendental truth, elusive and without words which could express, and which none the less found expression in the subtle and all but ungraspable connotations of common words. He, by some wonder of vision, saw beyond the farthest outpost of empiricism, where was no language for narration, and yet, by some golden miracle of speech, investing known words with unknown significances, he conveyed to Martin’s consciousness messages that were incommunicable to ordinary souls.

They talked. They covered a wide range of subjects, taking turns ordering Scotch and soda—now Brissenden, now Martin. Martin, whose head was unusually strong, was astonished by how much the other could drink, and every so often he paused to be amazed by Brissenden’s talk. It didn’t take him long to realize that Brissenden seemed to know everything, and to conclude that this was the second truly intellectual man he had ever encountered. Yet he observed that Brissenden possessed something Professor Caldwell lacked—namely, fire, that incisive flash of insight, the wild, unrestrained blaze of genius. Living speech poured out of him. His thin lips worked like a machine’s dies, stamping out phrases that cut and stung; then again, pursing tenderly around the half-formed sounds they were shaping, those lips produced soft, velvety utterances, mellow phrases full of warmth and splendor, of aching beauty, echoing with life’s mystery and unfathomability; and at other times those thin lips became a bugle, blaring the crash and chaos of cosmic struggle, phrases that rang as pure as silver, that glowed like star-filled expanses, that captured science’s ultimate conclusions yet said something more—the poet’s truth, the transcendent reality, elusive and beyond the reach of ordinary words, yet somehow conveyed through the subtle, nearly ungraspable overtones of everyday language. Through some miraculous vision, he saw past the furthest boundaries of empirical thought, where no language yet existed to tell of it, and yet, by a golden miracle of speech, investing familiar words with unfamiliar meanings, he delivered to Martin’s mind messages that would be incommunicable to ordinary souls.

Fidelity in love for fidelity’s sake had less attraction for her than for most women; fidelity because of love’s grip had much. A blaze of love, and extinction, was better than a lantern glimmer of the same which should last long years. On this head she knew by prevision what most women learn only by experience—she had mentally walked round love, told the towers thereof, considered its palaces, and concluded that love was but a doleful joy. Yet she desired it, as one in a desert would be thankful for brackish water.

Fidelity for its own sake held little charm for her—less than for most women—but fidelity born from love’s fierce hold meant everything. A sudden blaze of passion, even if it ended in darkness, was preferable to a faint, enduring glimmer that lasted through the years. On this point, she understood by foresight what most women only grasp through living: she had circled love in her mind, surveyed its towers, examined its palaces, and judged it a sorrowful delight. Still, she craved it, as someone lost in a desert would welcome even brackish water.

Great God! Why did I not then expire! Why am I here to relate the destruction of the best hope and the purest creature on earth? She was there, lifeless and inanimate, thrown across the bed, her head hanging down and her pale and distorted features half covered by her hair. Everywhere I turn I see the same figure—her bloodless arms and relaxed form flung by the murderer on its bridal bier. Could I behold this and live? Alas! Life is obstinate and clings closest where it is most hated. For a moment only did I lose recollection; I fell senseless on the ground.

Almighty God! Why did death not claim me in that instant? Why must I remain to tell of the destruction of the gentlest spirit and the most perfect being to ever walk the earth? She lay there, limp and devoid of life, sprawled across the bed with her head hanging low, her ashen and mangled features partially masked by her own hair. Wherever I look, I am haunted by that same vision—her pale, slack form discarded by the killer upon her bridal bier. How could I see this and survive? Truly, life is a cruel, persistent thing that refuses to let go even when it is most loathed. For a brief moment, my mind went dark, and I collapsed onto the floor, senseless.

She held the brand to the ground, blowing the red coal with her mouth at the same time; till it faintly illuminated the sod, and revealed a small object, which turned out to be an hourglass, though she wore a watch. She blew long enough to show that the sand had all slipped through.

She pressed the smoldering brand against the earth, breathing softly upon its crimson ember, until the faint glow lit the turf and uncovered a tiny shape—an hourglass, though a watch already circled her wrist. She blew steadily, just long enough to see that every grain of sand had already fallen through.

With the same perversity of feeling and irresolution that had fastened upon him, despite himself, all day, the murderer, finding that he was not followed, and that they most probably considered him some drunken sullen fellow, turned back up the town, and getting out of the glare of the lamps of a stage-coach that was standing in the street, was walking past, when he recognised the mail from London, and saw that it was standing at the little post-office. He almost knew what was to come; but he crossed over, and listened.

The murderer had been gripped all day by a strange, stubborn irresolution that clung to him against his will. Realizing no one pursued him and that the passersby likely dismissed him as some sullen drunk, he retraced his steps through the town. Steering clear of the bright lamps of a stage-coach waiting in the street, he moved past it—then halted. He recognized the London mail, standing at the little post-office. Some presentiment of what would follow stirred in him, yet he crossed over and listened.

Well, I liked the king, and as king I respected him--respected the office; at least respected it as much as I was capable of respecting any unearned supremacy; but as MEN I looked down upon him and his nobles--privately. And he and they liked me, and respected my office; but as an animal, without birth or sham title, they looked down upon me--and were not particularly private about it, either. I didn't charge for my opinion about them, and they didn't charge for their opinion about me: the account was square, the books balanced, everybody was satisfied.

I was fond of the king, and honored him in his royal role—respected the crown, at least as much as I could ever bring myself to respect any unearned authority. But as ordinary men? I thought little of him and his nobles, and kept that judgment strictly to myself. For their part, they liked me well enough, and respected the post I held. But as a man of no noble birth or counterfeit title, they looked down on me openly, with no pretense of secrecy. I never hid what I thought of them, and they never hid what they thought of me: the scales were even, the accounts tallied perfectly, and no one had cause to complain.

Having at this moment reached the end of a wistful gaze into mid-air, Henry did not refuse. He was a man of more than middle age, with eyebrows high up in his forehead, who laid it down that the law of the world was bad, with a long-suffering look through his listeners at the world alluded to, as it presented itself to his imagination. He always signed his name “Henery”—strenuously insisting upon that spelling, and if any passing schoolmaster ventured to remark that the second “e” was superfluous and old-fashioned, he received the reply that “H-e-n-e-r-y” was the name he was christened and the name he would stick to—in the tone of one to whom orthographical differences were matters which had a great deal to do with personal character.

Right as he pulled himself out of a soft, wistful stare into empty space, Henry didn’t turn the offer down. He was well past middle age, with eyebrows set high on his forehead, and he held firm to the belief that the entire governing order of the world was rotten. When he spoke on the subject, he’d fix a weary, long-suffering gaze right through whoever he was talking to, as if he was staring directly at the flawed world he described, the version of it that played out so clearly in his imagination. He always signed his name “Henery,” and would strenuously defend that spelling whenever it was questioned. If some passing schoolteacher dared to remark that the second “e” was unnecessary and hopelessly old-fashioned, he’d retort that “H-e-n-e-r-y” was the name he’d been given at his christening, and the only one he’d ever answer to. He spoke in the tone of someone who considered differences in spelling to be matters deeply tied to a person’s core character.

It was a second to remember another phase of the matter. The sheep were not insured. All the savings of a frugal life had been dispersed at a blow; his hopes of being an independent farmer were laid low—possibly for ever. Gabriel’s energies, patience, and industry had been so severely taxed during the years of his life between eighteen and eight-and-twenty, to reach his present stage of progress that no more seemed to be left in him. He leant down upon a rail, and covered his face with his hands.

In the space of a heartbeat, he registered the other cruel detail of the disaster. The flock had no insurance. Every penny he had scraped together over a lifetime of frugality was wiped out in a single stroke; his dream of working as an independent farmer was shattered, likely for good. Gabriel had stretched his energy, patience, and relentless industry to their absolute limits over the decade between his eighteenth and twenty-eighth birthdays to reach his current standing, and he had no more reserves left to draw on. He sagged against a fence rail, and buried his face in his hands.

I was fast getting adjusted to my situation and circumstances. For a time, I used to wake up, mornings, and smile at my “dream,” and listen for the Colt's factory whistle; but that sort of thing played itself out, gradually, and at last I was fully able to realize that I was actually living in the sixth century, and in Arthur's court, not a lunatic asylum. After that, I was just as much at home in that century as I could have been in any other; and as for preference, I wouldn't have traded it for the twentieth. Look at the opportunities here for a man of knowledge, brains, pluck, and enterprise to sail in and grow up with the country. The grandest field that ever was; and all my own; not a competitor; not a man who wasn't a baby to me in acquirements and capacities; whereas, what would I amount to in the twentieth century? I should be foreman of a factory, that is about all; and could drag a seine down street any day and catch a hundred better men than myself.

I was swiftly growing accustomed to my new state and surroundings. For a while, I’d rise at dawn, grinning at what I took for a “vision,” and strain to hear the factory horn from Colt’s; but that phase faded little by little, until at length I could fully grasp that I dwelt truly in the sixth century, in King Arthur’s realm, and not in some madhouse. Once that settled in, I felt as native to that age as I could to any other; and if it came to choosing, I’d not have swapped it for the twentieth. Consider the prospects here for a fellow of learning, wit, nerve, and ambition to plunge in and rise alongside the land itself. The noblest arena ever conceived; and mine alone; no rival; not a soul who wasn’t an infant next to me in knowledge and gifts; whereas, what would I count for in the twentieth century? A factory foreman, more or less; and I could haul a net down any street and land a hundred men better than me.

Already he had been on the verge of his phrase while wandering across the rushy fields towards Market Saffron; now it came to him again like a legitimate monarch returning from exile.

The inveterate cheerfulness of his sunny disposition had once more lifted Mr. Ainsworth clean out of the pit of pessimism. “There is a type of ‘Man About Town’ in New York,” he answered. “The term is quite familiar to me, but I don’t think I was ever called upon to define the character before. It would be difficult to point you out an exact specimen. I would say, offhand, that it is a man who had a hopeless case of the peculiar New York disease of wanting to see and know. At 6 o’clock each day life begins with him. He follows rigidly the conventions of dress and manners; but in the business of poking his nose into places where he does not belong he could give pointers to a civet cat or a jackdaw. He is the man who has chased Bohemia about the town from rathskeller to roof garden and from Hester street to Harlem until you can’t find a place in the city where they don’t cut their spaghetti with a knife. Your ‘Man About Town’ has done that. He is always on the scent of something new. He is curiosity, impudence and omnipresence. Hansoms were made for him, and gold-banded cigars; and the curse of music at dinner. There are not so many of him; but his minority report is adopted everywhere.

“There’s a specific sort of ‘Metropolitan Roamer’ native to San Francisco,” he replied. “I know the label well enough, though no one’s ever asked me to pin down exactly what makes one before. Pointing you to a perfect example would be tricky. Off the top of my head, I’d describe him as someone incurable with that unique San Francisco fever of needing to witness and learn every little thing the city has to offer. His day doesn’t start until six in the evening. He sticks unflinchingly to all the unwritten rules of tailored dress and polished social graces, but when it comes to sticking his nose where he’s not invited, he could out-snoop a raccoon or a magpie without half trying. He’s the soul who’s chased bohemian haunts all across the city, from basement beer halls to sky-high cocktail lounges, from the Mission District out to the Richmond, until there’s not a corner left where folks don’t hack their pasta with a butter knife to save time. That’s the Metropolitan Roamer’s doing, plain and simple. He’s forever hot on the trail of some fresh novelty. He is pure nosiness, unapologetic cheek, and impossible to escape. Horse-drawn cabs were built for him, and gold-foil wrapped cigars, and that bothersome fad of hiring string quartets to play over supper. There are precious few of these men scattered across the city, but every odd little preference they pick up is adopted by everyone else before the month is out.”

“Ah, you don’t know. You have seen more than I, and have been into cities and among people that I have only heard of, and have lived more years than I; but yet I am older at this than you. I loved another man once, and now I love you.”

“Ah, you cannot know. You have seen farther than I have, and have been into towns and among persons of whom I have only ever heard, and have lived more years than I; yet in this one thing I am older than you. There was another man whom I loved once, and now it is you that I love.”

Twilight was falling as Martin left the fruit store and turned homeward, his marketing on his arm. At the corner an electric car had stopped, and at sight of a lean, familiar figure alighting, his heart leapt with joy. It was Brissenden, and in the fleeting glimpse, ere the car started up, Martin noted the overcoat pockets, one bulging with books, the other bulging with a quart bottle of whiskey.

As dusk settled, Martin stepped out of the greengrocer’s and headed back toward his flat, the day’s purchases dangling from his arm. At the intersection, a streetcar had paused, and when he spotted a gaunt, well-known silhouette stepping down, a surge of gladness rushed through him. It was Brissenden—and in that brief instant before the tram jolted onward, Martin caught the bulges in his overcoat pockets: one stuffed with books, the other with a quart of whiskey.

Occasionally she came to a spot where independent worlds of ephemerons were passing their time in mad carousal, some in the air, some on the hot ground and vegetation, some in the tepid and stringy water of a nearly dried pool. All the shallower ponds had decreased to a vaporous mud amid which the maggoty shapes of innumerable obscure creatures could be indistinctly seen, heaving and wallowing with enjoyment. Being a woman not disinclined to philosophize she sometimes sat down under her umbrella to rest and to watch their happiness, for a certain hopefulness as to the result of her visit gave ease to her mind, and between important thoughts left it free to dwell on any infinitesimal matter which caught her eyes.

Every now and then she came upon a spot where entire self-contained worlds of ephemerons whiled away their short lives in giddy, frantic revelry: some darted through the open air, some scurried over sunbaked earth and foliage, others wriggled through the lukewarm, slimy water of a half-evaporated pool. All the shallower ponds in the area had shrunk to little more than steamy, mist-wreathed mud, where the maggot-like forms of countless unidentifiable small creatures drifted in and out of view, squirming and rolling contentedly in the muck. As a woman given to quiet philosophical musing, she would sometimes settle beneath her umbrella to rest and observe their small, uncomplicated joy. A soft, steady hopefulness about the outcome of her visit had lightened her mind entirely, so that in the lulls between weightier ruminations, she was free to fix her attention on whatever tiny, trivial thing happened to catch her eye.

Whenever my missionaries overcame a knight errant on the road they washed him, and when he got well they swore him to go and get a bulletin-board and disseminate soap and civilization the rest of his days. As a consequence the workers in the field were increasing by degrees, and the reform was steadily spreading. My soap factory felt the strain early. At first I had only two hands; but before I had left home I was already employing fifteen, and running night and day; and the atmospheric result was getting so pronounced that the king went sort of fainting and gasping around and said he did not believe he could stand it much longer, and Sir Launcelot got so that he did hardly anything but walk up and down the roof and swear, although I told him it was worse up there than anywhere else, but he said he wanted plenty of air; and he was always complaining that a palace was no place for a soap factory anyway, and said if a man was to start one in his house he would be damned if he wouldn't strangle him. There were ladies present, too, but much these people ever cared for that; they would swear before children, if the wind was their way when the factory was going.

Whenever my missionaries bested a knight-errant on the highway, they would bathe him, and once he recovered, they made him swear to acquire a bulletin-board and spend the rest of his life spreading soap and civilization. As a result, the number of laborers in the field gradually grew, and the reform steadily expanded. My soap factory soon felt the pressure. Initially, I employed only two workers; but before I departed home, I was already running fifteen hands, operating day and night. The atmospheric effects became so pronounced that the king staggered about, faint and gasping, declaring he doubted he could endure it much longer. Sir Launcelot grew so troubled that he did little besides pace the rooftop and curse, even though I warned him it was worse up there than anywhere else—but he insisted he needed plenty of air. He constantly grumbled that a palace was no fit site for a soap factory, and said if any man tried to start one in his own house, he’d be damned if he wouldn’t throttle him. There were ladies present as well, but these folk hardly cared a whit for that; they would swear in front of children if the wind blew their way while the factory was in operation.

It is difficult to picture bucolic calm awakening to intellectual ambitions without imagining social ambition as the bridge between them. Yeobright’s local singularity was that, while he labored after high thinking, he still clung to plain living—indeed, in many respects to a rough and meager life—and to a brotherly fellowship with rustics.

“It is not too late,” she cried out. “Let me show you. Let me prove my love has deepened—that it now outweighs my class and everything I once held sacred. I will scorn all that the bourgeoisie cherishes. I am no longer afraid of living. I will abandon my father and mother, let my name become a mockery among my friends. I will come to you here, this very moment, in free love if you’ll have me, and I shall be proud and joyful at your side. If I once betrayed love, I will now, for love’s sake, betray everything that drove me to that earlier betrayal.”

“Should the legal code maintain such a premise,” remarked Mr. Pringle, wringing his headgear with fervent intensity, “then the law is a fool—a simpleton. If such is the vision of the law, the law is an unmarried man; and the most I might wish for the law is that its eye be opened by the lessons of experience—of experience.”

The deep grief which this scene had at first excited quickly gave way to rage and despair. They were dead, and I lived; their murderer also lived, and to destroy him I must drag out my weary existence. I knelt on the grass and kissed the earth and with quivering lips exclaimed, “By the sacred earth on which I kneel, by the shades that wander near me, by the deep and eternal grief that I feel, I swear; and by thee, O Night, and the spirits that preside over thee, to pursue the dæmon who caused this misery, until he or I shall perish in mortal conflict. For this purpose I will preserve my life; to execute this dear revenge will I again behold the sun and tread the green herbage of earth, which otherwise should vanish from my eyes for ever. And I call on you, spirits of the dead, and on you, wandering ministers of vengeance, to aid and conduct me in my work. Let the cursed and hellish monster drink deep of agony; let him feel the despair that now torments me.”

The searing sorrow that first wracked me at the sight of the carnage curdled fast into white-hot rage and bottomless despair. They lay cold and gone, while I still breathed; their killer walked free too, and to tear him apart I would drag my heavy, hollow bones through every last weary day left to me. I sank to my knees in the cool grass, pressed my lips to the loam, and cried out through trembling teeth: “By the holy soil under my knees, by the ghosts of the slain that hover all around me, by the endless, fathomless grief gnawing through my ribs, I swear it. And by you, O endless Night, and all the shadowed spirits that tend your domains, I will hunt the fiend who wrought this suffering until one of us falls cold in a fight to the death. For this cause alone I will cling to life; to carry out this sacred vengeance I will let the sun still touch my face and walk the green wilds of this world, which I would otherwise have turned from forever the moment I saw their bodies. I call to you now, spirits of the beloved dead, and you, roving messengers of retribution, guide and strengthen me in this task. Let that accursed, hell-spawned brute choke on his own suffering; let him know the full weight of the despair that is eating me alive right now.”

A beautiful landscape, with the corn bright in it, but not abundant. Patches of poor rye where corn should have been, patches of poor peas and beans, patches of most coarse vegetable substitutes for wheat. On inanimate nature, as on the men and women who cultivated it, a prevalent tendency towards an appearance of vegetating unwillingly--a dejected disposition to give up, and wither away.

A wide rolling landscape, with sun-ripened sorghum glowing vividly across its folds, but far too sparse to bring in a plentiful harvest. Stunted, sallow rye poked up in plots where sorghum ought to have grown, next to straggling clumps of lentils and fava beans, and wide stretches of tough, unpalatable coarse vegetables planted in place of wheat. The lifeless land bore the same worn, listless cast as the men and women who tended its soils: a pervasive air of half-heartedly clinging to growth against their will, a downcast, defeated inclination to surrender entirely and crumble into dry, dead dust.

Afraid, in her extreme perturbation, of the loneliness of the deserted rooms, and of half-imagined faces peeping from behind every open door in them, Miss Pross got a basin of cold water and began laving her eyes, which were swollen and red. Haunted by her feverish apprehensions, she could not bear to have her sight obscured for a minute at a time by the dripping water, but constantly paused and looked round to see that there was no one watching her. In one of those pauses she recoiled and cried out, for she saw a figure standing in the room.

Terrified by her overwhelming distress and the emptiness of the abandoned chambers—where she half-fancied faces lurking behind each ajar door—Miss Pross fetched a bowl of cool water and started bathing her puffy, crimson eyes. Tormented by restless dread, she couldn’t stand even a moment’s blindness from the trickling water, and kept stopping to glance about, checking that no one observed her. During one such halt, she flinched and shrieked, for she spotted a silhouette standing within the room.

We had a rough time for a month, tramping to and fro in the earth, and suffering. And what Englishman was the most interested in the slavery question by that time? His grace the king! Yes; from being the most indifferent, he was become the most interested. He was become the bitterest hater of the institution I had ever heard talk. And so I ventured to ask once more a question which I had asked years before and had gotten such a sharp answer that I had not thought it prudent to meddle in the matter further. Would he abolish slavery?

We spent a grueling month wandering the countryside, enduring constant hardship and pain. By then, which Englishman was most preoccupied with the question of slavery? It was the king himself. From his former state of total indifference, he had become the most fervent observer of the cause. He had grown into the most venomous hater of the practice I had ever heard speak. Thus, I dared to pose a question I had first asked years earlier, which had then met with such a sharp rebuff that I had considered it dangerous to ever raise the matter again. Would he finally abolish slavery?

I’ve come home because, all things considered, I can be a trifle less useless here than anywhere else. But I have only lately found this out. When I first got away from home I thought this place was not worth troubling about. I thought our life here was contemptible. To oil your boots instead of blacking them, to dust your coat with a switch instead of a brush—was there ever anything more ridiculous? I said.”

“I’ll explain,” Yeobright said with a sudden, intense sincerity. “I’m actually glad for the chance to say this. I’ve moved back because, looking at the big picture, I might be slightly more useful here than in any other place. But that’s a realization I’ve only come to recently. When I first left home, I believed this area wasn't worth a second thought. I viewed our way of life here as beneath me. Conditioning your boots with oil instead of polish, or beating the dust off your jacket with a twig rather than a proper brush—I used to ask myself if anything could be more absurd.”

England was not yet at war, but all the stars were marching to that end. It was as if an event so vast must needs take its time to happen. No doubt was left upon Mr. Britling's mind, though a whole-page advertisement in the _Daily News_, in enormous type and of mysterious origin, implored Great Britain not to play into the hands of Russia, Russia the Terrible, that bugbear of the sentimental Radicals. The news was wide and sweeping, and rather inaccurate. The Germans were said to be in Belgium and Holland, and they had seized English ships in the Kiel Canal. A moratorium had been proclaimed, and the reports of a food panic showed Mrs. Faber to be merely one example of a large class of excitable people.

Britain had not yet officially entered the conflict, but the heavens themselves seemed to be aligning toward that inevitable conclusion. It was as though a catastrophe of such immense proportions required a gradual onset. Mr. Britling harbored no uncertainty, even as a full-page advertisement in the *Daily News*—set in massive font by an anonymous sponsor—desperately urged the nation not to support "Russia the Terrible," the traditional bogeyman of emotional Radicals. The prevailing reports were broad and dramatic, though often lacking in accuracy. Claims circulated that German troops had invaded Belgium and the Netherlands, and that British ships were being impounded in the Kiel Canal. With a moratorium now in effect, the emerging stories of food hoarding proved that Mrs. Faber was just one of many highly-strung individuals reacting to the crisis.

In the interests of renown the forwardness should lie chiefly in the capacity to handle things. Successful propagandists have succeeded because the doctrine they bring into form is that which their listeners have for some time felt without being able to shape. A man who advocates æsthetic effort and deprecates social effort is only likely to be understood by a class to which social effort has become a stale matter. To argue upon the possibility of culture before luxury to the bucolic world may be to argue truly, but it is an attempt to disturb a sequence to which humanity has been long accustomed. Yeobright preaching to the Egdon eremites that they might rise to a serene comprehensiveness without going through the process of enriching themselves was not unlike arguing to ancient Chaldeans that in ascending from earth to the pure empyrean it was not necessary to pass first into the intervening heaven of ether.

To gain true influence, one's primary strength should be a mastery over practical affairs. The most effective leaders are those who give structure to the vague sentiments their audience has long harbored but could never quite define. One who promotes intellectual growth while devaluing material ambition will likely only be heard by those who have already grown weary of the struggle for wealth. To suggest to the laboring poor that culture should take precedence over comfort may be theoretically sound, yet it disrupts a developmental path that humanity has followed for ages. In this sense, Yeobright’s attempt to convince the reclusive folk of Egdon that they could achieve a state of enlightened wisdom without first escaping their poverty was much like telling ancient Chaldean scholars that one might ascend to the highest celestial sphere without ever passing through the lower heavens.

Where had they fled when the next morning I awoke? All of soul-inspiriting fled with sleep, and dark melancholy clouded every thought. The rain was pouring in torrents, and thick mists hid the summits of the mountains, so that I even saw not the faces of those mighty friends. Still I would penetrate their misty veil and seek them in their cloudy retreats. What were rain and storm to me? My mule was brought to the door, and I resolved to ascend to the summit of Montanvert. I remembered the effect that the view of the tremendous and ever-moving glacier had produced upon my mind when I first saw it. It had then filled me with a sublime ecstasy that gave wings to the soul and allowed it to soar from the obscure world to light and joy. The sight of the awful and majestic in nature had indeed always the effect of solemnising my mind and causing me to forget the passing cares of life. I determined to go without a guide, for I was well acquainted with the path, and the presence of another would destroy the solitary grandeur of the scene.

Where had they gone when I woke the next morning? With sleep, everything that had lifted my spirits was gone, and a heavy gloom settled over every thought. The rain was coming down in sheets, and dense mist concealed the mountain peaks, so that I could not even see the faces of those grand companions. Yet I still longed to push through that veil of cloud and find them in their hidden retreats. What were rain and storm to me? My mule was brought to the door, and I decided to ride up to the summit of Montanvert. I recalled the effect the sight of the vast, ever-changing glacier had once had on me when I first beheld it. It had filled me then with a sublime rapture, one that seemed to give the soul wings and lift it out of the dim world into light and joy. The vision of nature’s terrible and magnificent works had always, in fact, solemnized my mind and made me forget life’s fleeting troubles. I resolved to go without a guide, for I knew the path well, and another person’s presence would spoil the lonely grandeur of the scene.

“Several changes of day and night passed, and the orb of night had greatly lessened, when I began to distinguish my sensations from each other. I gradually saw plainly the clear stream that supplied me with drink and the trees that shaded me with their foliage. I was delighted when I first discovered that a pleasant sound, which often saluted my ears, proceeded from the throats of the little winged animals who had often intercepted the light from my eyes. I began also to observe, with greater accuracy, the forms that surrounded me and to perceive the boundaries of the radiant roof of light which canopied me. Sometimes I tried to imitate the pleasant songs of the birds but was unable. Sometimes I wished to express my sensations in my own mode, but the uncouth and inarticulate sounds which broke from me frightened me into silence again.

Days and nights cycled by a handful of times, and the moon had shrunk to a thin sliver by the time I started telling my different sensations apart. Slowly, I could make out crisp details: the bright, running stream that gave me water to drink, and the trees that draped their leaves over me for shade. A thrill of joy shot through me when I first realized the soft, pretty sounds that kept drifting to my ears came from the small winged creatures that had so often darted between my eyes and the sun, cutting off the light. I also started noticing the shapes around me more clearly, and could pick out the edges of the glowing, bright ceiling of sky that arched overhead. Every now and then I tried to copy the birds’ sweet songs, but I couldn’t get the notes right. Other times I wanted to put the things I was feeling into my own kind of expression, but the rough, garbled noises that burst out of my mouth scared me so badly I went quiet all over again.

At first Mrs. Britling had listened to Mr. Direck with an almost undivided attention, but as he had developed his opening the feast upon the blue linen table had passed on to a fresh phase that demanded more and more of her directive intelligence. The two little boys appeared suddenly at her elbows. "Shall we take the plates and get the strawberries, Mummy?" they asked simultaneously. Then one of the neat maids in the background had to be called up and instructed in undertones, and Mr. Direck saw that for the present Robinson's illuminating experience was not for her ears. A little baffled, but quite understanding how things were, he turned to his neighbour on his left....

At the outset, Mrs. Britling had been giving nearly her full attention to Mr. Direck as he spoke, but just as he was getting into the core of his opening remarks, the meal laid out on the blue linen table moved into a new phase that demanded more and more of her active direction. The two little boys appeared out of nowhere right at her elbows. “Can we take the plates to get the strawberries, Mummy?” they asked in perfect unison. Next, she had to call over one of the crisply put-together maids lingering in the background and murmur quiet instructions to her, and Mr. Direck caught on that for the time being, she was not going to be available to listen to Robinson’s illuminating anecdote. A little thrown off, but fully understanding how these domestic interruptions go, he turned to the guest seated to his left....
