In the black prison of the Conciergerie, the doomed of the day awaited their fate. They were in number as the weeks of the year. Fifty-two were to roll that afternoon on the life-tide of the city to the boundless everlasting sea. Before their cells were quit of them, new occupants were appointed; before their blood ran into the blood spilled yesterday, the blood that was to mingle with theirs to-morrow was already set apart.

Within the dark keep of the Conciergerie, the day’s condemned held vigil for their end. Their count matched the full cycle of the seasons’ weeks. Fifty-two souls were to be swept that afternoon upon the city’s living current into the shoreless, eternal ocean. Ere their chambers were emptied of them, fresh lodgers had been named; before their lifeblood joined the gore shed the day before, the blood destined to blend with theirs on the morrow was already chosen.

“Why, the traps have got him, and that’s all about it,” said the Dodger, sullenly. “Come, let go o’ me, will you!” And, swinging himself, at one jerk, clean out of the big coat, which he left in the Jew’s hands, the Dodger snatched up the toasting fork, and made a pass at the merry old gentleman’s waistcoat; which, if it had taken effect, would have let a little more merriment out than could have been easily replaced.

“Well, the officers nabbed him, and that’s the end of it,” the Dodger muttered darkly. “Now, take your hands off me, will you!” And with a single twist, he slipped free of the oversized coat, leaving it dangling in the Jew’s grip, then seized the toasting fork and jabbed at the merry old gentleman’s waistcoat—a blow that, had it landed, would have released a good deal more merriment than could have been readily put back.

A terrible restlessness that was akin to hunger afflicted Martin Eden. He was famished for a sight of the girl whose slender hands had gripped his life with a giant’s grasp. He could not steel himself to call upon her. He was afraid that he might call too soon, and so be guilty of an awful breach of that awful thing called etiquette. He spent long hours in the Oakland and Berkeley libraries, and made out application blanks for membership for himself, his sisters Gertrude and Marian, and Jim, the latter’s consent being obtained at the expense of several glasses of beer. With four cards permitting him to draw books, he burned the gas late in the servant’s room, and was charged fifty cents a week for it by Mr. Higginbotham.

A gnawing restlessness, much like a physical hunger, took hold of Martin Eden. He starved for a glimpse of the girl whose delicate hands had seized his life with a giant’s strength. He lacked the resolve to visit her, fearing he might arrive too soon and violate the formidable rules of etiquette. Instead, he spent long hours in the Oakland and Berkeley libraries, filling out membership forms for himself, his sisters Gertrude and Marian, and Jim—whose permission he bought with several rounds of beer. With four cards to borrow books, he burned the gaslight late into the night in his small room, for which Mr. Higginbotham charged him a weekly fee of fifty cents.

"They say the relations of men and officers in the new army are beautiful. Some day I may learn to love my officer--but not just yet. Not till I've forgotten the operations leading up to the occupation of Cheasingholt.... He muffs his real job without a blush, and yet he would rather be shot than do his bootlaces up criss-cross. What I say about officers applies only and solely to him really.... How well I understand now the shooting of officers by their men.... But indeed, fatigue and exasperation apart, this shift has been done atrociously...."

Word has it that the bond between the ranks and the brass in this new army is a thing of beauty. Maybe I’ll come to appreciate my commander eventually—but not today. Not until I’ve managed to forget the mess of movements that led to us taking Cheasingholt. He blunders through his actual duties without a flicker of shame, yet he would sooner face death than tie his boots in anything but the approved pattern. Truly, my bitterness toward the officer class is meant for him alone. I finally understand why soldiers are driven to kill their own superiors. But honestly, even setting aside my fatigue and fury, this whole deployment was handled quite miserably.

Her eyes wandered along the strong throat, dimly seen in the starlight, and over the firm-poised head, and the old desire to lay her hands upon his neck came back to her. The strength she abhorred attracted her. Her feeling of loneliness became more pronounced, and she felt tired. Her position on the heeling boat irked her, and she remembered the headache he had cured and the soothing rest that resided in him. He was sitting beside her, quite beside her, and the boat seemed to tilt her toward him. Then arose in her the impulse to lean against him, to rest herself against his strength—a vague, half-formed impulse, which, even as she considered it, mastered her and made her lean toward him. Or was it the heeling of the boat? She did not know. She never knew. She knew only that she was leaning against him and that the easement and soothing rest were very good. Perhaps it had been the boat’s fault, but she made no effort to retrieve it. She leaned lightly against his shoulder, but she leaned, and she continued to lean when he shifted his position to make it more comfortable for her.

Her gaze drifted over the broad line of his throat, barely visible in the faint starlight, then trailed up to his steady, held-high head, and that familiar old urge to rest her palms against the nape of his neck bubbled up again in her. That raw, unyielding strength she claimed to loathe drew her in anyway. Her loneliness swelled sharper, heavier, and a bone-deep weariness settled over her. Perched on the tilting sailboat felt awkward and uncomfortable, and she thought back to the throbbing headache he’d once eased for her, how just being near him wrapped her in a quiet, calming rest. He was right next to her, so close their arms almost brushed, and every small lurch of the boat seemed to tip her straight into his space. Then a faint, half-formed impulse flickered to life in her chest: to slump against him, to let his steady strength hold her weight. She’d barely even registered the thought before it took over, pulling her slowly in his direction. Or was that just the boat shifting under them? She couldn’t tell. She never could, later, when she thought back to the moment. All she was aware of in that second was the press of her body against his, and how good that soft, uncomplicated relief felt, how the quiet calm she’d remembered wrapped around her at once. Maybe it really had just been the boat’s unsteady tilt, but she didn’t make any move to right herself, to pull back. Her weight rested light on his shoulder, but it was there, solid, and she didn’t shift away even when he adjusted his posture to make the spot she was leaning against softer, more comfortable for her.

To England, therefore, I was bound, and it was understood that my union with Elizabeth should take place immediately on my return. My father’s age rendered him extremely averse to delay. For myself, there was one reward I promised myself from my detested toils—one consolation for my unparalleled sufferings; it was the prospect of that day when, enfranchised from my miserable slavery, I might claim Elizabeth and forget the past in my union with her.

To England, then, I was destined; and it was agreed that my marriage with Elizabeth should be celebrated as soon as I returned. My father’s years made him exceedingly unwilling to suffer any delay. As for myself, there was but one recompense I allowed myself to hope for from my hated labors—one solace for my unequaled sufferings: the prospect of that day when, freed from my wretched bondage, I might claim Elizabeth, and, in my union with her, lose the memory of all that had gone before.

So now Della’s beautiful hair fell about her, rippling and shining like a cascade of brown waters. It reached below her knee and made itself almost a garment for her. And then she did it up again nervously and quickly. Once she faltered for a minute and stood still while a tear or two splashed on the worn red carpet.

Right then, Della’s beautiful hair tumbled loose around her, rippling and glinting like a cascading fall of chestnut brown water. It hung all the way below her knees, so long it practically functioned as a second garment for her. A second later, she twisted it back up again, jittery and moving in quick, fumbling bursts. For a full minute she wavered, standing stock still, as one or two tears splattered onto the well-worn red carpet beneath her feet.

Hugh's letters were becoming a very important influence upon Mr. Britling's thought. Hugh had always been something of a letter-writer, and now what was perhaps an inherited desire to set things down was manifest. He had been accustomed to decorate his letters from school with absurd little sketches--sometimes his letters had been all sketches--and now he broke from drawing to writing and back to drawing in a way that pleased his father mightily. The father loved this queer trick of caricature; he did not possess it himself, and so it seemed to him the most wonderful of all Hugh's little equipment of gifts. Mr. Britling used to carry these letters about until their edges got grimy; he would show them to any one he felt capable of appreciating their youthful freshness; he would quote them as final and conclusive evidence to establish this or that. He did not dream how many thousands of mothers and fathers were treasuring such documents. He thought other sons were dull young men by comparison with Hugh.

Elias’s letters had grown to shape a great deal of Mr. Halewood’s daily thinking. Elias had always had a knack for correspondence, and what might have been an inherited urge to commit every stray thought to paper was coming through clearly now. Back in his boarding school days, he’d liven up his notes with daft little doodles, sometimes sending nothing but drawings across the post, and now he hopped between scrawled paragraphs and quick, lopsided sketches mid-letter in a way that delighted his father endlessly. The older man adored this odd knack for caricature; he had never been able to pull off anything of the sort himself, so to him it was the most dazzling of all Elias’s small, charming talents. Mr. Halewood would tuck these letters into his coat pocket and tote them around until their corners were smudged and frayed; he’d pass them around to anyone he sensed would pick up on their bright, boyish freshness, and pull lines from them as unassailable proof to win whatever small debate he was wrapped up in. It never crossed his mind that thousands of other mothers and fathers were tucking exactly the same sort of mementos away with equal care. In his eyes, every other young man his son’s age was a dull, unremarkable thing next to Elias.

"_You_ will we hate with a lasting hate; We will never forgo our hate-- Hate by water and hate by land, Hate of the head and hate of the hand, Hate of the hammer and hate of the crown, Hate of seventy millions, choking down; We love as one, we hate as one, We have _one_ foe, and one alone-- ENGLAND!"

We will despise you with an enduring contempt; our loathing shall never be abandoned—loathing across the oceans and loathing over the earth, loathing from the mind and loathing from the fist, loathing from the forge and loathing from the throne, loathing of seventy million souls, swallowed hard; we cherish as one, we detest as one, we have a single enemy, and only one—ENGLAND!

The tea was prepared and served in absolute silence. Mr. Bumble, having draped a handkerchief across his knees to shield the glory of his breeches from stray crumbs, began to eat and drink. He punctuated these activities with an occasional heavy sigh, which appeared to have no ill effect on his hunger; instead, the outbursts seemed to only hasten his industrious progress through the tea and toast.

“Do you particularly like the man?” he muttered, at his own image; “why should you particularly like a man who resembles you? There is nothing in you to like; you know that. Ah, confound you! What a change you have made in yourself! A good reason for taking to a man, that he shows you what you have fallen away from, and what you might have been! Change places with him, and would you have been looked at by those blue eyes as he was, and commiserated by that agitated face as he was? Come on, and have it out in plain words! You hate the fellow.”

“Do you actually care for that man?” he growled low at the face glowering back at him from the fogged mirror. “Why on earth would you fixate on someone who is nearly your spitting image? There is nothing about you worth liking; you know that full well. Oh, blast you! Look how thoroughly you’ve warped the person you once were! That’s the real reason you cling to any thought of him, isn’t it? He holds up a mirror to every part of yourself you abandoned, every better life you might have lived if you’d had the spine for it. If you traded places with him, do you think those soft hazel eyes would have rested on you the way they rested on him? That that taut, anxious face would have brimmed with the same sympathy for you? Stop dancing around the truth, say it plain. You loathe the man.”

“No, it's not your head, Sandy. Your head's all right, as far as it goes, but you don't know business; that's where the trouble is. It unfits you to argue about business, and you're wrong to be always trying. However, that aside, it was a good haul, anyway, and will breed a handsome crop of reputation in Arthur's court. And speaking of the cowboys, what a curious country this is for women and men that never get old. Now there's Morgan le Fay, as fresh and young as a Vassar pullet, to all appearances, and here is this old duke of the South Marches still slashing away with sword and lance at his time of life, after raising such a family as he has raised. As I understand it, Sir Gawaine killed seven of his sons, and still he had six left for Sir Marhaus and me to take into camp. And then there was that damsel of sixty winter of age still excursioning around in her frosty bloom--How old are you, Sandy?”

“No, it isn’t your head, Sandy. Your head’s all right, so far as it goes, but you don’t understand business; that’s where the trouble lies. Still, setting that aside, it was a good haul after all, and it will bring forth a handsome crop of reputation in Arthur’s court. And then there was that damsel of sixty winters still tripping about in her frosty bloom—how old are you, Sandy?”

Singletree, Darnley & Co. had cautiously brought out an edition of fifteen hundred copies, but the first reviews had started a second edition of twice the size through the presses; and ere this was delivered a third edition of five thousand had been ordered. A London firm made arrangements by cable for an English edition, and hot-footed upon this came the news of French, German, and Scandinavian translations in progress. The attack upon the Maeterlinck school could not have been made at a more opportune moment. A fierce controversy was precipitated. Saleeby and Haeckel indorsed and defended “The Shame of the Sun,” for once finding themselves on the same side of a question. Crookes and Wallace ranged up on the opposing side, while Sir Oliver Lodge attempted to formulate a compromise that would jibe with his particular cosmic theories. Maeterlinck’s followers rallied around the standard of mysticism. Chesterton set the whole world laughing with a series of alleged non-partisan essays on the subject, and the whole affair, controversy and controversialists, was well-nigh swept into the pit by a thundering broadside from George Bernard Shaw. Needless to say the arena was crowded with hosts of lesser lights, and the dust and sweat and din became terrific.

Harthaven, Hale & Rowe had tentatively pressed a first run of fifteen hundred copies, but glowing early notices sent a second edition of double that size whirring through the presses before the first had even fully shipped; before the second run could hit bookstalls, a third printing of five thousand had already been commissioned. A London publishing house struck a deal via transatlantic cable to secure UK distribution rights, and hard on the heels of that announcement came word that French, German, and Norwegian translation efforts were already underway. The volume’s takedown of the neo-symbolist school of continental thought could not have landed at a more serendipitous cultural moment. A roaring public dispute erupted almost overnight. Biologists Julian Hargrove and Ernst Voss stepped forward to endorse and defend *The Blight of the Noon Sky*, for the first time in their decades-long public rivalry finding themselves aligned on an intellectual question. Physicists Arthur Croft and Alfred Waller lined up in the opposing camp, while Sir Alistair Loddon tried to craft a middle ground that aligned neatly with his own idiosyncratic theories of cosmic order. The neo-symbolist faithful rallied behind the banner of transcendental mysticism, penning fiery open letters and essays in popular weeklies. Humorist and essayist Gilbert K. Pembroke sent the entire reading public cackling with a string of supposedly neutral takes on the fracas, and the entire debate, along with nearly every public figure tangled up in it, was almost blown clean off the cultural stage by a scathing, thunderous takedown from playwright George Seaborne. Needless to say, the arena was packed with throngs of lesser-known commentators, and the dust, sweat, and roar of clashing opinions grew deafening.

There lay the cat asleep on the bare gravel of the path, as if beds, rugs, and carpets were unendurable. The leaves of the hollyhocks hung like half-closed umbrellas, the sap almost simmered in the stems, and foliage with a smooth surface glared like metallic mirrors. A small apple tree, of the sort called Ratheripe, grew just inside the gate, the only one which throve in the garden, by reason of the lightness of the soil; and among the fallen apples on the ground beneath were wasps rolling drunk with the juice, or creeping about the little caves in each fruit which they had eaten out before stupefied by its sweetness. By the door lay Clym’s furze-hook and the last handful of faggot-bonds she had seen him gather; they had plainly been thrown down there as he entered the house.

The brindle tabby was curled fast asleep on the path’s bare crushed stone, as if cushioned beds, woven rugs, and plush carpets were too stifling to bear. Hollyhock fronds drooped like umbrellas left half-furled after a storm, sap throbbed so hot in their stalks it might have been simmering, and every waxy leaf glinted as bright as polished tin mirrors. A tiny Earlyblush apple tree, the only fruit tree that thrived in the whole yard thanks to the loose, sandy soil, grew just inside the picket gate; beneath it, among the windfall apples scattered across the dirt, wasps stumbled woozy with fermented juice, or picked their way through the tiny hollowed-out cavities they’d gnawed into each fruit before the sugar rush left them sluggish. Propped against the back door lay Luka’s gorse-cutting sickle and the last bundle of kindling ties she’d watched him collect earlier; they’d clearly been dropped in a hurry the second he stepped across the threshold.

As the conversation played out, Oliver flicked his gaze back and forth between the two speakers, stupefied as though in a trance, barely able to grasp what was unfolding around him. The second Bill Sikes stopped speaking, however, he bolted abruptly to his feet, tore wildly out of the room, and shrieked for help so loud his cries bounced off the walls of the bare, aging house and rang all the way up to its roof.

Thomasin took the child and ran. When she came to the covered car the horse, though fresh from the stable, was standing perfectly still, as if conscious of misfortune. She saw for the first time whose it was. She nearly fainted, and would have been unable to proceed another step but that the necessity of preserving the little girl from harm nerved her to an amazing self-control. In this agony of suspense she entered the house, put the baby in a place of safety, woke the lad and the female domestic, and ran out to give the alarm at the nearest cottage.

For the first time she recognized whose vehicle it was. She nearly swooned, and would have been unable to take another step had not the urgent need to protect the little girl steeled her into extraordinary self-command. In this torment of suspense she entered the house, placed the baby somewhere safe, roused the boy and the maid, and then hurried out to give the alarm at the nearest cottage.

Volume No. 2 was an essay on the world, declaring it to be a very excellent place. Volume No. 3 disclosed the belief that in occupying the highest seat in a Rubberneck auto they were travelling the pace that passes all understanding.

Across the features of Mrs. Alistair Finch was inscribed a brief trilogy of the soul’s most satisfied conclusions. The first volume maintained the steadfast premise that Alistair Finch was a specimen of the highest order. The second was an appreciative treatise on the world, pronouncing it a thoroughly hospitable and elegant domain. The third volume confessed the exhilarating truth that, from their perch atop a sightseeing omnibus, they were sampling a velocity that passeth all understanding.

“It's too expensive--uses up force for nothing. You don't want any ground-connection except the one through the negative brush. The other end of every wire must be brought back into the cave and fastened independently, and _without_ any ground-connection. Now, then, observe the economy of it. A cavalry charge hurls itself against the fence; you are using no power, you are spending no money, for there is only one ground-connection till those horses come against the wire; the moment they touch it they form a connection with the negative brush _through the ground_, and drop dead. Don't you see?--you are using no energy until it is needed; your lightning is there, and ready, like the load in a gun; but it isn't costing you a cent till you touch it off. Oh, yes, the single ground-connection--”

“It’s wasteful—squanders energy for no reason. You don’t want any ground link except the one through the negative terminal. Every wire’s far end must loop back into the cave and be secured separately, with absolutely no connection to the earth. Now watch how efficient it is. A cavalry charge slams into the barrier; you’re expending no power, you’re spending no money, because there’s only a single ground link until those horses hit the wire; the instant they make contact, they complete a circuit with the negative terminal through the ground and fall dead. Do you see?—you’re using no energy until the moment it’s required; your lightning sits there, ready, like a bullet in a chamber; but it doesn’t cost you a penny until you trigger it. Oh yes, the lone ground connection—”

Mr. Van der Pant had not the extreme tactfulness of his so typically Catholic wife; he made it only too plain that he thought the British postal and telegraph service slow and slack, and the management of the Great Eastern branch lines wasteful and inefficient. He said the workmen in the fields and the workmen he saw upon some cottages near the junction worked slowlier and with less interest than he had ever seen any workman display in all his life before. He marvelled that Mr. Britling lit his house with acetylene and not electric light. He thought fresh eggs were insanely dear, and his opinion of Matching's Easy pig-keeping was uncomplimentary. The roads, he said, were not a means of getting from place to place, they were a _dédale_; he drew derisive maps with his finger on the table-cloth of the lane system about the Dower House. He was astonished that there was no Café in Matching's Easy; he declared that the "public house" to which he went with considerable expectation was no public house at all; it was just a sly place for drinking beer.... All these were things Mr. Britling might have remarked himself; from a Belgian refugee he found them intolerable.

Unlike his wife, whose traditional Catholic background made her exceptionally diplomatic, Mr. Van der Pant lacked any sense of subtlety. He made it abundantly clear that he found the British postal and telegraph systems sluggish and disorganized, while he viewed the management of the Great Eastern railway lines as both wasteful and incompetent. He remarked that the laborers in the fields and the workmen he observed near the junction moved with a lethargy and lack of focus unlike anything he had ever encountered. It baffled him that Mr. Britling still lit his home with acetylene instead of electricity. He felt the price of fresh eggs was absurdly high, and his assessment of the local pig-farming was scathing. The roads, he complained, were not proper routes but a confusing maze; he even drew mocking maps on the tablecloth with his finger to show the absurdity of the lanes around the Dower House. He was stunned by the lack of a café in Matching’s Easy, and he dismissed the local "public house"—which he had visited with high expectations—as no true public space at all, but rather a dismal spot for solitary drinking. These were all complaints Mr. Britling might have made himself, yet coming from a Belgian refugee, he found them absolutely galling.

“You can bet he was,” Martin proclaimed. “Three dollars a week, an’ four dollars a week, an’ a young boy cookin’ for himself on an oil-burner an’ layin’ up money, workin’ all day an’ studyin’ all night, just workin’ an’ never playin’, never havin’ a good time, an’ never learnin’ how to have a good time—of course his thirty thousand came along too late.”

“You’d better believe he was,” Martin declared. “Three dollars one week, four the next, just a young kid cooking all his own meals on an old oil burner, stashing every cent he made. He worked all day long, then studied straight through every night—nothing but constant grind, no time to play, no opportunities to have a good time, not even a clue how to enjoy himself even if he’d had the chance. Of course that thirty thousand dollars landed in his lap far too late.”

Still, every playhouse in the district was packed to the rafters, and crowds spilled gaily onto the pavement as he walked past, chattering as they drifted homeward. Outside the entrance of one playhouse, a small girl huddled beside her mother, peering uncertainly at the mud-caked crosswalk, unsure how to cross without soaking her stockings. He lifted the little one easily and carried her to the opposite curb, and before her shy, small arms had loosened from around his throat, he asked her for a kiss.

“I believe,” returned Doctor Manette, “that there had been a strong and extraordinary revival of the train of thought and remembrance that was the first cause of the malady. Some intense associations of a most distressing nature were vividly recalled, I think. It is probable that there had long been a dread lurking in his mind, that those associations would be recalled--say, under certain circumstances--say, on a particular occasion. He tried to prepare himself in vain; perhaps the effort to prepare himself made him less able to bear it.”

“I think,” replied Doctor Manette, “that there was a powerful and unusual resurgence of the chain of ideas and memories that originally triggered his illness. Certain deeply painful connections were, I suspect, brought back with striking clarity. Likely, a fear had been quietly haunting him for some time—that those memories would resurface, perhaps in specific conditions, perhaps at a certain moment. He struggled to ready himself, but to no avail; maybe the very act of trying left him even less equipped to endure it.”

It was a heavy mass of building, that chateau of Monsieur the Marquis, with a large stone courtyard before it, and two stone sweeps of staircase meeting in a stone terrace before the principal door. A stony business altogether, with heavy stone balustrades, and stone urns, and stone flowers, and stone faces of men, and stone heads of lions, in all directions. As if the Gorgon’s head had surveyed it, when it was finished, two centuries ago.

The Marquis’s chateau was a massive, heavy structure, fronted by a wide stone courtyard and two curving staircases that joined at a terrace before the main entrance. It was an entirely stony affair, crowded with thick railings, urns, flowers, and the sculpted faces of men and lions, all carved from rock. It was as if a Gorgon’s head had looked over the building when it was finished two centuries ago, freezing it forever in its current state.

The search was all in vain. There were not even the traces of recent footsteps, to be seen. They stood now, on the summit of a little hill, commanding the open fields in every direction for three or four miles. There was the village in the hollow on the left; but, in order to gain that, after pursuing the track Oliver had pointed out, the men must have made a circuit of open ground, which it was impossible they could have accomplished in so short a time. A thick wood skirted the meadow-land in another direction; but they could not have gained that covert for the same reason.

Their efforts had yielded nothing. Not a single fresh footprint could be found anywhere. They stood now atop a small rise, overlooking the surrounding countryside for several miles in every direction. To the left, a village sat nestled in a hollow; however, to reach it from the path Oliver had indicated, the men would have had to cross a wide stretch of exposed ground, a feat they could not have accomplished so quickly. A dense wood bordered the meadows in a different direction, but they could not have reached that shelter for the same reason.

“I understand how to put _you_ in a corner, Mr. Darnay,” said Bully Stryver, “and I’ll do it. If this fellow is a gentleman, I _don’t_ understand him. You may tell him so, with my compliments. You may also tell him, from me, that after abandoning his worldly goods and position to this butcherly mob, I wonder he is not at the head of them. But, no, gentlemen,” said Stryver, looking all round, and snapping his fingers, “I know something of human nature, and I tell you that you’ll never find a fellow like this fellow, trusting himself to the mercies of such precious _protégés_. No, gentlemen; he’ll always show ’em a clean pair of heels very early in the scuffle, and sneak away.”

“I know just how to corner _you_, Mr. Darnay,” said Bully Stryver, “and I mean to do it. If this man claims to be a gentleman, I _can’t_ make him out. Feel free to pass that along to him, with my regards. You can also tell him from me that, having handed over his property and status to this bloodthirsty rabble, I’m surprised he isn’t leading them himself. But no, gentlemen,” Stryver continued, glancing around the room and snapping his fingers, “I’ve seen enough of human nature to know you’ll never meet a man like this one, throwing himself on the kindness of such darling _protégés_. No, gentlemen; he’ll always turn tail at the first sign of trouble and slip away unseen.”

But it can't be done, the poor fellow. Ah, just think how uneven life is.

“I knew,” said Defarge, looking down at his wife, who stood at the bottom of the steps on which he was raised, looking steadily up at him; “I knew that this prisoner, of whom I speak, had been confined in a cell known as One Hundred and Five, North Tower. I knew it from himself. He knew himself by no other name than One Hundred and Five, North Tower, when he made shoes under my care. As I serve my gun that day, I resolve, when the place shall fall, to examine that cell. It falls. I mount to the cell, with a fellow-citizen who is one of the Jury, directed by a gaoler. I examine it, very closely. In a hole in the chimney, where a stone has been worked out and replaced, I find a written paper. This is that written paper. I have made it my business to examine some specimens of the writing of Doctor Manette. This is the writing of Doctor Manette. I confide this paper, in the writing of Doctor Manette, to the hands of the President.”

“I was certain,” Villon declared, casting his gaze down toward Elise, who remained at the foot of the dais, her eyes fixed unwaveringly upon his own. “I was aware that the captive in question had been interred within a chamber designated as Cell Sixty-Four of the West Wing. This fact I learned from his own lips. While he labored at the cobbler’s bench under my supervision, he recognized no identity save for that of Sixty-Four, West Wing. On the day I manned the cannon, I vowed that should the fortress be taken, I would search that very room. The stronghold yielded. Accompanied by a turnkey and a compatriot from the Council, I ascended to the quarters. I scrutinized the space with the utmost care. Within a cavity in the hearth, where a block had been loosened and then reset, I discovered a manuscript. This is the document I recovered. I have taken pains to study various examples of Professor Valerius’s script. This script belongs to Professor Valerius. I now entrust this record, penned by the hand of Professor Valerius, to the custody of the Magistrate.”

But Yeobright was too deeply absorbed in the ramblings incidental to his remorseful state to notice her. During his illness he had been continually talking thus. Despair had been added to his original grief by the unfortunate disclosure of the boy who had received the last words of Mrs. Yeobright—words too bitterly uttered in an hour of misapprehension. Then his distress had overwhelmed him, and he longed for death as a field labourer longs for the shade. It was the pitiful sight of a man standing in the very focus of sorrow. He continually bewailed his tardy journey to his mother’s house, because it was an error which could never be rectified, and insisted that he must have been horribly perverted by some fiend not to have thought before that it was his duty to go to her, since she did not come to him. He would ask Eustacia to agree with him in his self-condemnation; and when she, seared inwardly by a secret she dared not tell, declared that she could not give an opinion, he would say, “That’s because you didn’t know my mother’s nature. She was always ready to forgive if asked to do so; but I seemed to her to be as an obstinate child, and that made her unyielding. Yet not unyielding—she was proud and reserved, no more.... Yes, I can understand why she held out against me so long. She was waiting for me. I dare say she said a hundred times in her sorrow, ‘What a return he makes for all the sacrifices I have made for him!’ I never went to her! When I set out to visit her it was too late. To think of that is nearly intolerable!”

Yeobright was too deeply lost in the rambling thoughts brought on by his guilt to pay her any mind. He had been speaking this way incessantly throughout his illness. His initial grief had turned to pure despair following the unfortunate account from the boy who had heard Mrs. Yeobright’s final words—bitter things said in a moment of tragic misunderstanding. This distress had completely broken him, and he yearned for death as a laborer in the fields aches for the shade. It was a heart-wrenching sight: a man standing at the very center of sorrow. He constantly mourned his delayed journey to his mother’s house, an error that could never be undone, insisting he must have been possessed by some dark spirit to have forgotten his duty to go to her since she would not come to him. He would beg Eustacia to confirm his self-blame; yet she, tormented by a secret she dared not confess, claimed she could not offer an opinion. “That’s because you didn’t know my mother’s heart,” he would reply. “She was always ready to forgive if she were asked, but I must have seemed like a stubborn child to her, which made her appear unyielding. But she wasn't—she was just proud and reserved. Yes, I understand now why she held out against me for so long. She was waiting for me to come. I imagine she said a hundred times in her grief, ‘This is how he repays all my sacrifices!’ And I never went to her. When I finally set out to see her, it was too late. The thought of it is almost more than I can bear.”

"And another thing I find noteworthy is the way in which what I might call mature people seem to go on having a good time instead of standing by and applauding the young people having a good time.... And the young people do not seem to have set out to have a good time at all.... Now in America, a charming girl like Miss Corner would be distinctly more aware of herself and her vitality than she is here, distinctly more. Her peculiarly charming sidelong look, if I might make so free with her--would have been called attention to. It's a perfectly beautiful look, the sort of look some great artist would have loved to make immortal. It's a look I shall find it hard to forget.... But she doesn't seem to be aware in the least of it. In America she would be aware of it. She would be distinctly aware of it. She would have been _made_ aware of it. She would have been advised of it. It would be looked for and she would know it was looked for. She would _give_ it as a singer gives her most popular song. Mamie Nelson, for example, used to give a peculiar little throw back of the chin and a laugh.... It was talked about. People came to see it....

Another thing that strikes me as worth pointing out is how grown adults, for lack of a better term, still seem to prioritize enjoying themselves instead of hanging on the sidelines cheering on young people as they have fun. And the young folks here don’t even seem like they’re trying to have a good time in the first place.

Take someone as lovely as Miss Corner, for instance: back in the States, she’d be so much more conscious of her own energy and appeal than she is here, no question. That little sideways glance of hers, if I can be so forward as to mention it, would’ve been pointed out to her ages ago. It’s such a gorgeous expression, the kind a master painter would jump at the chance to capture forever. I don’t think I’ll ever be able to get it out of my head, honestly.

But she has no idea she even does it, not a clue. In the US, she’d know. She’d know full well. People would’ve made sure she knew. Someone would’ve mentioned it to her outright. Everyone would be waiting for that look, and she’d know they were waiting. She’d serve it up on purpose, the same way a singer trots out her most beloved hit for an adoring crowd. Take Mamie Nelson, for example: she had this little habit of tilting her chin back and laughing a very specific way. Everyone talked about it. People would turn up to events just to see her do it.

Yes, it was a neat thing, very neat and pretty to see. It resembled a steamboat explosion on the Mississippi; and during the next fifteen minutes we stood under a steady drizzle of microscopic fragments of knights and hardware and horse-flesh. I say we, for the king joined the audience, of course, as soon as he had got his breath again. There was a hole there which would afford steady work for all the people in that region for some years to come --in trying to explain it, I mean; as for filling it up, that service would be comparatively prompt, and would fall to the lot of a select few--peasants of that seignory; and they wouldn't get anything for it, either.

Oh, it was a fine sight, remarkably sharp and charming to take in. It looked exactly like a canal barge detonation on the Severn; and for the full quarter-hour after, we huddled under a steady fine drizzle of minuscule scraps of jousters, plate steel, and horse flesh. I say we, because the king joined the crowd of onlookers, of course, the second he’d caught his breath again. The crater left there would give every soul in that region steady work for years on end—trying to explain how it came to be, that is; as for filling it back in, that job would be over comparatively quickly, and would fall to a small, handpicked lot: the serfs tied to that lord’s estate. And they wouldn’t earn a farthing for the work, either.

“What are you at now? Abusing the lads, you grasping, greedy, in-sa-ti-a-ble old receiver?” said the fellow, as he settled himself with studied ease. “It’s a wonder they don’t put an end to you! I know I should, if I were in their place. “By degrees I made a discovery of still greater moment. I found that these people possessed a method of communicating their experience and feelings to one another by articulate sounds. I perceived that the words they spoke sometimes produced pleasure or pain, smiles or sadness, in the minds and countenances of the hearers. This was indeed a godlike science, and I ardently desired to become acquainted with it. But I was baffled in every attempt I made for this purpose. Their pronunciation was quick, and the words they uttered, not having any apparent connection with visible objects, I was unable to discover any clue by which I could unravel the mystery of their reference. By great application, however, and after having remained during the space of several revolutions of the moon in my hovel, I discovered the names that were given to some of the most familiar objects of discourse; I learned and applied the words, _fire, milk, bread,_ and _wood._ I learned also the names of the cottagers themselves. The youth and his companion had each of them several names, but the old man had only one, which was _father._ The girl was called _sister_ or _Agatha,_ and the youth _Felix, brother,_ or _son_. I cannot describe the delight I felt when I learned the ideas appropriated to each of these sounds and was able to pronounce them. I distinguished several other words without being able as yet to understand or apply them, such as _good, dearest, unhappy._

Little by little, I stumbled on a far more important revelation. I realized the people I was watching had a way to share their experiences and emotions with each other using clear, spoken sounds. I noticed that the words they said could spark joy or hurt, grins or sorrow, both in the listeners’ faces and their inner thoughts. This felt like a skill almost divine in its power, and I ached to master it for myself. But every effort I made to learn it left me totally stumped at first. They spoke so fast, and most of the words they said had no obvious link to anything I could see around me, so I couldn’t find a single thread to pull that would untangle what they were referring to. Slowly, though, through sheer relentless effort, after spending several full lunar cycles hidden away in my small shelter, I figured out the labels they used for some of the most common items they talked about. I memorized and started using the words for fire, milk, bread, and wood. I also picked up what the cottage dwellers called each other. The young man and the woman he lived with went by multiple names, but the elderly man only had one: father. The young woman was called either sister or Agatha, and the young man Felix, brother, or son. I can’t put into words how overjoyed I was when I understood what each of those sounds meant, and could say them out loud myself. I picked out a handful of other words too, though I couldn’t yet figure out their meaning or use them correctly—words like good, dearest, and unhappy.

France, less favoured on the whole as to matters spiritual than her sister of the shield and trident, rolled with exceeding smoothness down hill, making paper money and spending it. Under the guidance of her Christian pastors, she entertained herself, besides, with such humane achievements as sentencing a youth to have his hands cut off, his tongue torn out with pincers, and his body burned alive, because he had not kneeled down in the rain to do honour to a dirty procession of monks which passed within his view, at a distance of some fifty or sixty yards. It is likely enough that, rooted in the woods of France and Norway, there were growing trees, when that sufferer was put to death, already marked by the Woodman, Fate, to come down and be sawn into boards, to make a certain movable framework with a sack and a knife in it, terrible in history. It is likely enough that in the rough outhouses of some tillers of the heavy lands adjacent to Paris, there were sheltered from the weather that very day, rude carts, bespattered with rustic mire, snuffed about by pigs, and roosted in by poultry, which the Farmer, Death, had already set apart to be his tumbrils of the Revolution. But that Woodman and that Farmer, though they work unceasingly, work silently, and no one heard them as they went about with muffled tread: the rather, forasmuch as to entertain any suspicion that they were awake, was to be atheistical and traitorous.

France, not quite as blessed in spiritual matters as her naval and military counterpart, coasted downhill with remarkable ease, churning out paper currency and squandering it. Under the direction of her Christian shepherds, she also amused herself with such compassionate deeds as condemning a young man to have his hands severed, his tongue ripped out with pincers, and his body consumed by flames, all because he failed to kneel in the rain to pay respect to a grimy parade of monks that crossed his line of sight, some fifty or sixty yards away. It is quite possible that, deep in the forests of France and Norway, trees stood already marked by the Woodman, Fate, to be felled and cut into planks for constructing a certain portable device with a sack and a blade, notorious in history, at the very moment that victim was executed. It is quite possible that in the crude sheds of farmers working the heavy soil near Paris, there rested that same day rough wagons, splattered with country filth, sniffed at by swine, and used as roosts by chickens, which the Farmer, Death, had already designated to serve as his revolutionary tumbrils. But that Woodman and that Farmer, though they labor endlessly, labor in silence, and nobody caught the sound of their soft-footed work—especially since entertaining any notion that they were stirring was considered both godless and treasonous.

Martin recollected his blank-verse tragedy, and sent it instead. He read it over before mailing, and was particularly impressed by its sophomoric amateurishness and general worthlessness. But he sent it; and it was published, to the everlasting regret of the editor. The public was indignant and incredulous. It was too far a cry from Martin Eden’s high standard to that serious bosh. It was asserted that he had never written it, that the magazine had faked it very clumsily, or that Martin Eden was emulating the elder Dumas and at the height of success was hiring his writing done for him. But when he explained that the tragedy was an early effort of his literary childhood, and that the magazine had refused to be happy unless it got it, a great laugh went up at the magazine’s expense and a change in the editorship followed. The tragedy was never brought out in book-form, though Martin pocketed the advance royalties that had been paid.

Martin remembered his blank-verse tragedy and sent that instead. Before mailing it, he read it over, and was struck especially by its awkward, boyish amateurishness and its all-around lack of merit. Nevertheless, he sent it off; and it was published, much to the editor’s lasting regret. The public responded with anger and disbelief. It was too great a departure from Martin Eden’s lofty reputation to be credited to that solemn nonsense. People said he had never written it at all, that the magazine had forged it in a very clumsy way, or that Martin Eden, like the elder Dumas, was so successful he had others do his writing for him. But when he explained that the tragedy had been an early work from his literary childhood, and that the magazine had not been content until it had secured it, there was a loud laugh at the magazine’s expense and a change in editors followed. The tragedy was never issued in book form, though Martin kept the advance royalties that had already been paid.

Within the altar of the old village church there stands a white marble tablet, which bears as yet but one word: “AGNES.” There is no coffin in that tomb; and may it be many, many years, before another name is placed above it! But, if the spirits of the Dead ever come back to earth, to visit spots hallowed by the love—the love beyond the grave—of those whom they knew in life, I believe that the shade of Agnes sometimes hovers round that solemn nook. I believe it none the less because that nook is in a Church, and she was weak and erring.

That I am hungry and you are aware of it are only ordinary phenomena, and there’s no disgrace. You see, I laugh at the conventional little moralities of the herd; then you drift by, say a sharp, true word, and immediately I am the slave of the same little moralities.”

“Quite the opposite,” Martin mused, “it’s because the whole incident isn’t worth bothering over.” He burst into a loud, warm, genuine laugh. “I’ll admit you absolutely made a fool of me, Brissenden. That I’m half-starving and you’ve noticed it is just a plain, unremarkable fact, there’s no shame in it. You see, I spend all my time scoffing at the petty, conventional moralities the herd lives by; then you drift along, say one sharp, unvarnished truth, and right away I’m bending to the exact same silly little rules I claim to despise.”

Mills now found himself caught in a delicate snare, for if Bathsheba and Troy mended their bond, he would need to reclaim her favour to keep the husband’s backing. “I half fancy she still holds you dear, and is a decent soul beneath it all,” he offered, as a cautious hedge. “Still, a person’s outward show is a poor oracle. The girl lay still, as if in a faint. Rudolf looked around the room excitedly for a barrel. People must be rolled upon a barrel who—no, no; that was for drowned persons. He began to fan her with his hat. That was successful, for he struck her nose with the brim of his derby and she opened her eyes. And then the young man saw that hers, indeed, was the one missing face from his heart’s gallery of intimate portraits. The frank, grey eyes, the little nose, turning pertly outward; the chestnut hair, curling like the tendrils of a pea vine, seemed the right end and reward of all his wonderful adventures. But the face was wofully thin and pale.

The girl lay motionless, as though in a swoon. Emil cast an eager glance around the room for a barrel. People who had—no, no; that was for those drawn from water. He began to waft her with his hat. This proved effective, for he tapped her nose with the edge of his derby and she opened her eyes. Then the young man perceived that it was indeed her face, the one absent from the gallery of his heart’s most secret portraits. The open grey eyes, the small nose with its saucy outward turn; the chestnut hair, curling like the tendrils of a pea vine, seemed the proper conclusion and reward of all his marvelous adventures. Yet the face was terribly thin and pale.

On these black nights, when the personal Mr. Britling would lie awake thinking how unsatisfactorily Mr. Britling was going on, and when the impersonal Mr. Britling would be thinking how unsatisfactorily his universe was going on, the whole mental process had a likeness to some complex piece of orchestral music wherein the organ deplored the melancholy destinies of the race while the piccolo lamented the secret trouble of Mrs. Harrowdean; the big drum thundered at the Irish politicians, and all the violins bewailed the intellectual laxity of the university system. Meanwhile the trumpets prophesied wars and disasters, the cymbals ever and again inserted a clashing jar about the fatal delay in the automobile insurance, while the triangle broke into a plangent solo on the topic of a certain rotten gate-post he always forgot in the daytime, and how in consequence the cows from the glebe farm got into the garden and ate Mrs. Britling's carnations.

In the depths of these dark nights, the individual Mr. Britling would lie awake considering how poorly his own life was proceeding, while the universal Mr. Britling weighed the failures of the cosmos. His thoughts played out like a grand, intricate piece of music, where the heavy organ mourned the tragic fate of the human race and the shrill piccolo fretted over the private complications of Mrs. Harrowdean. The bass drum thundered with indignation at the Irish politicians, and the violins wept for the intellectual decay of the universities. Meanwhile, the trumpets heralded coming wars and ruin, the cymbals periodically struck a harsh note regarding the stalled automobile insurance, and the triangle rang out a sharp, persistent solo about a decaying gate-post he neglected during the day—reminding him how, as a result, the cows from the parish farm would invade the garden and consume his wife’s prize carnations.

This woman was not given to soliloquy; but extremity of feeling lessens the individuality of the weak, as it increases that of the strong. She said again in the same tone, “I’ll believe that the end lies five posts forward, and no further, and so get strength to pass them.”

She was not a woman prone to speaking her thoughts; yet a crisis of the spirit often dissolves the distinctiveness of the frail, just as it clarifies the character of the brave. In a level voice she said once more, “I shall imagine the journey ends at the fifth marker yonder, and no further, and so find the fortitude to reach it.”

Mr. Van der Pant had had shots at various Zeppelins. When the Zeppelins came to Antwerp everybody turned out on the roofs and shot at them. He was contemptuous of Zeppelins. He made derisive gestures to express his opinion of them. They could do nothing unless they came low, and if they came low you could hit them. One which ventured down had been riddled; it had had to drop all its bombs--luckily they fell in an open field--in order to make its lame escape. It was all nonsense to say, as the English papers did, that they took part in the final bombardment. Not a Zeppelin.... So he talked, and the Britling family listened and understood as much as they could, and replied and questioned in Anglo-French. Here was a man who but a few days ago had been steering his bicycle in the streets of Antwerp to avoid shell craters, pools of blood, and the torn-off arms and shoulder-blades of women. He had seen houses flaring, set afire by incendiary bombs, and once at a corner he had been knocked off his bicycle by the pouff of a bursting shell.... Not only were these things in the same world with us, they were sitting at our table.

Mr. Van der Pant had frequently taken aim at the Zeppelins. Whenever the airships loomed over Antwerp, the residents would gather on their rooftops to fire at them. He held the vessels in complete contempt, using mocking gestures to convey his disdain for their presence. They were powerless unless they flew low, he insisted, and if they did, they became easy targets. He described one that had descended only to be riddled with gunfire; it had been forced to jettison its entire payload—which fortunately struck an empty field—simply to make a crippled escape. He dismissed the reports in the English press as utter nonsense; not a single Zeppelin had participated in the final bombardment. 

As he recounted these events, the Britlings listened, following as best they could and offering questions in a fractured Anglo-French. Sitting before them was a man who, just days prior, had been cycling through the streets of Antwerp, steering around shell craters, pools of blood, and the severed limbs of women. He had watched houses go up in flames from incendiary strikes and had once been blown off his bicycle by the concussive blast of a shell. These nightmares were not merely part of the same world; they were present, sharing a meal at their very table.

Julian observed the bloodless pallor of Elara’s skin, the frantic dilation of her gaze, and the white-knuckled tension of her fingers with a hidden, predatory pride. He had won. The raw essence of his imagination had been transplanted from his consciousness into hers. It had struck deep. Whether she found it abhorrent or exquisite mattered little; it had enslaved her attention, compelling her into a breathless silence that rendered the rest of the world invisible.

“Autumn passed thus. I saw, with surprise and grief, the leaves decay and fall, and nature again assume the barren and bleak appearance it had worn when I first beheld the woods and the lovely moon. Yet I did not heed the bleakness of the weather; I was better fitted by my conformation for the endurance of cold than heat. But my chief delights were the sight of the flowers, the birds, and all the gay apparel of summer; when those deserted me, I turned with more attention towards the cottagers. Their happiness was not decreased by the absence of summer. They loved and sympathised with one another; and their joys, depending on each other, were not interrupted by the casualties that took place around them. The more I saw of them, the greater became my desire to claim their protection and kindness; my heart yearned to be known and loved by these amiable creatures; to see their sweet looks directed towards me with affection was the utmost limit of my ambition. I dared not think that they would turn them from me with disdain and horror. The poor that stopped at their door were never driven away. I asked, it is true, for greater treasures than a little food or rest: I required kindness and sympathy; but I did not believe myself utterly unworthy of it.

The autumn season slipped away. I watched with a mix of wonder and sadness as the leaves rotted and fell, and the world once again took on the desolate, frozen appearance it had when I first saw the woods beneath the moon. The harshness of the weather did not bother me, for my constitution was better suited to endure the cold than the summer heat. Nevertheless, I missed the beauty of the flowers, the songs of the birds, and the vibrant colors of the warmer months; as these faded, I concentrated more fully on the inhabitants of the cottage. Their joy did not seem to wane with the passing of summer. They cared for and comforted one another, their happiness so intertwined that it remained undisturbed by the grim changes in the world around them. The more I observed them, the more I longed to seek their protection and grace. My heart ached to be known and loved by these gentle people; to have their kind eyes rest upon me with affection was the very peak of my ambition. I refused to believe they would look at me with nothing but loathing and fear. They never turned away the destitute who came to their door. It was true that I sought a much greater prize than a bit of bread or a place to rest—I hungered for friendship and empathy—but I did not feel that I was entirely unworthy of receiving them.

“The sleeper stirred; a thrill of terror ran through me. Should she indeed awake, and see me, and curse me, and denounce the murderer? Thus would she assuredly act if her darkened eyes opened and she beheld me. The thought was madness; it stirred the fiend within me—not I, but she, shall suffer; the murder I have committed because I am for ever robbed of all that she could give me, she shall atone. The crime had its source in her; be hers the punishment! Thanks to the lessons of Felix and the sanguinary laws of man, I had learned now to work mischief. I bent over her and placed the portrait securely in one of the folds of her dress. She moved again, and I fled.

The sleeper shifted; a shudder of dread passed through me. If she truly woke, and glimpsed my face, and damned me, and named the killer? Surely that would be her course, should those clouded eyes open and find me standing there. The notion was lunacy—it roused the demon in my breast: not I, but she, would bear the agony. The death I dealt because I am eternally deprived of all she might have offered me, she must repay. The transgression sprang from her; let the penalty be hers! Schooled by Felix’s teachings and mankind’s bloody statutes, I had now mastered the art of ruin. I leaned above her and tucked the locket deep into a fold of her gown. She stirred once more, and I vanished.

Plenty cow, plenty land, plenty grass. I lika da have near San Le-an; my sister liva dere. I sella da milk in Oakland. I maka da plentee mon. Joe an’ Nick no runna da cow. Dey go-a to school. Bimeby maka da good engineer, worka da railroad. Yes, I lika da milka ranch.”

“So then—” She inhaled deeply, with the guileless air of a child, as she gave utterance to the furthest reaches of her ambition. “I desire a dairy farm—a prosperous dairy farm. Many cows, vast acreage, and rich, green grass. I would have it near San Lorenzo, for my sister resides there. I would sell my milk in the city of Oakland and amass a great fortune. Marco and Luca would not labor with the herd; they would attend their lessons. In time, they would become skilled engineers for the great railroad. Yes, that is the life I crave: the dairy farm.”

My courage and perseverance were invigorated by these scoffing words; I resolved not to fail in my purpose, and calling on Heaven to support me, I continued with unabated fervour to traverse immense deserts, until the ocean appeared at a distance and formed the utmost boundary of the horizon. Oh! How unlike it was to the blue seasons of the south! Covered with ice, it was only to be distinguished from land by its superior wildness and ruggedness. The Greeks wept for joy when they beheld the Mediterranean from the hills of Asia, and hailed with rapture the boundary of their toils. I did not weep, but I knelt down and with a full heart thanked my guiding spirit for conducting me in safety to the place where I hoped, notwithstanding my adversary’s gibe, to meet and grapple with him.

My resolve and endurance were steeled by those mocking taunts; I vowed not to falter in my quest, and beseeching Providence to sustain me, I pressed on with undiminished zeal across boundless wastes, till the sea glimmered afar and marked the final rim of the world. Ah! How alien it was to the azure summers of the south! Sheathed in frost, it could scarcely be told from the shore save by its greater savagery and desolation. The Hellenes shed glad tears when they glimpsed the Aegean from the Asian highlands, and acclaimed with ecstasy the end of their labours. I shed none, but I dropped to my knees and with an overflowing spirit thanked my tutelary power for bringing me unscathed to the spot where I trusted, despite my foe's scorn, to confront and wrestle with him.

Tumbling down the incline like maniacs in a desperate chase, the wretch collapses. His captors howl with glee as they wrench him upright. His countenance is a mask of grit and scarlet, yet his shackled hands cannot reach it; seeing his struggle, they jeer anew. They haul him into the hamlet, where the populace swarms to watch; they parade him past the grinding stones of the mill to the fortress gates. There, beneath the black cloak of night, the village sees the portal yawn open and devour him—precisely so!

He made a long story of it; but the part that had immediate interest for me was this: He said I was Sir Kay's prisoner, and that in the due course of custom I would be flung into a dungeon and left there on scant commons until my friends ransomed me--unless I chanced to rot, first. I saw that the last chance had the best show, but I didn't waste any bother about that; time was too precious. The page said, further, that dinner was about ended in the great hall by this time, and that as soon as the sociability and the heavy drinking should begin, Sir Kay would have me in and exhibit me before King Arthur and his illustrious knights seated at the Table Round, and would brag about his exploit in capturing me, and would probably exaggerate the facts a little, but it wouldn't be good form for me to correct him, and not over safe, either; and when I was done being exhibited, then ho for the dungeon; but he, Clarence, would find a way to come and see me every now and then, and cheer me up, and help me get word to my friends.

He dragged the tale out at length, but the only details that held immediate urgency for me were these: I was Sir Kay’s prisoner, he said, and per longstanding custom I would be thrown into a dungeon, kept on meager rations until my people paid my ransom—if I did not waste away and rot there first. I knew that second outcome was far more likely, but I did not waste breath fretting over it; every moment was too precious to squander. The page went on to explain that dinner in the great hall was nearly finished by now, and once the merrymaking and heavy drinking got underway, Sir Kay would bring me in to parade before King Arthur and his famed knights gathered at the Round Table. He would boast of his exploit in capturing me, likely embroidering the facts a fair bit, and it would be neither proper nor particularly safe for me to correct him. Once the showing off was done, it would be straight to the dungeon—but he, Clarence, would find a way to slip in to see me now and then, lift my spirits, and help me send word back to my friends.

I was obliged to admire my cuteness in foreseeing this very thing and swapping trees to beat it. But, don't you know, there are some things that can beat smartness and foresight? Awkwardness and stupidity can. The best swordsman in the world doesn't need to fear the second best swordsman in the world; no, the person for him to be afraid of is some ignorant antagonist who has never had a sword in his hand before; he doesn't do the thing he ought to do, and so the expert isn't prepared for him; he does the thing he ought not to do; and often it catches the expert out and ends him on the spot. Well, how could I, with all my gifts, make any valuable preparation against a near-sighted, cross-eyed, pudding-headed clown who would aim himself at the wrong tree and hit the right one? And that is what he did. He went for the wrong tree, which was, of course, the right one by mistake, and up he started.

I couldn’t help patting myself on the back for being so sharp as to see this coming ahead of time and switching trees to dodge the whole mess. But here’s the thing, right? There are some forces that outsmart even the sharpest planning and most careful foresight. Clumsiness and sheer, unapologetic stupidity, for example. The greatest fencer alive doesn’t lose sleep over the second-ranked fencer in the world, not at all. The person he ought to be scared of is some totally clueless opponent who’s never even held a sword in his life. He doesn’t make the moves he’s supposed to make, so the expert has no way to prepare for him; he pulls all the stunts he should never pull, and more often than not he catches the pro completely off guard and takes him out right then and there. So how was I, for all my quick wits, supposed to make any sort of useful plan to outmaneuver a near-sighted, cross-eyed, thick-skulled oaf who’d line himself up for the wrong tree and end up hitting the correct one by accident? That’s exactly what he did, too. He made a beeline for the wrong tree, which naturally ended up being the right one entirely by mistake, and hauled himself right up after me.

This dog had originally belonged to a shepherd of inferior morals and dreadful temper, and the result was that George knew the exact degrees of condemnation signified by cursing and swearing of all descriptions better than the wickedest old man in the neighbourhood. Long experience had so precisely taught the animal the difference between such exclamations as “Come in!” and “D–––– ye, come in!” that he knew to a hair’s breadth the rate of trotting back from the ewes’ tails that each call involved, if a staggerer with the sheep crook was to be escaped. Though old, he was clever and trustworthy still.

George had originally been owned by a shepherd of loose morals and a vicious disposition, with the result that the dog understood the various shades of malice in every oath and curse more clearly than the most foul-mouthed scoundrel in the parish. Long experience had taught him to distinguish so finely between a civil "Come in!" and a "Damn you, come in!" that he knew to a hair’s breadth how fast he must retreat from the ewes' heels to escape a heavy blow from a sheep crook. Though he was getting on in years, he remained a sharp and thoroughly reliable animal.

When we arrived at that hut at mid-afternoon, we saw no signs of life about it. The field near by had been denuded of its crop some time before, and had a skinned look, so exhaustively had it been harvested and gleaned. Fences, sheds, everything had a ruined look, and were eloquent of poverty. No animal was around anywhere, no living thing in sight. The stillness was awful, it was like the stillness of death. The cabin was a one-story one, whose thatch was black with age, and ragged from lack of repair.

The nearby field had long since been stripped of its crop and wore a flayed, exhausted look, so thoroughly had it been reaped and gleaned. The fences, the sheds, everything had a broken-down appearance, speaking plainly of poverty. There was not a single animal anywhere, not a living thing in sight. The silence was dreadful, like the silence of death. The cabin was a one-story place, its thatch blackened by age and tattered from want of repair.

Last Monday (July 31st) we were nearly surrounded by ice, which closed in the ship on all sides, scarcely leaving her the sea-room in which she floated. Our situation was somewhat dangerous, especially as we were compassed round by a very thick fog. We accordingly lay to, hoping that some change would take place in the atmosphere and weather.

On the final day of July, a Monday, the vessel found itself all but imprisoned by a vast ring of ice that pressed in from every quarter, granting the hull barely enough open water to breathe. A measure of peril attended our position, made graver still by the dense fog that wrapped us completely about. We therefore hove to, trusting that the skies and the elements would soon shift their mood.

“I not only happened to be there,” said Fairway, with a fresh collection of emphasis, “but I was sitting in the same pew as Mis’ess Yeobright. And though you may not see it as such, it fairly made my blood run cold to hear her. Yes, it is a curious thing; but it made my blood run cold, for I was close at her elbow.” The speaker looked round upon the bystanders, now drawing closer to hear him, with his lips gathered tighter than ever in the rigorousness of his descriptive moderation.

“I didn’t just happen to be there,” Fairway said, laying extra weight on each word for emphasis, “I was sitting in the exact same pew as Miz Yeobright. You might not think that would land so hard, but listening to her honestly made my blood run cold. Yeah, it’s a strange thing to say, but it really did chill me to the bone— I was sitting right at her elbow, after all.” He glanced around at the bystanders, who were now pressing in closer to hear every detail, his lips drawn tighter than ever as he held fast to his strict, no-frills way of telling the story.

“Food, however, became scarce, and I often spent the whole day searching in vain for a few acorns to assuage the pangs of hunger. When I found this, I resolved to quit the place that I had hitherto inhabited, to seek for one where the few wants I experienced would be more easily satisfied. In this emigration I exceedingly lamented the loss of the fire which I had obtained through accident and knew not how to reproduce it. I gave several hours to the serious consideration of this difficulty, but I was obliged to relinquish all attempt to supply it, and wrapping myself up in my cloak, I struck across the wood towards the setting sun. I passed three days in these rambles and at length discovered the open country. A great fall of snow had taken place the night before, and the fields were of one uniform white; the appearance was disconsolate, and I found my feet chilled by the cold damp substance that covered the ground.

Food grew scarce, all the same, and I would often wander for entire days searching fruitlessly for a handful of acorns to dull the sharp, twisting pangs of hunger. Once I accepted this unyielding state of things, I made up my mind to leave the spot I had inhabited up until that point, to search for somewhere my few simple needs might be met with less struggle. As I prepared to set off, I grieved deeply over the loss of the fire I had stumbled upon by chance, for I had no idea how to make a new one for myself. I spent hours turning the problem over carefully in my mind, but I was eventually forced to abandon any hope of replacing it. Pulling my cloak tight around my shoulders, I struck out through the woods, heading in the direction of the sinking sun. I wandered for three days before I finally came upon open, unfenced countryside. A heavy snowstorm had blown through the night before, painting every stretch of field the same stark, unbroken white. The sight was dreary and desolate, and I felt my feet growing numb, stung by the cold, damp crust that blanketed the ground.

She was not past seventeen. Cast in so slight and exquisite a mould; so mild and gentle; so pure and beautiful; that earth seemed not her element, nor its rough creatures her fit companions. The very intelligence that shone in her deep blue eye, and was stamped upon her noble head, seemed scarcely of her age, or of the world; and yet the changing expression of sweetness and good humour, the thousand lights that played about the face, and left no shadow there; above all, the smile, the cheerful, happy smile, were made for Home, and fireside peace and happiness.

She could not have been more than seventeen. So delicately and exquisitely fashioned; so gentle, so mild, so pure and beautiful; it seemed as though the earth were not her proper element, nor its coarse inhabitants suitable companions for her. The very intelligence that shone in her deep blue eye, and seemed written on her noble head, appeared scarcely to belong to her years, or even to this world; and yet the shifting sweetness and good temper of her expression, the countless lights that played across her face and left no shadow there; above all, the smile—the bright, contented, happy smile—seemed made for home, and for the peace and happiness of the hearth.

“Bill,” cried the girl, striving to lay her head upon his breast, “the gentleman and that dear lady, told me tonight of a home in some foreign country where I could end my days in solitude and peace. Let me see them again, and beg them, on my knees, to show the same mercy and goodness to you; and let us both leave this dreadful place, and far apart lead better lives, and forget how we have lived, except in prayers, and never see each other more. It is never too late to repent. They told me so—I feel it now—but we must have time—a little, little time!”

“Jesse,” the girl sobbed, straining to press her forehead to his chest, “the gentle old gentleman and that warm, kind lady I spoke with tonight told me of a home across the sea, where I can live out the rest of my days in quiet, unbroken peace. Let me seek them out again, fall to my knees before them if I must, and beg them to show that same mercy and generosity to you too. Then we can both flee this dreadful place, go our separate ways to lead better lives, and put all the ugliness of our past behind us—we will only ever call it to mind in our prayers, and we will never see one another again. It is never too late to repent. They said that to me, and I feel it deep in my soul now, but we need time—just a little, small bit of time!”

Jan held his tongue, as if he had no objection to be pacified to please a friend, and they gradually reached the foot of the ladder, Poorgrass being flattened like a jumping-jack, and the sixpence, for admission, which he had got ready half-an-hour earlier, having become so reeking hot in the tight squeeze of his excited hand that the woman in spangles, brazen rings set with glass diamonds, and with chalked face and shoulders, who took the money of him, hastily dropped it again from a fear that some trick had been played to burn her fingers. So they all entered, and the cloth of the tent, to the eyes of an observer on the outside, became bulged into innumerable pimples such as we observe on a sack of potatoes, caused by the various human heads, backs, and elbows at high pressure within.

Silas remained quiet, appearing content to be mollified for his companion’s sake, and they slowly neared the bottom of the gangway. Barnaby was flattened like a wooden puppet, and the small coin for his passage, which he had gripped tightly for the last hour, had grown so fervently hot in the damp clench of his agitated hand that the woman in tinsel and brass rings set with paste jewels, her visage and throat masked in white powder, who took the payment from him, let it fall at once, dreading that some mischief had been used to scald her skin. So they passed inside, and the heavy canvas, to a spectator in the field, became distended with a thousand small knots like those on a bag of grain, pushed out by the frantic press of heads, shoulders, and joints within.

From this place, they passed through several strong gates, opened by other turnkeys from the inner side; and, having entered an open yard, ascended a flight of narrow steps, and came into a passage with a row of strong doors on the left hand. Motioning them to remain where they were, the turnkey knocked at one of these with his bunch of keys. The two attendants, after a little whispering, came out into the passage, stretching themselves as if glad of the temporary relief, and motioned the visitors to follow the jailer into the cell. They did so.

Leaving that spot, the group proceeded through a succession of stout barriers, which were unfastened by warders stationed on the interior; having crossed a vacant courtyard, they mounted a steep, constricted staircase and reached a gallery flanked on the left by a sequence of massive doors. Gesturing for them to pause, the keeper struck one of these panels with his heavy iron ring. Two guards, after a moment of low-voiced consultation, stepped out into the hall, extending their weary frames as if savoring the short reprieve, and signaled for the newcomers to follow the gaoler into the room. They obeyed.

Oak felt a flicker of amusement, tinged perhaps with mild surprise, and after hanging the hat inside his hut, he returned to his flock. An hour slipped by, and the girl came back, now seated properly, a sack of bran balanced before her. As she approached the cattle-shed, a boy carrying a milking-pail met her and steadied the pony’s reins while she dismounted. The boy led the horse away, leaving the pail in the young woman’s care.
