“Just a minute!” said the girl. “I wouldn’t be in such a rush if it were you coming out to be hanged the next time eight o’clock struck, Bill. I’d keep walking round and round the place till I dropped, if there were snow on the ground and I didn’t have a shawl to wrap myself in.”

Riding across the gap between the cottages was a string of horsemen, and then a grey cart, and then a team drawing a heavy object--a gun, and then more horsemen, and then a second gun. It was all a dim brown procession in the moonlight. A mounted officer came up beside him and looked at him and then went back to the cross-roads, but as yet England was not troubling about spies. Four more guns passed, and then a string of carts and more mounted men, sitting stiffly. Nobody was singing or shouting; scarcely a word was audible, and through all the column there was an effect of quiet efficient haste. And so they passed, and rumbled and jingled and clattered out of the scene, leaving Mr. Britling in his car in the dreaming village. He restarted his engine once more, and went his way thoughtfully.

A line of riders emerged from the gap between the cottages, followed by a grey wagon and a team hauling a heavy field gun. More horsemen came next, then another cannon. In the pale moonlight, the entire column appeared as a murky, brownish procession. An officer on horseback rode up to inspect him before returning to the intersection; at that time, England was not yet preoccupied with the threat of spies. Four more guns rolled past, trailed by a series of carts and more soldiers sitting rigidly in their saddles. No one sang or shouted; hardly a word was spoken, as the entire group moved with a sense of hushed, disciplined urgency. They eventually rumbled and clattered out of sight, leaving Mr. Britling alone in his car within the sleeping village. He restarted his engine and drove off, lost in thought.

She had been talking to Mr. Britling for an hour, and her mind was full of the thought of this changed and simplified man, who talked of God as he might have done of a bird he had seen or of a tree he had sheltered under. And all mixed up with this thought of Mr. Britling was this strange idea of God who was also a limited person, who could come as close as Teddy, whispering love in the darkness. She had a ridiculous feeling that God really struggled like Mr. Britling, and that with only some indefinable inferiority of outlook Mr. Britling loved like God. She loved him for his maps and his dreams and the bareness of his talk to her. It was strange how the straining thought of the dead Teddy had passed now out of her mind. She was possessed by a sense of ending and beginning, as though a page had turned over in her life and everything was new. She had never given religion any thought but contemptuous thought for some years, since indeed her growing intelligence had dismissed it as a scheme of inexcusable restraints and empty pretences, a thing of discords where there were no discords except of its making. She had been a happy Atheist. She had played in the sunshine, a natural creature with the completest confidence in the essential goodness of the world in which she found herself. She had refused all thought of painful and disagreeable things. Until the bloody paw of war had wiped out all her assurance. Teddy, the playmate, was over, the love game was ended for ever; the fresh happy acceptance of life as life; and in the place of Teddy was the sorrow of life, the pity of life, and this coming of God out of utter remoteness into a conceivable relation to her own existence.

For an hour she had sat with Mr. Britling, and her thoughts now circled entirely around this altered, unadorned man who spoke of the Almighty as casually as he might describe a sparrow he had glimpsed or an oak beneath which he had once rested. Entwined with this impression of Mr. Britling was the peculiar notion of a God who was Himself a bounded being, able to draw as near as Teddy had, murmuring affection in the dark. She entertained the absurd conviction that God strove much as Mr. Britling did, and that Mr. Britling, with only some nameless smallness of vision, loved after the fashion of God. She cherished him for his charts and his reveries and the stark simplicity of his words to her. It was curious how the taut memory of the dead Teddy had now slipped entirely from her consciousness. She was seized by a sensation of closure and commencement, as if a leaf had been turned in her existence and all things were remade. For years she had met religion with nothing but disdain, ever since her ripening mind had cast it off as a system of unpardonable prohibitions and hollow shows, a discordant invention where no discords had existed save those it manufactured. She had been a contented unbeliever. She had frolicked in the sunlight, a child of nature wholly trusting in the fundamental benevolence of the world she inhabited. She had barred every thought of suffering or ugliness. Until the gory fist of conflict had shattered all her certainties. Teddy, the companion, was finished, the amorous sport concluded forever; the buoyant, unthinking embrace of life as mere life; and where Teddy had stood now rose the grief of existence, the compassion of existence, and this arrival of God from absolute distance into a graspable bond with her own being.

Venn soon after went away, and in the evening Yeobright strolled as far as Fairway’s cottage. It was a lovely May sunset, and the birch trees which grew on this margin of the vast Egdon wilderness had put on their new leaves, delicate as butterflies’ wings, and diaphanous as amber. Beside Fairway’s dwelling was an open space recessed from the road, and here were now collected all the young people from within a radius of a couple of miles. The pole lay with one end supported on a trestle, and women were engaged in wreathing it from the top downwards with wild-flowers. The instincts of merry England lingered on here with exceptional vitality, and the symbolic customs which tradition has attached to each season of the year were yet a reality on Egdon. Indeed, the impulses of all such outlandish hamlets are pagan still—in these spots homage to nature, self-adoration, frantic gaieties, fragments of Teutonic rites to divinities whose names are forgotten, seem in some way or other to have survived mediæval doctrine.

Venn departed shortly thereafter, and as evening fell, Yeobright wandered as far as Fairway’s cottage. It was a radiant sunset in May, and the birches lining the edge of the immense Egdon wasteland were cloaked in new leaves, as delicate as butterfly wings and as translucent as amber. Next to Fairway’s house was a clearing tucked away from the road, where the youth from the surrounding miles had gathered. A pole rested with one end upon a trestle, and the women were occupied with winding wildflowers around it from top to bottom. The ancient spirit of a festive England endured here with remarkable strength, and the symbolic rites traditionally tied to the seasons remained a lived reality on Egdon. In truth, the heart of such isolated hamlets is still pagan—in these corners, the veneration of nature, wild revelry, and traces of Teutonic ceremonies to forgotten deities seem to have somehow outlived the influence of medieval creeds.

The two gladiators were well matched. Dempsey had, perhaps, ten pounds of weight to give away. The O’Sullivan had breadth with quickness. Dempsey had a glacial eye, a dominating slit of a mouth, an indestructible jaw, a complexion like a belle’s and the coolness of a champion. The visitor showed more fire in his contempt and less control over his conspicuous sneer. They were enemies by the law written when the rocks were molten. They were each too splendid, too mighty, too incomparable to divide pre-eminence. One only must survive.

The two combatants were evenly paired. Dempsey was perhaps ten pounds lighter than his opponent, while O’Sullivan combined a broad frame with remarkable agility. Dempsey possessed a frozen gaze, a thin and commanding mouth, an indestructible jaw, a complexion as clear as a debutante’s, and the unshakable poise of a champion. The visitor displayed a more volatile disdain, unable to fully mask his obvious sneer. They were adversaries by a primal law established when the earth was still molten. Both were far too formidable and unique to share the pinnacle of their craft. Only one could remain standing.

“Can’t be helped,” said Miss Pross, shaking her head. “Touch that string, and he instantly changes for the worse. Better leave it alone. In short, must leave it alone, like or no like. Sometimes, he gets up in the dead of the night, and will be heard, by us overhead there, walking up and down, walking up and down, in his room. Ladybird has learnt to know then that his mind is walking up and down, walking up and down, in his old prison. She hurries to him, and they go on together, walking up and down, walking up and down, until he is composed. But he never says a word of the true reason of his restlessness, to her, and she finds it best not to hint at it to him. In silence they go walking up and down together, walking up and down together, till her love and company have brought him to himself.”

“There’s no remedy for it,” Miss Pross said, shaking her head. “Pluck that chord, and he turns darker in an instant. Best to let it lie. Indeed, it must be let alone, whether one wishes it or not. Now and then, in the deep stillness of night, he rises, and we above can hear him pacing, pacing, pacing in his chamber. Ladybird has learned to read the sign—his mind is pacing, pacing, pacing within the old cell of memory. She hastens to his side, and together they walk, pacing, pacing, until he quiets. Yet he never breathes a word to her of what truly stirs him, and she senses it is wiser not to allude to it. Silently they move together, pacing, pacing, until her devotion and nearness restore him to himself.”

The monster saw my determination in my face and gnashed his teeth in the impotence of anger. “Shall each man,” cried he, “find a wife for his bosom, and each beast have his mate, and I be alone? I had feelings of affection, and they were requited by detestation and scorn. Man! You may hate, but beware! Your hours will pass in dread and misery, and soon the bolt will fall which must ravish from you your happiness for ever. Are you to be happy while I grovel in the intensity of my wretchedness? You can blast my other passions, but revenge remains—revenge, henceforth dearer than light or food! I may die, but first you, my tyrant and tormentor, shall curse the sun that gazes on your misery. Beware, for I am fearless and therefore powerful. I will watch with the wiliness of a snake, that I may sting with its venom. Man, you shall repent of the injuries you inflict.”

The monster perceived my resolve in my face and ground his teeth in helpless rage. “Must every man,” he exclaimed, “find a wife to cherish, and every beast its mate, while I remain alone? I once felt affection, and for that I was met with loathing and contempt. Man, you may hate me, but take care! Your days shall be passed in terror and misery, and soon the blow will come that must forever tear your happiness from you. Are you to be blessed while I crawl in the depth of my wretchedness? You may destroy all my other passions, but revenge remains—revenge, from this moment dearer to me than light or sustenance! I may die, but first you, my tyrant and persecutor, shall curse the sun that shines upon your misery. Beware, for I am fearless and therefore strong. I will keep watch with the craft of a serpent, that I may strike with its poison. Man, you shall rue the injuries you have done me.”

Henry rejoiced in my gaiety, and sincerely sympathised in my feelings: he exerted himself to amuse me, while he expressed the sensations that filled his soul. The resources of his mind on this occasion were truly astonishing: his conversation was full of imagination; and very often, in imitation of the Persian and Arabic writers, he invented tales of wonderful fancy and passion. At other times he repeated my favourite poems, or drew me out into arguments, which he supported with great ingenuity.

Julian delighted in my lightheartedness and mirrored my every emotion with genuine warmth; he labored constantly to divert me, even as he voiced the grand sensations that stirred his own soul. The vastness of his intellect in those hours was nothing short of marvelous; his speech sparkled with invention, and frequently, in the manner of Eastern storytellers, he composed fables of extraordinary passion and magic. At other times, he recited the verses I most cherished or enticed me into spirited debates, which he defended with remarkable brilliance.

He awoke next morning from rosy scenes of dream to a steamy atmosphere that smelled of soapsuds and dirty clothes, and that was vibrant with the jar and jangle of tormented life. As he came out of his room he heard the slosh of water, a sharp exclamation, and a resounding smack as his sister visited her irritation upon one of her numerous progeny. The squall of the child went through him like a knife. He was aware that the whole thing, the very air he breathed, was repulsive and mean. How different, he thought, from the atmosphere of beauty and repose of the house wherein Ruth dwelt. There it was all spiritual. Here it was all material, and meanly material.

Julian emerged from the amber remnants of sleep into a stifling mist that reeked of lye and sodden wool, a space vibrating with the discordant clamor of a desperate existence. Crossing the threshold, he encountered the rhythmic splashing of water and a sudden, sharp rebuke, punctuated by the heavy thud of a palm against skin as his sister discharged her resentment upon a whimpering child. The boy’s subsequent shriek lanced through him like a jagged blade. He felt a profound revulsion for the entire spectacle; the very air he inhaled seemed thick with a base and grinding misery. His mind drifted toward the serene elegance of the manor where Beatrice lived. There, life was an affair of the spirit, whereas here, it was a wretchedly corporeal struggle, devoid of any higher grace.

He was rugged and bearded and wind-dried. He had just come off the “trail,” he said, at one of the North River ferries. I fancied I could see the snow dust of Chilcoot yet powdering his shoulders. And then he strewed the table with the nuggets, stuffed ptarmigans, bead work and seal pelts of the returned Klondiker, and began to prate to us of his millions.

He was rough-cut, full-bearded, and looked as if constant wind had scoured every last trace of softness from his skin. He told us he’d just come off the long northern trail, stepping ashore only moments before at one of the North River ferry docks. I could have sworn fine, powdery snow from Chilcoot Pass still flecked the slope of his shoulders. Next, he spread the classic haul of a returning Klondike prospector all across the table: glinting gold nuggets, taxidermied ptarmigans, delicate hand-beaded craftwork, and plush seal pelts, before launching into a giddy, boastful rant about the multi-million dollar fortune he’d staked up in the Yukon.

The air was as an eye suddenly struck blind. The waggon and its load rolled no longer on the horizontal division between clearness and opacity, but were imbedded in an elastic body of a monotonous pallor throughout. There was no perceptible motion in the air, not a visible drop of water fell upon a leaf of the beeches, birches, and firs composing the wood on either side. The trees stood in an attitude of intentness, as if they waited longingly for a wind to come and rock them. A startling quiet overhung all surrounding things—so completely, that the crunching of the waggon-wheels was as a great noise, and small rustles, which had never obtained a hearing except by night, were distinctly individualized.

The air felt like an eye struck blind mid-blink. The heavy wagon and its crated cargo no longer balanced on the thin line between clear air and mist, but was swallowed whole by a springy, uniform haze that stretched pale and unbroken in every direction. Not a breath of wind stirred the stillness, not a single glistening drop of moisture fell onto the leaves of the firs, birches, and beeches that made up the dense wood crowding both sides of the track. The trees held themselves rigid with a quiet intensity, as if they had been waiting for hours, hungry for a gust of wind to sway their heavy boughs. A jarring, thick silence draped over all the surrounding landscape—so complete that the crunch of the wagon’s wheels over packed earth sounded like a great roar, and faint rustles that would never have caught a listener’s ear except in the deepest dark of night stood out sharp and distinctly individual.

You have seen that kind of people who will never let on that they don't know the meaning of a new big word. The more ignorant they are, the more pitifully certain they are to pretend you haven't shot over their heads. The queen was just one of that sort, and was always making the stupidest blunders by reason of it. She hesitated a moment; then her face brightened up with sudden comprehension, and she said she would do it for me.

You’ve likely met the sort of person who would rather die than admit a complex word is over their head. In fact, the less they truly understand, the more pathetically determined they are to pretend your vocabulary hasn't left them in the dust. The queen was exactly like that, and her pride frequently resulted in the most idiotic mistakes. After a brief pause, her expression cleared as if she’d finally caught on, and she promised to handle the matter for me.

At first, he was pleased with the discovery: hoping that it might be the forerunner of his release; but such thoughts were quickly dispelled, on his sitting down to breakfast along with the Jew, who told him, in a tone and manner which increased his alarm, that he was to be taken to the residence of Bill Sikes that night.

Troy slept on in the porch, and the rain increased outside. Presently the gurgoyle spat. In due time a small stream began to trickle through the seventy feet of aerial space between its mouth and the ground, which the water-drops smote like duckshot in their accelerated velocity. The stream thickened in substance, and increased in power, gradually spouting further and yet further from the side of the tower. When the rain fell in a steady and ceaseless torrent the stream dashed downward in volumes.

Troy lay asleep on the porch as the rain beyond it swelled. After a while, the gargoyle gave its first spurt. Soon a slender rivulet began to thread the seventy feet of open air from its mouth to the earth, the drops striking the ground with the swift, scattered force of birdshot. The rivulet fattened and gathered strength, its arc reaching farther and farther from the tower’s flank. When the rain settled into a steady, unbroken deluge, the stream plunged down in thick, churning sheets.

“That’s the nature of the trade,” returned Silas; “and the household shares the same bent. You’ve struck the very core of the matter, and no bolt-hole in the city could hold you tighter. Not a corner in all this warren breathes more secure than the Lame Fox—when I choose to weave it so. And I’ve grown partial to you and the girl; so the word’s been given, and you may set your hearts at rest.”

“Yes, it was so; it is useless to excuse me! My conduct to her was too hideous—I made no advances; and she could not bring herself to forgive me. Now she is dead! If I had only shown myself willing to make it up with her sooner, and we had been friends, and then she had died, it wouldn’t be so hard to bear. But I never went near her house, so she never came near mine, and didn’t know how welcome she would have been—that’s what troubles me. She did not know I was going to her house that very night, for she was too insensible to understand me. If she had only come to see me! I longed that she would. But it was not to be.”

Indeed, it was so; to offer excuses now would be a hollow labor. My treatment of Catherine was deplorable—I maintained a stony silence, and she found it impossible to grant me grace. Now she has passed beyond the veil. If I had only signaled my desire for peace a little sooner, and we had reconciled before her departure, this sorrow would not weigh so heavily. But I shunned her door, and she avoided mine, never suspecting how gladly I would have received her—that is the thought that haunts me. She died without knowing I was bound for her cottage that very evening, for her spirit was already too far gone to hear my confession. If only she had stepped across my threshold! I yearned for it daily. But it was never meant to happen.

The struggle was resumed. And soon it became clear that the first goal was no earnest of the quality of the struggle. Teddy and Cecily formed a terribly efficient combination. Against their brilliant rushes, supported in a vehement but effective manner by the Indian to their right and guided by loud shoutings from Mr. Britling (centre), Mr. Direck and the side-car lady and Mr. Raeburn struggled in vain. One swift advance was only checked by the dust cloak, its folds held the ball until help arrived; another was countered by a tremendous swipe of Mr. Raeburn's that sent the ball within an inch of the youngest Britling's head and right across the field; the third resulted in a swift pass from Cecily to the elder Britling son away on her right, and he shot the goal neatly and swiftly through the lattice of Mr. Lawrence Carmine's defensive movements. And after that very rapidly came another goal for Mr. Britling's side and then another.

The match resumed, and it quickly became apparent that the opening goal had been no indicator of the true intensity of the contest. Teddy and Cecily proved to be a formidable duo. Their impressive charges—bolstered by the aggressive yet skillful play of the Indian man on their right and directed by Mr. Britling’s boisterous shouting from the center—left Mr. Direck, the woman from the side-car, and Mr. Raeburn struggling to keep up. One rapid advance was only halted by the dust cloak, its heavy folds trapping the ball until help could arrive; another was repelled by a massive strike from Mr. Raeburn that sent the ball whistling inches past the youngest Britling’s head to the far side of the pitch. A third attempt saw Cecily flick a quick pass to the elder Britling son on her right, who deftly threaded the ball through the gaps in Mr. Lawrence Carmine’s defense to score. Following that, two more goals were recorded for Mr. Britling’s side in rapid succession.

His judges sat upon the Bench in feathered hats; but the rough red cap and tricoloured cockade was the head-dress otherwise prevailing. Looking at the Jury and the turbulent audience, he might have thought that the usual order of things was reversed, and that the felons were trying the honest men. The lowest, cruelest, and worst populace of a city, never without its quantity of low, cruel, and bad, were the directing spirits of the scene: noisily commenting, applauding, disapproving, anticipating, and precipitating the result, without a check. Of the men, the greater part were armed in various ways; of the women, some wore knives, some daggers, some ate and drank as they looked on, many knitted. Among these last, was one, with a spare piece of knitting under her arm as she worked. She was in a front row, by the side of a man whom he had never seen since his arrival at the Barrier, but whom he directly remembered as Defarge. He noticed that she once or twice whispered in his ear, and that she seemed to be his wife; but, what he most noticed in the two figures was, that although they were posted as close to himself as they could be, they never looked towards him. They seemed to be waiting for something with a dogged determination, and they looked at the Jury, but at nothing else. Under the President sat Doctor Manette, in his usual quiet dress. As well as the prisoner could see, he and Mr. Lorry were the only men there, unconnected with the Tribunal, who wore their usual clothes, and had not assumed the coarse garb of the Carmagnole.

The judges on the Bench wore feathered hats, but the rough red cap with its tricolour cockade was the more common headgear. Gazing at the jury and the restless crowd, he might have imagined the natural order overturned, as though criminals were sitting in judgment on honest men. The basest, most pitiless, and worst elements of a city—never lacking in such—steered the proceedings: loudly weighing in, cheering, condemning, leaping ahead, and forcing the outcome without restraint. Most of the men were armed in some fashion; among the women, some carried knives, others daggers, some ate and drank while watching, and many knitted. One of these knitters had a spare piece of work tucked under her arm as her needles moved. She sat in a front row, beside a man he had not seen since reaching the Barrier, but whom he instantly recognized as Defarge. He noticed she whispered in his ear once or twice and appeared to be his wife; yet what struck him most about the pair was that, though they stood as near to him as possible, they never once glanced his way. They seemed to be waiting, with a stubborn fixedness, for something, and they looked only at the jury. Below the President sat Doctor Manette, in his customary quiet attire. As far as the prisoner could tell, he and Mr. Lorry were the only men there, apart from the Tribunal, who still wore their ordinary clothes and had not adopted the rough garb of the Carmagnole.

"Well," said Mr. Direck, "the point that strikes me most about all this is that that barn isn't a barn any longer, and that this farmyard isn't a farmyard. There isn't any wheat or chaff or anything of that sort in the barn, and there never will be again: there's just a pianola and a dancing floor, and if a cow came into this farmyard everybody in the place would be shooing it out again. They'd regard it as a most unnatural object."

To my mind," Mr. Sterling observed, "the most remarkable thing is that this outbuilding has ceased to be an outbuilding, just as the courtyard has abandoned its rustic vocation. There is no longer a trace of grain or husk to be found inside, nor will there ever be again; it contains only a player-piano and a floor for dancing. If a heifer were to wander into this square, the inhabitants would fall over themselves to drive it away. They would view the creature as a most grotesque and alien sight.

Of all the terrific yells that ever fell on mortal ears, none could exceed the cry of the infuriated throng. Some shouted to those who were nearest to set the house on fire; others roared to the officers to shoot him dead. Among them all, none showed such fury as the man on horseback, who, throwing himself out of the saddle, and bursting through the crowd as if he were parting water, cried, beneath the window, in a voice that rose above all others, “Twenty guineas to the man who brings a ladder!”

Of all the dreadful cries that ever struck mortal ears, none could surpass the shout of the enraged mob. Some called on those nearest at hand to set fire to the house; others bellowed to the officers to shoot him dead. Yet none among them displayed such savage fury as the man on horseback, who flung himself from the saddle and forced his way through the crowd as though parting water, crying beneath the window, in a voice louder than all the rest, “Twenty guineas to the man who brings a ladder!”

Although the Doctor’s daughter had known nothing of the country of her birth, she appeared to have innately derived from it that ability to make much of little means, which is one of its most useful and most agreeable characteristics. Simple as the furniture was, it was set off by so many little adornments, of no value but for their taste and fancy, that its effect was delightful. The disposition of everything in the rooms, from the largest object to the least; the arrangement of colours, the elegant variety and contrast obtained by thrift in trifles, by delicate hands, clear eyes, and good sense; were at once so pleasant in themselves, and so expressive of their originator, that, as Mr. Lorry stood looking about him, the very chairs and tables seemed to ask him, with something of that peculiar expression which he knew so well by this time, whether he approved?

Though the surgeon’s daughter had never once set foot in the land where she’d been born, she seemed to have inherited instinctively its signature knack for weaving plenty out of scarce resources, a trait as practical as it was endearing to anyone who witnessed it. Modest as the household furnishings were, they were elevated by dozens of tiny, hand-fashioned touches, worthless in coin but brimming with thoughtful taste and quiet whimsy, so that the entire space felt uncommonly welcoming and charming. The placement of every item in the chambers, from the heaviest oak chest to the smallest glass trinket; the pairing of soft, muted hues, the gentle variety and clever contrast achieved by making the most of trivial odds and ends, by deft fingers, sharp kind eyes, and sound practical judgment; were all so innately cheering on their own, and so clearly a reflection of the woman who’d arranged them, that as Mr. Thorne stood surveying the space, even the plain wooden chairs and scrubbed table seemed to tilt toward him with that familiar, quiet half-smile he’d come to know so well, as if curious if he found their arrangement to his liking.

“Do anybody know of a crooked man, or a lame, or any second-hand fellow at all that would do for poor me?” said Maryann. “A perfect one I don’t expect to get at my time of life. If I could hear of such a thing ’twould do me more good than toast and ale.”

“Does anyone round here know of a stooped man, or a gimpy-legged one, any secondhand sort of fellow at all that’d suit poor old me?” said Elspeth. “I’ve no hope of landing a perfect one at my time of life. If I heard tell of such a soul, it’d do me more good than hot buttered crumpets and cold hard cider.”

Eustacia awoke. The cracking had been that of the window shutter downstairs, which the maid-servant was opening to let in the day, now slowly increasing to Nature’s meagre allowance at this sickly time of the year. “O that I had seen his face!” she said again. “’Twas meant for Mr. Yeobright!”

Isolde started from her sleep. The sudden snap was the heavy blind in the hall below, which the servant-girl was unlatching to invite the morning—a gray light now doled out with the parsimony of the heavens in this bleak stretch of winter. “Would that I had beheld his countenance!” she sighed again. “The vision was meant for Mr. Sterling!”

Samway, Tall, and Smallbury agreed to go to Warren’s, and went out at the gate, the remaining ones entering the house. The three soon drew near the malt-house, approaching it from the adjoining orchard, and not by way of the street. The pane of glass was illuminated as usual. Smallbury was a little in advance of the rest when, pausing, he turned suddenly to his companions and said, “Hist! See there.”

Samway, Tall, and Smallbury resolved to head over to Warren’s place, slipping out through the gate while the others retreated indoors. The trio soon neared the malt-house, cutting across the bordering orchard instead of following the road. As always, the windowpane glowed with light. Smallbury, walking slightly ahead, halted abruptly and wheeled toward his friends, whispering, “Quiet—look at that.”

When Mr. Direck and Mr. Britling returned to the Dower House the guest was handed over to Mrs. Britling and Mr. Britling vanished, to reappear at supper time, for the Britlings had a supper in the evening instead of dinner. When Mr. Britling did reappear every trace of his vexation with the levities of British politics and the British ruling class had vanished altogether, and he was no longer thinking of all that might be happening in Germany or India....

When Mr. Direck and Mr. Britling got back to the Dower House, the visitor was entrusted to Mrs. Britling, while Mr. Britling disappeared, only to surface again at supper—since the Britlings took their evening meal as supper rather than dinner. By the time Mr. Britling surfaced once more, every sign of his irritation with the frivolities of British politics and the governing class had completely dissolved, and his mind had drifted far from whatever might be unfolding in Germany or India.

Kraft was the first to act. He leaped up and pounded the Klondiker’s back. “Come out and drink,” he shouted. “Drink first and eat afterward.” Judkins seized one arm and I the other. Gaily, roaringly, irresistibly, in jolly-good-fellow style, we dragged him from the restaurant to a café, stuffing his pockets with his embalmed birds and indigestible nuggets.

Kraft was the first to make a move. He shot up from his seat and thumped the Klondiker squarely on the back. “Come on out with us for a drink,” he yelled. “Drinks first, we can get something to eat later.” Judkins latched onto one of his arms, and I snagged the other. Loud, giddy, completely impossible to push off, leaning hard into that silly, warm good-pal energy, we hauled him out of the restaurant and toward a nearby café, shoving his preserved bird specimens and those totally indigestible gold nuggets into his pockets as we went.

The boy nestled himself upon my shoulder and pretended to go to sleep. The old man began his tale; and presently the lad was asleep in reality; so also were the dogs, and the court, the lackeys, and the files of men-at-arms. The droning voice droned on; a soft snoring arose on all sides and supported it like a deep and subdued accompaniment of wind instruments. Some heads were bowed upon folded arms, some lay back with open mouths that issued unconscious music; the flies buzzed and bit, unmolested, the rats swarmed softly out from a hundred holes, and pattered about, and made themselves at home everywhere; and one of them sat up like a squirrel on the king's head and held a bit of cheese in its hands and nibbled it, and dribbled the crumbs in the king's face with naive and impudent irreverence. It was a tranquil scene, and restful to the weary eye and the jaded spirit.

The scrawny urchin curled into the crook of my shoulder, feigning drowsiness as the gray-haired storyteller cleared his throat to begin his far-fetched saga. Within minutes the boy had drifted off for real, as had the pair of shaggy hunting hounds at our feet, the clustered courtiers, the harried footmen, and the rigid ranks of royal guards lining the hall. The storyteller’s low, rumbling drawl droned unbroken, and soft snores rose from every corner to underscore it, deep and muted as the thrum of distant organ pipes. Some attendees had bowed their heads onto their crossed forearms, others lolled back with slack jaws spilling unselfconscious, rumbling breaths; flies buzzed and nipped at exposed skin entirely unhindered, plump grain rats slipped out of dozens of wall cracks to patter across the stone floors, making themselves comfortable on benches, sills, and even the raised dais. One bold little rat perched upright like a chipmunk atop the dozing sovereign’s crown, clutching a crumb of aged cheddar in its tiny paws to nibble, letting crumbs tumble down onto the ruler’s cheek with a guileless, cheeky disregard for all royal decorum. It was a perfectly peaceful scene, soft and soothing to fatigued eyes and frayed, overtaxed souls.

His visor was up; there was death in his smile. I should never be able to dodge his sword, that was plain. Somebody was going to die this time. If he got the drop on me, I could name the corpse. We rode forward together, and saluted the royalties. This time the king was disturbed. He said:

“He told′ her that she′ was the joy′ of his life′, And if′ she’d con-sent′ he would make her his wife′; She could′ not refuse′ him; to church′ so they went′, Young Will was forgot′, and young Sue′ was content′; And then′ was she kiss’d′ and set down′ on his knee′, No man′ in the world′ was so lov′-ing as he′!”

Why did a woman of this sort live on Egdon Heath? Budmouth was her native place, a fashionable seaside resort at that date. She was the daughter of the bandmaster of a regiment which had been quartered there—a Corfiote by birth, and a fine musician—who met his future wife during her trip thither with her father the captain, a man of good family. The marriage was scarcely in accord with the old man’s wishes, for the bandmaster’s pockets were as light as his occupation. But the musician did his best; adopted his wife’s name, made England permanently his home, took great trouble with his child’s education, the expenses of which were defrayed by the grandfather, and throve as the chief local musician till her mother’s death, when he left off thriving, drank, and died also. The girl was left to the care of her grandfather, who, since three of his ribs became broken in a shipwreck, had lived in this airy perch on Egdon, a spot which had taken his fancy because the house was to be had for next to nothing, and because a remote blue tinge on the horizon between the hills, visible from the cottage door, was traditionally believed to be the English Channel. She hated the change; she felt like one banished; but here she was forced to abide.

What had brought a woman of her temperament to settle on wind-scoured Bramble Moor? Brineport was her birthplace, a gilded coastal holiday spot wildly popular with fashionable visitors at the time. Her father had been the bandmaster of an infantry regiment posted to the town, a Sardinian by birth and a remarkably gifted musician, who first met his future wife when the young woman traveled there on holiday with her own father, a decorated naval captain from a well-respected gentry line. The match hardly aligned with the old man’s wishes, for the bandmaster’s pockets were as empty as his profession was deemed unfit for a gentleman’s daughter. But the musician did everything in his power to prove his worth: he adopted his wife’s family name, made England his permanent home, poured endless care into his daughter’s education (expenses the grandfather covered in full), and thrived as the town’s most in-demand local musician, until his wife’s sudden death broke his spirit. He abandoned his work, turned to drink, and passed away not long after. The girl was left to her grandfather’s care, and three broken ribs sustained in a violent shipwreck had led the old seaman to retire to this airy, elevated cottage on Bramble Moor. He’d settled on the spot because the house could be rented for next to nothing, and the faint blue smudge straddling the horizon between the distant hills, visible straight from the cottage door, was long rumored to be the distant stretch of the English Channel. She hated the shift, felt as if she’d been cast into exile from every part of the world she knew, but she had no choice but to abide there.

“Ah, yes, it is very well to see things in that business point of view,” murmured Eustacia with a smothered fire of feeling. “But why should you think there is anything between me and Mr. Wildeve? I have a spirit as well as you. I am indignant; and so would any woman be. It was a condescension in me to be Clym’s wife, and not a manœuvre, let me remind you; and therefore I will not be treated as a schemer whom it becomes necessary to bear with because she has crept into the family.”

“Ah, yes, there is much wisdom, no doubt, in viewing all matters from the cold standpoint of advantage,” said Lenora, with a fire of feeling kept down beneath her words. “But what reason have you for supposing there is aught between Mr. Vale and me? I, too, have a spirit, and no less than you. I am affronted; and any woman in my place would be. It was a gracious surrender on my part to become Adrian’s wife, and not a stratagem, let me remind you; and therefore I will not submit to being regarded as a plotter, merely tolerated because she has slipped herself into the household.”

“Gabriel,” she said, automatically, when he entered, turning up a face of which only the well-known lines remained to tell him it was hers, all else in the picture having faded quite. “Ride to Casterbridge instantly for a surgeon. It is, I believe, useless, but go. Mr. Boldwood has shot my husband.”

“Elias,” she murmured mechanically as he crossed the threshold, tilting up a visage so hollowed that only its familiar architecture remained to mark her identity, all other color having vanished from the portrait. “Ride to Blackwood this instant for a physician. It is, I fear, a futile errand, but go. Mr. Sterling has shot my husband.”

Sometimes her curious roomers would ask her what the doctor said to her.

On the first landing she met him returning with the astronomer cradled in his arms. He paused and set free the practised scalpel of his tongue, though not in any loud fashion. Little by little, Mrs. Vale collapsed like a starched garment slipping from a hook. Long afterward, folds remained in her mind and in her body. Now and then her inquisitive lodgers would ask what the doctor had said to her.

“A coronet with five points, and beneath, _Cedit amor rebus_—‘Love yields to circumstance.’ It’s the motto of the Earls of Severn. That watch belonged to the last lord, and was given to my mother’s husband, a medical man, for his use till I came of age, when it was to be given to me. It was all the fortune that ever I inherited. That watch has regulated imperial interests in its time—the stately ceremonial, the courtly assignation, pompous travels, and lordly sleeps. Now it is yours.”

Carved with a five-pointed coronet, it bears the inscription beneath *Cedit amor rebus*—“Love yields to circumstance.” That is the ancestral motto of the Earls of Severn. This watch belonged to the last holder of that title, and was entrusted to my mother’s husband, a physician, to use as his own until I came of age, at which point it was to be passed down to me. It was the entire inheritance I ever received. In its day, this watch kept time for affairs of imperial weight: grand state ceremonies, quiet court rendezvous, opulent official travels, and the unrushed slumbers of the peerage. Now it is yours.

Day by day he worked on, and day by day the postman delivered to him rejected manuscripts. He had no money for stamps, so the manuscripts accumulated in a heap under the table. Came a day when for forty hours he had not tasted food. He could not hope for a meal at Ruth’s, for she was away to San Rafael on a two weeks’ visit; and for very shame’s sake he could not go to his sister’s. To cap misfortune, the postman, in his afternoon round, brought him five returned manuscripts. Then it was that Martin wore his overcoat down into Oakland, and came back without it, but with five dollars tinkling in his pocket. He paid a dollar each on account to the four tradesmen, and in his kitchen fried steak and onions, made coffee, and stewed a large pot of prunes. And having dined, he sat down at his table-desk and completed before midnight an essay which he entitled “The Dignity of Usury.” Having typed it out, he flung it under the table, for there had been nothing left from the five dollars with which to buy stamps.

Day after day he kept at his labor, and day after day the mail carrier brought him back his manuscripts, each one refused. He had not a cent for postage, so the rejected pages piled up in a drift beneath the table. Then came a day when he had gone forty hours without food. He could not look for a meal at Clara’s, for she had gone to San Rafael for a fortnight’s stay; and, out of sheer shame, he could not bring himself to seek out his sister. As if to sharpen his bad luck, the mailman, on his afternoon round, delivered five more returned manuscripts. It was then that Adrian put on his overcoat, walked it down into Alameda, and returned without it, though with five dollars singing in his pocket. He paid a dollar apiece on account to the four merchants, and in his kitchen he fried a steak with onions, brewed coffee, and set a big pot of prunes to simmering. Then, having eaten, he went to his table-desk and, before midnight, finished an essay he called “The Majesty of Interest.” When he had typed it, he cast it beneath the table, for not a penny of the five dollars remained to buy stamps.

Mr. Britling pleaded for particulars of the late Lord Chancellor's treasons. There were no particulars. It was just an idea the good lady had got into her head, that had got into a number of accessible heads. There was only one strong man in all the country now, Lady Frensham insisted. That was Sir Edward Carson.

Mr. Britling pressed for concrete details about the late Lord Chancellor’s supposed acts of treason. No such specifics existed at all. The whole story was nothing more than a notion the well-meaning lady had latched onto, an idea that had worked its way into the minds of a great many easily influenced people. Lady Frensham insisted there was only one strong, steadfast man left in the entire country right now, and that man was Sir Edward Carson.

Everywhere there were the flags of the Allies; in shop windows, over doors, on the bonnets of automobiles, on people's breasts, and there was a great quantity of recruiting posters on the hoardings and in windows: "Your King and Country Need You" was the chief text, and they still called for "A Hundred Thousand Men" although the demand of Lord Kitchener had risen to half a million. There were also placards calling for men on nearly all the taxicabs. The big windows of the offices of the Norddeutscher Lloyd in Cockspur Street were boarded up, and plastered thickly with recruiting appeals.

Everywhere the emblems of the Allies fluttered—draped in shop windows, pinned above doorways, fixed to the bonnets of motorcars, worn upon lapels—and the hoardings and glass fronts were thick with recruiting posters. “Your King and Country Need You” ran the dominant refrain, and the placards still cried for “A Hundred Thousand Men,” though Lord Kitchener’s summons had already swelled to half a million. Nearly every taxicab carried a call for volunteers. The great windows of the Norddeutscher Lloyd offices in Cockspur Street stood shuttered and plastered over with layer upon layer of urgent recruiting appeals.

Now ensued one of the dullest quarter-hours I had ever endured. We waited in a silent solitude enclosed by our circles of wire, and by a circle of heavy smoke outside of these. We couldn't see over the wall of smoke, and we couldn't see through it. But at last it began to shred away lazily, and by the end of another quarter-hour the land was clear and our curiosity was enabled to satisfy itself. No living creature was in sight! We now perceived that additions had been made to our defenses. The dynamite had dug a ditch more than a hundred feet wide, all around us, and cast up an embankment some twenty-five feet high on both borders of it. As to destruction of life, it was amazing. Moreover, it was beyond estimate. Of course, we could not _count_ the dead, because they did not exist as individuals, but merely as homogeneous protoplasm, with alloys of iron and buttons.

There followed one of the dreariest quarter-hours I have ever been forced to endure. We waited in a lonely stillness, shut in by our rings of wire and by a farther ring of dense smoke. We could not look beyond that wall of smoke, nor could we pierce it with our eyes. But at last it began to tear itself apart, drifting off sluggishly; and by the end of another quarter-hour the ground was once more visible, and our curiosity was at last satisfied. Not a living thing was to be seen! We now discovered that our defenses had been strengthened. The dynamite had gouged out a trench more than a hundred feet wide all around us, and thrown up on either side of it an embankment some twenty-five feet high. As for the destruction of life, it was astonishing. Indeed, it was beyond reckoning. Naturally, we could not count the dead, since they no longer existed as separate beings, but only as indistinguishable protoplasm, mixed with bits of iron and buttons.

The shining Bull’s Eye of the Court was gone, or it would have been the mark for a hurricane of national bullets. It had never been a good eye to see with--had long had the mote in it of Lucifer’s pride, Sardanapalus’s luxury, and a mole’s blindness--but it had dropped out and was gone. The Court, from that exclusive inner circle to its outermost rotten ring of intrigue, corruption, and dissimulation, was all gone together. Royalty was gone; had been besieged in its Palace and “suspended,” when the last tidings came over.

The gleaming Bull’s Eye of the Court had vanished; otherwise it would have drawn a storm of the nation’s gunfire. It had never served well for sight—long clouded by the mote of Lucifer’s pride, Sardanapalus’s excess, and a mole’s blindness—but now it had fallen away and was no more. The Court, from its innermost sanctum to its farthest decayed rim of scheming, venality, and deceit, had collapsed as one. Royalty itself had disappeared; besieged within its Palace and “suspended,” according to the latest reports that had arrived.

Among these heavy yeomen a feminine figure glided, the single one of her sex that the room contained. She was prettily and even daintily dressed. She moved between them as a chaise between carts, was heard after them as a romance after sermons, was felt among them like a breeze among furnaces. It had required a little determination—far more than she had at first imagined—to take up a position here, for at her first entry the lumbering dialogues had ceased, nearly every face had been turned towards her, and those that were already turned rigidly fixed there.

Amid these burly countrymen there drifted a womanly presence, the sole one of her kind that the chamber held. She was dressed with a neatness that was almost delicate. She passed among them as a light carriage among drays, was heard after them as a ballad after a homily, was felt in their midst like a fresh wind among furnaces. It had demanded a little resolution—much more, indeed, than she had at first supposed—to establish herself in such a place, for when she first entered the heavy talk had fallen silent, almost every countenance had swung toward her, and those already turned remained fixed there with a kind of stern attention.

Editors wrote to him telling him to name his own terms, which he did, but it was always for work performed. He refused resolutely to pledge himself to any new thing. The thought of again setting pen to paper maddened him. He had seen Brissenden torn to pieces by the crowd, and despite the fact that him the crowd acclaimed, he could not get over the shock nor gather any respect for the crowd. His very popularity seemed a disgrace and a treason to Brissenden. It made him wince, but he made up his mind to go on and fill the money-bag.

Publishers dispatched letters urging him to dictate his own price, and he obliged, though always for labor already delivered. He would not bind himself to any fresh undertaking. The mere notion of once more lowering nib to page sent a fury through his veins. He had watched the multitude rip Carrington limb from limb, and even though that same multitude now lionized him, he could neither shake the horror nor muster a shred of reverence for them. Very well, I will give you your answer. I am distressed because I know a scoundrel like that carries corruption in every fiber of his being, ready to taint anyone who associates with him. That is why.”

They possessed a delightful house (for such it was in my eyes) and every luxury; they had a fire to warm them when chill and delicious viands when hungry; they were dressed in excellent clothes; and, still more, they enjoyed one another’s company and speech, interchanging each day looks of affection and kindness. I was at first unable to solve these questions, but perpetual attention and time explained to me many appearances which were at first enigmatic.

"There's no end of such little incidents," said Mr. Britling, cutting in without apparent effort. "You see, we get it on both sides. Our manufacturer class was, of course, originally an insurgent class. It was a class of distended craftsmen. It had the craftsman's natural enterprise and natural radicalism. As soon as it prospered and sent its boys to Oxford it was lost. Our manufacturing class was assimilated in no time to the conservative classes, whose education has always had a mandarin quality--very, very little of it, and very cold and choice. In America you have so far had no real conservative class at all. Fortunate continent! You cast out your Tories, and you were left with nothing but Whigs and Radicals. But our peculiar bad luck has been to get a sort of revolutionary who is a Tory mandarin too. Ruskin and Morris, for example, were as reactionary and anti-scientific as the dukes and the bishops. Machine haters. Science haters. Rule of Thumbites to the bone. So are our current Socialists. They've filled this country with the idea that the ideal automobile ought to be made entirely by the hand labour of traditional craftsmen, quite individually, out of beaten copper, wrought iron and seasoned oak. All this electric-starter business and this electric lighting outfit I have here, is perfectly hateful to the English mind.... It isn't that we are simply backward in these things, we are antagonistic. The British mind has never really tolerated electricity; at least, not that sort of electricity that runs through wires. Too slippery and glib for it. Associates it with Italians and fluency generally, with Volta, Galvani, Marconi and so on. The proper British electricity is that high-grade useless long-sparking stuff you get by turning round a glass machine; stuff we used to call frictional electricity. Keep it in Leyden jars.... At Claverings here they still refuse to have electric bells. There was a row when the Solomonsons, who were tenants here for a time, tried to put them in...."

These sorts of episodes are endless," Mr. Britling interjected with effortless ease. "You see, we suffer from both ends of the spectrum. Our industrialist class began as a group of rebels—essentially expanded artisans. They once possessed the innate drive and progressive spirit of the skilled worker. However, as soon as they gained wealth and enrolled their sons at Oxford, that spirit vanished. The manufacturing elite was quickly absorbed into the old aristocracy, whose schooling has always been rather elitist and detached—refined, sparse, and intentionally aloof. In America, you’ve managed to avoid a genuine conservative caste thus far. A lucky continent! By expelling your Loyalists, you were left with nothing but liberals and reformers. 

Our specific misfortune, however, is a breed of radical who is also a traditionalist snob. Figures like Ruskin and Morris, for instance, were just as regressive and hostile to science as any bishop or nobleman. They loathed machinery and despised scientific rigor, clinging to outdated intuition. Modern Socialists follow suit. They’ve convinced the public that a perfect car should be hand-forged by lone artisans using hammered copper, forged iron, and aged wood. This modern convenience of electric ignitions and lighting systems is utterly loathsome to the British psyche. We aren't merely behind the times; we are actively hostile. The English temperament has never truly accepted electricity—at least not the kind that flows through circuits. It feels too slick, too foreign. It brings to mind Italian cleverness—Volta, Galvani, Marconi, and the like. To us, respectable electricity is that dignified, impractical variety that produces long sparks from a hand-cranked glass wheel—the old frictional sort kept safely in Leyden jars. At the Claverings estate, they still won't allow electric doorbells. There was quite a scandal when the Solomonsons, during their tenancy, attempted to install them.

I watched my fifty-two boys narrowly; watched their faces, their walk, their unconscious attitudes: for all these are a language --a language given us purposely that it may betray us in times of emergency, when we have secrets which we want to keep. I knew that that thought would keep saying itself over and over again in their minds and hearts, _All England is marching against us!_ and ever more strenuously imploring attention with each repetition, ever more sharply realizing itself to their imaginations, until even in their sleep they would find no rest from it, but hear the vague and flitting creatures of the dreams say, _All England_ --ALL ENGLAND!--_is marching against you_! I knew all this would happen; I knew that ultimately the pressure would become so great that it would compel utterance; therefore, I must be ready with an answer at that time--an answer well chosen and tranquilizing.

I watched my fifty-two boys closely, studying their faces, the way they moved, their unconscious postures; for all these things are a kind of language—a language deliberately given to us so that it may betray us in moments of crisis, when we are trying to hide our secrets. I knew that one thought would go on repeating itself in their minds and hearts: All England is marching against us! And with every repetition it would demand attention more urgently, and take sharper shape in their imaginations, until even in sleep they would find no relief from it, but hear the faint, shifting creatures of dreams saying, All England—ALL ENGLAND—is marching against you! I knew that all this would come to pass; I knew that in the end the strain would grow so intense that it would force itself into words. So I had to be ready then with an answer—an answer carefully chosen and meant to calm them.

Matters were serious now. We remained still, and awaited developments. The peasant toiled his difficult way up. There was a wild outbreak of anger below, and the mob swarmed in from all around, and there we were treed, and prisoners. Another man started up; the bridging bough was detected, and a volunteer started up the tree that furnished the bridge. The king ordered me to play Horatius and keep the bridge. For a while the enemy came thick and fast; but no matter, the head man of each procession always got a buffet that dislodged him as soon as he came in reach. The king's spirits rose, his joy was limitless. He said that if nothing occurred to mar the prospect we should have a beautiful night, for on this line of tactics we could hold the tree against the whole country-side.

The situation had grown grave. We stayed quiet, waiting to see what would follow. The peasant struggled through his arduous ascent. The King pulled himself up and stood firm, readying a kick; as soon as the man’s head came within range, a heavy thud sounded, and the climber went sprawling to the earth. A fierce roar of rage rose from below as the mob surged in from every side, leaving us cornered and captive in the branches. Another man began to climb; the connecting limb had been spotted, and a volunteer started up the neighboring tree that served as our bridge. The King ordered me to act the part of Horatius and hold the crossing. For a time, the attackers came in quick succession, but it mattered little; the leader of each attempt received a blow that sent him falling as soon as he was close enough. The King’s spirits soared, his happiness absolute. He declared that if nothing ruined our chances, we were in for a fine night, for with these tactics, we could defend the tree against the entire countryside.

There never was such a country for wandering liars; and they were of both sexes. Hardly a month went by without one of these tramps arriving; and generally loaded with a tale about some princess or other wanting help to get her out of some far-away castle where she was held in captivity by a lawless scoundrel, usually a giant. Now you would think that the first thing the king would do after listening to such a novelette from an entire stranger, would be to ask for credentials--yes, and a pointer or two as to locality of castle, best route to it, and so on. But nobody ever thought of so simple and common-sense a thing at that. No, everybody swallowed these people's lies whole, and never asked a question of any sort or about anything. Well, one day when I was not around, one of these people came along--it was a she one, this time--and told a tale of the usual pattern. Her mistress was a captive in a vast and gloomy castle, along with forty-four other young and beautiful girls, pretty much all of them princesses; they had been languishing in that cruel captivity for twenty-six years; the masters of the castle were three stupendous brothers, each with four arms and one eye--the eye in the center of the forehead, and as big as a fruit. Sort of fruit not mentioned; their usual slovenliness in statistics.

No land was ever so plagued by wandering fabricators, both men and women alike. Scarcely a month passed without some vagrant arriving, usually peddling a story about a princess needing rescue from a distant castle where she was held by a lawless rogue, typically a giant. You would think the king’s first response to such a melodrama from a complete stranger would be to ask for credentials—or at least some directions to the castle and the best route to take. But no one ever thought of anything so practical. Instead, they swallowed these lies whole, never posing a single question. While I was away one day, another of these people appeared—a woman, this time—and spun the usual yarn. Her mistress was a prisoner in a vast, somber fortress along with forty-four other beautiful maidens, nearly all of them princesses; they had supposedly languished in that cruel captivity for twenty-six years. The masters of the castle were three monstrous brothers, each with four arms and a single eye—an eye centered in the forehead as large as a fruit. The specific type of fruit wasn't mentioned; it was their usual sloppiness with the details.

They passed out through the gates of the park with, straggling in the rear, a second gang, the friends that Lizzie’s young man had collected to avenge the loss of his lady. Several constables and special police officers, anticipating trouble, trailed along to prevent it, and herded the two gangs separately aboard the train for San Francisco. Martin told Jimmy that he would get off at Sixteenth Street Station and catch the electric car into Oakland. Lizzie was very quiet and without interest in what was impending. The train pulled in to Sixteenth Street Station, and the waiting electric car could be seen, the conductor of which was impatiently clanging the gong.

I visited Edinburgh with languid eyes and mind; and yet that city might have interested the most unfortunate being. Clerval did not like it so well as Oxford, for the antiquity of the latter city was more pleasing to him. But the beauty and regularity of the new town of Edinburgh, its romantic castle and its environs, the most delightful in the world, Arthur’s Seat, St. Bernard’s Well, and the Pentland Hills, compensated him for the change and filled him with cheerfulness and admiration. But I was impatient to arrive at the termination of my journey.

I wandered through Edinburgh dull-eyed and listless, even though the city held enough charm to captivate even the most miserable soul. Clerval liked it less than Oxford, truth be told—he was far fonder of the deep, ancient character of the latter city. Still, Edinburgh’s sleek, orderly New Town, its atmospheric old castle, and the surrounding landscapes, easily some of the most delightful in the world, from Arthur’s Seat and St. Bernard’s Well to the rolling Pentland Hills, made up for the change of scene, leaving him bright with cheer and quiet admiration. I, however, could barely tamp down my restlessness, eager only to reach the final stop of our journey.

I opened my door and held out my lantern, and just as far as the light would reach I saw a woman; she turned her head when the light sheened on her, and then hurried on downhill. I hung up the lantern, and was curious enough to pull on my things and dog her a few steps, but I could see nothing of her any more. That was where I had been when you came up; and when I saw you I thought you were the same one.”

“Curious, that. Not five minutes past, I was stretched out here dozing, the door pulled to against the damp, when a rustle of skirts over the gorse just beyond roused me—I’m a light sleeper—and with it came the sound of a woman weeping, or catching her breath. I unlatched the door and thrust out my lantern, and at the very edge of its beam I made out a female figure; she glanced back the instant the glow touched her, then hastened away down the slope. I set the lantern on its hook, and being somewhat stirred, I threw on my coat and followed a little way, but she had vanished utterly. That’s where I was when you arrived; and seeing you, I took you for the same soul.”

Yet it was the man who flushed, not the girl.

To many persons this Egdon was a place which had slipped out of its century generations ago, to intrude as an uncouth object into this. It was an obsolete thing, and few cared to study it. How could this be otherwise in the days of square fields, plashed hedges, and meadows watered on a plan so rectangular that on a fine day they looked like silver gridirons? The farmer, in his ride, who could smile at artificial grasses, look with solicitude at the coming corn, and sigh with sadness at the fly-eaten turnips, bestowed upon the distant upland of heath nothing better than a frown. But as for Yeobright, when he looked from the heights on his way he could not help indulging in a barbarous satisfaction at observing that, in some of the attempts at reclamation from the waste, tillage, after holding on for a year or two, had receded again in despair, the ferns and furze-tufts stubbornly reasserting themselves.

To many, this tract of Egdon seemed a relic stranded from a bygone age, thrust incongruously into the present. It was an anachronism, and scarcely anyone cared to examine it. How could it be otherwise in an era of squared fields, trimmed hedgerows, and meadows irrigated with such rigid geometry that on bright days they gleamed like silver grates? The farmer, riding out, who could smile at cultivated grasses, eye the swelling grain with concern, and sigh mournfully over fly-blighted turnips, offered the far upland heath no more than a scowl. Yet Yeobright, gazing down from the ridges along his route, could not suppress a fierce, primitive pleasure at seeing how, in certain patches where men had tried to reclaim the wild, the ploughed ground, after clinging on for a year or two, had faltered and retreated in defeat, the bracken and gorse stubbornly surging back.

“And do you imagine,” the creature asked, “that I was then numb to pain and regret? A ghastly selfishness drove me onward, even while my soul was poisoned by remorse. Do you truly think that Clerval’s cries were like music to my ears? My heart was designed for love and empathy, and when it was twisted by wretchedness into malice and hate, it did not endure that transformation without a level of torture you cannot even conceive.”

He had discovered, in the course of his reading, two schools of fiction. One treated of man as a god, ignoring his earthly origin; the other treated of man as a clod, ignoring his heaven-sent dreams and divine possibilities. Both the god and the clod schools erred, in Martin’s estimation, and erred through too great singleness of sight and purpose. There was a compromise that approximated the truth, though it flattered not the school of god, while it challenged the brute-savageness of the school of clod. It was his story, “Adventure,” which had dragged with Ruth, that Martin believed had achieved his ideal of the true in fiction; and it was in an essay, “God and Clod,” that he had expressed his views on the whole general subject.

Through his reading, he had come to recognize two distinct traditions in fiction. One portrayed man as a god, disregarding his earthbound beginnings; the other cast him as mere dirt, blind to his heaven-sent visions and divine potential. In Martin’s view, both the godly and the earthy traditions failed, each erring through an overly narrow focus and intent. A middle path came closer to the truth—one that offered no flattery to the godly school, yet pushed back against the brute savagery of the earthy school. Martin believed his story “Adventure,” which had bored Ruth so thoroughly, had reached his ideal of truth in fiction, and he had laid out his broader thoughts on the matter in an essay titled “God and Clod.”

“When my hunger was appeased, I directed my steps towards the well-known path that conducted to the cottage. All there was at peace. I crept into my hovel and remained in silent expectation of the accustomed hour when the family arose. That hour passed, the sun mounted high in the heavens, but the cottagers did not appear. I trembled violently, apprehending some dreadful misfortune. The inside of the cottage was dark, and I heard no motion; I cannot describe the agony of this suspense.

Once my hunger had been fully sated, I turned onto the well-worn path leading to the cottage. The whole place lay still and peaceful. I crept into my small hovel, waiting silently for the usual hour when the family would rouse for the day. That hour came and went, the sun climbing high overhead, yet none of the cottagers stepped outside. I trembled violently, seized by dread that some horrible misfortune had occurred. The cottage interior was dark, and not a single sound of movement drifted out to me; there are no words to convey the gut-wrenching agony of that torturous suspense.

The stillness consequent on the cessation of the rumbling and labouring of the coach, added to the stillness of the night, made it very quiet indeed. The panting of the horses communicated a tremulous motion to the coach, as if it were in a state of agitation. The hearts of the passengers beat loud enough perhaps to be heard; but at any rate, the quiet pause was audibly expressive of people out of breath, and holding the breath, and having the pulses quickened by expectation.

Once the coach’s grinding, laborious rumble cut out, the hush that fell layered over the preexisting quiet of the night, wrapping everything in a profound, almost weighty silence. The horses’ heavy, ragged pants sent a faint, trembling shiver through the coach’s frame, as if the vehicle itself was thrumming with suppressed unease. The passengers’ heartbeats might well have been loud enough to carry through the stillness; in any case, the quiet lull hummed with the unmistakeable edge of people half-out of breath, holding their air, pulses thrumming sharp with taut, waiting expectation.

Oh, it makes me sick of mankind to see so many soldiers and so few assassins in the world… after all we have witnessed. If men did their duty with the blade, there would be no such creature as a War Lord. Not one. The Kaiser and his sons and his sons’ sons would know nothing but terror for the rest of their days. Fear would only stop its hunt when the coffin sank into the earth. Fear on the water, fear on the land, for the ship he boarded, the train he rode in; fear while he slept, for the death lurking in his dreams; fear when he woke, for the death hiding in every shadow; fear in every crowd, fear whenever he was alone. Fear would track him through the woods, crouch in the turn of the stair; it would taint every mouthful so strangely that he would long to spit it out.

Eustacia watched his shadowy form till it had disappeared. She placed her hand to her forehead and breathed heavily; and then her rich, romantic lips parted under that homely impulse—a yawn. She was immediately angry at having betrayed even to herself the possible evanescence of her passion for him. She could not admit at once that she might have overestimated Wildeve, for to perceive his mediocrity now was to admit her own great folly heretofore. And the discovery that she was the owner of a disposition so purely that of the dog in the manger had something in it which at first made her ashamed.

Genevieve followed his retreating shadow until it vanished into the night. She rested a hand against her brow and exhaled a long, weighted breath; then, those plush, poetic lips gave way to a vulgar necessity—a yawn. She felt an immediate sting of anger at having signaled, if only to her own soul, the waning of her fire. To confess to Julian’s mediocrity at this late hour was to acknowledge the depth of her own previous delusions. Moreover, the dawning sense that her heart was ruled by the spiteful spirit of the dog in the manger left her, for a moment, profoundly ashamed.

“And I’ll tell you,” he interrupted. “The chief qualification of ninety-nine per cent of all editors is failure. They have failed as writers. Don’t think they prefer the drudgery of the desk and the slavery to their circulation and to the business manager to the joy of writing. They have tried to write, and they have failed. And right there is the cursed paradox of it. Every portal to success in literature is guarded by those watch-dogs, the failures in literature. The editors, sub-editors, associate editors, most of them, and the manuscript-readers for the magazines and book-publishers, most of them, nearly all of them, are men who wanted to write and who have failed. And yet they, of all creatures under the sun the most unfit, are the very creatures who decide what shall and what shall not find its way into print—they, who have proved themselves not original, who have demonstrated that they lack the divine fire, sit in judgment upon originality and genius. And after them come the reviewers, just so many more failures. Don’t tell me that they have not dreamed the dream and attempted to write poetry or fiction; for they have, and they have failed. Why, the average review is more nauseating than cod-liver oil. But you know my opinion on the reviewers and the alleged critics. There are great critics, but they are as rare as comets. If I fail as a writer, I shall have proved for the career of editorship. There’s bread and butter and jam, at any rate.”

Which Farmer-General, carrying an appropriate cane with a golden apple on the top of it, was now among the company in the outer rooms, much prostrated before by mankind--always excepting superior mankind of the blood of Monseigneur, who, his own wife included, looked down upon him with the loftiest contempt.

Still, le Comte de Valroux had gradually come to realize that crass, unseemly misfortunes were seeping into every corner of his dealings, both personal and state-related; and he had, of necessity, struck a partnership with a Tax-Farmer for both spheres of his life. Where public finances were concerned, the comte was entirely incapable of untangling their knotted ledgers, and so had no choice but to contract the work out to someone with the skill to make them function; as for his personal accounts, Tax-Farmers accumulated fortunes faster than any other class in the realm, and the comte, after generations of his line’s unbridled luxury and reckless spending, was sliding rapidly into crippling debt. For this reason, the comte had pulled his younger sister from the abbey while there was still time to spare her the impending nun’s veil—the only garment in the world that would cost him nothing to clothe her in—and offered her hand as a reward to a phenomenally wealthy Tax-Farmer of exceedingly common lineage. This same Tax-Farmer, who carried a polished ebony cane capped with a gilded pomegranate, was now lingering among the crowd in the outer antechambers, bowed and scraped to by nearly everyone in attendance—always excepting those of the comte’s own noble bloodline, who, his new wife included, regarded him with the most unassailable, icy scorn.

As for himself, he loved beauty passionately, and the joy of serving her was to him sufficient wage. And more than beauty he loved Ruth. He considered love the finest thing in the world. It was love that had worked the revolution in him, changing him from an uncouth sailor to a student and an artist; therefore, to him, the finest and greatest of the three, greater than learning and artistry, was love. Already he had discovered that his brain went beyond Ruth’s, just as it went beyond the brains of her brothers, or the brain of her father. In spite of every advantage of university training, and in the face of her bachelorship of arts, his power of intellect overshadowed hers, and his year or so of self-study and equipment gave him a mastery of the affairs of the world and art and life that she could never hope to possess.

He possessed a profound adoration for beauty, finding that the mere privilege of its service was reward enough. But his love for Ruth was deeper still. In his eyes, love was the world's most noble pursuit. It was the catalyst for his own radical transformation, the power that had molded a coarse sailor into a man of letters and art; thus, he valued love above all else, placing it higher than either scholarship or creative talent. He had already come to see that his intellect surpassed Ruth’s, just as it did those of her father and brothers. Despite her prestigious university background and her formal degree, his inherent mental force eclipsed her own. The brief period he had spent educating himself had provided him with a profound understanding of life, art, and human affairs—a mastery she could never truly hope to attain.
