He came upon the spot. There were half-dressed figures tearing to and fro, some endeavouring to drag the frightened horses from the stables, others driving the cattle from the yard and out-houses, and others coming laden from the burning pile, amidst a shower of falling sparks, and the tumbling down of red-hot beams. The apertures, where doors and windows stood an hour ago, disclosed a mass of raging fire; walls rocked and crumbled into the burning well; the molten lead and iron poured down, white hot, upon the ground. Women and children shrieked, and men encouraged each other with noisy shouts and cheers. The clanking of the engine-pumps, and the spirting and hissing of the water as it fell upon the blazing wood, added to the tremendous roar. He shouted, too, till he was hoarse; and flying from memory and himself, plunged into the thickest of the throng. Hither and thither he dived that night: now working at the pumps, and now hurrying through the smoke and flame, but never ceasing to engage himself wherever noise and men were thickest. Up and down the ladders, upon the roofs of buildings, over floors that quaked and trembled with his weight, under the lee of falling bricks and stones, in every part of that great fire was he; but he bore a charmed life, and had neither scratch nor bruise, nor weariness nor thought, till morning dawned again, and only smoke and blackened ruins remained.

He arrived at the scene just as the chaos peaked. Half-clothed figures darted frantically to and fro: some strained to drag spooked horses free of the stables, others herded cattle out of the yard and outbuildings, while more staggered out of the burning structure loaded with salvaged goods, weaving through a shower of falling sparks and tumbling red-hot support beams. Where doors and windows had stood barely an hour before, gaping openings now poured forth roiling, raging fire. Walls swayed before crumbling into the fiery chasm below, and molten lead and iron, searing white-hot, streamed down to splash against the ground. Women and children shrieked and wailed, while men called encouragement to one another in loud, rough shouts and cheers. The clanking of fire engine pumps, and the sharp sputter and hiss of water as it hit blazing wood, swelled the already deafening roar of the blaze. He shouted too, until his throat went raw, and fleeing both his memories and his own self, threw himself headlong into the thickest part of the crowd. All night he wove back and forth through the chaos: now manning the pumps, now racing through plumes of smoke and licks of flame, never pausing, always seeking out where the noise was loudest and the crowd most dense. He clambered up and down ladders, trod across building roofs, stepped over floorboards that quivered and buckled under his weight, ducked clear of falling bricks and rubble, turning up in every corner of the massive fire. By some charm he escaped entirely unharmed: not a scratch or bruise marked his skin, he felt no weariness and held no thought beyond the work at hand, until dawn broke at last, and all that remained of the blaze was curling smoke and blackened, gutted ruins.

It merits a paragraph to note that you can observe this striking ritual any evening in countless New York City cafés. Gallons of beer have been drained while patrons spin explanations for it. Some have jumped to the conclusion that every Southerner in the city rushes to a café at dusk. The popularity of the “rebel” tune in a Northern metropolis is mildly baffling, but not beyond untangling. The Spanish-American War, a string of abundant mint and watermelon harvests, a few long-odds triumphs at the New Orleans racetrack, and the lavish dinners thrown by the Indiana and Kansas transplants who make up the North Carolina Society have turned the South into something of a craze in Manhattan. Your manicurist will murmur that your left index finger puts her so in mind of a gentleman’s back in Richmond, Virginia. Well, of course; but many women have to earn a living now—the war, you understand.

“I always observed that their pride bitterly resented the younger brother’s (as I call him) having crossed swords with a peasant, and that peasant a boy. The only consideration that appeared to affect the mind of either of them was the consideration that this was highly degrading to the family, and was ridiculous. As often as I caught the younger brother’s eyes, their expression reminded me that he disliked me deeply, for knowing what I knew from the boy. He was smoother and more polite to me than the elder; but I saw this. I also saw that I was an incumbrance in the mind of the elder, too.

I’d long noticed that the Voss brothers’ fierce family pride burned sharp at the fact that the younger of the two, as I privately referred to him, had crossed blades with a common farmer’s son, no more than a scrawny adolescent. The only thought that seemed to occupy either man was that the incident was a humiliating, absurd stain on their household’s honor. Every time our gazes locked, the younger brother’s cold stare made plain how thoroughly he loathed me, for I’d heard the full truth of the fight straight from the boy’s own mouth. He laid on charm and formal courtesy far thicker than his older sibling did, but I was not fooled for an instant. I could tell just as clearly that the older brother, for his part, considered me a tedious, unwelcome burden to be endured.

There were no horses to be procured, and I must return by the lake; but the wind was unfavourable, and the rain fell in torrents. However, it was hardly morning, and I might reasonably hope to arrive by night. I hired men to row and took an oar myself, for I had always experienced relief from mental torment in bodily exercise. But the overflowing misery I now felt, and the excess of agitation that I endured rendered me incapable of any exertion. I threw down the oar, and leaning my head upon my hands, gave way to every gloomy idea that arose. If I looked up, I saw scenes which were familiar to me in my happier time and which I had contemplated but the day before in the company of her who was now but a shadow and a recollection. Tears streamed from my eyes. The rain had ceased for a moment, and I saw the fish play in the waters as they had done a few hours before; they had then been observed by Elizabeth. Nothing is so painful to the human mind as a great and sudden change. The sun might shine or the clouds might lower, but nothing could appear to me as it had done the day before. A fiend had snatched from me every hope of future happiness; no creature had ever been so miserable as I was; so frightful an event is single in the history of man.

No horses could be secured for the journey, so I had to make my return along the lake route; the wind was blowing directly against us, and rain poured down in thick, unrelenting sheets. It was barely dawn, however, so I still had reasonable cause to hope I would reach my destination by nightfall. I hired a pair of men to man the oars, and took one for myself as well; physical exertion had always softened the edge of my mental anguish in the past. But the overwhelming grief that gnawed at me now, the sharp, thrumming restlessness that wracked every nerve, left me unable to summon even the smallest effort. I dropped my oar, rested my forehead against my clasped hands, and surrendered entirely to the grim, swirling thoughts that rose unbidden in my mind. When I lifted my gaze, I was surrounded by landscapes I’d known in far brighter days, spots I’d wandered only the previous afternoon in the company of the woman who was now little more than a ghost, a faint, fading recollection. Tears poured freely down my cheeks. The rain had let up for a brief moment, and I watched fish dart and leap just beneath the surface, exactly as they had done only a few hours earlier; Elizabeth had been the one to notice them then. There is no sharper agony for the human spirit than a sudden, shattering reversal of fortune. The sun might break through the clouds, or the sky might remain heavy and overcast, but nothing in the world looked as it had just twenty-four hours prior. A demon had torn every last spark of future joy from my grasp; no living being had ever suffered the depths of misery I felt now, no horror so unthinkable had ever been recorded in the whole of human experience.

Then Bathsheba said to herself that others were assured in their opinion; strange that she should not be. A strange reflection occurred to her, causing her face to flush. Suppose that Troy had followed Fanny into another world. Had he done this intentionally, yet contrived to make his death appear like an accident? Nevertheless, this thought of how the apparent might differ from the real—made vivid by her bygone jealousy of Fanny, and the remorse he had shown that night—did not blind her to the perception of a likelier difference, less tragic, but to herself far more disastrous.

Bathsheba reflected on how convinced everyone else seemed, finding it peculiar that she could not share their certainty. A startling thought struck her, bringing a sudden heat to her face. What if Troy had pursued Fanny into the next life? Could he have planned his own end, yet managed to make it look like a mere accident? However, this vision of how reality might diverge from appearances—intensified by her old resentment toward Fanny and the profound regret he had shown that night—did not prevent her from seeing a more probable truth. It was a possibility less tragic in nature, but for her, one that would be far more ruinous.

But some thoughtful persons, who had seen him walking across one of his fields on a certain December morning—sunny and exceedingly mild—might have regarded Gabriel Oak in other aspects than these. In his face one might notice that many of the hues and curves of youth had tarried on to manhood: there even remained in his remoter crannies some relics of the boy. His height and breadth would have been sufficient to make his presence imposing, had they been exhibited with due consideration. But there is a way some men have, rural and urban alike, for which the mind is more responsible than flesh and sinew: it is a way of curtailing their dimensions by their manner of showing them. And from a quiet modesty that would have become a vestal, which seemed continually to impress upon him that he had no great claim on the world’s room, Oak walked unassumingly and with a faintly perceptible bend, yet distinct from a bowing of the shoulders. This may be said to be a defect in an individual if he depends for his valuation more upon his appearance than upon his capacity to wear well, which Oak did not.

Yet an observant person, watching him cross one of his pastures on a remarkably warm and sunlit December morning, might have perceived Gabriel Oak in a different light. His face still retained the soft lines and colors of his youth, which had persisted well into his adult years; in the quiet corners of his expression, the boy he had been was still visible. His tall, sturdy frame was substantial enough to be commanding, had he ever bothered to carry himself with any sense of importance. But certain men, in both town and country, possess a psychological trait that has more to do with their spirit than their muscles: a habit of shrinking their physical presence through their own bearing. Out of a deep, almost saintly modesty—a constant sense that he had no special right to take up room in the world—Oak walked with an unassuming air and a slight, nearly imperceptible bend that was not quite a slouch. This could be seen as a shortcoming in someone who values their outward image more than their inner resilience, but Gabriel Oak was not a man who cared for such things.

The force of the stream had, until very lately, been received upon some loose stones spread thereabout, which had acted as a shield to the soil under the onset. These during the summer had been cleared from the ground, and there was now nothing to resist the down-fall but the bare earth. For several years the stream had not spouted so far from the tower as it was doing on this night, and such a contingency had been over-looked. Sometimes this obscure corner received no inhabitant for the space of two or three years, and then it was usually but a pauper, a poacher, or other sinner of undignified sins.

Until just a few weeks prior, the gully’s surging runoff had been caught by a jumble of loose field stones strewn across the bank, which buffered the underlying soil from the full brunt of the current. Those stones had been hauled away over the warm summer months, leaving nothing but unprotected packed earth to hold back the cascade. The creek hadn’t sprayed so far past the old watchtower’s foundation in close to a decade, and no one had thought to account for such an unlikely turn. This tucked-away hollow often sat empty for two or three years at a stretch, and the rare soul who settled there was almost always a destitute vagrant, a deer poacher, or some other petty offender whose misdeeds carried little public weight.

When my books were all written, no one who had read the manuscripts seemed to care for them. In point of fact, because of the stuff I had written they seemed to care even less for me. In writing the stuff it seemed that I had committed acts that were, to say the least, derogatory. ‘Get a job,’ everybody said.”

“Look, here’s how it struck me,” he continued. “Back when I was fully myself, not a soul beyond my own circle gave a damn about me. Once my pages were all penned, those who glanced at the drafts showed no feeling for them. Indeed, on account of the things I’d set down, they seemed to hold me in still lower regard. To have written such things felt as though I’d performed deeds that were, to put it mildly, degrading. ‘Find work,’ they all told me.”

She waited one minute—two minutes—thought of Troy’s disappointment at her non-fulfilment of a promised engagement, till she again ran along the field, clambered over the bank, and followed the original direction. She was now literally trembling and panting at this her temerity in such an errant undertaking; her breath came and went quickly, and her eyes shone with an infrequent light. Yet go she must. She reached the verge of a pit in the middle of the ferns. Troy stood in the bottom, looking up towards her.

She waited a minute—then two—thinking of Troy’s disappointment at her failure to keep the engagement she had promised, until once more she ran across the field, scrambled over the bank, and took up the original path. By now she was literally trembling and breathless at her boldness in such a reckless venture; her breathing came fast and uneven, and her eyes were bright with an uncommon light. Yet go on she must. She came to the edge of a pit set among the ferns. Troy was standing in the bottom of it, looking up at her.

“Not dead! He feared us so much--and with reason--that he caused himself to be represented as dead, and had a grand mock-funeral. But they have found him alive, hiding in the country, and have brought him in. I have seen him but now, on his way to the Hotel de Ville, a prisoner. I have said that he had reason to fear us. Say all! _Had_ he reason?”

He is not dead! He was so terrified of us—and with good cause—that he staged his own death and held a grand, fake funeral. But they found him alive, hiding in the provinces, and have dragged him back. I just saw him myself, a captive being led toward the Hotel de Ville. I told you he had reason to tremble before us. Speak up, everyone! Was his fear not justified?

He woke to find his morning tea beside him and the housemaid going out of the room. He knew that something stupendous had happened to the world, but for a few moments he could not remember what it was. Then he remembered that France was invaded by Germany and Germany by Russia, and that almost certainly England was going to war. It seemed a harsh and terrible fact in the morning light, a demand for stresses, a certainty of destruction; it appeared now robbed of all the dark and dignified beauty of the night. He remembered just the same feeling of unpleasant, anxious expectation as he now felt when the Boer War had begun fifteen years ago, before the first news came. The first news of the Boer War had been the wrecking of a British armoured train near Kimberley. What similar story might not the overdue paper tell when presently it came?

He stirred awake to find his morning tea set beside his bed, the housemaid just slipping out through the bedroom door. Some quiet part of him registered that a world-altering event had occurred, but for a few disoriented seconds he could not call it to mind. Then it flooded back: Germany had invaded France, Russia had crossed into German territory, and England was all but guaranteed to enter the fray. In the pale morning light, the truth felt raw and unthinkable: it would demand crippling strain, it promised unavoidable ruin, stripped entirely of the somber, stately gravity it had carried when he’d heard the news the night before. He recognized this same sharp, gnawing dread of the unknown from fifteen years earlier, in the lull right after the Boer War was declared, before any frontline dispatches had arrived. The first report from that conflict had been of a British armoured train wrecked near Kimberley. What equally devastating, unforeseen tidings might the overdue morning paper hold when it finally arrived?

“Beauty has significance, but I never knew its significance before. I just accepted beauty as something meaningless, as something that was just beautiful without rhyme or reason. I did not know anything about beauty. But now I know, or, rather, am just beginning to know. This grass is more beautiful to me now that I know why it is grass, and all the hidden chemistry of sun and rain and earth that makes it become grass. Why, there is romance in the life-history of any grass, yes, and adventure, too. The very thought of it stirs me. When I think of the play of force and matter, and all the tremendous struggle of it, I feel as if I could write an epic on the grass.

Beauty carries great meaning, but I never understood what that meaning was until now. I’d always written beauty off as something hollow, nothing more than a pretty thing with no rhyme or reason behind it. I truly knew nothing of what beauty actually is. But now I know—well, more accurately, I am only just starting to learn. This grass feels far more lovely to me now that I grasp what makes it grass, all the unseen chemical dance of sunlight, rain, and soil that coaxes it into being. Why, there is real romance in the life story of any blade of grass, yes, and adventure too. Just the thought of it sends a thrill through me. When I consider the interplay of force and matter, and all the staggering, immense struggle wrapped up in that process, I feel as if I could write an entire epic about grass alone.

Mr. Cruncher’s apartments were not in a savoury neighbourhood, and were but two in number, even if a closet with a single pane of glass in it might be counted as one. But they were very decently kept. Early as it was, on the windy March morning, the room in which he lay abed was already scrubbed throughout; and between the cups and saucers arranged for breakfast, and the lumbering deal table, a very clean white cloth was spread.

Mr. Cruncher’s residence was located in a rather unpleasant district and consisted of only two rooms, even if one included a tiny storage space with a single window in the count. Nevertheless, the home was kept in excellent condition. Early as it was on that blustery March morning, the room where he remained in bed had already been scrubbed clean; a spotless white cloth was spread over the heavy wooden table, which was neatly set with cups and saucers for breakfast.

Dig--dig--dig--until an impatient movement from one of the two passengers would admonish him to pull up the window, draw his arm securely through the leathern strap, and speculate upon the two slumbering forms, until his mind lost its hold of them, and they again slid away into the bank and the grave.

Digging, digging, digging—until a restless stir from one of his companions prompted him to slide the window shut, hook his arm firmly through the leather strap, and study the two sleeping figures. Slowly, his concentration would slip, and they would once again dissolve into the twin images of the bank and the grave.

At midnight all was over, and we sat in the presence of four corpses. We covered them with such rags as we could find, and started away, fastening the door behind us. Their home must be these people's grave, for they could not have Christian burial, or be admitted to consecrated ground. They were as dogs, wild beasts, lepers, and no soul that valued its hope of eternal life would throw it away by meddling in any sort with these rebuked and smitten outcasts.

The clock had just struck twelve when the last breath faded, and we huddled numb across the small cottage from four still, cold bodies. We draped whatever tattered scraps of bedding and old clothing we could scrounge up over their faces, then slipped out into the crisp dark, sliding the rusted iron latch shut tight behind us. This hovel would have to serve as their final resting place, for the church would never grant them a blessed funeral, nor would they be allowed to lie in the hallowed soil of the village graveyard. They were treated no better than feral hounds, rabid vermin, untouchable lepers; no devout soul who prized their shot at salvation would dare risk damning themselves by so much as meddling in the smallest way with these shunned, cursed outcasts.

“There’s just one thing that prevents me from taking you.” Brissenden waited a moment. “The thing is big—the biggest I’ve ever done. I know that. It’s my swan song. I am almighty proud of it. I worship it. It’s better than whiskey. It is what I dreamed of—the great and perfect thing—when I was a simple young man, with sweet illusions and clean ideals. And I’ve got it, now, in my last grasp, and I’ll not have it pawed over and soiled by a lot of swine. No, I won’t take the bet. It’s mine. I made it, and I’ve shared it with you.”

“Only one obstacle stands between you and my consent.” Brissenden paused briefly. “It is vast—the grandest work I have ever shaped. I am certain of that. It is my farewell piece. I am fiercely proud of it. I adore it. It surpasses any spirit. It is the vision I chased—the flawless and magnificent creation—when I was an untested youth, filled with tender hopes and unsullied convictions. And now I hold it, in these final moments, and I will not let it be mauled and defiled by a herd of beasts. No, I refuse the wager. It belongs to me. I forged it, and I have given it to you.”

“Well, we’ll leave that out,” said Yeobright. “But anyhow God has set a mark upon me which wouldn’t look well in a love-making scene. I have two ideas in my head, and no others. I am going to keep a night-school; and I am going to turn preacher. What have you got to say to that, Humphrey?”

“Alright, let’s set that whole subject aside for now,” Yeobright said. “But either way, God’s put a mark on me that would stick out like a sore thumb in any kind of romantic courtship moment. I’ve only got two plans locked in my head, no more. I’m going to run a night school, and I’m going to become a preacher. What do you make of that, Humphrey?”

He broke in passionately. “But don’t mistake me like that! Because I am open enough to own what every man in my shoes would have thought of, you make your colours come up your face, and get crabbed with me. That about your not being good enough for me is nonsense. You speak like a lady—all the parish notice it, and your uncle at Weatherbury is, I have heerd, a large farmer—much larger than ever I shall be. May I call in the evening, or will you walk along with me o’ Sundays? I don’t want you to make-up your mind at once, if you’d rather not.”

He cut in, his voice thrumming with urgent feeling. “Don’t misread me like that, okay? I’m just being upfront about what any man in my shoes would’ve thought, and now you’re flushing bright red and snapping at me for it. That whole line about you not being good enough for me is total nonsense. Everyone in the parish notices how poised and well-spoken you are, like a proper gentlewoman, and I’ve heard your uncle over in Weatherbury runs a massive farm—way bigger than anything I’ll ever own. Can I stop by to visit on evenings, or would you come for a walk with me on Sundays? You don’t have to make your mind up right away, if you’d rather take more time to think it over.”

As for Wildeve, his feelings are easy to guess. Obstacles were a ripening sun to his love, and he was at this moment in a delirium of exquisite misery. To clasp as his for five minutes what was another man’s through all the rest of the year was a kind of thing he of all men could appreciate. He had long since begun to sigh again for Eustacia; indeed, it may be asserted that signing the marriage register with Thomasin was the natural signal to his heart to return to its first quarters, and that the extra complication of Eustacia’s marriage was the one addition required to make that return compulsory.

Wildeve’s internal state was not difficult to discern. To him, impediments served as a nourishing sun that brought his passion to full bloom, and he was currently submerged in a feverish, beautiful agony. The chance to claim for a brief moment what belonged to another man for the rest of the year was a thrill he was uniquely suited to enjoy. He had long ago resumed his pining for Eustacia; indeed, the very act of signing the marriage register with Thomasin seemed to be the instinctive signal for his heart to retreat to its original obsession. The added complication of Eustacia’s own wedding was the final element required to make that return unavoidable.

The situation of the door was peculiar. The sill was three or four feet above the ground, and for a moment one was at a loss for an explanation of this exceptional altitude, till ruts immediately beneath suggested that the door was used solely for the passage of articles and persons to and from the level of a vehicle standing on the outside. Upon the whole, the door seemed to advertise itself as a species of Traitor’s Gate translated to another sphere. That entry and exit hereby was only at rare intervals became apparent on noting that tufts of grass were allowed to flourish undisturbed in the chinks of the sill.

The door was placed in a singular way. Its threshold stood three or four feet above the ground, and for a moment one could not account for so unusual a height, until the ruts directly below suggested that the opening served only for the passage of goods and people to and from the level of a vehicle waiting outside. In all, the door seemed to proclaim itself a kind of Traitor’s Gate, transported into another realm. That it was entered and left only at rare intervals was plain from the tufts of grass permitted to grow unmolested in the cracks of the sill.

On the night of the day on which he left the house, Mr. Lorry went into his room with a chopper, saw, chisel, and hammer, attended by Miss Pross carrying a light. There, with closed doors, and in a mysterious and guilty manner, Mr. Lorry hacked the shoemaker’s bench to pieces, while Miss Pross held the candle as if she were assisting at a murder--for which, indeed, in her grimness, she was no unsuitable figure. The burning of the body (previously reduced to pieces convenient for the purpose) was commenced without delay in the kitchen fire; and the tools, shoes, and leather, were buried in the garden. So wicked do destruction and secrecy appear to honest minds, that Mr. Lorry and Miss Pross, while engaged in the commission of their deed and in the removal of its traces, almost felt, and almost looked, like accomplices in a horrible crime.

Late that evening, after the doctor had departed, Mr. Lorry retreated to his room equipped with a hatchet, saw, chisel, and hammer, accompanied by Miss Pross bearing a light. Behind closed doors, acting with a strange sense of guilt, Lorry broke the shoemaker’s bench into fragments. Miss Pross held the candle as though she were an accessory to a homicide—a part her somber appearance was well-suited for. They wasted no time burning the remains, which had been chopped into manageable sizes, in the kitchen fireplace; meanwhile, the tools, shoes, and leather scraps were hidden away in the garden. Destruction and secrecy felt so unnatural to these two honest souls that, as they performed the deed and erased the evidence, they looked and felt like conspirators in a terrible crime.

In England, there was scarcely an amount of order and protection to justify much national boasting. Daring burglaries by armed men, and highway robberies, took place in the capital itself every night; families were publicly cautioned not to go out of town without removing their furniture to upholsterers’ warehouses for security; the highwayman in the dark was a City tradesman in the light, and, being recognised and challenged by his fellow-tradesman whom he stopped in his character of “the Captain,” gallantly shot him through the head and rode away; the mail was waylaid by seven robbers, and the guard shot three dead, and then got shot dead himself by the other four, “in consequence of the failure of his ammunition:” after which the mail was robbed in peace; that magnificent potentate, the Lord Mayor of London, was made to stand and deliver on Turnham Green, by one highwayman, who despoiled the illustrious creature in sight of all his retinue; prisoners in London gaols fought battles with their turnkeys, and the majesty of the law fired blunderbusses in among them, loaded with rounds of shot and ball; thieves snipped off diamond crosses from the necks of noble lords at Court drawing-rooms; musketeers went into St. Giles’s, to search for contraband goods, and the mob fired on the musketeers, and the musketeers fired on the mob, and nobody thought any of these occurrences much out of the common way. In the midst of them, the hangman, ever busy and ever worse than useless, was in constant requisition; now, stringing up long rows of miscellaneous criminals; now, hanging a housebreaker on Saturday who had been taken on Tuesday; now, burning people in the hand at Newgate by the dozen, and now burning pamphlets at the door of Westminster Hall; to-day, taking the life of an atrocious murderer, and to-morrow of a wretched pilferer who had robbed a farmer’s boy of sixpence.

In England, the state of public safety and civil order hardly justified any sense of national pride. Bold burglaries by armed gangs and highway robberies occurred nightly within the capital itself; families were openly advised not to leave for the country without first moving their furniture to secure warehouses for safekeeping. The man who was a respectable shopkeeper by day often became a masked bandit by night; if he were recognized and addressed by a peer while acting the part of “the Captain,” he would simply shoot his acquaintance through the head and gallop away. The mail coach was frequently ambushed by bands of robbers; in one instance, the guard killed three attackers before running out of ammunition, only to be slain by the remaining four, who then looted the mail in peace. Even the Lord Mayor of London, that most powerful of figures, was forced to surrender his purse at Turnham Green to a lone highwayman who robbed the great man in full view of his attendants. In the city's prisons, inmates fought pitched battles with their jailers, and the authorities responded by firing blunderbusses loaded with heavy shot into the fray. Thieves at Court drawing-rooms snipped diamond jewelry from the necks of the nobility, while in the slums of St. Giles, soldiers searching for contraband traded fire with the local mobs—and yet, no one found any of these events particularly remarkable. Amidst this turmoil, the hangman was perpetually in demand, though his efforts were as futile as they were frequent. He was forever busy: stringing up rows of varied criminals, executing a housebreaker on Saturday for a crime committed on Tuesday, branding the palms of convicts at Newgate, or burning seditious pamphlets at Westminster Hall. One day he took the life of a monstrous murderer, and the next, he hanged a pathetic thief who had stolen only sixpence from a farm boy.

This evening Bathsheba was unusually excited, her red cheeks and lips contrasting lustrously with the mazy skeins of her shadowy hair. She seemed to expect assistance, and the seat at the bottom of the table was at her request left vacant until after they had begun the meal. She then asked Gabriel to take the place and the duties appertaining to that end, which he did with great readiness.

That night, Bathsheba was stirred by an uncommon fervor, her flushed cheeks and vivid lips gleaming against the tangled darkness of her hair. She appeared to be awaiting support, and at her insistence, the chair at the table's foot remained empty until the meal was underway. Then she called upon Gabriel to occupy that seat and assume its attendant responsibilities, which he accepted without hesitation.

“O, O, O!” she cried, breaking down at last; and, shaking with sobs which choked her, she sank upon her knees. “O, will you have done! O, you are too relentless—there’s a limit to the cruelty of savages! I have held out long—but you crush me down. I beg for mercy—I cannot bear this any longer—it is inhuman to go further with this! If I had—killed your—mother with my own hand—I should not deserve such a scourging to the bone as this. O, O! God have mercy upon a miserable woman!... You have beaten me in this game—I beg you to stay your hand in pity!... I confess that I—wilfully did not undo the door the first time she knocked—but—I should have unfastened it the second—if I had not thought you had gone to do it yourself. When I found you had not I opened it, but she was gone. That’s the extent of my crime—towards _her_. Best natures commit bad faults sometimes, don’t they?—I think they do. Now I will leave you—for ever and ever!”

“Ah, God, ah!” she wailed, the last of her resolve shattering all at once; wracked by gasping sobs that closed up her throat, she crumpled to her knees on the scuffed floorboards. “Oh, can’t you just stop? You’re so unyielding—even wild beasts don’t torment their prey this endlessly! I held out for so long, but you’re grinding me to dust. I beg you for mercy—I can’t take another minute of this, it’s cruel beyond all decency to keep pressing! Even if I’d slit your sister’s throat with my own two hands, I wouldn’t deserve to be flayed raw like this. Ah, ah! Lord have pity on a wretched, broken woman!... You’ve won, you’ve beaten me at this whole ugly game—I beg you, just stay your hand, for pity’s sake!... I admit it—I purposely didn’t unbolt the door the first time she rapped on it—but I would’ve undone it the second time, if I hadn’t assumed you’d already gotten up to let her in. When I realized you hadn’t moved, I wrenched it open, but she was already gone. That’s all I did wrong to her. Even the kindest people slip up and do terrible things sometimes, don’t they? I know they do. Now I’ll go—leave you for ever and ever, I swear!”

“I am,” said she, “the cousin of the unhappy child who was murdered, or rather his sister, for I was educated by and have lived with his parents ever since and even long before his birth. It may therefore be judged indecent in me to come forward on this occasion, but when I see a fellow creature about to perish through the cowardice of her pretended friends, I wish to be allowed to speak, that I may say what I know of her character. I am well acquainted with the accused. I have lived in the same house with her, at one time for five and at another for nearly two years. During all that period she appeared to me the most amiable and benevolent of human creatures. She nursed Madame Frankenstein, my aunt, in her last illness, with the greatest affection and care and afterwards attended her own mother during a tedious illness, in a manner that excited the admiration of all who knew her, after which she again lived in my uncle’s house, where she was beloved by all the family. She was warmly attached to the child who is now dead and acted towards him like a most affectionate mother. For my own part, I do not hesitate to say that, notwithstanding all the evidence produced against her, I believe and rely on her perfect innocence. She had no temptation for such an action; as to the bauble on which the chief proof rests, if she had earnestly desired it, I should have willingly given it to her, so much do I esteem and value her.”

“I am,” she began, “the kinswoman of the wretched boy who was slain—his sister, in truth, for I was reared alongside his parents and have dwelt with them from long before his birth. It may be thought unseemly that I should step forward at such a moment, yet when I behold a fellow soul doomed by the faint-heartedness of those who feign friendship, I crave leave to speak, that I may tell what I know of her nature. I have been intimately acquainted with the prisoner. I shared a roof with her, once for a span of five years, and again for nearly two. Throughout that time she seemed to me the gentlest and kindest of beings. She tended Madame Frankenstein, my aunt, through her final decline with the tenderest devotion and vigilance, and later cared for her own mother in a protracted sickness with a grace that drew the wonder of all who witnessed it; afterward she returned to my uncle’s household, where every member cherished her. She was deeply devoted to the child now lost, and behaved toward him as the fondest of mothers. For myself, I do not scruple to declare that, despite every proof arrayed against her, I trust wholly in her blamelessness. She lacked any motive for such a deed; as for the trinket on which the accusation so heavily leans, had she truly longed for it, I would have bestowed it upon her freely—so dearly do I prize and reverence her.”

“Come here, Alfred,” he called to the crying child, at the same time thrusting his hand into his trousers pocket, where he carried his money loose in the same large way that he lived life in general. He put a quarter in the youngster’s hand and held him in his arms a moment, soothing his sobs. “Now run along and get some candy, and don’t forget to give some to your brothers and sisters. Be sure and get the kind that lasts longest.”

Alfred, come over here," he called to the weeping boy, reaching into his pocket as he spoke. He carried his money loose and uncounted, reflecting the same expansive, carefree manner with which he approached life. Be certain to get the kind that lasts the longest.

On the Exchange there were hurricanes and landslides and snowstorms and glaciers and volcanoes, and those elemental disturbances were reproduced in miniature in the broker’s offices. Maxwell shoved his chair against the wall and transacted business after the manner of a toe dancer. He jumped from ticker to ’phone, from desk to door with the trained agility of a harlequin.

The trading floor of the Exchange roiled with upheavals as violent as hurricanes, landslides, blizzards, glacial shifts, and volcanic eruptions, and all those raw elemental disturbances played out in miniature, concentrated form inside the broker’s offices. Maxwell shoved his chair hard against the wall and worked through his transactions with the light, precise step of a toe dancer. He darted from stock ticker to telephone, from desk to doorway, with the honed, effortless agility of a trained harlequin.

“The things is well enough in their way,” observed Mr. Sikes: a little soothed as he glanced over the table; “but what have you got to say for yourself, why you should leave me here, down in the mouth, health, blunt, and everything else; and take no more notice of me, all this mortal time, than if I was that ’ere dog.—Drive him down, Charley!”

“The spread’s decent, I’ll grant you,” said Mr. Sikes, his temper easing a fraction as his eyes swept the table. “But what excuse can you offer for deserting me here, low in spirits, sick, short of cash, the whole lot—and paying me no more mind all this everlasting while than you would that dog there. Send him off, Charley!”

“He wastes his time over his writing, trying to accomplish what geniuses and rare men with college educations sometimes accomplish. A man thinking of marriage should be preparing for marriage. But not he. As I have said, and I know you agree with me, he is irresponsible. And why should he not be? It is the way of sailors. He has never learned to be economical or temperate. The spendthrift years have marked him. It is not his fault, of course, but that does not alter his nature. And have you thought of the years of licentiousness he inevitably has lived? Have you thought of that, daughter? You know what marriage means.”

“He fritters away his hours over his writing, seeking to achieve what men of genius and those rare fellows with a university training sometimes contrive. A man who has marriage in view ought to be making himself ready for it. But not he. As I have said, and I know you will agree with me, he is not to be relied upon. And why should he be? Such is the sailor’s way. He has never learned thrift or moderation. The lavish years have left their mark upon him. It is not his fault, certainly, but that does not change the grain of him. And have you considered the years of dissipation he must have led? Have you considered that, my dear? You know what marriage requires.”

But as soon as one is at rest, in this world, off he goes on something else to worry about. It occurred to me that I had made another blunder: I had sent the boy off to alarm his betters with a threat--I intending to invent a calamity at my leisure; now the people who are the readiest and eagerest and willingest to swallow miracles are the very ones who are hungriest to see you perform them; suppose I should be called on for a sample? Suppose I should be asked to name my calamity? Yes, I had made a blunder; I ought to have invented my calamity first. “What shall I do? what can I say, to gain a little time?” I was in trouble again; in the deepest kind of trouble...

The moment a man finds peace in this world, he invariably stumbles into a fresh anxiety. It struck me then that I had made a serious mistake: I had dispatched the boy to frighten his superiors with a vague threat, planning to invent the specifics of the disaster at my own convenience. Yet, those most willing to swallow a miracle are always the hungriest for a demonstration. What if I were called upon for a sample? Suppose they asked me to name the coming plague? I had indeed blundered; the lie should have been ready before the messenger was gone. “What can I say to buy myself some time?” I was back in the soup, sinking into the deepest sort of trouble.

Mr. Britling had ceased to write about the war at all. All his later writings about it had been abandoned unfinished. He could not imagine them counting, affecting any one, producing any effect. Indeed he was writing now very intermittently. His contributions to _The Times_ had fallen away. He was perpetually thinking now about the war, about life and death, about the religious problems that had seemed so remote in the days of the peace; but none of his thinking would become clear and definite enough for writing. All the clear stars of his mind were hidden by the stormy clouds of excitement that the daily newspaper perpetually renewed and by the daily developments of life. And just as his professional income shrank before his mental confusion and impotence, the private income that came from his and his wife's investments became uncertain. She had had two thousand pounds in the Constantinople loan, seven hundred in debentures of the Ottoman railway; he had held similar sums in two Hungarian and one Bulgarian loan, in a linoleum factory at Rouen and in a Swiss Hotel company. All these stopped payments, and the dividends from their other investments shrank. There seemed no limit set to the possibilities of shrinkage of capital and income. Income tax had leapt to colossal dimensions, the cost of most things had risen, and the tangle of life was now increased by the need for retrenchments and economies. He decided that Gladys, the facetiously named automobile, was a luxury, and sold her for a couple of hundred pounds. He lost his gardener, who had gone to higher priced work with a miller, and he had great trouble to replace him, so that the garden became disagreeably unkempt and unsatisfactory. He had to give up his frequent trips to London. He was obliged to defer Statesminster for the boys. For a time at any rate they must go as day boys to Brinsmead. At every point he met this uncongenial consideration of ways and means. For years now he had gone easy, lived with a certain self-indulgence. It was extraordinarily vexatious to have one's greater troubles for one's country and one's son and one's faith crossed and complicated by these little troubles of the extra sixpence and the untimely bill.

Mr. Britling had altogether stopped writing about the war. Every later attempt lay discarded, half-made. He could not conceive of his words reaching anyone, swaying opinion, stirring the least result. His pen moved now only in fits and starts. The pieces he had once sent to _The Times_ had dwindled to nothing. His mind churned endlessly with the war, with mortality and eternity, with religious questions that had seemed impossibly distant in the tranquil years; yet none of his reflections crystallized enough for composition. The steady constellations of his intellect were smothered beneath the squalls of agitation that each morning’s paper revived and each day’s unfolding events fed. And even as his professional earnings withered in the face of his confusion and paralysis, the private income drawn from his and his wife’s holdings grew precarious. She had placed two thousand pounds in the Constantinople loan, seven hundred in Ottoman railway debentures; he had committed comparable sums to two Hungarian bonds and one Bulgarian, to a linoleum works at Rouen and a Swiss hotel venture. All ceased to pay, and the returns on their remaining investments shrank. There appeared no boundary to the possible contraction of capital and revenue. The income tax had vaulted to gigantic heights, the price of nearly everything had climbed, and the snarl of daily existence was now tightened by the imperative of thrift and sacrifice. He concluded that Gladys, the motorcar they had christened in jest, was an extravagance, and let her go for two hundred pounds. His gardener departed for better wages at a miller’s, and finding a replacement proved so vexing that the grounds fell into a shabby, disheartening neglect. He was forced to abandon his frequent journeys to London. Statesminster had to be postponed for the boys; for the present, at least, they would attend Brinsmead as day pupils. At every turn he collided with this uncongenial reckoning of means. For years he had moved lightly, lived with a measure of indulgence. It was maddeningly bitter to have one’s large sorrows for country and son and creed crossed and tangled by these petty vexations of the extra shilling and the inopportune account.

Once, it entered his mind that there was a deliberate rebuff in all this. He caught her spirit of antagonism and strove to divine the message that her hands pronounced upon the keys. Then he dismissed the thought as unworthy and impossible, and yielded himself more freely to the music. The old delightful condition began to be induced. His feet were no longer clay, and his flesh became spirit; before his eyes and behind his eyes shone a great glory; and then the scene before him vanished and he was away, rocking over the world that was to him a very dear world. The known and the unknown were commingled in the dream-pageant that thronged his vision. He entered strange ports of sun-washed lands, and trod market-places among barbaric peoples that no man had ever seen. The scent of the spice islands was in his nostrils as he had known it on warm, breathless nights at sea, or he beat up against the southeast trades through long tropic days, sinking palm-tufted coral islets in the turquoise sea behind and lifting palm-tufted coral islets in the turquoise sea ahead. Swift as thought the pictures came and went. One instant he was astride a broncho and flying through the fairy-colored Painted Desert country; the next instant he was gazing down through shimmering heat into the whited sepulchre of Death Valley, or pulling an oar on a freezing ocean where great ice islands towered and glistened in the sun. He lay on a coral beach where the cocoanuts grew down to the mellow-sounding surf. The hulk of an ancient wreck burned with blue fires, in the light of which danced the _hula_ dancers to the barbaric love-calls of the singers, who chanted to tinkling _ukuleles_ and rumbling tom-toms. It was a sensuous, tropic night. In the background a volcano crater was silhouetted against the stars. Overhead drifted a pale crescent moon, and the Southern Cross burned low in the sky.

For a moment it crossed his mind that there was intent in the slight. He had felt the strain of her hostility, and he tried to read the meaning her fingers were uttering on the keyboard. Then he put the notion away as absurd and impossible, and surrendered himself the more wholly to the strain of the music. The old enchantment began again to steal over him. His feet were no longer of clay, and his body seemed turned to spirit; before his eyes and behind them there shone a vast radiance; and then the room dissolved, and he was gone, drifting upon the world that was to him so dear. The familiar and the strange mingled in the dream-procession that crowded his sight. He came into far harbors of sun-drenched coasts, and wandered through bazars among savage peoples no man had ever beheld. The odor of the spice islands filled his nostrils as it had on hot, breathless nights at sea, or he drove into the southeast trades through long tropical days, leaving behind him palm-clad coral islets in the blue-green sea and raising palm-clad coral islets in the blue-green sea before him. The visions flashed by with the speed of thought. In one instant he was on a mustang, riding hard through the radiant country of the Painted Desert; in the next he peered through wavering heat into the chalk-white tomb of Death Valley, or rowed across a frozen ocean where towering ice islands shone and sparkled in the sun. He stretched on a coral strand where the coco palms leaned down to the soft, murmuring surf. The hulk of some ancient wreck flamed with sapphire fire, and in that glow the hula girls moved to the wild love-songs of the chanters, who sang to the bright plink of ukuleles and the deep boom of tom-toms. It was a lush, tropical night. Behind them a volcano’s crater stood black against the stars. Above, a slender moon drifted pale through the heavens, and the Southern Cross burned faintly low in the sky.

Between this half-wooded half-naked hill, and the vague still horizon that its summit indistinctly commanded, was a mysterious sheet of fathomless shade—the sounds from which suggested that what it concealed bore some reduced resemblance to features here. The thin grasses, more or less coating the hill, were touched by the wind in breezes of differing powers, and almost of differing natures—one rubbing the blades heavily, another raking them piercingly, another brushing them like a soft broom. The instinctive act of humankind was to stand and listen, and learn how the trees on the right and the trees on the left wailed or chaunted to each other in the regular antiphonies of a cathedral choir; how hedges and other shapes to leeward then caught the note, lowering it to the tenderest sob; and how the hurrying gust then plunged into the south, to be heard no more.

Between this half-wooded, half-bare hill and the dim, motionless horizon that its summit faintly overlooked lay a strange expanse of unfathomable shadow, from which came sounds suggesting that whatever it concealed bore some diminished likeness to the forms visible here. The sparse grass, which covered the hill in patches, was stirred by winds of varying force and almost varying character—one that rubbed the blades heavily, another that cut across them with a sharp rasp, another that swept them as lightly as a broom. Instinctively, a person would stop and listen, and hear how the trees on the right and the trees on the left wailed or chanted to one another in the measured alternation of a cathedral choir; how hedges and other shapes downwind took up the sound, softening it to the gentlest sob; and how the rushing gust then plunged southward, never to be heard again.

Turning off the light in his room so that it might not betray him, he went out the port-hole feet first. His shoulders stuck, and he forced himself back so as to try it with one arm down by his side. A roll of the steamer aided him, and he was through, hanging by his hands. When his feet touched the sea, he let go. He was in a milky froth of water. The side of the _Mariposa_ rushed past him like a dark wall, broken here and there by lighted ports. She was certainly making time. Almost before he knew it, he was astern, swimming gently on the foam-crackling surface.

He extinguished the cabin light to keep his movements hidden, then eased himself feet-first through the porthole. When his shoulders jammed, he pulled back inside to readjust, tucking one arm tight against his flank. The ship gave a timely lurch, helping him slide through until he was suspended by his hands. As soon as his feet dipped into the ocean, he let go. He found himself immersed in a pale, frothing turbulence. The *Mariposa*’s flank surged past like a massive, shadowed wall, its dark surface flickering with illuminated windows. She was moving fast. Before he could fully grasp it, the ship had pulled away, leaving him to tread water gently in the crackling foam of its trail.

If you do not know Bogle’s Chop House and Family Restaurant it is your loss. For if you are one of the fortunate ones who dine expensively you should be interested to know how the other half consumes provisions. And if you belong to the half to whom waiters’ checks are things of moment, you should know Bogle’s, for there you get your money’s worth—in quantity, at least.

If you’ve never stepped foot in Mabel’s Hearth Grill and Family Diner, you’re missing out. Because if you count yourself among the fortunate few who regularly splurge on high-end meals, you ought to be curious about how the other half eats their fill. And if you belong to the group for whom every line on a server’s check feels like a pressing concern, you need to seek out Mabel’s: there, you get every bit of what you pay for, in sheer volume at the very least.

The most thoroughgoing ascetic could feel that he had a natural right to wander on Egdon—he was keeping within the line of legitimate indulgence when he laid himself open to influences such as these. Colours and beauties so far subdued were, at least, the birthright of all. Only in summer days of highest feather did its mood touch the level of gaiety. Intensity was more usually reached by way of the solemn than by way of the brilliant, and such a sort of intensity was often arrived at during winter darkness, tempests, and mists. Then Egdon was aroused to reciprocity; for the storm was its lover, and the wind its friend. Then it became the home of strange phantoms; and it was found to be the hitherto unrecognized original of those wild regions of obscurity which are vaguely felt to be compassing us about in midnight dreams of flight and disaster, and are never thought of after the dream till revived by scenes like this.

Even the most uncompromising ascetic might feel that he had an inborn right to roam Egdon—he would still be keeping within the bounds of proper indulgence if he exposed himself to influences like these. Such subdued colors and beauties, at the very least, belonged to everyone by birth. Only on summer days at the height of its splendor did the heath’s spirit rise to something like gaiety. More often, it achieved its force through solemnity rather than brilliance, and that kind of intensity was commonly reached in winter darkness, in storms, and in mist. Then Egdon seemed to awaken in answer; the storm was its lover and the wind its friend. Then it became the dwelling place of uncanny apparitions, and proved to be the long-unrecognized original of those vast dim regions that we feel, without clearly seeing them, to surround us in midnight dreams of escape and ruin, and which are never remembered after the dream until scenes like this call them back to life.

But while he told her of his two acceptances, of his despair when he received the one from the _Transcontinental_, and of the corresponding delight with which he received the one from the _White Mouse_, she did not follow him. She heard the words he uttered and understood their literal import, but she was not with him in his despair and his delight. She could not get out of herself. She was not interested in selling stories to magazines. What was important to her was matrimony. She was not aware of it, however, any more than she was aware that her desire that Martin take a position was the instinctive and preparative impulse of motherhood. She would have blushed had she been told as much in plain, set terms, and next, she might have grown indignant and asserted that her sole interest lay in the man she loved and her desire for him to make the best of himself. So, while Martin poured out his heart to her, elated with the first success his chosen work in the world had received, she paid heed to his bare words only, gazing now and again about the room, shocked by what she saw.

But while he told her about the two acceptances, about the despair he had felt when the one from the _Transcontinental_ arrived, and the matching delight with which he had received the one from the _White Mouse_, she did not truly follow him. She heard the words he spoke and understood their plain meaning, but she was not with him in his despair or his delight. She could not step outside herself. She had no interest in selling stories to magazines. What mattered to her was marriage. Yet she was not conscious of this, any more than she was conscious that her wish for Martin to accept a position was the instinctive, preparatory urge of motherhood. She would have blushed had anyone put it to her so bluntly, and then perhaps she would have been angered and insisted that her only concern was the man she loved and her wish that he should make the best of himself. So while Martin poured out his heart to her, exultant over the first success his chosen work in the world had won, she attended only to his actual words, glancing now and then around the room, startled by what she saw.

“Overdue” was rushed upon the market by the Meredith-Lowell Company in the height of his popularity, and being fiction, in point of sales it made even a bigger strike than “The Shame of the Sun.” Week after week his was the credit of the unprecedented performance of having two books at the head of the list of best-sellers. Not only did the story take with the fiction-readers, but those who read “The Shame of the Sun” with avidity were likewise attracted to the sea-story by the cosmic grasp of mastery with which he had handled it. First he had attacked the literature of mysticism, and had done it exceeding well; and, next, he had successfully supplied the very literature he had exposited, thus proving himself to be that rare genius, a critic and a creator in one.

At the peak of his widespread popularity, the Meredith-Lowell Company rushed *Overdue* to market, and as a work of fiction, it notched even stronger sales than his earlier hit *The Shame of the Sun*. For weeks on end, he claimed the unprecedented distinction of holding the top two spots on the national bestseller lists simultaneously. The sea story did not just land well with casual fiction readers; those who’d devoured *The Shame of the Sun* with great enthusiasm were equally drawn to it, captivated by the sweeping, masterful command he brought to its narrative. He had first targeted the body of mystical literature, dismantling its premises with exceptional skill; next, he had successfully produced exactly the kind of literature he had laid out in his critique, proving himself to be that rare genius: both a sharp, perceptive critic and a gifted creator.

“I made the patient swallow, with great difficulty, and after many efforts, the dose that I desired to give. As I intended to repeat it after a while, and as it was necessary to watch its influence, I then sat down by the side of the bed. There was a timid and suppressed woman in attendance (wife of the man down-stairs), who had retreated into a corner. The house was damp and decayed, indifferently furnished--evidently, recently occupied and temporarily used. Some thick old hangings had been nailed up before the windows, to deaden the sound of the shrieks. They continued to be uttered in their regular succession, with the cry, ‘My husband, my father, and my brother!’ the counting up to twelve, and ‘Hush!’ The frenzy was so violent, that I had not unfastened the bandages restraining the arms; but, I had looked to them, to see that they were not painful. The only spark of encouragement in the case, was, that my hand upon the sufferer’s breast had this much soothing influence, that for minutes at a time it tranquillised the figure. It had no effect upon the cries; no pendulum could be more regular.

I managed, though only with great difficulty and after many attempts, to make the patient swallow the dose I had meant to give. Since I planned to repeat it later and needed to observe its effect, I took a seat beside the bed. A shy, subdued woman was in attendance— the man’s wife from downstairs— and she had drawn back into a corner. The house was damp and falling apart, poorly furnished, plainly only recently occupied and meant for temporary use. Thick, old draperies had been hung over the windows to muffle the sound of the screams. They went on in their steady order, with the cry, “My husband, my father, and my brother!” the count up to twelve, and then “Hush!” The agitation was so extreme that I had not removed the bandages binding the arms, though I had inspected them to make sure they were not causing pain. The one slight sign of hope in the case was that when I laid my hand on the sufferer’s breast, it had a soothing effect enough to calm the figure for several minutes at a time. It did nothing to stop the cries; no clockwork could have been more exact.

Perhaps things are best left as they stand.

The evening air had turned crisp all around them, but every time he crossed a stretch of bare, vegetation-free sand, the warmth the earth had soaked up through the daylight hours billowed up to sear his cheeks. When he’d first lifted her, he’d given barely any thought to how far they still had to go to reach Thistle Corner; even though he’d caught a long nap that very afternoon, the heft of her in his arms soon began to make his muscles burn. He pressed onward, like Aeneas bearing Anchises from the ruins of Troy; bats looped lazy arcs around his head, nightjars beat their soft wings barely a foot from his face, and not a single human soul was within shouting distance.

Clarence was with me as concerned the revolution, but in a modified way. His idea was a republic, without privileged orders, but with a hereditary royal family at the head of it instead of an elective chief magistrate. He believed that no nation that had ever known the joy of worshiping a royal family could ever be robbed of it and not fade away and die of melancholy. I urged that kings were dangerous. He said, then have cats. He was sure that a royal family of cats would answer every purpose. They would be as useful as any other royal family, they would know as much, they would have the same virtues and the same treacheries, the same disposition to get up shindies with other royal cats, they would be laughably vain and absurd and never know it, they would be wholly inexpensive; finally, they would have as sound a divine right as any other royal house, and “Tom VII, or Tom XI, or Tom XIV by the grace of God King,” would sound as well as it would when applied to the ordinary royal tomcat with tights on. “And as a rule,” said he, in his neat modern English, “the character of these cats would be considerably above the character of the average king, and this would be an immense moral advantage to the nation, for the reason that a nation always models its morals after its monarch's. The worship of royalty being founded in unreason, these graceful and harmless cats would easily become as sacred as any other royalties, and indeed more so, because it would presently be noticed that they hanged nobody, beheaded nobody, imprisoned nobody, inflicted no cruelties or injustices of any sort, and so must be worthy of a deeper love and reverence than the customary human king, and would certainly get it. The eyes of the whole harried world would soon be fixed upon this humane and gentle system, and royal butchers would presently begin to disappear; their subjects would fill the vacancies with catlings from our own royal house; we should become a factory; we should supply the thrones of the world; within forty years all Europe would be governed by cats, and we should furnish the cats. The reign of universal peace would begin then, to end no more forever.... Me-e-e-yow-ow-ow-ow--fzt!--wow!”

Clarence supported my revolutionary goals, though with a particular caveat. He favored a republic that abolished social castes but retained a hereditary monarchy at its helm instead of an elected leader. He argued that any people who had experienced the delight of idolizing a royal family could never be deprived of it without withering away from profound sadness. When I pointed out that kings were a hazard, he suggested substituting them with cats. He was certain a feline dynasty would serve every necessary function. They would be just as effective as any human royals, possess equal intellect, and display the same blend of virtues and vices, including a tendency to pick fights with other royal cats. They would be hilariously conceited and absurd without ever realizing it, and they would cost absolutely nothing. Furthermore, their claim to divine right would be as legitimate as any other royal line; "Tom VII, or Tom XI, or Tom XIV, King by the Grace of God," would sound just as dignified as the same title applied to a typical human monarch in his finery. 

"Moreover," he noted in his polished, modern tongue, "the temperament of these cats would generally be far superior to that of the average king. This would provide a tremendous moral boost to the public, as a nation always patterns its ethics after its ruler. Since the veneration of royalty is based on emotion rather than logic, these elegant and harmless animals would easily become as hallowed as any other sovereign—perhaps even more so. People would eventually observe that they never hang, behead, or imprison anyone, nor do they perpetrate any cruelties or injustices. Thus, they would be entitled to a deeper level of affection and respect than a standard human king, and they would surely receive it. The gaze of a troubled world would soon settle upon this kind and gentle model, causing royal despots to vanish. Their subjects would fill those empty thrones with kittens from our own royal stock; we would become a sort of factory, exporting monarchs to every nation. Within forty years, all of Europe would be under feline rule, and we would be the ones supplying the cats. An age of eternal global peace would then begin... Me-e-e-yow-ow-ow-ow—fzt!—wow!"

Mr. Coggan poured the cider with a generous, unsparing hand into Cain’s wide, gasping mouth; half of it spilled down the side of the jug, and half of what reached his lips trickled down his neck, and half of what entered his throat went down the wrong pipe, only to be coughed and sputtered over the surrounding reapers in a fine cider mist. For a brief moment, this spray hung in the warm sunlight like a small, hazy breath.

After the ceremony was performed a large party assembled at my father’s, but it was agreed that Elizabeth and I should commence our journey by water, sleeping that night at Evian and continuing our voyage on the following day. The day was fair, the wind favourable; all smiled on our nuptial embarkation.

Once the marriage rites were concluded, a great company gathered at my father’s house, yet it was arranged that Elizabeth and I would begin our journey by water, resting that night at Evian and continuing our travels the next morning. The day was bright and the wind proved steady; everything seemed to smile upon our wedding departure.

Mr. Bumble sat in the workhouse parlour, with his eyes moodily fixed on the cheerless grate, whence, as it was summer time, no brighter gleam proceeded, than the reflection of certain sickly rays of the sun, which were sent back from its cold and shining surface. A paper fly-cage dangled from the ceiling, to which he occasionally raised his eyes in gloomy thought; and, as the heedless insects hovered round the gaudy net-work, Mr. Bumble would heave a deep sigh, while a more gloomy shadow overspread his countenance. Mr. Bumble was meditating; it might be that the insects brought to mind, some painful passage in his own past life.

Mr. Barrow sat in the parish parlour, his eyes fixed in a sullen reverie upon the cheerless grate, from which, though it was summertime, no warmer light was returned than the faint reflection of a few wan sunbeams cast back from its cold, polished face. From the ceiling there hung a paper cage for flies, toward which he now and then lifted his glance in despondent reflection; and as the foolish creatures drifted about the showy mesh, Mr. Barrow would draw a long sigh, while a deeper cloud seemed to settle upon his brow. Mr. Barrow was in thought; perhaps the insects recalled to him some bitter chapter in his own earlier life.

The officiating undertakers made some protest against these changes in the ceremonies; but, the river being alarmingly near, and several voices remarking on the efficacy of cold immersion in bringing refractory members of the profession to reason, the protest was faint and brief. The remodelled procession started, with a chimney-sweep driving the hearse--advised by the regular driver, who was perched beside him, under close inspection, for the purpose--and with a pieman, also attended by his cabinet minister, driving the mourning coach. A bear-leader, a popular street character of the time, was impressed as an additional ornament, before the cavalcade had gone far down the Strand; and his bear, who was black and very mangy, gave quite an Undertaking air to that part of the procession in which he walked.

The undertaker gentlemen who had the charge of the affair offered a small remonstrance against these alterations in the rites; but, as the river lay uncomfortably close, and as more than one voice observed how effectually a plunge in cold water might restore obstinate members of the craft to prudence, the remonstrance was but weak and short-lived. So the new-ordered procession set forth, with a chimney-sweep at the reins of the hearse, instructed by the ordinary driver, who sat beside him under strict observation for that very end; while a pie-man, likewise accompanied by his own minister of state, guided the mourning coach. Before they had proceeded far down the Strand, a bear-keeper, one of the well-known street figures of the day, was enlisted as an extra embellishment; and his bear, a black creature, rough and most unkempt, lent a singularly funerary dignity to that section of the procession in which it marched.

“I’ll tell you what, Mr. Bumble,” returned his lady. “We don’t want any of your interference. You’re a great deal too fond of poking your nose into things that don’t concern you, making everybody in the house laugh, the moment your back is turned, and making yourself look like a fool every hour in the day. Be off; come!”

“Let me make this perfectly clear, Mr. Bumble,” his wife shot back. “We’ve got no use at all for your meddling here. You’re far too keen on sticking your nose into matters that are none of your business, making everyone in the house laugh at you the second your back is turned, and making a total fool of yourself every single hour of the day. Get moving, now!”

"Exactly. The village was a little startled. The cottager who had let the rooms came to me privately. Teddy is rather touchy on the point of his personal independence, he considers any demand for explanations as an insult, and probably all he had said to the old lady was, 'This is Letty--come to share my rooms.' I put the matter to him very gently. 'Oh, yes,' he said, rather in the manner of some one who has overlooked a trifle. 'I got married to her in the Christmas holidays. May I bring her along to see Mrs. Britling?' We induced him to go into a little cottage I rent. The wife was the daughter of a Colchester journalist and printer. I don't know if you talked to her."

Precisely. The local community was somewhat taken aback. The woman who had leased him the rooms came to see me in private. Teddy is quite defensive about his personal freedom; he perceives any request for an explanation as a slight. He probably told the elderly woman nothing more than, "This is Letty—she’s staying in my rooms now." I approached the topic with him very delicately. "Oh, that," he remarked, much like someone who had forgotten a minor detail. "We got married during the Christmas break. Would it be alright if I brought her to meet Mrs. Britling?" Eventually, we persuaded him to move into a small cottage I own. His wife is the daughter of a journalist and printer from Colchester. I’m not sure if you’ve had a conversation with her yet.
