He shone very well in this latter office. He was a wise and humane judge, and he clearly did his honest best and fairest,--according to his lights. That is a large reservation. His lights--I mean his rearing--often colored his decisions. Whenever there was a dispute between a noble or gentleman and a person of lower degree, the king's leanings and sympathies were for the former class always, whether he suspected it or not. It was impossible that this should be otherwise. The blunting effects of slavery upon the slaveholder's moral perceptions are known and conceded, the world over; and a privileged class, an aristocracy, is but a band of slaveholders under another name. This has a harsh sound, and yet should not be offensive to any--even to the noble himself--unless the fact itself be an offense: for the statement simply formulates a fact. The repulsive feature of slavery is the _thing_, not its name. One needs but to hear an aristocrat speak of the classes that are below him to recognize--and in but indifferently modified measure --the very air and tone of the actual slaveholder; and behind these are the slaveholder's spirit, the slaveholder's blunted feeling. They are the result of the same cause in both cases: the possessor's old and inbred custom of regarding himself as a superior being. The king's judgments wrought frequent injustices, but it was merely the fault of his training, his natural and unalterable sympathies. He was as unfitted for a judgeship as would be the average mother for the position of milk-distributor to starving children in famine-time; her own children would fare a shade better than the rest.

He performed exceedingly well in that latter role. A thoughtful, compassionate magistrate, he strove openly to rule with complete honesty and evenhandedness—so far as his own frame of reference allowed. That caveat carries great weight. His frame of reference, shaped entirely by his blue-blooded upbringing, tinted every ruling he handed down. Any dispute that pitted a lord or landed gentry member against a laborer or peasant found King Theron’s instincts and loyalties tilting invariably toward the privileged party, even when he believed he was being perfectly impartial. There was no way around this skew. The dulling effect of chattel slavery on an enslaver’s sense of right and wrong is a widely acknowledged truth across the globe; and a ruling elite, an aristocracy, is nothing more than a cohort of enslavers operating under a different label. That phrasing might sound harsh, but it ought not to offend anyone—even a peer of the realm—unless the reality it describes is itself offensive: it is merely a plain articulation of observable fact. The ugly core of slavery is the system itself, not what we choose to call it. One has only to listen to an aristocrat dismiss the ranks beneath him to pick out—only barely softened, at that—the exact haughty cadence and attitude of an actual enslaver; behind that tone lies the same sense of entitled dominance, the same stunted capacity for empathy. Both stem from the exact same root: the powerful party’s lifelong, ingrained habit of seeing themselves as inherently superior to those below them. Theron’s rulings often resulted in deep unfairness, but the flaw lay not in his character, but his conditioning, his innate, unshakable allegiances. He was as poorly suited to sit in judgment as the average mother would be to hand out milk rations to starving children during a famine; her own little ones would always get a crumb more than the rest.

“When I speak of success, I speak of success with the young lady; and when I speak of causes and reasons to make success probable, I speak of causes and reasons that will tell as such with the young lady. The young lady, my good sir,” said Mr. Lorry, mildly tapping the Stryver arm, “the young lady. The young lady goes before all.”

“When I talk about winning, I mean winning over the young lady; and when I talk about factors and arguments that might make winning likely, I mean factors and arguments that will carry weight with her. The young lady, my dear fellow,” Mr. Lorry said, gently patting Stryver’s arm, “the young lady. She comes before everything.”

The three went on, and Young Jerry went on, until the three stopped under a bank overhanging the road. Upon the top of the bank was a low brick wall, surmounted by an iron railing. In the shadow of bank and wall the three turned out of the road, and up a blind lane, of which the wall--there, risen to some eight or ten feet high--formed one side. Crouching down in a corner, peeping up the lane, the next object that Young Jerry saw, was the form of his honoured parent, pretty well defined against a watery and clouded moon, nimbly scaling an iron gate. He was soon over, and then the second fisherman got over, and then the third. They all dropped softly on the ground within the gate, and lay there a little--listening perhaps. Then, they moved away on their hands and knees.

The trio pressed forward, and Young Jerry followed, until they halted beneath a steep bank that loomed over the road. Atop the bank sat a squat brick wall topped with an iron rail. Shrouded in the gloom of the bank and wall, they veered off the road and into a dead-end lane, its one side formed by that same wall—now towering eight or ten feet high. Huddled in a corner, squinting up the lane, the next thing Young Jerry spotted was the silhouette of his revered father, starkly outlined against a bleary, cloud-streaked moon, briskly clambering over an iron gate. He cleared it in a moment, then the second fisherman scrambled over, followed by the third. They each landed softly on the ground inside the gate and lay still for a beat—perhaps straining to hear. Then, they crept forward on all fours.

His daily life was of a curious microscopic sort, his whole world being limited to a circuit of a few feet from his person. His familiars were creeping and winged things, and they seemed to enroll him in their band. Bees hummed around his ears with an intimate air, and tugged at the heath and furze-flowers at his side in such numbers as to weigh them down to the sod. The strange amber-coloured butterflies which Egdon produced, and which were never seen elsewhere, quivered in the breath of his lips, alighted upon his bowed back, and sported with the glittering point of his hook as he flourished it up and down. Tribes of emerald-green grasshoppers leaped over his feet, falling awkwardly on their backs, heads, or hips, like unskilful acrobats, as chance might rule; or engaged themselves in noisy flirtations under the fern-fronds with silent ones of homely hue. Huge flies, ignorant of larders and wire-netting, and quite in a savage state, buzzed about him without knowing that he was a man. In and out of the fern-dells snakes glided in their most brilliant blue and yellow guise, it being the season immediately following the shedding of their old skins, when their colours are brightest. Litters of young rabbits came out from their forms to sun themselves upon hillocks, the hot beams blazing through the delicate tissue of each thin-fleshed ear, and firing it to a blood-red transparency in which the veins could be seen. None of them feared him.

His existence had narrowed to a strangely minute scale, his entire world restricted to a small circle immediately surrounding him. The creeping and winged inhabitants of the heath were his constant associates, and they seemed to have inducted him into their society. Bees hummed with an air of intimacy near his head, crowding the heather and furze-blossoms at his side until the weight of their bodies pressed the flowers to the soil. The rare, amber-tinted butterflies found only on Egdon quivered in the very air he exhaled, alighting on his bent back and playing about the shining point of his hook as he swung it. Legions of emerald grasshoppers vaulted over his feet, landing haphazardly on their heads or backs like clumsy acrobats, or pursued noisy romances beneath the ferns with silent, drab-colored mates. Great flies, wild and unacquainted with kitchens or nets, buzzed around him without recognizing him as a human being. In the hollows of the bracken, snakes glided in vivid displays of blue and yellow, their scales gleaming with the fresh intensity that follows the shedding of a skin. Young rabbits ventured from their nests to warm themselves on the slopes, the fierce sun shining through their delicate ears until they glowed a translucent blood-red, exposing every fine vein. Not one of them felt any fear of him.

Yes, perhaps I am jealous; and who could be jealous with more reason than I, a half-blind man, over such a woman as you?”

“No. But I would go with you if that could give you any pleasure; though, as things are, perhaps you already have too much of me. Still, for some reason I wish you did not want to leave. Yes, perhaps I am jealous; and who could have more reason to be jealous than I, a half-blind man, of a woman like you?”

A minute ago, the boy had looked the quiet child, mild, dejected creature that harsh treatment had made him. But his spirit was roused at last; the cruel insult to his dead mother had set his blood on fire. His breast heaved; his attitude was erect; his eye bright and vivid; his whole person changed, as he stood glaring over the cowardly tormentor who now lay crouching at his feet; and defied him with an energy he had never known before.

Only a minute prior, the boy had looked every bit the quiet, mild, dispirited child that unforgiving harsh treatment had molded him into. But his spirit had been roused at last; the cruel slight against his dead mother had set his blood thrumming with white-hot rage. His chest heaved with ragged breaths, his posture pulled rigid and upright, his eyes glowed sharp and vivid with fury; his entire bearing transformed as he stood glowering down at the cowardly tormentor now huddled cringing at his feet, defying the other boy with a fierce, untested energy he had never known himself capable of before.

Of unjust treatment in detention and hardship, and in cruel separation from his wife and child, he foreshadowed the likelihood, or the certainty; but, beyond this, he dreaded nothing distinctly. With this on his mind, which was enough to carry into a dreary prison courtyard, he arrived at the prison of La Force.

He had no doubt he faced the strong likelihood, if not absolute certainty, of unjust abuse while in custody, unforgiving hardship, and the brutal, rending separation from his wife and young child. Yet outside that grim set of fates, no other distinct dread settled in his bones. That heavy, unshakable thought stayed with him as he stepped into the desolate, gray prison courtyard, arriving at last at the heavy iron doors of Saint-Lazare Prison.

We travelled at the time of the vintage and heard the song of the labourers as we glided down the stream. Even I, depressed in mind, and my spirits continually agitated by gloomy feelings, even I was pleased. I lay at the bottom of the boat, and as I gazed on the cloudless blue sky, I seemed to drink in a tranquillity to which I had long been a stranger. And if these were my sensations, who can describe those of Henry? He felt as if he had been transported to Fairy-land and enjoyed a happiness seldom tasted by man. “I have seen,” he said, “the most beautiful scenes of my own country; I have visited the lakes of Lucerne and Uri, where the snowy mountains descend almost perpendicularly to the water, casting black and impenetrable shades, which would cause a gloomy and mournful appearance were it not for the most verdant islands that relieve the eye by their gay appearance; I have seen this lake agitated by a tempest, when the wind tore up whirlwinds of water and gave you an idea of what the water-spout must be on the great ocean; and the waves dash with fury the base of the mountain, where the priest and his mistress were overwhelmed by an avalanche and where their dying voices are still said to be heard amid the pauses of the nightly wind; I have seen the mountains of La Valais, and the Pays de Vaud; but this country, Victor, pleases me more than all those wonders. The mountains of Switzerland are more majestic and strange, but there is a charm in the banks of this divine river that I never before saw equalled. Look at that castle which overhangs yon precipice; and that also on the island, almost concealed amongst the foliage of those lovely trees; and now that group of labourers coming from among their vines; and that village half hid in the recess of the mountain. Oh, surely the spirit that inhabits and guards this place has a soul more in harmony with man than those who pile the glacier or retire to the inaccessible peaks of the mountains of our own country.”

We journeyed during the grape harvest, and the bright, lilting songs of field workers drifted to us as we glided gently down the river. Even I, whose mind hung heavy with despair and whose spirits were endlessly roiled by dark, restless thoughts, found myself unexpectedly cheered. I stretched out on the floor of the boat, and as I stared up at the unblemished cerulean sky, I felt myself breathing in a quiet calm I had not known for years. If such was my state, who could possibly capture what Henry felt? He might have been spirited away to some enchanted realm, savoring a joy so rare few mortals ever knew it.
“I have laid eyes on the most stunning vistas my homeland has to offer,” he said. “I have wandered the shores of Lakes Lucerne and Uri, where snow-capped peaks drop nearly straight down to the water, throwing deep, impenetrable shadows that would lend the whole scene a somber, mournful air if not for the bright, lush islands that catch the eye and soften the view with their vivid warmth. I have watched that same lake roiled by storm, when the wind ripped great columns of water skyward, giving you a perfect sense of what a waterspout must look like out on the open ocean; the waves crashed with unbridled fury against the mountain’s base, the very spot where a priest and his lover were buried by an avalanche, and where local legend says their final cries still drift through the air during lulls in the midnight wind. I have trekked the peaks of La Valais and the rolling hills of the Pays de Vaud, but this land, Victor, delights me more than all those marvels combined. Switzerland’s mountains are grander, more otherworldly, but there is a gentle magic along the banks of this sacred river I have never encountered anywhere else. Look at that castle perched right on the edge of that crag, and the other one on the island, nearly hidden beneath the leaves of those beautiful trees; and see that group of workers making their way home from the vineyards, and that little village tucked away in the mountain’s hollow. Oh, I am certain the spirit that dwells here and watches over this place has a heart far more aligned with humanity than the cold spirits that stack glaciers and hide away in the unreachable, frozen peaks of our home mountains.”

And she began to run forward, with legs that seemed misfits, waving her hands about, and as she ran she saw more and more certainly that this wounded man in strange clothing was Teddy. She ran faster and still faster, stumbling and nearly falling. If she did not get to him speedily the world would burst.

She lurched into a run, legs that felt like they didn’t quite belong to her carrying her forward, her hands flailing wildly as she moved. With every pounding stride, she grew surer and surer that the injured man clad in odd, unrecognizable clothing was Teddy. She pushed herself faster and faster still, stumbling over ruts in the ground and nearly pitching to the dirt a half-dozen times. If she didn’t reach his side right away, she was convinced the entire world would burst apart at the seams.

“Well, yes. The fact is, these autumn races are the ruin of everybody. Never did I see such a day as ’twas! ’Tis a wild open place, just out of Budmouth, and a drab sea rolled in towards us like liquid misery. Wind and rain—good Lord! Dark? Why, ’twas as black as my hat before the last race was run. ’Twas five o’clock, and you couldn’t see the horses till they were almost in, leave alone colours. The ground was as heavy as lead, and all judgment from a fellow’s experience went for nothing. Horses, riders, people, were all blown about like ships at sea. Three booths were blown over, and the wretched folk inside crawled out upon their hands and knees; and in the next field were as many as a dozen hats at one time. Ay, Pimpernel regularly stuck fast, when about sixty yards off, and when I saw Policy stepping on, it did knock my heart against the lining of my ribs, I assure you, my love!”

“Yes, indeed. The truth is, these autumn meets ruin everyone. I’ve never witnessed a day like that one! It’s a bleak, exposed spot just outside Budmouth, and a dull gray sea surged toward us like liquid despair. Wind and rain—good heavens! Darkness? Why, it was pitch-black before the final race ended. It was five o’clock, and you couldn’t spot the horses until they were practically upon you, never mind their silks. The turf was heavy as iron, and all a man’s seasoned judgment counted for nothing. Horses, jockeys, spectators—all were tossed about like vessels in a storm. Three tents collapsed, and the poor souls inside scrambled out on hands and knees; in the next field, a dozen hats were aloft at once. Yes, Pimpernel got utterly bogged down about sixty yards out, and when I saw Policy striding forward, it sent my heart slamming against my ribs, I tell you, my dear!”

When the autumn sun slanted over Greenhill this morning and lighted the dewy flat upon its crest, nebulous clouds of dust were to be seen floating between the pairs of hedges which streaked the wide prospect around in all directions. These gradually converged upon the base of the hill, and the flocks became individually visible, climbing the serpentine ways which led to the top. Thus, in a slow procession, they entered the opening to which the roads tended, multitude after multitude, horned and hornless—blue flocks and red flocks, buff flocks and brown flocks, even green and salmon-tinted flocks, according to the fancy of the colourist and custom of the farm. Men were shouting, dogs were barking, with greatest animation, but the thronging travellers in so long a journey had grown nearly indifferent to such terrors, though they still bleated piteously at the unwontedness of their experiences, a tall shepherd rising here and there in the midst of them, like a gigantic idol amid a crowd of prostrate devotees.

When the morning sun broke over the crest of High Tor and caught the wet meadow on its brow, faint billows of dust could be seen drifting between the parallel hedgerows that scored the broad landscape in every quarter. These slowly drew together toward the foot of the rise, and the herds became distinct one by one, winding up the twisting paths that climbed to the summit. So, in a measured march, they passed into the gateway where the tracks converged, wave upon wave, horned and polled—russet flocks and slate flocks, tawny flocks and dun flocks, even emerald and rose-washed flocks, as the dyer’s whim or the usage of the holding dictated. Men were calling, dogs were yelping, in a high pitch of excitement, but the pressed travellers on so long a road had grown almost callous to such alarms, though they still cried out plaintively at the strangeness of their passage, a gaunt herdsman standing up here and there among them, like a towering fetish amidst a host of kneeling worshippers.

“And I am, in plain reality. The truth is, my dear Charles,” Mr. Lorry glanced at the distant House, and lowered his voice, “you can have no conception of the difficulty with which our business is transacted, and of the peril in which our books and papers over yonder are involved. The Lord above knows what the compromising consequences would be to numbers of people, if some of our documents were seized or destroyed; and they might be, at any time, you know, for who can say that Paris is not set afire to-day, or sacked to-morrow! Now, a judicious selection from these with the least possible delay, and the burying of them, or otherwise getting of them out of harm’s way, is within the power (without loss of precious time) of scarcely any one but myself, if any one. And shall I hang back, when Tellson’s knows this and says this--Tellson’s, whose bread I have eaten these sixty years--because I am a little stiff about the joints? Why, I am a boy, sir, to half a dozen old codgers here!”

“And that’s the honest truth. The fact is, my dear Charles,” Mr. Lorry shifted his gaze toward the distant building and dropped his voice, “you can’t imagine how hard it is to carry on our affairs, or what danger our records and papers over there are facing. Heaven only knows the ruinous fallout that would strike so many people if certain documents were seized or destroyed—and that could happen any moment, as you well know, for who’s to say Paris won’t be in flames today or looted tomorrow! Now, a careful sorting of those papers, with the briefest possible delay, and hiding them, or somehow getting them out of danger, is a task that practically no one but me can manage without wasting precious time. And should I hold back, when Tellson’s knows this and insists on it—Tellson’s, whose livelihood I’ve shared for sixty years—just because my joints are a little creaky? Good Lord, sir, next to half a dozen old fossils here, I’m a mere youngster!”

At fifty tables sat princes and queens clad in all the silks and gems of the world. And now and then one of them would look curiously at Jerry’s fare. They saw a plain figure dressed in a pink silk of the kind that is tempered by the word “foulard,” and a plain face that wore a look of love of life that the queens envied.

Half a hundred tables were crowded with royalty, draped in the finest fabrics and most precious stones the world could offer. Occasionally, one of them would glance inquisitively toward Jerry. They beheld an ordinary woman in a simple pink foulard silk, her face unexceptional but for a vibrant joy for living that the queens themselves secretly coveted.

"I have to go," he said. "I must go right up to London to-day. To an address in Bloomsbury. Then they will tell me how to go to Germany. I must pack and I must get the taxi-cab from the junction and I must go. Why are there no trains on the branch line on Sundays for me to go by it?"

“I have to go,” he said, sharp with unspoken urgency. “I need to get up to London today, to a listed address in Bloomsbury. The people waiting there will tell me exactly how to travel on to Germany. I still have to pack, then grab a taxi from the junction to make the main line connection—why on earth don’t the branch line run any trains on Sundays, right when I need one?”

The place contained two women and two cows. By the side of the latter a steaming bran-mash stood in a bucket. One of the women was past middle age. Her companion was apparently young and graceful; he could form no decided opinion upon her looks, her position being almost beneath his eye, so that he saw her in a bird’s-eye view, as Milton’s Satan first saw Paradise. She wore no bonnet or hat, but had enveloped herself in a large cloak, which was carelessly flung over her head as a covering.

Within the enclosure stood two women and a pair of cows. Beside the animals rested a pail of bran-mash, its warmth rising in faint wisps. The elder of the two had long since crossed the threshold of middle years. Her fellow seemed youthful and lithe in bearing; he could form no settled judgment of her face, for she remained almost directly beneath his gaze, so that he looked down upon her as from a height, much as Milton’s Satan first beheld Paradise. She wore neither bonnet nor hat, but had drawn about herself a heavy cloak, cast loosely over her head like a shroud.

The bay, as he remembered it, was magnificent, with water deep enough to accommodate the largest vessel afloat, and so safe that the South Pacific Directory recommended it as the best careening place for ships for hundreds of miles around. He would buy a schooner—one of those yacht-like, coppered crafts that sailed like witches—and go trading copra and pearling among the islands. He would make the valley and the bay his headquarters. He would build a patriarchal grass house like Tati’s, and have it and the valley and the schooner filled with dark-skinned servitors. He would entertain there the factor of Taiohae, captains of wandering traders, and all the best of the South Pacific riffraff. He would keep open house and entertain like a prince. And he would forget the books he had opened and the world that had proved an illusion.

The cove, as he recalled it, was splendid—its depths ample for the hugest ship on the seas, and so sheltered that the Marquesas Pilot hailed it as the finest careening spot within a thousand-mile radius. He’d acquire a cutter, one of those sleek, copper-sheathed vessels that ran like phantoms, and work the island routes for copra and pearls. The valley and the cove would serve as his base. He’d raise a sprawling native-thatched lodge after the fashion of Tati’s, and stock it and the valley and the cutter with dusky attendants. There he’d host the Taiohae agent, the masters of itinerant traders, and the choicest rogues of the southern seas. He’d keep a generous table and hold court like a nobleman. And he’d put aside the books he’d cracked open and the world that had shown itself a mirage.

Not yet trusting the tones of her voice, she sat down on the bench beside him. He recoiled, but she laid her hand upon his arm. A strange thrill struck him when she did so, and visibly passed over his frame; he laid the knife down softly, as he sat staring at her.

Still too unsteady to trust her voice not to waver, she lowered herself onto the bench next to him. He flinched back at her sudden nearness, but she rested her hand lightly on his upper arm. A strange, thrumming jolt shot through him the second her skin made contact, so sharp it visibly rippled across his entire body; he set the knife down softly, sitting frozen as he stared right at her.

“O yes. I once heard of an Italian, who sat down at a gaming table with only a louis, (that’s a foreign sovereign), in his pocket. He played on for twenty-four hours, and won ten thousand pounds, stripping the bank he had played against. Then there was another man who had lost a thousand pounds, and went to the broker’s next day to sell stock, that he might pay the debt. The man to whom he owed the money went with him in a hackney-coach; and to pass the time they tossed who should pay the fare. The ruined man won, and the other was tempted to continue the game, and they played all the way. When the coachman stopped he was told to drive home again: the whole thousand pounds had been won back by the man who was going to sell.”

“Oh yes. I once heard of an Italian who sat down at a gaming table with only a louis— that is, a foreign coin— in his pocket. He kept playing for twenty-four hours and won ten thousand pounds, cleaning out the bank he was playing against. Then there was another man who had lost a thousand pounds and went to the broker’s the next day to sell stock so he could pay the debt. The man he owed the money to went with him in a hackney coach, and to while away the time they tossed up to see who should pay the fare. The ruined man won, and the other was tempted to go on with the game, so they kept playing all the way. When the coachman stopped, he was told to drive back home again: the whole thousand pounds had been won back by the man who had gone to sell.”

“Wish to consult me on the matter?” reached his ears in the rich, impetuous accents of Eustacia Vye. “Consult me? It is an indignity to me to talk so—I won’t bear it any longer!” She began weeping. “I have loved you, and have shown you that I loved you, much to my regret; and yet you can come and say in that frigid way that you wish to consult with me whether it would not be better to marry Thomasin. Better—of course it would be. Marry her—she is nearer to your own position in life than I am!”

You want my advice on this?" Eustacia Vye’s voice reached him, resonant and full of her usual impulsive fire. "My advice? It’s an insult to even speak to me that way—I won’t stand for it a moment longer!" She broke into tears. "I’ve loved you, and I’ve let that love show, much to my own sorrow; yet you can stand there so coldly and ask if I think it would be better for you to marry Thomasin. Better—naturally it would be. Marry her, then—she fits your social circle far better than I do!

“And there’s another fellow—Parry—an Australian, a statistician and a sporting encyclopaedia. Ask him the grain output of Paraguay for 1903, or the English importation of sheetings into China for 1890, or at what weight Jimmy Britt fought Battling Nelson, or who was welter-weight champion of the United States in ’68, and you’ll get the correct answer with the automatic celerity of a slot-machine. And there’s Andy, a stone-mason, has ideas on everything, a good chess-player; and another fellow, Harry, a baker, red hot socialist and strong union man. By the way, you remember Cooks’ and Waiters’ strike—Hamilton was the chap who organized that union and precipitated the strike—planned it all out in advance, right here in Kreis’s rooms. Did it just for the fun of it, but was too lazy to stay by the union. Yet he could have risen high if he wanted to. There’s no end to the possibilities in that man—if he weren’t so insuperably lazy.”

“And then there’s another bloke—Kael, an Australian, a statistician and walking almanac of all things sports. Ask him the maize yield of Argentina for 1911, or British cotton yarn exports to Japan in 1897, what weight Joe Gans fought Battling Jim Johnson at, or who took the U.S. middleweight champion title in 1872, and you’ll get the right answer with the automatic quickness of a gumball machine spitting out a treat. Then there’s Javi, a stonemason who has opinions on every subject under the sun, and a wicked good chess player to boot; and another guy, Moe, a baker, fiery committed socialist and staunch union man. Oh, by the way, you remember that restaurant workers’ strike a few years back? Elias was the fellow who built that union from scratch and kicked off the strike, mapped every last step of it out beforehand, right here in Rainer’s rooms. He did it just for the rush of pulling it off, but was too lazy to stick with the union once it was up and running. He could have gone far in the labor movement if he’d ever wanted to put in the effort. There’s no end to what that man could do—if he weren’t so insurmountably, unshakably lazy.”

"There's a kind of hardening not only of the body but of the mind through all this life out here. One is living on a different level. You know--just before I came away--you talked of Dower-House-land--and outside. This is outside. It's different. Our men here are kind enough still to little things--kittens or birds or flowers. Behind the front, for example, everywhere there are Tommy gardens. Some are quite bright little patches. But it's just nonsense to suppose we are tender to the wounded up here--and, putting it plainly, there isn't a scrap of pity left for the enemy. Not a scrap. Not a trace of such feeling. They were tender about the wounded in the early days--men tell me--and reverent about the dead. It's all gone now. There have been atrocities, gas, unforgettable things. Everything is harder. Our people are inclined now to laugh at a man who gets hit, and to be annoyed at a man with a troublesome wound. The other day, they say, there was a big dead German outside the Essex trenches. He became a nuisance, and he was dragged in and taken behind the line and buried. After he was buried, a kindly soul was putting a board over him with 'Somebody's Fritz' on it, when a shell burst close by. It blew the man with the board a dozen yards and wounded him, and it restored Fritz to the open air. He was lifted clean out. He flew head over heels like a windmill. This was regarded as a tremendous joke against the men who had been at the pains of burying him. For a time nobody else would touch Fritz, who was now some yards behind his original grave. Then as he got worse and worse he was buried again by some devoted sanitarians, and this time the inscription was 'Somebody's Fritz. R.I.P.' And as luck would have it, he was spun up again. In pieces. The trench howled with laughter and cries of 'Good old Fritz!' 'This isn't the Resurrection, Fritz.'...

A certain calcification occurs out here, affecting the spirit as much as the flesh. We exist on a separate plane entirely. Before I left, you spoke of the "Manor-world" and the "Beyond." Well, this is the Beyond. It is an alien place. Our boys still show a lingering softness for trivialities—stray cats, or a sprig of green. Even near the reserve lines, you’ll find small, vibrant plots tended by the infantry. But don't imagine for a moment that we harbor any gentleness for the broken, and to be blunt, there isn't a shred of compassion remaining for the foe. Not a grain. That sentiment has vanished. They say in the beginning men were careful with the injured and solemn with the slain. That’s a ghost story now. Gas, massacres, and horrors beyond naming have seen to that. Everything has turned to flint. Now, the men are more likely to jeer at a comrade who takes a bullet, or curse one whose injuries prove inconvenient. Just recently, a massive dead Prussian lay before the Suffolk lines. He turned into a stench, so they hauled him in and shoveled him under behind the parapet. Once he was covered, some well-meaning fellow was hammering a plank over the mound—"A Mother’s Fritz" or some such—when a heavy shell struck. It threw the man twenty paces, bleeding, and tossed Fritz back into the light. He was launched skyward. He spun through the air like a broken weather-vane. The whole company thought it a magnificent prank on the burial party. For a while, no one would go near him, and he lay several yards from his first resting place. Eventually, as the rot grew unbearable, a few stretcher-bearers buried him once more, labeling the spot "Fritz’s Final Sleep." Naturally, the luck of the front held, and he was unearthed again. In fragments this time. The trench erupted in mockery, shouting "Bravo, Fritz!" and "Wrong day for the Second Coming, old boy!"...

So Martin thought, and so he spoke when Brissenden urged him to give them hell. He obeyed the mandate, walking up to the platform, as was the custom, and addressing the chairman. He began in a low voice, haltingly, forming into order the ideas which had surged in his brain while the Jew was speaking. In such meetings five minutes was the time allotted to each speaker; but when Martin’s five minutes were up, he was in full stride, his attack upon their doctrines but half completed. He had caught their interest, and the audience urged the chairman by acclamation to extend Martin’s time. They appreciated him as a foeman worthy of their intellect, and they listened intently, following every word. He spoke with fire and conviction, mincing no words in his attack upon the slaves and their morality and tactics and frankly alluding to his hearers as the slaves in question. He quoted Spencer and Malthus, and enunciated the biological law of development.

What she uttered was a lengthened sighing, apparently at something in her mind which had led to her presence here. There was a spasmodic abandonment about it as if, in allowing herself to utter the sound the woman’s brain had authorized what it could not regulate. One point was evident in this; that she had been existing in a suppressed state, and not in one of languor, or stagnation.

What escaped her was a long, drawn-out sigh, as though prompted by some thought that had brought her to this place. The sound came with a sudden, unguarded release, as if her mind had permitted what it could no longer control. One thing was clear from this: she had been living in a state of restraint, not of weariness or stillness.

It was a very dirty place. The rooms upstairs had great high wooden chimney-pieces and large doors, with panelled walls and cornices to the ceiling; which, although they were black with neglect and dust, were ornamented in various ways. From all of these tokens Oliver concluded that a long time ago, before the old Jew was born, it had belonged to better people, and had perhaps been quite gay and handsome: dismal and dreary as it looked now.

The whole building was thick with grime and long neglect. Upstairs, the chambers boasted towering carved wooden mantels, wide solid doorframes, wood-panelled walls, and decorative plaster cornices running along the ceiling’s edge. Though every surface was stained black by years of unaddressed dust and disuse, faint traces of carvings and ornamentation still peeked through the grime. From these faded clues, Luka guessed that long before old Silas, the ragged fence who’d taken him in, had been born, the house had belonged to far more refined people. It must once have been bright and handsome, he thought, hard as that was to believe beneath its current shroud of drear and decay.

His breath went, as it were, out of his body and the cry of anguish which would have escaped him died upon his lips. During the momentary interval that elapsed before he became conscious that something must be done all sense of time and place left him, and it seemed as if he and his mother were as when he was a child with her many years ago on this heath at hours similar to the present. Then he awoke to activity; and bending yet lower he found that she still breathed, and that her breath though feeble was regular, except when disturbed by an occasional gasp.

The wind was knocked out of him, and the agonized scream that rose in his throat never made it past his lips. In the brief pause before he understood he had to do something, he lost all track of time and place. It felt as if he were a young boy again, out on this same moor with his mother just as they used to be at this time of day. Then he jolted back to the task at hand; leaning down even closer, he found that she was still breathing. Though her breath was faint, it remained steady, save for the occasional ragged gasp.

Pinkney!—Joe, that old man nearly went distracted. “Clementina insisted on having Welsh rabbit straight after her lesson,” she explained to Joe. “She’s such an odd little thing, craving Welsh rabbit at five in the afternoon, of all times. General Pinkney was there that day, too—you ought to have seen him sprint for the chafing dish, acting like there wasn’t a single servant in the whole house. I know Clementina’s in poor health; she’s so terribly nervous all the time. When she was serving the rabbit, she spilled a huge amount of it, boiling hot, right across my hand and wrist. It hurt like anything at first, Joe. That dear girl was so distraught over it! But General Pinkney, Joe—that old man almost came undone, he was so upset. He rushed downstairs and sent someone—they said it was the furnace man or another worker from the basement—off to the drugstore for some healing oil and supplies to bind the burn up. It doesn’t hurt nearly as much now, honestly.”

Those frail, venerable old men were always seen by members of the public in the act of bowing, and a popular local belief held that once they had bowed a customer out the door, they kept right on bowing in the empty office until the next patron stepped through the threshold, to be greeted by the same steady, deferential dip of their heads.

“I’m Mister Noah Claypole,” said the charity-boy, “and you’re under me. Take down the shutters, yer idle young ruffian!” With this, Mr. Claypole administered a kick to Oliver, and entered the shop with a dignified air, which did him great credit. It is difficult for a large-headed, small-eyed youth, of lumbering make and heavy countenance, to look dignified under any circumstances; but it is more especially so, when superadded to these personal attractions are a red nose and yellow smalls.

“Name’s Noah Claypole—Mister Claypole, to you,” the charity ward said, “and you answer directly to me. Get those shutters pulled down, you lazy little lout!” With that, Claypole landed a sharp kick to Oliver’s leg, then marched into the shop with a lofty, dignified bearing he clearly thought suited him perfectly. It’s hard enough for a big-headed, beady-eyed, clumsily built, thick-featured teen to pull off dignity at the best of times, but the task becomes outright impossible when you add a blotchy red nose and scuffed yellow knee-breeches to that already unflattering list of traits.

He had often come up here without stating his purpose to his mother; but this was the first time that he had been ostensibly frank as to his purpose while really concealing it. It was a moral situation which, three months earlier, he could hardly have credited of himself. In returning to labour in this sequestered spot he had anticipated an escape from the chafing of social necessities; yet behold they were here also. More than ever he longed to be in some world where personal ambition was not the only recognized form of progress—such, perhaps, as might have been the case at some time or other in the silvery globe then shining upon him. His eye travelled over the length and breadth of that distant country—over the Bay of Rainbows, the sombre Sea of Crises, the Ocean of Storms, the Lake of Dreams, the vast Walled Plains, and the wondrous Ring Mountains—till he almost felt himself to be voyaging bodily through its wild scenes, standing on its hollow hills, traversing its deserts, descending its vales and old sea bottoms, or mounting to the edges of its craters.

He had often before gone up there without telling his mother why; but never yet had he seemed so open about his errand while in truth he was hiding it. Three months earlier, he could scarcely have believed himself capable of such a moral posture. When he came back to work in that remote place, he had hoped to find release from the abrasion of social demands; and yet here, too, they had followed him. More than ever he yearned for some realm in which private ambition was not the sole accepted measure of advancement—perhaps such a world as might once have been reflected in the argent sphere shining down upon him then. His gaze roved across the breadth and length of that far-off country: the Bay of Rainbows, the dark Sea of Crises, the Ocean of Storms, the Lake of Dreams, the immense Walled Plains, and the marvelous Ring Mountains—until he almost seemed to be travelling there in body, standing upon its hollow hills, crossing its deserts, descending its valleys and ancient sea floors, or climbing to the lips of its craters.

In one of the great juvenile periodicals he noted whole columns of incident and anecdote. Here was a chance. His paragraphs were returned, and though he tried repeatedly he never succeeded in placing one. Later on, when it no longer mattered, he learned that the associate editors and sub-editors augmented their salaries by supplying those paragraphs themselves. The comic weeklies returned his jokes and humorous verse, and the light society verse he wrote for the large magazines found no abiding-place. Then there was the newspaper storiette. He knew that he could write better ones than were published. Managing to obtain the addresses of two newspaper syndicates, he deluged them with storiettes. When he had written twenty and failed to place one of them, he ceased. And yet, from day to day, he read storiettes in the dailies and weeklies, scores and scores of storiettes, not one of which would compare with his. In his despondency, he concluded that he had no judgment whatever, that he was hypnotized by what he wrote, and that he was a self-deluded pretender.

In one of the leading juvenile magazines he discovered whole columns of anecdote and incident. Here, surely, was his opening. Yet his paragraphs were sent back, and though he tried again and again, he never managed to get a single one accepted. Much later, when it no longer mattered, he learned that the associate editors and sub-editors padded their salaries by writing those very paragraphs themselves. The comic weeklies rejected his jokes and comic verse, and the light society verse he contributed to the larger magazines found no foothold anywhere. Then there was the newspaper storiette. He knew he could write better ones than the ones in print. After obtaining the addresses of two newspaper syndicates, he showered them with storiettes. When he had written twenty and failed to place even one, he gave up. And yet day after day he read storiettes in the dailies and weeklies—scores upon scores of them—not one of which could bear comparison with his. In his dejection, he came to believe that he possessed no judgment at all, that he was mesmerized by his own writing, and that he was merely a self-deceived pretender.

We put up at the inn in a village just at nightfall, and when I rose next morning and looked abroad, I was ware where a knight came riding in the golden glory of the new day, and recognized him for knight of mine--Sir Ozana le Cure Hardy. He was in the gentlemen's furnishing line, and his missionarying specialty was plug hats. He was clothed all in steel, in the beautifulest armor of the time--up to where his helmet ought to have been; but he hadn't any helmet, he wore a shiny stove-pipe hat, and was ridiculous a spectacle as one might want to see. It was another of my surreptitious schemes for extinguishing knighthood by making it grotesque and absurd. Sir Ozana's saddle was hung about with leather hat boxes, and every time he overcame a wandering knight he swore him into my service and fitted him with a plug and made him wear it. I dressed and ran down to welcome Sir Ozana and get his news.

We came to a village inn just as dusk was falling, and when I rose the next morning and gazed out, I saw a knight riding through the golden blaze of dawn. I knew him at once for one of my own—Sir Ozana le Cure Hardy. His trade was gentlemen’s outfitting, and his particular missionary charge was the promotion of plug hats. He was sheathed from head to foot in steel, clad in the finest armor of the age—up to the point where his helmet should have sat; but he wore no helmet. Instead, a glossy stovepipe hat crowned his head, and a more ludicrous figure you could hardly hope to behold. This was another of my quiet stratagems to kill off knighthood by rendering it grotesque and absurd. Sir Ozana’s saddle was festooned with leather hat-boxes, and whenever he vanquished a knight-errant he made him swear fealty to me, fitted him with a plug hat, and forced him to wear it. I dressed quickly and hurried down to greet Sir Ozana and hear his tidings.

He decided he would not play. He took various people into his confidence. He told Mr. Britling, and Mr. Britling said, "We'll make you full back, where you'll get a hit now and then and not have very much to do. All you have to remember is to hit with the flat side of your stick and not raise it above your shoulders." He told Teddy, and Teddy said, "I strongly advise you to dress as thinly as you can consistently with decency, and put your collar and tie in your pocket before the game begins. Hockey is properly a winter game." He told the maiden aunt-like lady with the prominent nose, and she said almost enviously, "Every one here is asked to play except me. I assuage the perambulator. I suppose one mustn't be envious. I don't see why I shouldn't play. I'm not so old as all that." He told Hugh, and Hugh warned him to be careful not to get hold of one of the sprung sticks. He considered whether it wouldn't be wiser to go to his own room and lock himself in, or stroll off for a walk through Claverings Park. But then he would miss Miss Corner, who was certain, it seemed, to come up for hockey. On the other hand, if he did not miss her he might make himself ridiculous in her eyes, and efface the effect of the green silk stuff with the golden pheasants.

He had made up his mind that he would not play. Yet he confided this resolution to several people in turn. He told Mr. Harbury, and Mr. Harbury said, “We shall put you at full-back, where you’ll have a stroke now and then and not much else to trouble you. The only thing to remember is to strike with the flat of your stick and never lift it above your shoulders.” He told Ned, and Ned replied, “My best advice is that you dress as lightly as decency will allow, and put your collar and tie in your pocket before the game starts. Hockey is, by rights, a winter game.” He mentioned it to the elderly lady with the sharp nose and the air of a maiden aunt, and she said, almost with envy, “Everybody here is asked except me. I mind the perambulator. One mustn’t be jealous, I suppose. I don’t know why I shouldn’t play. I’m not nearly as old as all that.” He told Philip, and Philip cautioned him to be very sure he did not get hold of one of the springed sticks. He began to wonder whether it would not be wiser to retire to his own room and lock the door, or else wander away for a walk in Cedarings Park. But then he would miss Miss Ware, who, it seemed certain, was coming up for hockey. Yet if he did not miss her, he might only expose himself to ridicule in her eyes, and wipe out the effect of the green silk gown with the golden pheasants.

“I do not think so. I do not think,” said Doctor Manette with the firmness of self-conviction, “that anything but the one train of association would renew it. I think that, henceforth, nothing but some extraordinary jarring of that chord could renew it. After what has happened, and after his recovery, I find it difficult to imagine any such violent sounding of that string again. I trust, and I almost believe, that the circumstances likely to renew it are exhausted.”

“I don’t see that playing out. Not at all,” Doctor Manette said, his tone steady with the unshakable certainty of someone who knows their own mind fully. “I can’t imagine anything besides that exact chain of linked triggers bringing it back. From here on out, I truly believe only an extraordinarily harsh, unexpected jolt to that old, tender nerve could set it off again. After everything that’s unfolded, and given how fully he’s recovered, I can barely picture that nerve ever being struck that violently a second time. I’m confident, almost to the point of total certainty, that all the circumstances that might be capable of reigniting it are gone for good.”

How often did Oliver start from his bed that night, and stealing out, with noiseless footstep, to the staircase, listen for the slightest sound from the sick chamber! How often did a tremble shake his frame, and cold drops of terror start upon his brow, when a sudden trampling of feet caused him to fear that something too dreadful to think of, had even then occurred! And what had been the fervency of all the prayers he had ever muttered, compared with those he poured forth, now, in the agony and passion of his supplication for the life and health of the gentle creature, who was tottering on the deep grave’s verge!

How many times did Marcus spring from his cot that evening, and slip out, with silent tread, to the stairwell, straining to catch any murmur from the invalid’s room! How many times did a shudder ripple through his limbs, and icy beads of dread break out across his temples, when an abrupt clatter of footsteps made him panic that some horror too awful to name had already come to pass! And what was the heat of all the entreaties he had ever whispered, set beside those he now released, in the torment and fervor of his pleading for the life and strength of the tender soul who wavered on the dark pit’s brink!

“Those were his words,” said Nancy, glancing uneasily round, as she had scarcely ceased to do, since she began to speak, for a vision of Sikes haunted her perpetually. “And more. When he spoke of you and the other lady, and said it seemed contrived by Heaven, or the devil, against him, that Oliver should come into your hands, he laughed, and said there was some comfort in that too, for how many thousands and hundreds of thousands of pounds would you not give, if you had them, to know who your two-legged spaniel was.”

“That’s what he said,” Nancy replied, her gaze flicking nervously about the room—a habit she’d barely broken since she started talking, for Sikes’s specter never left her. “And worse. When he mentioned you and the other woman, claiming it felt like Heaven itself, or the devil, had schemed against him by putting Oliver in your path, he let out a laugh and said even that brought a kind of satisfaction, because how many thousands and hundreds of thousands of pounds wouldn’t you hand over, if you could, just to learn who your two-legged lapdog really was.”

Now, as they thus pursued their way, the reddleman occasionally left his companion’s side, and, stepping behind the van, looked into its interior through a small window. The look was always anxious. He would then return to the old man, who made another remark about the state of the country and so on, to which the reddleman again abstractedly replied, and then again they would lapse into silence. The silence conveyed to neither any sense of awkwardness; in these lonely places wayfarers, after a first greeting, frequently plod on for miles without speech; contiguity amounts to a tacit conversation where, otherwise than in cities, such contiguity can be put an end to on the merest inclination, and where not to put an end to it is intercourse in itself.

As they continued their journey, the reddleman would occasionally drift away from his companion and move to the back of the wagon, peering through a small window into its interior. Each time he looked, his expression was one of deep concern. He would then rejoin the old man, who might offer another observation about the local landscape, to which the reddleman would give a distracted reply before they both lapsed back into quiet. Neither of them found this silence uncomfortable; in such desolate regions, travelers often walk together for miles without speaking after their initial greetings. In these isolated places, proximity serves as a silent form of communication, especially since—unlike in a crowded city—such closeness can be ended at any moment. To choose to remain together is, in itself, a kind of conversation.
